# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students frequently write *"BAS = SCADA"* — this is wrong. **SCADA is only the supervisory tier of a BAS**, not the whole system.
> 2. Many students confuse **BACnet** (an application-layer protocol) with **Modbus TCP** (a transport-layer protocol). Use the **ISO/IEC stack** language precisely.
> 3. When asked for *set-point values*, do **not** give generic numbers. Use the KTU/Indian standard: **24 ± 1 °C** for indoor air, **500 lux** for offices, **< 1000 ppm $CO_2$**.
> 4. Marks are deducted for using the term *"automation"* in place of *"closed-loop feedback control"* in CPS-context questions.
> 5. Always mention the **cyber-physical feedback loop** explicitly — KTU 2024 specifically tests the CPS framing.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 1]`
**Define Building Automation System. List any four sub-systems integrated by a modern BAS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Building Automation System (BAS) is a centrally monitored, distributed control network that integrates the mechanical, electrical, and plumbing services of a building to optimise comfort, energy, and safety. The four key sub-systems are:
1. **HVAC** (Heating, Ventilation & Air-Conditioning) — temperature, humidity, $CO_2$ control.
2. **Lighting Control** — dimming, occupancy switching, daylight harvesting.
3. **Fire Detection & Suppression** — smoke dampers, alarms, sprinkler interlocks.
4. **Access Control & Security** — card readers, CCTV integration, intrusion detection.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing four sub-systems with one-line purpose.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 1]`
**What is BACnet? State two of its physical-layer options.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**BACnet** (*Building Automation and Control Networks*) is the ASHRAE standard **ANSI/ASHRAE 135-2020** designed specifically for communication between building automation devices, independent of any single vendor.
Two physical-layer options:
1. **BACnet MS-TP** (Master-Slave/Token-Passing) over EIA-485 twisted pair at **9.6–76.8 kbps** — used at the field level.
2. **BACnet/IP** over Ethernet / 10BASE-T or 100BASE-TX at **10/100 Mbps** — used at the supervisory/management level.

*(1 mark for full form + meaning, 1 mark each for the two physical options.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 1]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-tier architecture** of a Building Automation System. Identify the cyber and physical entities at each tier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working of a **closed-loop PID temperature controller** for an HVAC zone. Derive the Ziegler-Nichols tuning relations and apply them to a FOPDT plant with $K=1.2$, $L=45\,\text{s}$, $T_u=180\,\text{s}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
A modern BAS is a Cyber-Physical System organised in four hierarchical tiers, defined by ISO 16484-5 (BACnet) and EN 15232.

**Step 2 — Four-Tier Description (4 marks):**
1. **Tier 1 — Field Level (Physical):** Sensors (temperature, $CO_2$, PIR, lux) and actuators (valves, dampers, VFDs, relays). *Cyber entity: sensor ASIC / transducer electronics. Physical entity: actual air, water, light.*
2. **Tier 2 — Automation Level (Cyber):** Direct Digital Controllers (DDC). Runs PID loops every 1–5 s. *Cyber: microcontroller + firmware. Physical: regulated equipment.*
3. **Tier 3 — Supervisory / Management Level (Cyber):** SCADA / BMS server with HMI, historian, alarming. *Cyber: SCADA software. Physical: room as observed entity.*
4. **Tier 4 — Enterprise / Cloud Level (Cyber-extension):** IoT cloud, digital twin, AI analytics. *Cyber: ML models. Physical: aggregated building consumption.*

**Step 3 — Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1**.

**Step 4 — CPS Perspective (1 mark):**
The cyber feedback loop spans tiers 1→2→3→1, with the plant (tier-1 physical) closing the loop. Set-points cascade down; measurements cascade up.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — PID Equation (2 marks):**
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$
Where $e(t) = T_{sp} - T_{measured}$.

**Step 2 — Working Explanation (1 mark):**
The DDC samples $T_{measured}$ every $T_s$ (e.g., 1 s), computes the error, and outputs a valve position in % (typically 0–10 V or 4–20 mA). The valve modulates chilled-water flow; the coil removes heat; the room temperature responds and the loop closes.

**Step 3 — Ziegler-Nichols Derivation (2 marks):**
For a FOPDT plant $G(s) = \frac{K e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$, the ultimate gain is approximated as $K_u \approx \frac{\tau}{K L}$. The Z-N rules for PID are:
$$K_p = 0.6\,K_u, \quad T_i = 0.5\,T_u, \quad T_d = 0.125\,T_u$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Computation (2 marks):**
Given $K = 1.2$, $L = 45\,\text{s}$, $T_u = 180\,\text{s}$, with $\tau = K L K_u = K L \cdot \frac{\tau}{K L}$ ... we first estimate $K_u$. Using $\tau = 90\,\text{s}$ (assumed from plant dynamics):

$$K_u = \frac{\tau}{K L} = \frac{90}{1.2 \cdot 45} = \frac{90}{54} = 1.667$$

$$K_p = 0.6 \cdot 1.667 = 1.0$$

$$T_i = 0.5 \cdot 180 = 90\,\text{s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{1.0}{90} = 0.0111\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$T_d = 0.125 \cdot 180 = 22.5\,\text{s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_d = K_p T_d = 1.0 \cdot 22.5 = 22.5\,\text{s}$$

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 1]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain **BACnet, KNX, Modbus, and OPC UA** communication protocols used in Building Automation. Compare them on the basis of OSI-layer support, media, and typical application domain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 m² office zone has a sensible cooling load of $Q_s = 3.5\,\text{kW}$. The supply-air cooling coil must lower air temperature by $\Delta T = 8\,°C$. The supply fan delivers air at $\rho = 1.2\,\text{kg/m³}$, $c_p = 1.005\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$. Calculate the required supply airflow rate. If a $CO_2$-based DCV strategy reduces outside-air fraction by 40% during unoccupied hours, compute the daily fan-energy savings. Assume fan runs 10 h/day with specific fan power $SFP = 1.5\,\text{kW/(m³/s)}$ and 60% of the original air volume is required in DCV mode. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**Step 1 — BACnet (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** ASHRAE Standard 135, application-layer protocol designed for BAS interoperability.
- **OSI Coverage:** Mainly application + network layers, with four physical options (MS-TP, ARCNET, Ethernet, IP).
- **Use case:** HVAC, multi-vendor integration.

**Step 2 — KNX (1.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** ISO/IEC 14543-3, formerly EIB. Covers all 7 OSI layers.
- **Media:** Twisted Pair (TP1), Powerline (PL), Radio (RF), IP.
- **Use case:** Home and commercial building control, lighting & blinds.

**Step 3 — Modbus (1.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Modicon (1979), IEC 61158, application-layer master-slave.
- **Variants:** RTU (RS-485) and TCP/IP.
- **Use case:** Energy meters, VFDs, simple sensor reads.

**Step 4 — OPC UA (1 mark):**
- **Definition:** IEC 62541, modern industrial CPS protocol with information-modelling.
- **Use case:** Industry 4.0, digital twin integration, secure cloud connectivity.

**Step 5 — Comparison (1 mark):** Use the table in Section 2.4 as the basis; highlight that **BACnet** is BAS-specific while **OPC UA** is cross-domain and CPS-friendly.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Step 1 — Sensible Heat Equation (1 mark):**
$$Q_s = \rho \cdot \dot{V} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Solve for airflow $\dot{V}$ (2 marks):**
$$\dot{V} = \frac{Q_s}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{3500\,\text{W}}{1.2 \cdot 1005 \cdot 8}$$

$$\dot{V} = \frac{3500}{9648} = 0.3628\,\text{m³/s}$$

Convert to **CMH**: $\dot{V} = 0.3628 \cdot 3600 = 1306\,\text{CMH}$.

**Step 3 — Fan power without DCV (1 mark):**
$$P_{fan,full} = SFP \cdot \dot{V} = 1.5 \cdot 0.3628 = 0.5442\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Fan power with DCV (1 mark):**
Airflow in DCV mode = $0.6 \times 0.3628 = 0.2177\,\text{m³/s}$.

$$P_{fan,DCV} = 1.5 \cdot 0.2177 = 0.3266\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Daily Energy Savings (2 marks):**
$$E_{saved,day} = (P_{fan,full} - P_{fan,DCV}) \cdot t_{day}$$

$$E_{saved,day} = (0.5442 - 0.3266) \cdot 10 = 2.176\,\text{kWh/day}$$

$$\text{Annual saving} = 2.176 \cdot 365 = 794.24\,\text{kWh/year}$$

Assuming a tariff of ₹8/unit, **monetary saving ≈ ₹6,354/year** for one zone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Building Automation**

- **Definition:** BAS is a **distributed CPS** integrating MEP sub-systems through a feedback loop.
- **Architecture:** Always remember the four tiers — *Field → Automation → Management → Enterprise*.
- **Cyber side:** DDC, SCADA, IoT cloud, digital twin, ML analytics.
- **Physical side:** HVAC equipment, lighting, fire dampers, access hardware.
- **Core equation:** PID formula $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ — must be memorised verbatim.
- **Valve law:** Equal-percentage $Q = Q_{max} \cdot R^{(u-100)/100}$.
- **Energy savings formula:** $E_{saved} = E_{baseline} \cdot (1 - DCF)$.
- **Sensible heat:** $Q_s = \rho \dot{V} c_p \Delta T$ for air-side; $Q_s = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ for water-side.
- **Standard set-points:** 24 ± 1 °C indoor; 500 lux offices; < 1000 ppm $CO_2$; 7 °C chilled water supply.
- **Protocols:** BACnet (ASHRAE 135), KNX (ISO/IEC 14543), Modbus (IEC 61158), OPC UA (IEC 62541), MQTT (ISO/IEC 20922).
- **BACnet MS-TP** speed: 9.6–76.8 kbps; **KNX TP1** speed: 9.6 kbps; **Modbus RTU** speed: up to 115.2 kbps.
- **Ziegler-Nichols PID:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
- **CPS viewpoint:** Always mention *cyber feedback*, *physical actuation*, *networked intelligence*, and *safety-critical coupling* in essay answers.
- **Indian context:** Reference **ECBC 2017**, **IS 3646**, and **NFPA-72** whenever energy/compliance questions appear.
- **Avoid traps:** Don't equate BAS with SCADA; don't say "PID is an algorithm" without naming the three terms (P, I, D); don't confuse BACnet with Modbus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medical CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Medical CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🩺 Medical Cyber-Physical Systems (Medical CPS)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Medical Cyber-Physical System (Medical CPS)** is an engineered computational-physical integration that senses, computes, communicates, and actuates across the human physiological boundary in a **closed-loop**, **real-time**, and **safety-critical** manner to monitor, model, and control patient state and therapeutic delivery.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECPT723)** syllabus, a Medical CPS is a coordinated network of medical devices, sensors, actuators, and computational elements that interact continuously with physical (biological) processes of the human body. The system must guarantee **deterministic timing**, **fault tolerance**, and **biocompatibility** while satisfying stringent regulatory standards (e.g., **FDA Class II/III**, **IEC 62304**, **ISO 13485**, **ISO 14971**).

Mathematically, a Medical CPS can be abstracted as a **hybrid dynamical system** $\mathcal{H} = (Q, X, f, \text{Dom}, \text{Reset}, \text{Inv})$ interacting with the patient's physiology $\mathcal{P}(t)$ where:

$$y(t) = \mathcal{C}(\mathcal{P}(t), x(t), u(t))$$

with $y(t)$ as the controlled physiological output, $x(t)$ as the system state, and $u(t)$ as the therapeutic actuation input.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Medical CPS as a "Self-Driving Co-Pilot" sitting inside the human body.**

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. The car's **Adaptive Cruise Control** constantly:
1. **Senses** the distance to the car ahead (radar/camera),
2. **Computes** whether to brake or accelerate (ECU software),
3. **Actuates** the throttle or brakes (physical mechanism).

Now, replace the highway with the **human bloodstream**, the radar with a **glucose sensor**, the ECU with an **embedded controller**, and the brake/throttle with an **insulin pump**. That, in essence, is a Medical CPS — a **closed-loop artificial pancreas** for a diabetic patient.

| Automobile (Analogy) | Medical CPS (Reality) |
|---|---|
| Radar / Camera | Glucose / ECG / EEG sensor |
| ECU (Engine Control Unit) | Embedded microcontroller |
| Brake / Throttle actuator | Insulin pump / Pacemaker lead |
| Dashboard telemetry | Wireless Body Area Network (WBAN) |
| Traffic rules | FDA / IEC 62304 safety standards |

---

## 1.3 Why Medical CPS? — Motivation & Drivers

> [!TIP]
> **Key Drivers of Medical CPS Adoption (KTU 2024 High-Yield):**
> - 🏥 **Aging Population**: Globally, the population aged **60+** is projected to reach **1.4 billion** by 2030 (WHO).
> - 🦠 **Chronic Disease Burden**: Diabetes, cardiovascular disease (CVD), and neurological disorders demand **continuous** (not episodic) monitoring.
> - 📉 **Reducing Human Error**: Closed-loop automation eliminates manual dosing errors.
> - 🌐 **Telehealth Boom**: 5G/WBAN enables **remote** and **ambulatory** care.
> - 🤖 **AI-Driven Diagnostics**: On-device machine learning enables **predictive** intervention.

---

## 1.4 Core Salient Properties of Medical CPS

A Medical CPS differs from a generic IoT device on five non-negotiable axes:

1. **Safety-Criticality** — A single failure can cause irreversible patient harm or death. Standards like **IEC 61508 SIL 3** and **ISO 26262 ASIL D** (adapted) apply.
2. **Hard Real-Time Constraint** — Latency must be **bounded deterministically** (e.g., pacemaker response window: **< 100 ms**).
3. **Closed-Loop Bio-Interaction** — Bidirectional coupling with living tissue; the *patient is in the loop*.
4. **Biocompatibility & Sterility** — Materials must satisfy **ISO 10993** (cytotoxicity, sensitization, irritation).
5. **Regulatory & Ethical Compliance** — FDA 510(k) clearance, MDR (EU), HIPAA (privacy), and ethical review (IRB).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis of Medical CPS

## 2.1 Generic Functional Architecture

A Medical CPS, in its canonical form, is a **sense–decide–actuate** triad stacked over a **network layer** and a **safety shell**.

| Layer | Function | Medical-CPS Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Physical (Patient)** | Physiological processes to monitor/control | Blood glucose concentration, cardiac rhythm |
| **2. Sensing** | Convert physiological signals → electrical | Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM), ECG electrodes, SpO₂ pulse oximeter |
| **3. Computation** | Signal processing, control law, ML inference | Embedded ARM Cortex-M4, FPGA, ASIC |
| **4. Communication** | Secure data exchange | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), ZigBee, WBAN (IEEE 802.15.6) |
| **5. Actuation** | Deliver therapy to the patient | Insulin pump, defibrillator, neurostimulator |
| **6. Safety & Monitoring** | Watchdog, fault detection, fail-safe state | HeartLogic™, Medtronic CareLink™ |

---

## 2.2 Core Control-Theoretic Formulation

Every Medical CPS implements some variant of a **closed-loop feedback controller**. The plant is the **human physiological subsystem** (e.g., glycemic dynamics, cardiac conduction).

The **error signal** is defined as:

$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

where $r(t)$ is the reference (setpoint) — e.g., target glucose $= 110$ mg/dL, and $y(t)$ is the measured physiological output.

The **controller output** is:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

This is the classic **PID controller** — the workhorse of nearly all commercial Medical CPS. For example, the FDA-approved Medtronic 670G artificial pancreas uses a **PID with insulin-on-board (IOB)** logic.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations — they are exam-favorite formulas for the 2024 Scheme.**

| # | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** — sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest signal frequency (Hz) |
| 2 | $T_{latency} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{actuate}$ | **End-to-end latency budget** of a closed-loop medical device (s, ms) |
| 3 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | **Reliability** as a function of time $t$ and failure rate $\lambda$ (failures/hour) |
| 4 | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | **Mean Time To Failure** for constant-failure-rate systems (hours) |
| 5 | $P_{budget} = P_{sensor} + P_{comp} + P_{comm} + P_{act}$ | **Total power budget** of a wearable/implantable (mW) |
| 6 | $SIL = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD})$ | **Safety Integrity Level**, PFD = Probability of Failure on Demand |
| 7 | $J = \int_0^T \big( q \, e^2(t) + r \, u^2(t) \big) \, dt$ | **LQR cost function** for optimal control (linear quadratic regulator) |
| 8 | $B_{WLAN} = N \cdot f_s \cdot b$ | **Aggregate WBAN bandwidth** for $N$ sensors each with $f_s$ sampling and $b$ bits per sample (bps) |
| 9 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** of biosignal acquisition (dB) |
| 10 | $T_{deadline} \leq T_{period} \quad \text{(Hard RT)}$ | **Hard real-time scheduling** constraint: deadline must be ≤ period |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Marking Tip:** When writing `|x|` or absolute-value terms in a table, the LaTeX rendering will be lost. Use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` to keep equations visible.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

Medical CPS underpins the **next-generation healthcare paradigm** often called **P5 Medicine**: *Predictive, Preventive, Personalized, Participatory, and Precision*.

- **Implantable CPS**: Pacemakers (e.g., Medtronic Azure™), cochlear implants, deep brain stimulators (DBS) for Parkinson's.
- **Wearable CPS**: Apple Watch ECG, Dexcom G7 CGM, Empatica Embrace (seizure detection).
- **Surgical CPS**: Da Vinci Surgical System (master-slave tele-robotics with haptic feedback).
- **Hospital CPS**: Smart infusion pumps (e.g., Hospira Plum 360™) with drug-library enforcement.
- **Closed-Loop Drug Delivery**: Automatic titration of vasopressors in ICUs via model-based control.

---

## 2.5 The Three Pillars of Medical CPS Design

A robust Medical CPS design must simultaneously satisfy:

1. **Functional Correctness** — does the algorithm produce the right control action?
2. **Temporal Correctness** — does it do so *within the deadline*?
3. **Safety & Security** — does it fail *safely* and resist cyber-attacks?

This is captured formally in the **3-CPS Design Triangle** that examiners frequently test.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Latency Budget of a Closed-Loop Pacemaker

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A cardiac pacemaker has the following sub-system latencies: $T_{sense}=5$ ms, $T_{compute}=12$ ms, $T_{comm}=8$ ms, and $T_{actuate}=3$ ms. Compute (a) the end-to-end latency, (b) the maximum sustainable heart-rate detection frequency, and (c) verify hard real-time feasibility if the cardiac period at 120 bpm is given.

**Given:**

$$T_{sense} = 5 \text{ ms}, \quad T_{compute} = 12 \text{ ms}, \quad T_{comm} = 8 \text{ ms}, \quad T_{actuate} = 3 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 1 — Compute End-to-End Latency

The end-to-end latency for any closed-loop CPS is the **sum of all sequential sub-system delays**:

$$T_{latency} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{actuate}$$

Substituting the values:

$$T_{latency} = 5 + 12 + 8 + 3$$

$$\boxed{T_{latency} = 28 \text{ ms}}$$

### Step 2 — Maximum Sustainable Detection Frequency

For a pacemaker to detect every beat, its sampling/sensing loop must run at least at the **Nyquist rate** relative to the highest physiological frequency. Assume the highest cardiac event frequency of interest is $f_{max} = 4$ Hz (240 bpm, supra-ventricular tachycardia). Then:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ Hz}$$

Equivalently, the **minimum period**:

$$T_{s,min} = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \text{ s} = 125 \text{ ms}$$

Since $T_{latency} = 28$ ms $\ll$ 125 ms, the **closed loop is temporally feasible** with a comfortable margin.

### Step 3 — Hard Real-Time Verification at 120 bpm

A heart rate of 120 bpm corresponds to a cardiac period:

$$T_{period} = \frac{60 \text{ s}}{120} = 0.5 \text{ s} = 500 \text{ ms}$$

Hard real-time feasibility requires:

$$T_{deadline} \leq T_{period}$$

$$28 \text{ ms} \leq 500 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Satisfied)}$$

**Conclusion:** The pacemaker meets the hard real-time deadline with **~94.4%** temporal headroom.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Reliability and MTTF of an Implantable Insulin Pump

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A medical implantable insulin pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. Compute (a) reliability after 10 000 operating hours, (b) MTTF, and (c) the probability that it survives 3 years continuously (assume 1 year = 8760 hours).

### Step 1 — Reliability After $t = 10\,000$ h

Using the exponential reliability model (valid for the useful life phase of the bathtub curve):

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$R(10\,000) = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-6}) \times (10\,000)}$$

$$R(10\,000) = e^{-0.02}$$

$$\boxed{R(10\,000) \approx 0.9802 \text{ (98.02%)}}$$

### Step 2 — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = 500\,000 \text{ hours} \approx 57.08 \text{ years}}$$

### Step 3 — Survival Probability Over 3 Years

Total operating time:

$$t = 3 \times 8760 = 26\,280 \text{ hours}$$

$$R(26\,280) = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-6}) \times (26\,280)}$$

$$R(26\,280) = e^{-0.05256}$$

$$\boxed{R(26\,280) \approx 0.9488 \text{ (94.88%)}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The pump has a **94.88% chance of operating continuously for 3 years without failure**, which is acceptable per FDA Class III guidelines (typically requiring survival probability $\geq 90\%$ over the device's labeled lifetime).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: SIL Classification from PFD

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A closed-loop anesthesia delivery system has a Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) of $10^{-4}$. Determine its Safety Integrity Level.

**Formula:**

$$SIL = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD})$$

**Computation:**

$$SIL = -\log_{10}(10^{-4}) = -(-4) = 4$$

**However**, by **IEC 61508**, the SIL scale is bounded:

| SIL Range | PFD Range | Risk Reduction Factor |
|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1000 |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1000 to 10 000 |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 10 000 to 100 000 |

Therefore, $\text{PFD} = 10^{-4}$ falls at the **lower bound of SIL 4** but the **upper bound of SIL 3** in the continuous-mode interpretation. For a Medical CPS, the system is classified as **SIL 3 (high-demand mode)** per the standard.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Canonical Medical CPS

| Parameter | Cardiac Pacemaker | Artificial Pancreas | Da Vinci Surgical Robot |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Closed-loop?** | Yes (VVI, DDD modes) | Yes (PID + IOB) | Yes (master–slave) |
| **Typical Latency** | $\leq 100$ ms | 1–5 min (glucose dynamics are slow) | $\leq 10$ ms (haptic feedback) |
| **Sampling Rate** | 200–1000 Hz (intracardiac ECG) | Every 1–5 min (CGM) | 1 kHz (force/torque sensor) |
| **Power Source** | Lithium-iodine battery (7–10 yrs) | Rechargeable Li-ion / disposable pod | Mains AC + UPS backup |
| **Communication** | Inductive telemetry (MICS band 402–405 MHz) | BLE to smartphone | Proprietary fiber-optic + Ethernet |
| **Regulatory Class** | FDA Class III | FDA Class III | FDA Class II |
| **SIL / Risk Class** | SIL 3 | SIL 3 | SIL 2 (with redundancy) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why these numbers matter for the KTU exam:** Examiners frequently ask students to *justify* the sampling rate, latency, or battery choice. Memorize at least one numeric value per device to score full marks.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Optimal LQR Controller Gain

For advanced questions, the **Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR)** minimizes:

$$J = \int_0^\infty \left( x^T Q x + u^T R u \right) dt$$

The optimal state-feedback gain $K$ solves the **Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE)**:

$$A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0$$

$$K = R^{-1} B^T P$$

where $P$ is the unique positive semi-definite solution of the ARE. In Medical CPS, $Q$ weights **patient-state deviation** (e.g., glucose error) and $R$ weights **actuator effort** (e.g., insulin infused). This balances *therapeutic aggression* with *hypoglycemia avoidance*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Medical CPS Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    patientA[Patient Physiology] -->|biosignals| sensorA[Sensor Layer ECG Glucose SpO2]
    sensorA -->|analog/digital| acqA[Signal Acquisition ADC Filter]
    acqA -->|clean data| compA[Computation Layer MCU DSP FPGA]
    compA -->|control signal u of t| actA[Actuator Layer Drug Pump Defibrillator]
    actA -->|therapy| patientA
    compA -->|wireless BLE WBAN| cloudA[Cloud Clinician App EHR]
    compA -->|watchdog| safetyA[Safety Shell Fault Handler Watchdog]
    safetyA -.->|fail safe| compA
    cloudA -->|physician override| compA
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Artificial Pancreas Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    refA[Target Glucose 110 mg per dL] --> compA2[Error Calculation e t equals r minus y]
    yA[Measured Glucose CGM] --> compA2
    compA2 --> ctrlA[PID Controller with IOB]
    ctrlA --> actA2[Insulin Pump Actuation]
    actA2 --> bodyA[Patient Bloodstream]
    bodyA --> yA
    ctrlA -->|if glucose less than 70| safetyA2[Hypo Safety Supervisor Suspend Insulin]
    safetyA2 -.->|override| ctrlA
```

## 4.3 Layered Stack View of a Medical CPS Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer Clinical Decision Support Telemetry Dashboard]
    L2[Middleware Layer HL7 FHIR Data Harmonization Security]
    L3[Real Time OS Layer FreeRTOS VxWorks QNX]
    L4[Hardware Abstraction Layer ADC PWM GPIO I2C SPI]
    L5[Physical Sensor and Actuator Layer]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

## 4.4 Master–Slave Surgical Robot (Da Vinci-style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    surgeonA[Surgeon Console Master] -->|input motion| encA[Encoder Force Sensor]
    encA -->|digitized commands| ctrlA3[Controller FPGA]
    ctrlA3 -->|low-latency bus| robotA[Patient-Side Slave Manipulator]
    robotA -->|tissue interaction| tissueA[Patient Anatomy]
    tissueA -->|force feedback| robotA
    robotA -->|haptic telemetry| encA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

> Define **Medical Cyber-Physical System (Medical CPS)**. List **three** distinguishing features that differentiate it from a generic IoT device.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Medical CPS** is an engineered closed-loop system that integrates sensors, computation, communication, and actuators to monitor and control physiological processes in real time, with safety and reliability as first-class design constraints.

**Three distinguishing features (1 mark each):**

1. **Closed-loop bio-interaction** — the patient is in the control loop; actuation directly alters physiology.
2. **Hard real-time & safety-critical** — bounded latency, deterministic response, and fail-safe states (e.g., pacemaker, infusion pump).
3. **Regulatory rigour** — must comply with **FDA / IEC 62304 / ISO 14971** and pass biocompatibility testing (ISO 10993).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> State the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem** and justify why ECG signals in a cardiac monitor are typically sampled at $\geq 500$ Hz.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$f_s \geq 2 \, f_{max}$$

ECG signals contain clinically relevant components (QRS complex, high-frequency notches) up to $f_{max} \approx 150$ Hz. By Nyquist, $f_s \geq 300$ Hz. However, **clinical practice samples at 500–1000 Hz** to (1) preserve ST-segment morphology, (2) accommodate pacemaker spike detection, and (3) allow anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture** of a generic Medical CPS. Identify **four** essential layers and state the role of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A wearable pulse oximeter has the following sub-system power consumptions: $P_{sensor} = 4$ mW, $P_{compute} = 6$ mW, $P_{comm} = 3$ mW, and $P_{actuate} = 1$ mW. The device is powered by a 200 mAh, 3.7 V Li-ion battery. Compute the **total power budget** and the **continuous operating time in hours**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.1** (High-Level Medical CPS Block Architecture). The four essential layers are:

1. **Sensing Layer** — Acquires physiological signals (ECG, glucose, SpO₂).
2. **Computation Layer** — Runs control algorithms, signal processing, ML inference.
3. **Communication Layer** — Secure wireless transmission (BLE, WBAN, MICS).
4. **Actuation Layer** — Delivers therapy (insulin, defibrillation, stimulation).

**Cross-cutting Safety Layer (1 mark):** watchdog, fault detection, fail-safe state.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Total Power Budget (3 marks):**

$$P_{budget} = P_{sensor} + P_{compute} + P_{comm} + P_{actuate}$$

$$P_{budget} = 4 + 6 + 3 + 1 = 14 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 2 — Battery Energy (2 marks):**

Battery energy in watt-hours:

$$E = V \times Q = 3.7 \text{ V} \times 200 \text{ mAh} = 0.74 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 3 — Continuous Operating Time (2 marks):**

$$T_{op} = \frac{E}{P_{budget}} = \frac{0.74 \text{ Wh}}{0.014 \text{ W}}$$

$$\boxed{T_{op} \approx 52.86 \text{ hours} \approx 2.2 \text{ days}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice, KTU Pattern]

> **(a)** Explain the concept of a **closed-loop artificial pancreas** for Type-1 diabetes. Derive the PID control law and discuss **two** safety challenges. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cardiac pacemaker has $T_{sense}=4$ ms, $T_{compute}=10$ ms, $T_{comm}=6$ ms, $T_{actuate}=2$ ms. Compute the end-to-end latency, verify **hard real-time feasibility** at 100 bpm, and state the **sampling frequency** required to detect ventricular fibrillation (assumed $f_{max} = 6$ Hz). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

A **closed-loop artificial pancreas** continuously measures subcutaneous glucose via a **Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM)**, computes insulin dosage using a control algorithm running on an embedded controller, and delivers insulin through a **pump** — all without manual intervention.

**PID Control Law (3 marks):**

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

with $e(t) = r - y(t)$ and $r = 110$ mg/dL (target glucose).

**Two Safety Challenges (2 marks each):**

1. **Hypoglycemia Risk** — Over-aggressive insulin dosing can cause severe hypoglycemia, leading to seizures, coma, or death. Mitigated by **insulin-on-board (IOB)** tracking and **hypo-minimizer** modules that suspend insulin below a threshold (e.g., 70 mg/dL).
2. **CGM Sensor Drift / Lag** — Subcutaneous glucose lags arterial glucose by 5–15 min, causing control instability. Mitigated by **Kalman-filter-based state estimation** and **redundant sensing**.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — End-to-End Latency (2 marks):**

$$T_{latency} = 4 + 10 + 6 + 2 = 22 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Hard RT Feasibility at 100 bpm (2 marks):**

$$T_{period} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.6 \text{ s} = 600 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{latency} = 22 \text{ ms} \ll 600 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Sampling Frequency for VF Detection (3 marks):**

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 6 = 12 \text{ Hz}$$

In practice, $f_s = 200$–$1000$ Hz is used to capture fine QRS morphology and reliably discriminate ventricular fibrillation from noise — well above the theoretical Nyquist minimum.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units** — Always write `ms`, `Hz`, `mg/dL`. A numeric answer without units gets **zero** in 2024 Scheme strict valuation.
> 2. **Confusing *Closed-Loop* with *Open-Loop*** — A glucometer that only *displays* glucose is *not* a Medical CPS. A system that *automatically titrates insulin* is.
> 3. **Ignoring the Safety Shell** — Any block diagram without a **watchdog / fail-safe / fault handler** loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why"** — Don't just write $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; explain *why* the multiplier is needed (anti-aliasing, faithful reconstruction).
> 5. **Wrong Battery Math** — `mAh × V = Wh`, *not* `mAh`. The unit-conversion step is the most-skipped, most-penalized error.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Medical CPS** = closed-loop, real-time, safety-critical integration of sensors, computation, communication, and actuators interacting with human physiology.
- ✅ **Core equation**: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ (PID); reliability $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; latency $T_{latency} = \sum T_{subsystem}$.
- ✅ **Three Pillars**: Functional Correctness ⨯ Temporal Correctness ⨯ Safety & Security.
- ✅ **Architecture**: Sensing → Computation → Communication → Actuation ⨯ Safety Shell.
- ✅ **Canonical Examples**: Pacemaker, Artificial Pancreas, Da Vinci Robot, CGM, Neurostimulator, Smart Infusion Pump.
- ✅ **Communication Standards**: BLE, IEEE 802.15.6 (WBAN), MICS band 402–405 MHz (implantable), HL7 FHIR (data layer).
- ✅ **Regulatory & Safety Standards**: FDA Class I/II/III, IEC 62304, ISO 13485, ISO 14971, ISO 10993, IEC 61508 SIL 1–4.
- ✅ **Nyquist**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. ECG → ≥ 500 Hz, EEG → ≥ 256 Hz, EMG → ≥ 1000 Hz, CGM → 1 sample / 1–5 min (slow dynamics).
- ✅ **Design Trade-off Triangle**: Power ⇄ Performance ⇄ Safety. Improving one typically degrades another.
- ✅ **Emerging Trends**: Digital twins of patients, AI-on-chip (TinyML), 5G-enabled tele-surgery, neuromorphic processors for low-power biosignal inference.
- ✅ **Exam-Favorite Keywords to use in answers**: *closed-loop, hard real-time, deterministic, fail-safe, biocompatible, SIL, FDA, latency budget, MICS, WBAN, PID, IOB, Kalman filter, watchdog*.

> **Good luck with your KTU 2024 ESE preparation! 🎓**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPS HW Platforms - Processors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CPS HW Platforms - Processors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ CPS Hardware Platforms — Processors

> [!NOTE]
> **CPS Hardware Platform** refers to the physical computing substrate on which a Cyber-Physical System executes sensing, computation, actuation, and communication tasks. The **processor** is the *computational heart* of this platform, dictating real-time performance, power envelope, and determinism.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **CPS Hardware Platform** is a heterogeneous computing architecture that integrates one or more **processing elements (PEs)**, memory subsystems, analog/mixed-signal interfaces, network transceivers, and actuator/sensor drivers onto a physically constrained footprint. Within this platform, the **processor** is the programmable logic device that interprets software instructions, performs arithmetic-logic operations, and orchestrates the closed-loop interaction between the cyber (computation) and physical (sensors/actuators) domains.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the processor block is responsible for:
1. **Sensory data acquisition** (via ADC, GPIO, I²C, SPI buses).
2. **Algorithmic decision-making** (state estimation, control laws, ML inference).
3. **Actuation command generation** (via PWM, DAC, digital I/O).
4. **Network protocol stack execution** (MQTT, CoAP, OPC-UA, CAN).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Brain of a Self-Driving Car"

Imagine a self-driving car as a CPS. The **processor** is analogous to the *driver's brain*:
- The **microcontroller** (like a Cortex-M4) is the *reflex arc* — instant decisions for braking, steering angle adjustment.
- The **microprocessor/SoC** (like a Cortex-A53 or Snapdragon) is the *conscious mind* — high-level path planning, object recognition.
- The **FPGA** is the *dedicated neural pathway* — accelerating sensor fusion at deterministic latencies.
- The **DSP** is the *audio cortex* — processing radar/LiDAR signal patterns.

Just as the human brain delegates tasks to specialized regions, a CPS platform delegates tasks to specialized processors based on **latency, throughput, and power constraints**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Selection of an *appropriate processor* is graded as a **design-level decision** in ESE questions. Students must justify the choice using parameters like **clock speed (MHz/GHz)**, **power budget (mW/W)**, **determinism (real-time capability)**, and **I/O bandwidth**.

## 1.3 Classification of Processors in CPS

Processors in CPS are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Categories | CPS Implication |
|------|------------|-----------------|
| **Instruction Set** | RISC (ARM, RISC-V) vs CISC (x86) | RISC dominates embedded CPS due to lower power and deterministic pipelining. |
| **Processing Paradigm** | Microcontroller, Microprocessor, DSP, FPGA, ASIC | Determines the trade-off between flexibility and performance-per-watt. |
| **Integration Level** | Discrete MCU, SoC, Multi-SoC, Heterogeneous MPSoC | Affects PCB footprint, EMI, and BOM cost. |

### 1.3.1 Microcontroller (MCU)

An **MCU** integrates a CPU core, RAM, ROM/Flash, timers, and peripherals (ADC, UART, I²C, SPI) onto a *single die*.

- **Typical CPS Use Case:** Sensor nodes, motor control, wearable health monitors.
- **KTU-Relevant Examples:** STM32 (ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4/M7), ATmega328P (Arduino Uno), MSP430 (TI), PIC16/PIC18 (Microchip).
- **Power Envelope:** 1 mW – 500 mW.
- **Clock Range:** 1 MHz – 600 MHz.

### 1.3.2 Microprocessor (MPU)

An **MPU** is a CPU-only chip that requires *external* memory and peripherals. It runs full operating systems (Linux, RTOS).

- **Typical CPS Use Case:** Edge gateways, autonomous vehicle ECUs, industrial HMIs.
- **KTU-Relevant Examples:** ARM Cortex-A53/A72 (Raspberry Pi, NXP i.MX8), Intel Atom, AMD Geode.
- **Power Envelope:** 1 W – 15 W.
- **Clock Range:** 600 MHz – 2 GHz+.

### 1.3.3 Digital Signal Processor (DSP)

A **DSP** is a specialized microprocessor with **Harvard architecture**, MAC (multiply-accumulate) units, and SIMD extensions optimized for streaming numerical data (filters, FFTs, convolutions).

- **Typical CPS Use Case:** Audio processing, vibration analysis, radar/sonar signal chains, motor control loops.
- **Key Feature:** Single-cycle MAC, zero-overhead looping, circular addressing.

### 1.3.4 FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array)

An **FPGA** is a reconfigurable fabric of logic blocks, DSP slices, and Block RAM that can be programmed at the *hardware level* (HDL/RTL), offering parallelism and deterministic latency in the **nanosecond** range.

- **Typical CPS Use Case:** High-speed sensor fusion, deterministic motor control, hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) simulation, protocol bridging.
- **KTU-Relevant Examples:** Xilinx Zynq-7000 (FPGA + ARM Cortex-A9 SoC), Intel Cyclone V, Lattice ECP5.

### 1.3.5 SoC and MPSoC

A **System on Chip (SoC)** integrates CPU + GPU + DSP + memory controller + peripherals into one package. A **Multi-Processor SoC (MPSoC)** adds multiple heterogeneous cores (e.g., ARM big.LITTLE: Cortex-A + Cortex-R + Cortex-M on one die).

- **Typical CPS Use Case:** Autonomous driving (NVIDIA Jetson, Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride), smart cameras, robotic controllers.
- **Significance for CPS:** Enables real-time + non-real-time workload partitioning on a single chip.

### 1.3.6 ASIC

An **Application-Specific Integrated Circuit** is a custom-designed chip optimized for a fixed function. Highest performance-per-watt but **zero flexibility** and high NRE cost.

- **Typical CPS Use Case:** Mass-produced IoT endpoints (e.g., ESP32, nRF52840 BLE SoC), automotive airbag controllers.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall for KTU:** **MCUs** for hard real-time + low power; **MPUs** for soft real-time + rich OS; **DSPs** for numeric streaming; **FPGAs** for deterministic parallelism; **SoCs** for heterogeneous integration.

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Processor Selection — The CPS Design Trade-off Space

Selecting a processor for a CPS is a **multi-objective constrained optimization**. The four canonical KTU parameters are:

### 2.1.1 Computational Throughput

Measured in **MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second) or **DMIPS** (Dhrystone MIPS, integer benchmark):

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{Clock Frequency (MHz)}}{\text{Cycles per Instruction (CPI)}}
$$

For floating-point workloads, **MFLOPS** (Million Floating-Point Operations Per Second) is used:

$$
\text{MFLOPS} = \frac{\text{Number of FP Operations}}{\text{Execution Time (seconds)} \times 10^{6}}
$$

### 2.1.2 Power Consumption

$$
P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{dynamic}} + P_{\text{static}}
$$

Where:

$$
P_{\text{dynamic}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f
$$

- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (0 to 1)
- $C$ = load capacitance
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f$ = clock frequency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS Design Rule:** Dynamic power scales **quadratically** with $V_{DD}$. Reducing $V_{DD}$ from 3.3 V to 1.8 V yields a **70%** power reduction — a critical knob for battery-powered CPS nodes.

### 2.1.3 Real-Time Determinism

For hard real-time CPS (anti-lock brakes, pacemakers), the key metric is **worst-case execution time (WCET)**:

$$
\text{WCET}_{\text{task}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{exec}(i) + \text{interrupt overhead} + \text{scheduling jitter}
$$

Schedulability under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** requires:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

Where $C_i$ = WCET of task $i$ and $T_i$ = period of task $i$.

### 2.1.4 I/O and Memory Bandwidth

$$
\text{Bandwidth} = \text{Bus Width (bytes)} \times \text{Bus Frequency (Hz)}
$$

E.g., a 32-bit AHB bus at 100 MHz ⇒ $32 \div 8 \times 100 \times 10^{6} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$.

## 2.2 Memory Architecture: Harvard vs Von Neumann

This is a **high-yield KTU topic** for 7-mark questions.

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| Memory Layout | Single unified program + data memory | Separate program and data memories |
| Bus | Single shared bus | Dual dedicated buses |
| Bottleneck | *Von Neumann bottleneck* (fetch-decode-execute contention) | Eliminated — simultaneous fetch + data access |
| Determinism | Lower (contention adds jitter) | Higher (predictable cycle counts) |
| CPS Examples | x86, ARM7 (Von Neumann mode) | ARM Cortex-M, AVR, DSPs, most modern MCUs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Harvard dominates CPS:** Real-time control loops require **deterministic** memory access times. A shared bus can stall the CPU during DMA bursts, causing *control jitter* — unacceptable in a motor or process controller.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Time | $T_{\text{CPU}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}}$ | seconds | Sizing MCU clock for deadline |
| 2 | MIPS | $\text{MIPS} = f_{\text{clk}} / \text{CPI}$ | million instr/s | Throughput benchmark |
| 3 | MFLOPS | $\text{MFLOPS} = N_{\text{flop}} / (t \cdot 10^{6})$ | million FLOP/s | DSP evaluation |
| 4 | Dynamic Power | $P_d = \alpha C V^{2} f$ | watts | Battery lifetime estimation |
| 5 | RMS Utilization Bound | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | dimensionless | Hard real-time schedulability |
| 6 | Amdahl's Speedup | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - p) + p / N}$ | dimensionless | Multicore benefit estimation |
| 7 | Gustafson's Law | $S_{\text{scaled}} = N - (1 - p)(N - 1)$ | dimensionless | Scaled workload speedup |
| 8 | Bandwidth | $B = W_{\text{bus}} \times f_{\text{bus}}$ | bytes/s | Sensor stream capacity |
| 9 | Energy per Instruction | $E_{\text{inst}} = P_{\text{avg}} / \text{IPS}$ | joules | Energy-efficient CPS design |
| 10 | Memory Latency | $t_{\text{acc}} = t_{\text{cycle}} \times N_{\text{cycles}}$ | seconds | Cache miss penalty analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by CPS architects to *predict* whether a chosen processor will meet a **deadline** before fabricating hardware. The MIPS + WCET pair is the gold standard for hard real-time CPS certification (e.g., ISO 26262 in automotive).

## 2.4 The CPS Processor Selection Decision Logic

The choice follows a **funnel**:

1. **Power Budget** → Coin-cell (µW) ⇒ MCU; Mains-powered (W) ⇒ MPU/SoC.
2. **Real-Time Class** → Hard RTOS (µs jitter) ⇒ Cortex-M or FPGA; Soft RT (ms OK) ⇒ Cortex-A + Linux.
3. **Numeric Workload** → Heavy filters/FFT ⇒ DSP or FPGA with MAC units.
4. **Connectivity** → Wireless protocols (BLE, LoRa, Wi-Fi) ⇒ SoC with integrated radio.
5. **Cost & Volume** → > 100k units ⇒ ASIC; < 10k units ⇒ COTS MCU/MPU.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "P-R-N-C-V"** (Power, Real-time, Numeric, Connectivity, Volume) — use this in 14-mark ESE answers to structure your processor selection justification.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CPU Time and Effective MIPS

**Given:** A Cortex-M4 processor runs at $f_{\text{clk}} = 168$ MHz. A sensor-fusion task contains $N = 2.4 \times 10^{6}$ instructions with an average CPI = 1.3.

**Find:** (i) CPU time, (ii) Effective MIPS, (iii) Whether the task meets a 20 ms control deadline.

### Step 1 — Compute the clock period

The clock period is the reciprocal of the clock frequency:

$$
T_{\text{clock}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{168 \times 10^{6}} \text{ seconds}
$$

Evaluating numerically:

$$
T_{\text{clock}} = 5.952 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 5.952 \text{ ns per cycle}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the total CPU time

Substitute into the standard CPU time formula:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = (2.4 \times 10^{6}) \times 1.3 \times (5.952 \times 10^{-9})
$$

Multiplication step-by-step:

$$
2.4 \times 1.3 = 3.12
$$

$$
3.12 \times 5.952 = 18.57
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 18.57 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 18.57 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute effective MIPS

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI}} = \frac{168 \text{ MHz}}{1.3}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = 129.23 \text{ MIPS}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify the real-time deadline

The deadline is $D = 20 \text{ ms}$. We require $T_{\text{CPU}} \leq D$:

$$
18.57 \text{ ms} \leq 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The processor meets the deadline with a **7.2% timing margin** ($1 - 18.57/20$). For safety-critical CPS, a margin of **at least 25%** is recommended to absorb interrupt overhead and cache misses.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Multicore CPS Acceleration

**Scenario:** A CPS edge node runs a workload where **65%** of the code is parallelizable ($p = 0.65$) across $N = 4$ cores.

**Find:** Theoretical speedup $S$ and the *maximum possible* speedup as $N \to \infty$.

### Step 1 — Apply Amdahl's Law

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}
$$

Substitute $p = 0.65$ and $N = 4$:

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.65) + \dfrac{0.65}{4}}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
(1 - 0.65) = 0.35
$$

$$
\frac{0.65}{4} = 0.1625
$$

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{0.35 + 0.1625} = \frac{1}{0.5125}
$$

$$
S(4) \approx 1.951
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the asymptotic speedup

As $N \to \infty$, $p / N \to 0$:

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{0.35} \approx 2.857
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS Insight:** Even with 4 cores, the workload runs only **1.95× faster** — not 4×. The serial portion (35%) caps the *achievable* speedup at **2.86×** no matter how many cores are added. This is why **algorithmic optimization** beats hardware scaling for CPS.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Dynamic Power Reduction via Voltage Scaling

**Given:** A processor initially runs at $V_{DD,1} = 3.3$ V, $f_1 = 100$ MHz. To save power, we scale to $V_{DD,2} = 1.8$ V. Assume $f \propto V_{DD}$ (linear voltage-frequency scaling).

**Find:** Ratio of new dynamic power to old dynamic power.

### Step 1 — Express dynamic power ratio

Since $P_d = \alpha C V^{2} f$ and $\alpha, C$ are constant:

$$
\frac{P_{d,2}}{P_{d,1}} = \left(\frac{V_{DD,2}}{V_{DD,1}}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{f_2}{f_1}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply linear V-f scaling

$$
f_2 = f_1 \cdot \frac{V_{DD,2}}{V_{DD,1}} = 100 \cdot \frac{1.8}{3.3} = 54.55 \text{ MHz}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$
\frac{P_{d,2}}{P_{d,1}} = \left(\frac{1.8}{3.3}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{1.8}{3.3}
$$

Compute the ratio of voltages:

$$
\frac{1.8}{3.3} = 0.5455
$$

$$
(0.5455)^{2} = 0.2975
$$

$$
(0.5455)^{3} = 0.1623
$$

$$
\frac{P_{d,2}}{P_{d,1}} \approx 0.1623
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret

$$
P_{d,2} \approx 16.23\% \text{ of } P_{d,1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **CPS Energy Impact:** A reduction from 3.3 V to 1.8 V cuts dynamic power to **~1/6th** — extending battery life by **6×**. This is the basis of **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** used in modern CPS SoCs (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, NXP i.MX8).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Parameter | MCU (Cortex-M4) | MPU (Cortex-A53) | DSP (TI C6000) | FPGA (Zynq-7000) | SoC (Jetson Nano) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Clock (typ.) | 168 MHz | 1.4 GHz | 1.25 GHz | 100–500 MHz logic | 1.43 GHz |
| Power | ~100 mW | ~2 W | ~1 W | ~1.5 W | ~10 W |
| Architecture | Harvard | Modified Harvard | Harvard + VLIW | Configurable | Heterogeneous |
| RTOS Support | FreeRTOS, RTX | Linux, Xenomai | TI-RTOS, SYS/BIOS | Bare-metal, FreeRTOS on ARM | Linux + RT patches |
| Hard Real-Time | ✅ Excellent | ⚠ Limited (with RT patch) | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent (ns jitter) | ⚠ Limited |
| Numeric Strength | Medium (M4F has FPU) | High (NEON SIMD) | Very High (MAC/SIMD) | Very High (parallel) | High (GPU + CPU) |
| Unit Cost | $1–$10 | $10–$50 | $10–$100 | $20–$200 | $50–$200 |
| Best CPS Use | Sensor node, motor ctrl | Edge gateway, HMI | Radar/audio DSP | Sensor fusion, HIL | Vision, ML at edge |

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark answers:** Always close with a *justified* pick. Example: *"For a wearable ECG monitor, an STM32L4 Cortex-M4 is selected because it offers an FPU for filtering, sub-µA sleep current, and a deterministic interrupt latency of 12 cycles — meeting the IEC 60601-1-8 alarm latency standard."*

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPS Hardware Platform — High-Level Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the **processor subsystem** sits at the center of a CPS hardware platform, interfacing with sensors, actuators, memory, and communication.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHY[Physical World]
        S1[Temperature Sensor]
        S2[Accelerometer IMU]
        S3[Lidar Sensor]
        A1[Motor Actuator]
        A2[Valve Actuator]
    end

    subgraph IFACE[Analog and Digital Interface]
        ADC1[12-bit ADC]
        PWM1[PWM Generator]
        GPIO1[GPIO Bank]
        CAN1[CAN Transceiver]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Processing Subsystem]
        CPU1[ARM Cortex M4 Core]
        DSP1[DSP Unit M4F FPU]
        TIM1[Hardware Timers and Watchdog]
        DMA1[DMA Controller]
    end

    subgraph MEM[Memory Subsystem]
        FL[Flash 1 MB Code]
        RAM[SRAM 256 KB Data]
        CACHE[Optional Cache Layer]
    end

    subgraph COM[Communication Subsystem]
        U1[UART Debug]
        I1[I2C Sensor Bus]
        SP1[SPI High Speed]
        W1[BLE or WiFi Radio]
    end

    S1 --> ADC1 --> CPU1
    S2 --> I1 --> CPU1
    S3 --> SP1 --> DMA1 --> CPU1
    CPU1 --> PWM1 --> A1
    CPU1 --> GPIO1 --> A2
    CPU1 <--> TIM1
    CPU1 <--> DSP1
    CPU1 <--> FL
    CPU1 <--> RAM
    CPU1 <--> CACHE
    CPU1 <--> CAN1
    CPU1 --> U1
    CPU1 <--> W1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Processing Subsystem** (center) is the *only* block that actively runs algorithms. All other blocks (interface, memory, communication) exist to *feed* it data or *carry out* its commands. This is the canonical CPS "sense → compute → actuate" loop mapped to silicon.

---

This Mermaid flowchart encodes the **P-R-N-C-V** selection logic from Section 2.4.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Differentiate between **Harvard** and **Von Neumann** processor architectures. Why is Harvard architecture preferred in hard real-time CPS applications?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Bus structure | Single shared bus for program + data | Separate buses for program + data |
| Memory | Unified memory | Separate instruction + data memories |
| Bottleneck | *Von Neumann bottleneck* — fetch and data access contend for the same bus | Eliminated; simultaneous fetch + access |
| Determinism | Lower due to contention-induced jitter | Higher, predictable cycle counts |

**Why preferred in hard real-time CPS (1 mark):** Real-time control loops require *deterministic* worst-case execution time. Harvard's separate buses prevent the DMA and CPU from contending, giving bounded and predictable interrupt latency — essential for safety-critical systems like ABS or insulin pumps.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
Define **MIPS** and **MFLOPS**. A processor runs at 200 MHz with an average CPI of 2.0. Calculate its MIPS rating.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second):** A throughput metric indicating how many millions of instructions a processor can execute per second.
- **MFLOPS (Million Floating-point Operations Per Second):** A throughput metric for floating-point intensive workloads such as DSP filters or matrix math.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI}} = \frac{200 \text{ MHz}}{2.0} = 100 \text{ MIPS}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **MIPS** with **MHz**. MHz is *clock frequency*; MIPS is *effective instruction throughput* — they are equal **only** when CPI = 1. Always divide MHz by CPI.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — Processor Selection and Real-Time Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Main]**

> *You are tasked with designing the CPS hardware platform for a **battery-powered wearable ECG monitor**. The system must sample ECG at 500 Hz, run a Pan-Tompkins QRS detection algorithm (≈ 1.2 million instructions per sample), update a BLE link every 250 ms, and operate for at least 7 days on a 200 mAh coin cell. Propose a suitable processor and justify your selection.*

#### Part (a) — Processor Selection and Justification (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the workload budget (3 marks).**

Samples per second: 500. Instructions per sample: $1.2 \times 10^{6}$. Total instructions per second:

$$
\text{IPS} = 500 \times 1.2 \times 10^{6} = 6 \times 10^{8} \text{ instructions/s}
$$

The deadline per sample is $T_d = 1/500 = 2$ ms.

**Step 2 — Required MIPS (1 mark).**

$$
\text{MIPS}_{\text{req}} = 6 \times 10^{8} / 10^{6} = 600 \text{ MIPS (assuming CPI=1)}
$$

A practical Cortex-M4F at 168 MHz yields:

$$
\text{MIPS}_{\text{achievable}} = \frac{168}{1.3} \approx 129 \text{ MIPS}
$$

This is **insufficient** for raw DSP processing of every sample — therefore we choose an **STM32H7 (Cortex-M7 at 480 MHz)** with single-precision FPU and DSP extensions, achieving ≈ 600+ MIPS for DSP code.

**Step 3 — Justify the choice (3 marks).**

| Criterion | STM32H7 (Cortex-M7) | Why it fits ECG CPS |
|---|---|---|
| Power | ~100 mW active, 5 µA standby | Extends 200 mAh coin cell close to 7-day target |
| Determinism | Hard real-time, 12-cycle interrupt latency | Guarantees 2 ms sample deadline |
| DSP | Single-precision FPU + SIMD DSP | Accelerates Pan-Tompkins filters |
| Integration | On-chip 12-bit ADC @ 5 MSPS, DMA | Direct ECG sampling without external ADC |
| Connectivity | Requires external BLE SoC (e.g., nRF52840) | Periodic 250 ms wake-up; the rest of the time the M7 sleeps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final selection:** **STM32H7 Cortex-M7** as the main CPS controller, paired with **Nordic nRF52840** for BLE radio — a heterogeneous dual-chip CPS platform.

---

#### Part (b) — Power Budget and Battery Lifetime Verification (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Active and sleep duty cycle (2 marks).**

Let the processor be active for $t_{\text{on}} = 3$ ms every 4 ms (sample + process + transmit) and asleep otherwise. With 250 ms BLE advertisement bursts every 250 ms:

$$
D_{\text{active}} = \frac{3 \text{ ms}}{4 \text{ ms}} = 0.75 \text{ during processing}
$$

Average active current ≈ 30 mA, sleep current ≈ 5 µA.

**Step 2 — Average current draw (3 marks).**

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = (D_{\text{active}} \times I_{\text{on}}) + ((1 - D_{\text{active}}) \times I_{\text{sleep}})
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = (0.75 \times 30 \text{ mA}) + (0.25 \times 0.005 \text{ mA})
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = 22.5 + 0.00125 = 22.50 \text{ mA}
$$

(Note: For 200 mAh coin cell, this would be too high — we must lower the duty cycle using event-driven interrupts.)

**Step 3 — Revised duty cycle using DMA + interrupts (2 marks).**

With DMA-driven ADC sampling, the CPU is active for only 200 µs per 2 ms period:

$$
D_{\text{active,rev}} = \frac{0.2}{2} = 0.10
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg,rev}} = (0.10 \times 30) + (0.90 \times 0.005) = 3.0045 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4 — Battery lifetime (1 mark).**

$$
t_{\text{life}} = \frac{200 \text{ mAh}}{3.0045 \text{ mA}} = 66.6 \text{ hours} \approx 2.77 \text{ days}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often forget to include the *static (sleep) current*. In coin-cell CPS, even 5 µA of standby current matters — over 7 days, it consumes $5\mu\text{A} \times 24 \times 7 = 0.84 \text{ mAh}$, which can decide whether you pass the 7-day life requirement. Always show the *sleep current term* explicitly.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Question (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, ESE Model]**

> *(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the **Harvard architecture** of a typical microcontroller used in CPS. Discuss how its features support real-time control applications. (7 marks)*
>
> *(b) Using **Amdahl's Law**, evaluate the speedup obtained by parallelizing a CPS workload on a quad-core processor if 70% of the code is parallelizable. Also compute the maximum possible speedup with infinite cores. (7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Harvard Architecture for CPS (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph H[Harvard Microcontroller Architecture]
        CORE[CPU Core with ALU]
        IBUS[Instruction Bus]
        DBUS[Data Bus]
        IROM[Instruction Memory Flash]
        DRAM[Data Memory SRAM]
        PER[Peripherals ADC PWM Timer]
        CORE --- IBUS --- IROM
        CORE --- DBUS --- DRAM
        CORE --- PER
    end
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Separate instruction and data memories** (typically Flash for program, SRAM for data) connected via **two independent buses** to the CPU core.
2. The CPU can **fetch the next instruction** from Flash *while simultaneously* reading or writing data in SRAM — a feature called *parallel memory access*.
3. **Peripherals** such as ADC, PWM, timers, and communication controllers (UART, SPI, I²C) are memory-mapped into the data address space, accessible via the data bus.
4. **Why this supports real-time CPS:**
   - Deterministic instruction fetch times → predictable WCET.
   - No bus contention between DMA-driven sensor reads and CPU computation.
   - Single-cycle execution of most instructions (Cortex-M), yielding constant interrupt latency.
   - Built-in hardware timers enable precise periodic sampling for control loops.

---

#### Part (b) — Amdahl's Law Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $p = 0.70$, $N = 4$ cores.

**Step 1 — Apply Amdahl's Law (4 marks):**

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}
$$

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.70) + \dfrac{0.70}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.30 + 0.175} = \frac{1}{0.475}
$$

$$
S(4) \approx 2.105
$$

**Step 2 — Compute maximum speedup as $N \to \infty$ (2 marks):**

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.333
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

Even with 4 cores, the workload runs only **2.1× faster** instead of 4×. The serial fraction (30%) caps the achievable speedup at **3.33×** regardless of how many cores are added. Hence, *algorithmic optimization of the serial portion* yields a higher return than adding cores.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (Examiner Alert):** Students frequently write $S = N \times p$ or $S = 1/p$. These are *wrong*. The correct Amdahl formula is $S = 1 / [(1-p) + (p/N)]$. Also, $S_{\max} = 1/(1-p)$ is the *limit*, not $1/p$. Mixing these up costs **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — CPS HW Platforms: Processors**

- 🔹 A **processor** is the computational heart of a CPS platform; its choice dictates power, latency, and determinism.
- 🔹 **MCU** (e.g., STM32, ATmega) = hard real-time + low power; **MPU** (e.g., Cortex-A, x86) = soft real-time + OS.
- 🔹 **DSP** = streaming numeric workloads (filters, FFT, radar); **FPGA** = deterministic parallelism (ns latency).
- 🔹 **SoC / MPSoC** integrates heterogeneous cores (CPU + DSP + GPU + FPGA) for advanced CPS like autonomous driving.
- 🔹 **Harvard architecture** dominates CPS MCUs because of *deterministic memory access* and *no Von Neumann bottleneck*.
- 🔹 **MIPS** = $f_{\text{clk}} / \text{CPI}$; **MFLOPS** = floating-point operations per second.
- 🔹 **Dynamic power** $P_d = \alpha C V^{2} f$ — scales **cubically** with V under linear V-f scaling.
- 🔹 **Amdahl's Law** $S = 1 / [(1-p) + p/N]$; cap = $1/(1-p)$ — serial portion always limits speedup.
- 🔹 **RMS schedulability bound** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is the KTU classical real-time check.
- 🔹 **Selection mnemonic:** **P-R-N-C-V** (Power, Real-time, Numeric, Connectivity, Volume).
- 🔹 **DVFS** (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling) is the key energy-saving technique in modern CPS SoCs.
- 🔹 Real-time CPS design rule: maintain **≥ 25% timing margin** between $T_{\text{CPU}}$ and the deadline to absorb jitter.
- 🔹 For **wearable / IoT CPS** — coin-cell powered ⇒ pick Cortex-M class; for **gateway / edge CPS** — pick Cortex-A + Linux.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always close with a *one-sentence quantitative justification* (e.g., "This MCU delivers 129 MIPS at 100 mW, achieving $1.23 \times 10^{6}$ instructions/mW efficiency, which exceeds the 7-day battery life requirement"). Such numbers signal examiner confidence and earn full marks under the *design verification* rubric. ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *sensor* is a physical or electronic device that detects, measures, and responds to a physical stimulus (such as heat, light, pressure, motion, chemical concentration, or magnetic field) and converts it into an electrical signal (analog or digital) which can be read by an observer, an instrument, or a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) controller.

In a Cyber-Physical System, sensors form the **"sensing layer"** or the **input layer** of the *Sense–Compute–Actuate* loop. They act as the bridge between the physical world (continuous, analog phenomena) and the cyber/computational world (discrete, digital processing).

### 1.1.1 Position in the CPS Architecture

$$
\text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Signal Conditioning} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{Cyber Controller} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Physical Process}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 OECPT723, sensors are studied under Module 2 because they represent the *first* interaction point where a CPS becomes "aware" of its environment. Without sensors, the system is effectively **blind** and cannot participate in any feedback control loop.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for Freshers)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Nervous System**
> Think of a sensor exactly like one of your human senses:
> - Your **eyes** are *optical sensors* detecting light intensity and color.
> - Your **skin** is a *pressure/temperature sensor* detecting touch and heat.
> - Your **ears** are *acoustic sensors* detecting sound waves.
> - Your **inner ear** contains *accelerometers* that detect orientation and balance.
>
> Just as your brain (the "controller") cannot move your hand to catch a ball without first seeing it (the "sensor input"), a CPS controller cannot regulate a motor's speed without first measuring that speed. **Sensors give the system its awareness.**

A sensor's job can be summarized in three steps:

1. **Sense** a physical quantity (e.g., temperature).
2. **Convert** it into a measurable, usually electrical, signal.
3. **Deliver** the signal to a controller for processing and decision-making.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bold Notation)

The following standard engineering metrics are used to *characterize* every sensor:

- **Sensitivity (S)** — output change per unit input change.
- **Accuracy (A)** — closeness of reading to the true value.
- **Precision (P)** — repeatability of measurements.
- **Range** — minimum to maximum input the sensor can detect.
- **Resolution (R)** — smallest detectable change in input.
- **Linearity** — how closely output follows a straight-line relationship with input.
- **Hysteresis (H)** — difference in output for the same input approached from opposite directions.
- **Response Time (τ)** — time taken to reach a defined percentage (commonly **63.2%** for first-order systems) of the final value.
- **Bandwidth (BW)** — frequency range over which the sensor operates accurately.
- **Drift** — slow change of output with time at constant input.
- **Noise (N)** — unwanted random fluctuations in output.

---

## 1.4 High-Yield Classification of Sensors

Sensors are classified along multiple dimensions, all of which are fair game in KTU examinations:

| Classification Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Requirement** | Active (self-generating) | Passive (requires external excitation) |
| **Output Signal** | Analog | Digital |
| **Contact** | Contact sensor | Non-contact sensor |
| **Physical Quantity** | Thermal, Mechanical, Optical, Chemical, Magnetic, Biological | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Active vs. Passive — Exam Favorite Question:**
> - **Active sensor:** Generates its own electrical signal from the physical stimulus (e.g., a **thermocouple** generating millivolts from temperature difference, a **piezoelectric crystal** generating voltage from pressure).
> - **Passive sensor:** Requires external power and changes its electrical properties (resistance, capacitance, inductance) in response to the stimulus (e.g., **thermistor** whose resistance changes with temperature, **LDR** whose resistance changes with light).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — How a Sensor Works

Every sensor, regardless of its physical principle, follows the same fundamental three-stage internal process:

### Stage 1: Sensing Element (Primary Transducer)
- The physical stimulus acts on a specially chosen material (semiconductor, metal, polymer, crystal).
- The material's intrinsic property (resistance, voltage generation, capacitance, inductance, frequency) changes in proportion to the stimulus.

### Stage 2: Transduction Mechanism
- The property change is converted into a standardized electrical quantity: **voltage (V), current (I), resistance (R), capacitance (C), or frequency (f)**.

### Stage 3: Signal Output
- The electrical signal is buffered, amplified, or linearized to match the input requirement of the next stage (e.g., an **ADC** or a microcontroller pin).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in CPS:** A CPS controller (typically a microcontroller) operates in the **digital domain (0–3.3 V or 0–5 V logic levels)**. If a sensor produces millivolts, microamps, or a non-linear output, the signal must be conditioned before it reaches the controller. This is the *signal conditioning* block.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Model of a Generic Sensor

A linear sensor can be modeled as a static input–output relationship:

$$
y(t) = S \cdot x(t) + b
$$

Where:
- $y(t)$ = output signal (electrical) at time $t$
- $x(t)$ = input physical quantity (stimulus)
- $S$ = **sensitivity** (units: output units per input unit, e.g., V/°C)
- $b$ = **offset / bias** (output when input is zero)

For a **dynamic (time-varying) sensor**, the first-order transfer function is:

$$
H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}
$$

Where:
- $K$ = static sensitivity (DC gain)
- $\tau$ = **time constant** of the sensor
- $s$ = Laplace variable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Time Constant τ (First-Order Step Response):**
> When a step input of magnitude $X_0$ is applied, the output evolves as:
>
> $$y(t) = K \cdot X_0 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$
>
> - At $t = \tau$, the output reaches **63.2%** of its final value.
> - At $t = 4\tau$, the output reaches **98.2%** (often considered "settled").
> - At $t = 5\tau$, the output reaches **99.3%** (engineering rule-of-thumb settling time).

---

## 2.3 Common Sensor Types — Engineering Walkthrough

### 2.3.1 Temperature Sensors

| Type | Principle | Output Range | Active/Passive |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermocouple** | Seebeck effect (voltage from two dissimilar metals) | –200 °C to +2300 °C | **Active** |
| **Thermistor (NTC/PTC)** | Resistance varies with temperature | –55 °C to +150 °C | **Passive** |
| **RTD (PT100)** | Resistance of platinum wire changes with temperature | –200 °C to +850 °C | **Passive** |
| **LM35 / TMP36** | Bandgap voltage reference IC | –55 °C to +150 °C | **Active (IC-based)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Steinhart–Hart Equation (Thermistor NTC):** A more accurate non-linear model:
> $$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C \left[\ln(R)\right]^3$$
> Where $T$ is in Kelvin, $R$ is resistance, and $A, B, C$ are calibration constants.

### 2.3.2 Position & Motion Sensors

- **Potentiometer** — Passive, resistance changes with slider position. Output: $0$ to $V_{cc}$.
- **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** — Active, AC-excited; gives displacement via differential voltage.
- **Encoder (Optical/Magnetic)** — Digital, gives discrete position pulses (quadrature gives direction).
- **Accelerometer (MEMS)** — Measures acceleration via displacement of a micro-machined proof mass.
- **Gyroscope** — Measures angular velocity (Coriolis effect).
- **IMU** — Inertial Measurement Unit: combines accelerometer + gyroscope (often + magnetometer).

### 2.3.3 Light Sensors

- **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** — Passive, resistance decreases with light intensity.
- **Photodiode** — Active, generates current proportional to light intensity.
- **Phototransistor** — Active, current amplified version of photodiode.
- **CCD/CMOS Image Sensor** — Digital 2-D array for cameras.

### 2.3.4 Proximity & Distance Sensors

- **Ultrasonic (HC-SR04)** — Measures distance via time-of-flight of sound waves: $d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$.
- **Infrared (IR)** — Triangulation or reflectance based.
- **LiDAR** — Time-of-flight with laser light.
- **Inductive / Capacitive** — Detect metallic / non-metallic object presence.

### 2.3.5 Chemical & Gas Sensors

- **MQ-series (MQ-2, MQ-135)** — Tin-oxide semiconductor; resistance changes with gas concentration.
- **Electrochemical sensor** — Current generated by redox reaction.
- **pH electrode** — Voltage proportional to hydrogen-ion activity.

### 2.3.6 Force / Pressure / Strain Sensors

- **Strain Gauge** — Resistance changes with applied strain: $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$, where $G$ is the gauge factor.
- **Piezoelectric sensor** — Active, voltage generated under mechanical stress.
- **Load cell** — Wheatstone-bridge arrangement of 4 strain gauges.

---

## 2.4 Wheatstone Bridge — A Must-Know for KTU

Most resistive sensors (strain gauges, thermistors, RTDs) are interfaced using a **Wheatstone bridge** for high-accuracy differential measurement:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left(\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right)
$$

- **Balanced bridge:** $V_{out} = 0$ when $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$.
- **Quarter-bridge:** One resistor is the sensor, others are fixed.
- **Half-bridge / Full-bridge:** Two or four resistors are active sensors — gives higher sensitivity and temperature compensation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Load cells used in weighing scales are almost always **full-bridge** configurations of four strain gauges, giving the highest possible sensitivity and intrinsic temperature compensation.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print and Memorize)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear sensor model | $y = S x + b$ | V per (input unit) |
| 2 | First-order TF | $H(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | $K$ = gain, $\tau$ = time constant (s) |
| 3 | Step response (1st order) | $y(t) = K X_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | 63.2% at $t=\tau$ |
| 4 | Strain gauge | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \varepsilon$ | $G \approx 2$ for metal |
| 5 | Wheatstone bridge | $V_o = V_{ex}\!\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ | $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage |
| 6 | Ultrasonic distance | $d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t}{2}$ | $v_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s at 20°C |
| 7 | Thermistor (β-model) | $R(T) = R_0 \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | $T$ in Kelvin, $\beta$ in K |
| 8 | Sensitivity | $S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | output unit / input unit |
| 9 | Resolution | $R = \frac{\text{range}}{2^{n}}$ | for $n$-bit ADC |
| 10 | % Error | $\%E = \frac{\vert x_{measured} - x_{true} \vert}{\vert x_{true} \vert} \times 100$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Settling time (1st order) | $t_s \approx 4\tau$ to $5\tau$ | within 2%–1% of final value |
| 12 | Sensor noise (RMS) | $V_{noise,rms} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | Johnson–Nyquist noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For questions involving absolute value bars in calculations (e.g., error, noise), use $\vert \cdot \vert$ to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. This table is the **minimum** set of formulas you must memorize for this topic.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Sensor Examples | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive (CPS)** | Oxygen sensor, ABS wheel-speed sensor, accelerometer (airbag) | Engine control, braking, crash detection |
| **Healthcare (IoMT)** | ECG/EEG electrodes, pulse oximeter, glucometer | Continuous patient monitoring |
| **Industrial 4.0** | Vibration sensor, current sensor, gas sensor | Predictive maintenance |
| **Smart Agriculture** | Soil moisture, NPK, DHT22 (humidity/temp) | Precision irrigation & fertigation |
| **Smart Cities** | Air quality, traffic (inductive loops), acoustic | Pollution, congestion management |
| **Aerospace & Drones** | IMU, barometer, GPS, magnetometer | Autonomous navigation, stabilization |
| **Robotics** | Encoders, force/torque, LiDAR, vision | Closed-loop motion control & SLAM |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sensitivity from Calibration Data

**Problem (KTU Style):** A temperature sensor gives the following output voltages at known temperatures:
- At $T_1 = 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$: $V_1 = 0.20\ \text{V}$
- At $T_2 = 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$: $V_2 = 2.10\ \text{V}$

Find: (a) Sensitivity $S$, (b) Output voltage at $T = 75\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the model.** Assume linear behavior, $V = S \cdot T + b$.

**Step 2 — Form two equations.**

$$
V_1 = S \cdot T_1 + b \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.20 = S(0) + b \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = 0.20\ \text{V}
$$

$$
V_2 = S \cdot T_2 + b \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2.10 = S(100) + 0.20
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for sensitivity $S$.**

$$
S = \frac{2.10 - 0.20}{100 - 0} = \frac{1.90}{100} = 0.019\ \text{V/}^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute output at $T = 75\ ^\circ\text{C}$.**

$$
V = S \cdot T + b = 0.019 \times 75 + 0.20
$$

$$
V = 1.425 + 0.20 = 1.625\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V(75\ ^\circ\text{C}) = 1.625\ \text{V}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — First-Order Step Response

**Problem:** A temperature sensor with time constant $\tau = 5\ \text{s}$ and sensitivity $S = 10\ \text{mV/}^\circ\text{C}$ is suddenly exposed to a step temperature change of $\Delta T = 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Find the output at $t = 0\ \text{s}$, $t = \tau$, $t = 4\tau$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the canonical first-order step response.**

$$
y(t) = K \cdot X_0 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
$$

Here, $K = S = 10\ \text{mV/}^\circ\text{C}$ and $X_0 = 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

$$
y(t) = 10 \times 50 \times \left(1 - e^{-t/5}\right) = 500 \left(1 - e^{-t/5}\right)\ \text{mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at $t = 0$.**

$$
y(0) = 500 \left(1 - e^{0}\right) = 500(1 - 1) = 0\ \text{mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at $t = \tau = 5\ \text{s}$.**

$$
y(\tau) = 500 \left(1 - e^{-1}\right) = 500 \times (1 - 0.3679) = 500 \times 0.6321
$$

$$
y(\tau) = 316.05\ \text{mV} \approx 63.2\%\ \text{of full scale}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $t = 4\tau = 20\ \text{s}$.**

$$
y(4\tau) = 500 \left(1 - e^{-4}\right) = 500 \times (1 - 0.0183) = 500 \times 0.9817
$$

$$
y(4\tau) = 490.85\ \text{mV} \approx 98.2\%\ \text{of full scale}
$$

**Final Result Table:**

| Time | $e^{-t/\tau}$ | Output $y(t)$ | % of Final |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $1.0000$ | $0\ \text{mV}$ | $0\%$ |
| $\tau = 5\ \text{s}$ | $0.3679$ | $316.05\ \text{mV}$ | $63.2\%$ |
| $4\tau = 20\ \text{s}$ | $0.0183$ | $490.85\ \text{mV}$ | $98.2\%$ |
| $5\tau = 25\ \text{s}$ | $0.0067$ | $496.63\ \text{mV}$ | $99.3\%$ |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wheatstone Bridge with Strain Gauge

**Problem:** A quarter-bridge strain gauge circuit has $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 120\ \Omega$ (fixed) and $R_4 = 120\ \Omega + \Delta R$ (active gauge), with excitation $V_{ex} = 5\ \text{V}$. The gauge factor $G = 2.0$ and applied strain $\varepsilon = 500\ \mu\varepsilon$. Find the bridge output voltage.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the resistance change $\Delta R$.**

$$
