| 3 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Shannon-Hartley channel capacity | bps |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{\log_2(M)}{1 + \alpha}$ | Spectral efficiency including roll-off $\alpha$ | bps/Hz |
| 5 | $t_{frame} = \dfrac{L_{frame}}{R_b} \times 8$ | Frame transmission time (L in bytes) | seconds |
| 6 | $L_{wire} = t_{prop} \times v_{prop}$ | Physical wire length for given latency | meters |
| 7 | $W_{saved} = n_{pairs} \times L_{cable} \times \rho_{Cu} \times A$ | Weight saved by reducing pairs | kg |
| 8 | $BER = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{th}}{\sigma_n \sqrt{2}}\right)$ | Bit Error Rate for binary PAM over AWGN | dimensionless |
| 9 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in decibels | dB |
| 10 | $R_{b,100BASE} = 66.667 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(3) \approx 105.2 \text{ Mbps}$ | Effective bit rate of 100BASE-T1 (with 4B3T mapping ≈ 100 Mbps) | bps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, all absolute-value or magnitude notation uses the explicit phrase "magnitude" or expression form, never the `|` pipe symbol, to preserve the KTU markdown table structure.

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Automotive Engineering

- **Equation 1** explains why PAM-3 in 100BASE-T1 is bandwidth-efficient: $\log_2(3) \approx 1.585$ bits per symbol, so a 66.67 MBd link carries ~105 Mbps gross, with overhead reducing it to **100 Mbps usable**.
- **Equation 3 (Shannon's limit)** sets the theoretical ceiling for cable bandwidth given in-vehicle SNR constraints (~30–40 dB at 100 MHz).
- **Equation 5 (Frame time)** determines whether a 1500-byte Ethernet frame at 100 Mbps arrives in **120 µs** — short enough for 10 ms ADAS control loops.
- **Equation 8 (BER)** justifies why Automotive Ethernet specifies BER $\le 10^{-10}$ — a thousand times stricter than commercial Ethernet's $10^{-7}$ requirement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Bit Rate of 100BASE-T1 (PAM-3 with 3B2T Mapping)

**Given:**
- Symbol (baud) rate: $R_s = 66.667$ MBd (Mega-Baud)
- Modulation: 3-level PAM (PAM-3)
- Block mapping: 3 binary bits encoded into 2 ternary symbols (3B2T)

**Find:** The nominal line bit rate $R_b$.

### Step 1 — Express bit rate from symbol rate and modulation order

For an M-ary PAM system, the gross bit rate is given by:

$$R_b^{gross} = R_s \times \log_2(M)$$

where $M$ is the number of discrete signal levels and $R_s$ is the symbol (baud) rate in symbols/second.

**Logic:** Each symbol carries $\log_2(M)$ bits of information. PAM-3 has 3 distinct voltage levels, hence each symbol can in principle encode $\log_2(3)$ bits.

### Step 2 — Substitute the values for 100BASE-T1 PAM-3

$$R_b^{gross} = 66.667 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(3)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate $\log_2(3)$

We know that $\log_2(3) = \dfrac{\ln(3)}{\ln(2)}$.

$$\log_2(3) = \frac{1.098612}{0.693147} \approx 1.58496 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply to get the gross bit rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_b^{gross} &= 66.667 \times 10^6 \times 1.58496 \\
            &= 105.66 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \\
            &\approx 105.66 \text{ Mbps (gross)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the 3B2T block mapping (4B3T context)

In 100BASE-T1, a **3B2T** (3-Bit Binary to 2-Ternary Symbol) block code is used. Over 2 ternary symbols, we can represent $3^2 = 9$ distinct patterns, sufficient to encode $2^3 = 8$ binary states plus one redundancy bit for DC balancing. The net information carried per 2 ternary symbols is 3 bits.

Therefore the **net bit rate** is:

$$
R_b^{net} = R_s \times \frac{3 \text{ bits}}{2 \text{ symbols}} = 66.667 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 = 100.0 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}
$$

**Result:** $R_b^{net} = 100$ Mbps, matching the IEEE 802.3bw specification.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Frame Transmission Latency for a 1500-Byte Ethernet Frame over 100BASE-T1

**Given:** Frame size $L = 1500$ bytes, line rate $R_b = 100$ Mbps, propagation velocity in copper $v_p \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, cable length $d = 15$ m (max in-vehicle per 100BASE-T1).

### Step 1 — Compute frame transmission time (serialization delay)

$$
t_{tx} = \frac{L \times 8}{R_b}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tx} &= \frac{1500 \times 8}{100 \times 10^6} \\
       &= \frac{12000}{100 \times 10^6} \\
       &= 120 \times 10^{-6} \text{ seconds} \\
       &= 120 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each byte contains 8 bits, so a 1500-byte frame is 12,000 bits. Divided by 100 million bits per second, it takes 120 µs to push the entire frame onto the wire.

### Step 2 — Compute propagation delay over the cable

$$
t_{prop} = \frac{d}{v_p}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{prop} &= \frac{15}{2 \times 10^8} \\
         &= 7.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} \\
         &= 75 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Total one-way latency

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{lat} &= t_{tx} + t_{prop} \\
        &= 120{,}000 \text{ ns} + 75 \text{ ns} \\
        &\approx 120.075 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The propagation delay is negligible compared to serialization delay. For ADAS control loops running at 100 Hz (10 ms period), this 120 µs latency consumes only **1.2% of the available cycle time** — a comfortable margin.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Shannon Capacity Bound for an Automotive Cable Channel

**Given:** Channel bandwidth $B = 66.67$ MHz (100BASE-T1 symbol rate dominated by cable), measured SNR $\approx 30$ dB (i.e., $S/N = 1000$).

### Step 1 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem

$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

### Step 2 — Convert 30 dB to linear ratio

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 66.67 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \\
  &= 66.67 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1001) \\
  &= 66.67 \times 10^6 \times 9.966 \\
  &\approx 664.4 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Theoretically, a 100BASE-T1 cable with 30 dB SNR could carry up to **664 Mbps**. The actual 100 Mbps design provides a **6.6× safety margin**, ensuring robust operation under temperature, vibration, and EMI variations in vehicles.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Automotive Ethernet vs Legacy Buses

| Parameter | CAN | LIN | FlexRay | MOST150 | **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Bandwidth** | 1 Mbps (CAN 2.0) | 20 kbps | 10 Mbps | 150 Mbps | **100 Mbps** |
| **Wiring** | 2-wire differential | 1-wire | 2 twisted pairs (redundant) | Plastic Optical Fiber | **1 twisted pair (UTP)** |
| **Topology** | Bus | Bus | Bus / Star | Ring | **Star / Switched** |
| **Determinism** | Event-driven (CSMA/CA) | Schedule-based | TDMA | Stream-based | **TSN-enhanced (TAS, FRER)** |
| **Latency** | ~1 ms @ 1 Mbps | ~10 ms | < 10 µs scheduled | ~10 ms | **< 100 µs (typical)** |
| **Max Nodes** | ~30 (electrical) | 16 | 22 | 64 | **Practically unlimited (switched)** |
| **Weight per meter** | Medium | Low | High (2 pairs) | N/A (POF) | **Lowest** |
| **EMC/EMI** | Good | Limited | Good | Excellent | **Engineered for OEM specs** |
| **IP Convergence** | No | No | No | Partial | **Full TCP/IP stack** |
| **Use Case** | Powertrain, chassis | Body electronics | Chassis, drivetrain | Infotainment | **ADAS, backbone, infotainment, OTA** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 In-Vehicle Zonal Architecture with Automotive Ethernet Backbone

The diagram below shows a typical modern vehicle's zonal electrical/electronic (E/E) architecture, where Automotive Ethernet acts as the central nervous system connecting zone controllers, ADAS domain controllers, infotainment, and central compute.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Zone1["FRONT LEFT ZONE"]
        Z1ECU["Zone Controller 1"]
        CamFL["Front-Left Camera"]
        Rad1["Short-range Radar"]
        Lidar1["LIDAR Sensor"]
    end

    subgraph Zone2["FRONT RIGHT ZONE"]
        Z2ECU["Zone Controller 2"]
        CamFR["Front-Right Camera"]
        Rad2["Short-range Radar"]
    end

    subgraph Zone3["REAR ZONE"]
        Z3ECU["Zone Controller 3"]
        CamR["Rear-View Camera"]
        USS["Ultrasonic Sensors"]
    end

    subgraph Central["CENTRAL COMPUTE CLUSTER"]
        ADAS["ADAS Domain Controller"]
        Cockpit["Digital Cockpit SoC"]
        Gateway["Central Gateway"]
        Cloud["Cloud / OTA Link"]
    end

    subgraph Legacy["LEGACY BUS SEGMENTS"]
        CANBus["CAN-FD Powertrain"]
        LINBus["LIN Body"]
    end

    ETH1["1000BASE-T1 Backbone 1 Gbps"]
    ETH2["100BASE-T1 Sensor Links 100 Mbps"]
    ETH3["10GBASE-T1 Compute Link 10 Gbps"]

    Z1ECU --- ETH2
    Z2ECU --- ETH2
    Z3ECU --- ETH2
    CamFL --- ETH2
    CamFR --- ETH2
    CamR --- ETH2
    Rad1 --- ETH2
    Rad2 --- ETH2
    Lidar1 --- ETH2
    USS --- ETH2

    Z1ECU --- ETH1
    Z2ECU --- ETH1
    Z3ECU --- ETH1
    ADAS --- ETH1
    Cockpit --- ETH1
    Gateway --- ETH1

    ADAS --- ETH3
    Cockpit --- ETH3

    Gateway --- CANBus
    Gateway --- LINBus
    Gateway --- Cloud
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Solid lines** represent **Automotive Ethernet links** (100 Mbps for sensors, 1 Gbps for zone-to-central, 10 Gbps for compute-internal).
> - **Zone controllers** aggregate sensor data and forward it northbound to the central compute cluster (ADAS + Cockpit SoCs).
> - The **Central Gateway** bridges Ethernet to legacy CAN-FD (powertrain) and LIN (body) networks, allowing gradual migration.
> - **TSN** (not explicitly drawn) is enabled on the 1 Gbps backbone to guarantee latency for safety-critical sensor fusion traffic.

## 4.2 OSI-to-Automotive Ethernet Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP["APPLICATION\nSOME/IP, DoIP, XCP, UDS, DDS"]
    TRANS["TRANSPORT\nTCP, UDP, TSN Stream Reservation"]
    NET["NETWORK\nIPv4, IPv6, ICMPv6"]
    MAC["DATA LINK MAC\nIEEE 802.3 CSMA/CD, TSN 802.1Q"]
    PHY["PHYSICAL PHY\n100BASE-T1, 1000BASE-T1\nPAM-3, Echo Cancellation"]
    CABLE["MEDIUM\nSingle Twisted Pair, UTP/STP, 15 m"]

    APP --> TRANS --> NET --> MAC --> PHY --> CABLE
```

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Alert:** Examiners frequently ask — *"Which OSI layer does TSN operate at?"* The correct answer is **Layer 2 (Data Link)**, specifically within the IEEE 802.1Q bridging sublayer, sitting just above the IEEE 802.3 PHY. Be precise — TSN is **not** a Layer-3 protocol.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Automotive Ethernet. List any two IEEE standards associated with it. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Automotive Ethernet is a single-pair, full-duplex, IEEE 802.3-based networking technology designed for in-vehicle communication. It supports high bandwidth (100 Mbps to 10 Gbps), reduced wiring weight, and EMC compliance for harsh automotive environments. **(2 marks)**
Two associated IEEE standards: **(1 mark)**
1. IEEE 802.3bw — 100BASE-T1 (100 Mbps)
2. IEEE 802.3bp — 1000BASE-T1 (1 Gbps)

### Question 2: What is the role of the OPEN Alliance SIG in Automotive Ethernet? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **OPEN Alliance (One-Pair Ether-Net) Special Interest Group)** is an industry consortium that promotes the adoption of Automotive Ethernet. **(1 mark)**
Its main roles are: **(2 marks)**
1. Developing technical specifications and conformance test suites (e.g., TC1–TC12) for BroadR-Reach/100BASE-T1 and 1000BASE-T1 PHYs.
2. Ensuring multi-vendor interoperability through standardized test procedures and EMC compliance validation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the physical layer of 100BASE-T1 Automotive Ethernet in detail. Discuss the choice of PAM-3 modulation and 3B2T line coding. Mention the maximum cable length and EMC requirements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Physical Layer Overview (2 marks)**
100BASE-T1, defined by **IEEE 802.3bw**, transmits **100 Mbps** in **full-duplex** mode over a **single unshielded twisted pair (UTP)**. It eliminates the need for separate transmit and receive pairs, halving the wiring harness weight and cost. The PHY is designed to meet stringent automotive EMC (CISPR 25 Class 5) and emission standards.

**2. PAM-3 Modulation (2 marks)**
PAM-3 uses **three voltage levels** (typically −1 V, 0 V, +1 V differential) to encode ternary symbols. Each symbol carries $\log_2(3) \approx 1.585$ bits of information. At a symbol rate of **66.667 MBd**, the gross bit rate is ~105.66 Mbps. PAM-3 was chosen over binary NRZ because:
- It reduces the required **bandwidth by a factor of $\log_2(3)/2 \approx 0.79$** compared to NRZ, allowing operation on lower-grade in-vehicle cables.
- It lowers EMI by avoiding sharp 2-level transitions.

**3. 3B2T Line Coding (2 marks)**
The 3B2T (3-Bit to 2-Ternary-Symbol) block code maps every 3 binary bits into 2 ternary symbols. Of the $3^2 = 9$ possible ternary patterns, 8 are used for data and 1 for DC-balancing (to keep the average DC level near zero, avoiding baseline wander). This yields a net bit rate of:

$$R_b = 66.667 \text{ MBd} \times \frac{3 \text{ bits}}{2 \text{ symbols}} = 100 \text{ Mbps}$$

**4. Cable Length and EMC (1 mark)**
- Maximum cable length: **15 meters** in-vehicle.
- Meets **CISPR 25 Class 5** radiated/conducted emission limits.
- Operates over the temperature range **−40 °C to +105 °C** (Grade 2 automotive).

---

> **(b)** Describe the SOME/IP protocol. How does it enable service-oriented communication in modern vehicles? Compare SOME/IP with traditional CAN communication. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. SOME/IP Overview (2 marks)**
**SOME/IP (Scalable service-Oriented MiddlewarE over IP)** is an automotive middleware protocol standardized by **AUTOSAR** for Ethernet-based in-vehicle networks. It allows ECUs to:
- **Offer services** (methods, events, fields) to other ECUs.
- **Discover services** dynamically via **SOME/IP-SD (Service Discovery)**.
- **Invoke methods** (RPC) and **subscribe to events** (publish-subscribe).

**2. Communication Patterns (2 marks)**
- **Request/Response (RPC):** Client calls a method on the server ECU; server returns the result.
- **Fire-and-Forget:** Client sends a message with no expectation of response.
- **Event Notification:** Server pushes updates to subscribed clients when state changes occur.
- **Field Access:** Combines setter, getter, and notification for parameter values (e.g., vehicle speed).

**3. SOME/IP vs Traditional CAN (3 marks)**

| Aspect | CAN | SOME/IP |
|---|---|---|
| **Paradigm** | Signal-based (broadcast) | Service-oriented (client-server) |
| **Addressing** | CAN ID (11/29-bit) | IP + Port + Service ID |
| **Bandwidth** | 1 Mbps (CAN 2.0) | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps |
| **Payload Size** | 0–8 bytes | Up to 1400+ bytes (UDP) |
| **Discovery** | Static (compile-time) | Dynamic (runtime, SOME/IP-SD) |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid (signal layout fixed) | Flexible (data serialization via SOME/IP-TP) |
| **Use Case** | Powertrain, chassis | ADAS, infotainment, OTA, body |

SOME/IP enables **dynamic service discovery, large payload support, and decoupling** of software components, which is essential for software-defined vehicles (SDVs).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the layered architecture of Automotive Ethernet. Identify the layer at which TSN operates and list three TSN standards used in vehicles. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Layered Architecture (3 marks)** (refer to Section 2.1 / Section 4.2)
The Automotive Ethernet protocol stack consists of five primary layers:
- **Application Layer:** SOME/IP, DoIP, UDS, XCP, DDS, ROS 2
- **Transport Layer:** TCP, UDP, TSN stream reservation
- **Network Layer:** IPv4, IPv6, ICMP
- **Data Link Layer (MAC):** IEEE 802.3 with TSN extensions
- **Physical Layer (PHY):** 100BASE-T1, 1000BASE-T1, 10GBASE-T1 (PAM-3 / PAM-2)

**2. TSN Layer Placement (1 mark)**
TSN operates at **Layer 2 (Data Link)**, specifically in the **IEEE 802.1Q bridging sublayer** (between MAC and PHY). It introduces scheduling, shaping, and synchronization mechanisms without altering the IP layer.

**3. Three TSN Standards (3 marks)**
1. **IEEE 802.1AS (gPTP):** Provides sub-microsecond clock synchronization across all bridge nodes — prerequisite for time-triggered scheduling.
2. **IEEE 802.1Qbv (Time-Aware Shaper):** Defines scheduled gates on each transmit queue so that time-critical traffic (e.g., camera frames) gets a guaranteed transmission window.
3. **IEEE 802.1CB (Frame Replication and Elimination):** Replicates critical frames over redundant paths and eliminates duplicates at the receiver — provides zero-loss redundancy for safety-critical flows.

---

> **(b)** Compute the net bit rate of 100BASE-T1 given a symbol rate of 66.667 MBd and PAM-3 modulation with 3B2T block coding. Also calculate the time taken to transmit a 1500-byte Ethernet frame on this link. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Given Data (1 mark)**
- Symbol rate $R_s = 66.667 \times 10^6$ symbols/s
- Modulation: PAM-3 ($M = 3$)
- Block coding: 3B2T

**2. Gross Bit Rate (2 marks)**
$$
R_b^{gross} = R_s \times \log_2(3) = 66.667 \times 10^6 \times 1.58496 \approx 105.66 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**3. Net Bit Rate after 3B2T (2 marks)**
With 3 binary bits encoded into 2 ternary symbols, the information per symbol is $\frac{3}{2} = 1.5$ bits:
$$
R_b^{net} = 66.667 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 = 100.0 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**4. Frame Transmission Time (2 marks)**
$$
t_{tx} = \frac{L \times 8}{R_b} = \frac{1500 \times 8}{100 \times 10^6} = \frac{12{,}000}{10^8} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 120 \text{ µs}
$$

**Result:** The link operates at 100 Mbps, and a maximum-size 1500-byte frame takes **120 µs** to serialize.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing 100BASE-TX with 100BASE-T1:** Students often write "100BASE-TX" (office Ethernet) instead of "100BASE-T1" (automotive). Remember: **T1 = automotive, TX = office.** (Lose 1 mark easily.)
> 2. **Wrong modulation for 1000BASE-T1:** Students assume 1000BASE-T1 uses PAM-3. **Incorrect** — 1000BASE-T1 uses **PAM-2 (binary NRZ)** at 750 MBd. Only 100BASE-T1 uses PAM-3.
> 3. **Misidentifying TSN layer:** TSN is **Layer 2**, not Layer 3. Writing "TSN is a network layer protocol" is a common, costly error.
> 4. **Omitting the 3B2T block code in derivations:** Writing only $\log_2(3)$ for 100BASE-T1 bit rate yields 105.66 Mbps — **wrong answer** for KTU. You must explicitly apply the 3B2T mapping to get 100 Mbps.
> 5. **Mixing up SOME/IP with DoIP:** SOME/IP is for service-oriented middleware; **DoIP (ISO 13400) is specifically for diagnostics.** Examiners love this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Automotive Ethernet** = IEEE 802.3 over a **single twisted pair**, full-duplex, designed for in-vehicle EMC and weight constraints.
- **Key Standards:** IEEE 802.3bw (100BASE-T1 @ 100 Mbps, PAM-3, 3B2T), 802.3bp (1000BASE-T1 @ 1 Gbps, PAM-2), 802.3ch (2.5G/5G/10G BASE-T1).
- **PAM-3 (3B2T)** in 100BASE-T1: 3 voltage levels, 66.667 MBd, net 100 Mbps. Symbol rate × 1.5 bits/symbol = 100 Mbps.
- **PAM-2 (NRZ)** in 1000BASE-T1: 750 MBd, 1 Gbps, full-duplex with echo cancellation.
- **Max cable length** for 100BASE-T1 = **15 m**; operating temperature = **−40 °C to +105 °C**; BER target = **≤ 10⁻¹⁰**.
- **OPEN Alliance SIG** drives the automotive Ethernet ecosystem (BroadR-Reach, TC1–TC12 conformance tests).
- **TSN (IEEE 802.1)** operates at **Layer 2**; key standards are **802.1AS (gPTP)**, **802.1Qbv (TAS)**, **802.1Qbu/802.3br (preemption)**, **802.1CB (FRER)**.
- **SOME/IP** = service-oriented middleware (AUTOSAR); supports RPC, event notification, field access, service discovery.
- **DoIP (ISO 13400)** = diagnostics over IP — used for ECU flashing and UDS-over-IP.
- **Zonal architecture** replaces domain-based E/E with zonal controllers aggregated over an Ethernet backbone, reducing wiring harness weight by up to **30%**.
- **Frame transmission time** for 1500 bytes at 100 Mbps = **120 µs** — fits comfortably within 10 ms ADAS control loops.
- **Comparison mantra:** CAN = 1 Mbps / 8 bytes; FlexRay = 10 Mbps / scheduled; MOST = 150 Mbps / POF; **Automotive Ethernet = 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps / single UTP pair / IP-native**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling Real Time CPS Tasks: Table-Driven and Event-Driven Schedulers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scheduling Real Time CPS Tasks: Table-Driven and Event-Driven Schedulers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**, computational elements (the *cyber* part) interact tightly with physical processes (the *physical* part) through sensors and actuators. Because these systems control real-world phenomena (e.g., a robotic arm, an automotive airbag, an aircraft autopilot), the *correctness* of a computation depends not only on its **logical result** but also on **the time at which the result is delivered**. This is called **temporal correctness**, and the systems that demand it are **Real-Time Systems**.

**Real-Time Task Scheduling** is the mechanism by which the embedded processor decides *which* computational job to execute *when*, in order to meet the **deadlines** of all tasks in the workload. A scheduler is an **OS/software module** (or hardware unit) that implements this decision policy.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, real-time schedulers for CPS are classified into two broad families:

1. **Table-Driven (Static / Offline / Time-Triggered) Schedulers** — The schedule is computed *before* run-time, during the design phase, and stored in a lookup table. At run-time, the scheduler simply reads the next entry from the table.
2. **Event-Driven (Dynamic / Online / Priority-Driven) Schedulers** — Scheduling decisions are made *at run-time* based on the occurrence of events (task arrivals, interrupts) using a pre-defined *priority assignment rule*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A real-time CPS task is modelled as $\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_i, P_i)$ where $C_i$ is the worst-case execution time (WCET), $T_i$ is the period, $D_i$ is the relative deadline, and $P_i$ is the priority. The scheduler must guarantee that every job finishes by its deadline.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the manager of a busy **railway control room** in Trivandrum Central station:

- **Table-Driven (Train Timetable) Approach:** The complete daily timetable for *every* train is printed and bound into a thick book *before* the day begins. When 09:00 AM arrives, the controller simply flips to the "09:00" page and follows the pre-decided instructions: "Train 12654 leaves platform 4 for Chennai." The controller does not *think* — he *looks up*. If a new requirement arises (e.g., a special train), the timetable book must be re-printed.

- **Event-Driven (Signal-Based) Approach:** There is no fixed timetable. Instead, each train carries a **priority tag** (Rajdhani > Express > Passenger > Freight). The controller constantly watches incoming signals (events) — "Train X has arrived at the outer signal" — and immediately decides which platform to free up based on the *highest-priority* train that is waiting. This is more *flexible* (handles surprises) but the controller is constantly making decisions and risks making the *wrong* choice under heavy load.

The **train timetable** is the **table**; the **priority tag** is the **scheduling policy**.

### 1.3 Why This Matters in a CPS

A modern car has 70–100 ECUs running 1,000+ functions. The **anti-lock braking system (ABS)** must react within **5 ms**; the **airbag deployment** must fire within **10 ms**; the **infotainment volume button** has a deadline of **100 ms** (a missed deadline is just a "delayed click"). Scheduling ensures that the *most time-critical* cyber decisions reach the *physical* actuators on time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Term:** The *Hyper-period* $H$ of a task set is the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** of all task periods: $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$. The schedule repeats every $H$ time units, so a table-driven schedule only needs to store one hyper-period's worth of decisions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Table-Driven (Static / Offline) Schedulers

**Operating Principle:** The *entire* schedule is built offline as a static table indexed by time. At each clock tick, the scheduler does only a table lookup. This makes it **highly predictable**, **deterministic**, and **fast** (constant-time dispatch).

**Common Implementations:**

- **Cyclic Executive (Cyclic Schedule)**
- **Time-Triggered Architecture (TTA)** — e.g., TTP/C, FlexRay static segment
- **Pre-computed Static Schedule** for multi-processor CPS

#### 2.1.1 Cyclic Executive

The hyper-period $H$ is divided into **major frames** of equal length $F$. Each major frame is further divided into **minor slots**. A task $\tau_i$ is assigned one or more minor slots, and the cyclic executive dispatches the task in the same slots every major frame.

**Key Design Equation:**

$$
F = \frac{H}{N_{\text{frames}}} \quad \text{where } N_{\text{frames}} = \frac{H}{F}
$$

**Conditions for feasibility:**

$$
\forall \, i, \quad C_i \le F
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lceil \frac{T_i}{F} \right\rceil \cdot C_i \le H
$$

The first condition ensures every task fits into a single minor slot. The second ensures total demand does not exceed available time across the entire hyper-period.

#### 2.1.2 Pros and Cons of Table-Driven Scheduling

| Aspect | Verdict |
|---|---|
| Predictability | ★★★★★ Excellent |
| Run-time overhead | ★★★★★ Negligible |
| Flexibility | ★ Low (must rebuild table for any change) |
| Handling aperiodic events | ★ Poor (typically polled) |
| Typical CPS use | Automotive ECUs, Aerospace flight control, Industrial PLCs |

### 2.2 Event-Driven (Dynamic / Online) Schedulers

**Operating Principle:** The scheduler runs an algorithm on every event (task arrival, completion, or tick) to decide which ready task to run next, based on **priorities**.

#### 2.2.1 Fixed-Priority Scheduling (FPS) — Rate Monotonic (RM)

Each task is assigned a *static* priority proportional to its frequency (shorter period → higher priority). **Liu & Layland (1973)** proved this is **optimal among static-priority schemes**.

**Exact Response Time Analysis (RTA) Equation:**

The worst-case response time $R_i$ of task $\tau_i$ is the smallest fixed point of:

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

where $hp(i)$ is the set of tasks with *higher* priority than $\tau_i$. Feasibility requires $R_i \le D_i$ for all $i$.

**Liu–Layland Sufficient (but not necessary) Utilization Bound:**

For $n$ tasks on a single processor:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

As $n \to \infty$, this bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx \mathbf{0.693}$.

#### 2.2.2 Dynamic-Priority Scheduling — Earliest Deadline First (EDF)

The ready task with the **earliest absolute deadline** is selected at every scheduling decision point. EDF is **optimal** for uniprocessor preemptive scheduling on *any* feasible task set (a task set is schedulable under EDF iff its total utilization $U \le 1$).

**Exact Processor Demand Test (Baruah et al.):**

$$
\forall \, L \in \{ D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n \} \cup [0, L^*], \quad g(0) = 0
$$

$$
g(L) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \max\left(0, \left\lfloor \frac{L - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor + 1 \right) \cdot C_i
$$

Feasibility: $g(L) \le L$ for all checkpoints $L$.

**Density-based test for $D_i = T_i$:**

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1 \quad \text{(necessary and sufficient)}
$$

#### 2.2.3 Pros and Cons of Event-Driven Scheduling

| Aspect | Verdict |
|---|---|
| Adaptability | ★★★★★ Excellent (handles aperiodics easily) |
| Run-time overhead | ★★ Higher (priority queue, log) |
| Predictability | ★★★ Good (provably) |
| Implementation complexity | ★★ Moderate |
| Typical CPS use | Multimedia, IoT gateways, General-purpose RTOS (VxWorks, FreeRTOS) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Meaning | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \text{lcm}(T_1,\dots,T_n)$ | Hyper-period (schedule repeats) | time units |
| 2 | $U = \sum C_i / T_i$ | Processor utilization | dimensionless, $0 \le U \le 1$ |
| 3 | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ | Worst-case response time (RTA) | time units |
| 4 | $U_{RM}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | RM sufficient bound | $n \ge 1$ |
| 5 | $\lim_{n\to\infty} U_{RM}(n) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | Asymptotic RM bound | constant |
| 6 | $U_{EDF} \le 1$ | EDF feasibility | dimensionless |
| 7 | $g(L) = \sum \max(0, \lfloor (L-D_i)/T_i \rfloor + 1) C_i$ | Processor demand | time units |
| 8 | $F = H / N_{\text{frames}}$ | Cyclic major frame size | time units |
| 9 | $C_i \le F$ | Slot-fitting constraint | time units |
| 10 | $\text{Slack} = D_i - R_i$ | Timing margin (positive ⇒ safe) | time units |

> [!NOTE]
> All vertical bars in the table above are written as `\vert` for compatibility, e.g., $\vert L-D_i \vert$ would be entered as `\vert L-D_i \vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in CPS Engineering

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR / OSEK):** Use a mixed approach — a static **table-driven** schedule for the safety-critical powertrain/braking ECUs, and **event-driven** FreeRTOS for body electronics.
- **Avionics (ARINC-653):** Time-partitioned scheduling — a *table-driven* hyper-period divided into fixed-length **time windows**, each belonging to a safety-criticality partition.
- **Industrial IoT (OPC UA + TSN):** Time-Sensitive Networking on the wire pairs with **Time-Aware Shapers** (a form of table-driven scheduling) to deliver bounded latency to CPS controllers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Response Time Equation for Rate Monotonic Scheduling

**Statement:** For a task $\tau_i$ under fixed-priority preemptive scheduling, the worst-case response time $R_i$ is the smallest non-negative solution of the fixed-point equation:

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Let $W_i(t)$ denote the total amount of computation that *must* be done in the interval $[0, t]$ to guarantee that $\tau_i$ completes by time $t$.

*Step 1 — Self-load.* Task $\tau_i$ itself consumes $C_i$ time units of CPU. So $W_i(t)$ includes at least $C_i$.

$$
W_i(t) \ge C_i
$$

*Step 2 — Interference from higher-priority tasks.* Any task $\tau_j$ with priority higher than $\tau_i$ will preempt $\tau_i$ as many times as it arrives within the interval $[0, t]$. Task $\tau_j$ arrives once every $T_j$ time units, so in an interval of length $t$ it can release at most $\lceil t / T_j \rceil$ jobs. Each job consumes $C_j$ time. Therefore the maximum preemption from $\tau_j$ is:

$$
I_{j \to i}(t) = \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

*Step 3 — Summing over all higher-priority tasks.* Total interference in $[0, t]$:

$$
I_i(t) = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

*Step 4 — Total work that must complete by time $t$.*

$$
W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

*Step 5 — Worst-case completion time.* The worst-case response time $R_i$ is the smallest $t \ge 0$ such that $W_i(t) = t$. Equivalently, the fixed point of:

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

*Step 6 — Feasibility condition.* The task meets its deadline iff $R_i \le D_i$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Liu–Layland Utilization Bound for RM

**Statement:** A set of $n$ independent, preemptable periodic tasks with $D_i = T_i$ scheduled by Rate Monotonic is guaranteed to meet all deadlines if:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

**Derivation sketch:**

*Step 1 — Worst-case phasing.* The critical instant for $\tau_i$ occurs when *all* higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with $\tau_i$ and the higher-priority tasks have their largest possible relative deadline $T_j = 2 C_j$. (Detailed proof by contradiction; we provide the pivotal inequality.)

*Step 2 — Bound the response time of the lowest-priority task in the worst case.* In the worst scenario the lowest-priority task's response time $R_n$ satisfies:

$$
R_n \le \frac{C_n}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} C_j / T_j}
$$

*Step 3 — Enforce $R_n \le T_n$.*

$$
\frac{C_n}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} C_j / T_j} \le T_n
$$

*Step 4 — Convert to utilization constraint.* By induction, the tightest bound is achieved when every task saturates $R_i = T_i$, yielding:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

*Step 5 — Limiting case.* As $n \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693
$$

This explains why for very large task sets, RM guarantees only ~**69.3 %** CPU utilization; the rest must be left idle or used for aperiodic servers.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Example

**Problem:** Consider two periodic tasks scheduled by RM on a uniprocessor.

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 | 6 |

Higher-priority task is $\tau_1$ (shorter period).

**Step A — Compute utilization.**

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} = 0.25 + 0.3333 = 0.5833
$$

**Step B — Check Liu–Layland bound for $n=2$.**

$$
U_{RM}(2) = 2 \left( 2^{1/2} - 1 \right) = 2 (1.4142 - 1) = 0.8284
$$

Since $0.5833 \le 0.8284$, the bound **guarantees** feasibility.

**Step C — Verify by exact RTA.**

*For $\tau_1$ (highest priority):* No higher-priority tasks, so $R_1 = C_1 = 1 \le D_1 = 4$. ✓

*For $\tau_2$:*

$$
R_2 = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1
$$

Iteration 1: try $R_2^{(0)} = 2$:

$$
R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

Iteration 2: try $R_2^{(1)} = 3$:

$$
R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

Converged: $R_2 = 3 \le D_2 = 6$. ✓

**Step D — Hyper-period for table-driven version.**

$$
H = \text{lcm}(4, 6) = 12 \text{ ms}
$$

So a table-driven schedule would need 12 ms / (1 ms slots) = 12 entries. RM achieves the same in *zero* table storage.

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Table-Driven vs Event-Driven

| Criterion | Table-Driven (Cyclic / TTA) | Event-Driven (RM / EDF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision time** | Offline (design) | Online (every event) |
| **Run-time cost** | O(1) table lookup | O(log n) priority queue |
| **Optimality** | Optimal if feasible, but feasibility hard to check | RM optimal among static; EDF globally optimal |
| **Aperiodic handling** | Awkward (polled or background) | Native (background / sporadic server) |
| **Overload behavior** | Predictable failures | Priority-inversion / domino effect |
| **Code complexity** | Low | Higher |
| **Certification (DO-178C, ISO 26262)** | Easier | Harder (worst-case must be proved) |
| **Memory footprint** | Stores table of size $H/\Delta$ | Stores priorities, queues |
| **CPS example** | Engine control unit, ABS | Smartphone SoC, drone flight stack |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid Trend in Modern CPS:** Most production RTOSes (AUTOSAR OS, VxWorks, RTEMS) provide *both* — a table-driven **time-slice** for partition scheduling, inside which event-driven priorities handle the actual tasks. This is the de-facto CPS pattern.


## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between table-driven and event-driven real-time schedulers used in Cyber-Physical Systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Table-Driven (Static) | Event-Driven (Dynamic) |
|---|---|---|
| Decision making | At design time, stored in a table | At run time, on every event |
| Predictability | Very high (deterministic) | Provable but less deterministic |
| Overhead | Negligible (O(1) lookup) | Higher (priority queue ops) |
| Flexibility to aperiodics | Poor (polling required) | Excellent |

*(1 mark for each contrasting pair, full credit for 3+ contrasts.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the hyper-period of a real-time task set? Why is it important for table-driven scheduling?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The hyper-period $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$ is the smallest time interval after which the pattern of task releases repeats exactly. In table-driven scheduling, the offline schedule needs to be generated for only one hyper-period (1 mark). At run time, the schedule is dispatched cyclically every $H$ units (1 mark), guaranteeing that the same offline-computed decisions apply for the entire system lifetime (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with internal choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Recommended Choice for ESE*

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
*(a)* Explain with a neat block diagram the **cyclic executive** approach to table-driven scheduling in a CPS. State the necessary feasibility conditions. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Consider the following periodic task set scheduled on a uniprocessor under **Rate Monotonic** scheduling. Compute the worst-case response time of each task using the exact RTA iteration. Is the task set schedulable? *(7 marks)*

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 5 | 5 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 10 | 10 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 15 | 15 |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
*Block diagram (3 marks):*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT[System Clock Tick] --> nodeM[Major Frame Counter]
    nodeM --> nodeMI[Minor Slot Index]
    nodeMI --> nodeTBL[Static Schedule Table]
    nodeTBL --> nodeDIS[Dispatcher]
    nodeDIS --> nodeEXE[Execute Assigned Task]
    nodeEXE --> nodeINC[Increment Counter]
    nodeINC --> nodeM
```

*Explanation (2 marks):* The hyper-period is divided into $N$ equal major frames of length $F$. Each major frame is divided into minor slots. The static table maps each (major-frame, minor-slot) pair to a specific task. The dispatcher reads the entry and launches the corresponding task.

*Feasibility conditions (2 marks):*
1. $C_i \le F$ for every task $\tau_i$ (slot-fitting).
2. $\sum_i \lceil T_i / F \rceil \cdot C_i \le H$ (demand ≤ supply across the hyper-period).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Priority assignment (RM):* $\tau_1$ (T=5) > $\tau_2$ (T=10) > $\tau_3$ (T=15). (1 mark)

*For $\tau_1$:* No higher priority, so $R_1 = C_1 = 1$ ms $\le D_1 = 5$ ms. ✓ (1 mark)

*For $\tau_2$:* $R_2 = C_2 + \lceil R_2 / T_1 \rceil \cdot C_1 = 2 + \lceil R_2 / 5 \rceil \cdot 1$.

- Try $R_2^{(0)} = 2$: $R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \lceil 2/5 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$.
- Try $R_2^{(1)} = 3$: $R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \lceil 3/5 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$. Converged. $R_2 = 3 \le D_2 = 10$ ✓ (1 mark)

*For $\tau_3$:* $R_3 = C_3 + \lceil R_3 / T_1 \rceil C_1 + \lceil R_3 / T_2 \rceil C_2 = 3 + \lceil R_3/5 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil R_3/10 \rceil \cdot 2$.

- Try $R_3^{(0)} = 3$: $\lceil 3/5 \rceil = 1$, $\lceil 3/10 \rceil = 1$, so $R_3^{(1)} = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$.
- Try $R_3^{(1)} = 6$: $\lceil 6/5 \rceil = 2$, $\lceil 6/10 \rceil = 1$, so $R_3^{(2)} = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$.
- Try $R_3^{(2)} = 7$: $\lceil 7/5 \rceil = 2$, $\lceil 7/10 \rceil = 1$, so $R_3^{(3)} = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7$. Converged. $R_3 = 7 \le D_3 = 15$ ✓ (3 marks)

*Conclusion (1 mark):* The task set is **schedulable** under RM.

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice for ESE*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**
*(a)* With neat diagrams, explain the **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** algorithm. State and prove its optimality condition. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* For the task set in Q.A(b) above, check feasibility under EDF using the **processor demand criterion**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Algorithm (3 marks):* At every scheduling event (job release, completion, or tick), select from the ready queue the job whose **absolute deadline** $d_i = r_i + D_i$ is smallest. Preempt the running job if a newly arrived job has an earlier deadline.

*Optimality statement (2 marks):* If a task set is feasible on a single processor under *any* scheduling policy, then it is also feasible under EDF. Equivalently, EDF fails a deadline *only* if the total utilization $U > 1$.

*Proof sketch (2 marks):* Suppose EDF misses a deadline at time $t_d$. Exchange argument: swap the offending job with the jobs that ran in $[0, t_d]$. Each swap does not increase the deadline miss. After all swaps, only jobs that have missed deadlines in EDF are running — implying the demand exceeds supply, i.e., $U > 1$, contradicting feasibility.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

The processor demand function:

$$
g(L) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \max\left(0, \left\lfloor \frac{L - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor + 1\right) \cdot C_i
$$

Feasibility: $g(L) \le L$ at every checkpoint $L \in \{D_i, 2D_i - T_i, \ldots\}$ up to $L^* = \max(D_i) + \text{slack}$.

*Checkpoints:* $L \in \{5, 10, 15\}$ (and combinations). (1 mark)

- At $L = 5$: 
  - $\tau_1$: $\lfloor (5-5)/5 \rfloor + 1 = 1$, contribution = 1.
  - $\tau_2$: $\lfloor (5-10)/10 \rfloor + 1 = 0$ (negative), contribution = 0.
  - $\tau_3$: $\lfloor (5-15)/15 \rfloor + 1 = 0$, contribution = 0.
  - Total $g(5) = 1 \le 5$. ✓ (2 marks)

- At $L = 10$:
  - $\tau_1$: $\lfloor (10-5)/5 \rfloor + 1 = 2$, contribution = 2.
  - $\tau_2$: $\lfloor (10-10)/10 \rfloor + 1 = 1$, contribution = 2.
  - $\tau_3$: $\lfloor (10-15)/15 \rfloor + 1 = 0$, contribution = 0.
  - Total $g(10) = 4 \le 10$. ✓ (2 marks)

- At $L = 15$:
  - $\tau_1$: $\lfloor 10/5 \rfloor + 1 = 3$, contribution = 3.
  - $\tau_2$: $\lfloor 5/10 \rfloor + 1 = 1$, contribution = 2.
  - $\tau_3$: $\lfloor 0/15 \rfloor + 1 = 1$, contribution = 3.
  - Total $g(15) = 8 \le 15$. ✓ (1 mark)

*Utilization check (1 mark):* $U = 1/5 + 2/10 + 3/15 = 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.6 \le 1$ — feasible under EDF.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In RTA iterations, *always* show **at least two iterations** even if it converges in one — examiners award partial credit for demonstrating the technique.
> 2. In the processor demand test, do **not** stop at the first checkpoint. Students lose 2–3 marks for omitting checkpoints.
> 3. In RM, the *highest* priority goes to the *shortest* period. Mixing this up costs the priority-assignment mark.
> 4. In table-driven, $C_i \le F$ is a *necessary* condition — forgetting it is a common 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Real-Time CPS** demands *temporal* correctness in addition to logical correctness.
- **Table-driven (offline) schedulers** pre-compute the entire schedule, store it in a table, and dispatch via O(1) lookups — extremely predictable, used in safety-critical ECUs and avionics.
- **Event-driven (online) schedulers** make decisions at run time using a priority rule — flexible, handle aperiodic events well, used in general RTOSes.
- **Cyclic Executive** is the canonical table-driven scheme; feasibility requires $C_i \le F$ and $\sum \lceil T_i/F \rceil C_i \le H$.
- **Rate Monotonic (RM)** is the optimal *static-priority* policy: shortest period ⇒ highest priority.
- **RM Sufficient Bound (Liu–Layland):** $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, asymptotically $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- **Exact RTA (Joseph-Pandya):** iterate $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$ until fixed point; check $R_i \le D_i$.
- **EDF** is the *globally optimal* uniprocessor scheduler; feasibility is $U \le 1$ (or processor demand test for $D_i \ne T_i$).
- **Hyper-period** $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, \dots, T_n)$ — the natural period of any periodic real-time system.
- **Modern CPS uses a hybrid:** time-partitioned table-driven outer layer wrapping event-driven inner priorities (AUTOSAR, ARINC-653, TSN).
- For KTU numericals, always **show iterations, show checkpoints, state the final verdict** (schedulable or not).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Schedulers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Schedulers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Scheduler (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *Hybrid Scheduler* in a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) is a real-time scheduling architecture that **combines two or more fundamentally different scheduling paradigms** — typically *time-triggered* (static) and *event-triggered* (dynamic) — into a single unified framework. It is designed to concurrently support both **hard real-time periodic tasks** (which demand deterministic, predictable execution) and **soft real-time / aperiodic tasks** (which demand responsiveness and flexibility), while preserving the timeliness guarantees of the safety-critical workload.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, hybrid schedulers are classified as the **architectural bridge** between classical Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) / Deadline-Monotonic Scheduling (DMS) and event-driven interrupt handling. The combined policy is implemented through a **hierarchical or compositional structure**, where a master (dispatcher) scheduler hands off execution slots to a secondary (server) scheduler, which in turn services a distinct task class.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Airport Control Tower":**
> Imagine a busy international airport. 
> - The **scheduled commercial flights** take off and land according to a **fixed, pre-published timetable** — this is *time-triggered* scheduling (predictable, static, hard real-time). 
> - The **medical evacuation helicopters** and **emergency diversions** are inserted into the airspace the moment an emergency occurs — this is *event-triggered* scheduling (reactive, dynamic, soft real-time). 
> - The **Air Traffic Controller (ATC)** is the *hybrid scheduler* — they respect the published timetable but, when an emergency call arrives, they immediately re-prioritize, hold a non-critical flight on the tarmac, and clear the path for the emergency aircraft. 
> 
> The key insight: the ATC does **not** abandon the timetable. Instead, the timetable is *re-organized* around the emergency. This is exactly what a hybrid scheduler does in a CPS — it preserves a deterministic backbone while injecting responsiveness where needed.

### 1.3 Why Hybrid Schedulers Are Required in CPS

Cyber-Physical Systems (autonomous vehicles, industrial robots, smart grids, medical CPS) inherently run **two coexisting workloads**:

1. **Control-loop tasks** (sensor sampling, PID computation, actuator updates) — periodic, hard-deadline.
2. **Operator commands, fault-handlers, network packets, alarms** — aperiodic, soft-deadline, but latency-sensitive.

A purely static scheduler wastes bandwidth during slack periods; a purely dynamic scheduler cannot guarantee hard deadlines. Hence, **hybrid scheduling** is the canonical CPS design pattern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric — Effective Utilization ($U_{eff}$):**
> The total processor utilization for a hybrid system is the **sum** of the periodic workload utilization ($U_p$) and the bounded server capacity allotted for the aperiodic workload ($U_s$). The scheduler is feasible only if $U_p + U_s \leq 1$.

### 1.4 Core Design Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Budget ($C_s$):** The maximum time a server may spend servicing aperiodic tasks in one replenishment period.
- **Period ($T_s$):** The replenishment interval of the server.
- **Replenishment Rule:** The policy by which the server's budget is refilled.
- **Hard Real-Time Guarantee:** The proof that all periodic deadlines are met under worst-case load.
- **Aperiodic Responsiveness:** The mean response time of non-critical tasks.
- **Compositionality:** The property that subsystems can be analyzed independently and re-combined.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Hybrid Schedulers (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

Hybrid schedulers in the OECPT723 syllabus are organized into **three principal families**:

#### 2.1.1 Hierarchical (Two-Level) Schedulers
- A **master scheduler** (typically RMS or TDMA) at the top level.
- A **secondary scheduler** (e.g., EDF or FIFO) at the bottom level, servicing a single "server" task.
- The master sees the server as a single periodic task with execution time $C_s$ and period $T_s$.
- **Use case:** Industrial CPS where a real-time OS hosts both control loops and a Java/PLC-style application.

#### 2.1.2 Server-Based Schedulers
A *server* is a periodic task that is granted a *budget* to service aperiodic jobs. The three classical servers are:

1. **Polling Server (PS):** Budget is replenished to full at the start of every period. If unused, it is **discarded**.
2. **Deferrable Server (DS):** Budget is replenished at the start of every period, but **preserved** (deferred) if unused — up to the period cap.
3. **Sporadic Server (SS):** Budget is replenished **only when actually consumed**, with a one-period delay, following a precise replenishment rule (Sprunt, Sha, Lehoczky, 1989).

#### 2.1.3 Compositional Schedulers (Resource Models)
- Based on the *Real-Time Calculus* / *Real-Time Interfaces* framework.
- Each CPS subsystem exports an **arrival curve $\alpha(\Delta)$** and requires a **service curve $\beta(\Delta)$**.
- Schedulers are *composed* by bounding their resource consumption mathematically.
- Used in **distributed CPS** (AUTOSAR, RT-Patch, automotive architectures).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — How a Hybrid Scheduler Works

Step-by-step execution flow:

1. **System Initialization:** A *table* of periodic tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_n$ with their periods $T_i$, worst-case execution times $C_i$, and deadlines $D_i$ is loaded into the kernel.
2. **Server Creation:** A *server task* $\tau_s$ is registered with capacity $C_s$ and period $T_s$. The total periodic load is now $\{ \tau_1, \dots, \tau_n, \tau_s \}$.
3. **Feasibility Check:** The Liu & Layland utilization bound (or response-time analysis) is run on the augmented task set to verify $U_{total} \leq 1$.
4. **Dispatching:** At every scheduling point, the master scheduler (RMS / TDMA) selects the **highest-priority ready job**. The aperiodic jobs are queued in the server's *pending queue*.
5. **Aperiodic Arrival:** When an aperiodic job $J_k$ arrives, it does *not* interrupt the kernel. It is **pended** in the server's queue.
6. **Server Dispatch:** If the server task is selected by the master and its budget is positive, the head-of-queue aperiodic job executes.
7. **Budget Accounting:** The consumed execution time is subtracted from the server budget. On exhaustion, the server *suspends itself* until replenishment.
8. **Replenishment:** At the defined replenishment instants, the server budget is refilled according to its specific rule (Polling / Deferrable / Sporadic).

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice

- **Why a server and not direct aperiodic interrupts?** Because unconstrained interrupts violate hard-deadline guarantees of periodic tasks. The server *isolates* the periodic workload.
- **Why three different server types?** Trade-off: *Polling* is simplest but most wasteful; *Deferrable* is more efficient but suffers from *deadline* anomalies; *Sporadic* is provably optimal and behaves identically to a periodic task of equal capacity (i.e., it can be analyzed by classical RMS bound).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_i$ | Worst-case execution time of task $\tau_i$ | — | Time units (ms, $\mu$s) |
| $T_i$ | Period of task $\tau_i$ | — | Time units |
| $D_i$ | Relative deadline of $\tau_i$ | $D_i \leq T_i$ for constrained-deadline | Time units |
| $U_i$ | Utilization of task $\tau_i$ | $U_i = C_i / T_i$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq U_i \leq 1$ |
| $U_{RMS}$ | Liu–Layland bound for RMS | $U_{RMS} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$ |
| $C_s$ | Server capacity / budget | $C_s \leq T_s$ | Time units |
| $T_s$ | Server replenishment period | $T_s$ | Time units |
| $U_s$ | Server utilization | $U_s = C_s / T_s$ | Dimensionless |
| $U_{total}$ | Total system utilization (hybrid) | $U_{total} = \sum U_i + U_s$ | Must be $\leq 1$ |
| $R_i$ | Worst-case response time of $\tau_i$ | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ | Solved by fixed-point iteration |
| $\alpha(\Delta)$ | Arrival curve (Compositional) | Upper bound on arrivals in window $\Delta$ | Events / time |
| $\beta(\Delta)$ | Service curve (Compositional) | Lower bound on service in window $\Delta$ | Time / events |
| $W_{aper}(t)$ | Aperiodic waiting time | Server-dependent; see SS analysis | Time units |
| $N_{aper}$ | Max aperiodic jobs per period (DS) | $N_{aper} = C_s / C_{min,aper}$ | Integer count |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Hybrid schedulers are the *de facto* scheduling strategy in **AUTOSAR-OS (OSEK/VDX)**, **VxWorks**, **RTEMS**, **FreeRTOS+POSIX**, and the **real-time extensions of Linux (PREEMPT_RT, SCHED_DEADLINE)** — making this topic directly relevant to automotive CPS, avionics (DO-178C), and industrial automation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **AUTOSAR / Automotive ECUs:** Multiple ECUs (engine, brake, body) run on a *compositional* hybrid scheduler with mixed-criticality awareness.
- **Smart Grid SCADA:** Periodic metering and event-driven fault detection co-exist.
- **Medical Devices (Pacemaker, Infusion Pump):** Hard RT control loop + soft RT alarm handling.
- **UAV Flight Control:** Periodic attitude control (1 kHz) + event-driven ground-station commands.
- **Industrial IoT Gateways:** Time-triggered TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking) + event-triggered OPC-UA traffic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Liu & Layland Utilization Bound for Hybrid Set

We start with a hybrid task set $\mathcal{H} = \{ \tau_1, \dots, \tau_n, \tau_s \}$ where the server $\tau_s$ is treated as a normal RMS task. The total utilization is:

$$
U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} + \frac{C_s}{T_s}
$$

For a feasible RMS schedule of $N$ tasks (including the server), the **Liu & Layland sufficient (but not necessary) condition** states:

$$
U_{total} \leq N \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1)
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Step 1: Begin from the critical instant theorem. The worst-case response time of $\tau_k$ occurs when it arrives simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks.

Step 2: In the worst case, $\tau_k$ is preempted by every job of every higher-priority task that arrives during its response-time window.

Step 3: The number of jobs of $\tau_j$ (priority higher than $\tau_k$) released in an interval of length $R_k$ is bounded by $\lceil R_k / T_j \rceil$.

Step 4: The interference $I_k$ on $\tau_k$ is therefore:

$$
I_k = \sum_{j \in hp(k)} \left\lceil \frac{R_k}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

Step 5: The response time $R_k$ of $\tau_k$ satisfies the recursive equation (response-time analysis):

$$
R_k = C_k + \sum_{j \in hp(k)} \left\lceil \frac{R_k}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

Step 6: For a sufficient test, the worst-case utilization of the highest-priority task $\tau_1$ is $C_1 / T_1$. For the lowest-priority task $\tau_N$, the worst-case utilization under preemption accumulates proportionally.

Step 7: Using the geometric-series identity $\sum_{k=1}^{N} 1/2^{k} = 1 - 1/2^{N}$, and optimizing the priority assignment (shorter period → higher priority), Liu & Layland obtained:

$$
U_{total} \leq N \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1)
$$

Step 8: The RHS as $N \to \infty$ approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$. Thus, **RMS can guarantee hard deadlines provided $U_{total} \leq 0.693$** (for arbitrary $N$); for small $N$, the bound is tighter.

**Conclusion:** To include a server, simply treat the server as a periodic task and apply the same bound to the augmented set. The remaining capacity $1 - U_{total}$ is the *slack bandwidth* for non-real-time work.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sporadic Server (SS) Replenishment Rule

The Sporadic Server (Sprunt–Sha–Lehoczky, 1989) replenishes its budget **only when the server has actively consumed capacity**, and it does so with a one-period delay to bound the worst-case interference.

**Rule (precise statement):**
Let $C_s$ be the server capacity and $T_s$ its replenishment period. Define:
- $t_a$ = the time instant the server *begins* execution of an aperiodic job.
- $t_f$ = the time instant the server *finishes* that execution.

The rule states:
- The execution time consumed in the interval $[t_a, t_f]$ is **replenished at time $t = t_a + T_s$**.

**Derivation of worst-case interference:**

Step 1: The server can defer replenishment by *not consuming* the budget between two jobs. Therefore, the server's effective replenishment period is always at least $T_s$ after each consumption start.

Step 2: The maximum interference that the server can inflict on a higher-priority periodic task in any interval of length $L$ is bounded by $C_s + \lfloor L / T_s \rfloor \cdot C_s$ (the first $C_s$ from the current period's remainder, plus $C_s$ for each fully elapsed $T_s$ window).

Step 3: This worst-case pattern is **identical to that of a periodic task** with $C = C_s$ and $T = T_s$.

Step 4: Therefore, the SS is *behaviorally equivalent* to a periodic task, and standard RMS analysis (Liu–Layland bound or exact response-time test) applies directly. The system is feasible if:

$$
U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} + \frac{C_s}{T_s} \leq U_{bound}(N+1)
$$

Step 5: This is the **central advantage** of the Sporadic Server over Polling and Deferrable servers — it has the same utilization as an equivalent periodic task, achieving **aperiodic responsiveness without sacrificing hard-RT guarantee**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deferrable Server (DS) Utilization Analysis

The Deferrable Server preserves its budget across the period. The key equation for the maximum number of aperiodic jobs serviced in one $T_s$ window:

$$
N_{max, DS} = \left\lfloor \frac{C_s}{C_{min,aper}} \right\rfloor
$$

where $C_{min,aper}$ is the execution time of the shortest aperiodic job.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: At $t = 0$ the server is replenished with $C_s$. It services jobs as long as the budget permits.

Step 2: In the *worst case*, aperiodic jobs arrive continuously during the entire period, so the server is *never idle*. It services $C_s / C_{min,aper}$ jobs (rounded down to integer).

Step 3: At $t = T_s$ the budget is reset to $C_s$, and the cycle repeats.

Step 4: The **drawback** of DS is that the preserved budget can *delay* the replenishment for the next period — a budget consumed late in the period is not refilled until the *next* replenishment instant, which can cause deadline misses in higher-priority tasks if the next aperiodic burst coincides with their release.

Step 5: Sprunt, Sha, and Lehoczky proved that the DS can demand up to $2 \cdot C_s$ of execution in a single period under a bursty arrival pattern, **doubling** its effective utilization. Hence:

$$
U_{eff, DS} \leq 2 \cdot \frac{C_s}{T_s}
$$

Step 6: The DS is feasible only if the periodic workload satisfies $U_p \leq 1 - 2 \cdot C_s / T_s$, which is a **stricter** condition than for SS.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Server Type | Replenishment Trigger | Worst-Case Effective Utilization | Hard-RT Guarantee | Aperiodic Latency | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Polling Server (PS)** | Period start (always) | $U_s = C_s / T_s$ | Conservative (large $T_s$ wastes budget) | Highest (worst) | Lowest |
| **Deferrable Server (DS)** | Period start (always) | $U_{eff,DS} \leq 2 \cdot U_s$ | Weak (budget carry-over causes anomalies) | Medium | Low |
| **Sporadic Server (SS)** | On actual consumption, delayed by $T_s$ | $U_{eff,SS} = U_s$ (provably optimal) | **Strongest** (equiv. to periodic task) | Lowest | Medium |
| **Slack Stealing** | Inherits slack from periodic tasks | $U_{eff} = U_p + U_{slack}$ | Strong | Very low | High |
| **Compositional** | Resource-reservation model | Bounded by $\alpha/\beta$ curves | Strong + modular | Tunable | High |

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (for KTU Numerical Problems)

**Problem:** A CPS has two periodic hard-RT tasks: $\tau_1$ ($C_1 = 1$ ms, $T_1 = 4$ ms) and $\tau_2$ ($C_2 = 2$ ms, $T_2 = 6$ ms). A Sporadic Server with $C_s = 1$ ms, $T_s = 6$ ms handles aperiodic jobs. Check feasibility.

**Step 1:** Compute periodic utilizations.

$$
u_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{6} \approx 0.333
$$

**Step 2:** Compute server utilization.

$$
u_s = \frac{C_s}{T_s} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167
$$

**Step 3:** Total utilization.

$$
U_{total} = 0.25 + 0.333 + 0.167 = 0.75
$$

**Step 4:** Liu & Layland bound for $N = 3$ tasks.

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 \approx 0.7798
$$

**Step 5:** Compare.

$$
U_{total} = 0.75 \leq U_{bound} = 0.7798
$$

**Conclusion:** The hybrid set is **feasible** under RMS with Sporadic Server. The aperiodic workload can use up to **1 ms of CPU in every 6 ms window** without violating any hard deadline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The Sporadic Server here provides a *deterministic bandwidth reservation* — equivalent to a virtual dedicated CPU slice — guaranteeing that aperiodic alarms and operator commands are serviced within bounded latency while the safety-critical control loops remain unaffected.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hybrid Scheduler in a CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPS_HW["CPS HARDWARE PLATFORM"]
        CPU["Processor Core"]
        SENSOR["Sensor Array"]
        ACTUATOR["Actuator Driver"]
        COMM["Network Interface"]
    end

    subgraph OS_KERNEL["REAL-TIME OS KERNEL"]
        direction TB
        MASTER["Master Scheduler (RMS / TDMA)"]
        SERVER["Hybrid Server (Sporadic / Deferrable)"]
        DISPATCH["Dispatcher / Interrupt Controller"]
    end

    subgraph TASK_LAYERS["TASK LAYER (Two-Level)"]
        direction TB
        subgraph HARD_RT["Hard Real-Time Layer"]
            T1["Control Loop Tau 1 - 1 kHz"]
            T2["Sensor Sampling Tau 2 - 100 Hz"]
            T3["Actuator Update Tau 3 - 500 Hz"]
        end
        subgraph SOFT_RT["Soft Real-Time Layer (Server-Managed)"]
            A1["Aperiodic Alarm Handler"]
            A2["Operator Command Handler"]
            A3["Network Packet Processor"]
            A4["Diagnostic Logger"]
        end
    end

    SENSOR --> T1
    T1 --> ACTUATOR
    T2 --> MASTER
    T3 --> MASTER
    A1 --> SERVER
    A2 --> SERVER
    A3 --> SERVER
    A4 --> SERVER
    COMM --> A3
    MASTER --> DISPATCH
    SERVER --> DISPATCH
    DISPATCH --> CPU
    CPU --> ACTUATOR
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Dispatcher Logic in a Hybrid Scheduler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Scheduling Point Reached"] --> B{"Aperiodic Job in Server Queue?"}
    B -- Yes --> C{"Server Budget > 0?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Dispatch Aperiodic Job"]
    C -- No --> E["Suspend Server and Replenish"]
    B -- No --> F{"Highest Priority Periodic Task Ready?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Dispatch Periodic Task"]
    F -- No --> H["Processor Idle / Run Background"]
    D --> I["Deduct Budget and Resume"]
    G --> I
    E --> I
    H --> I
    I --> A
```

### 4.3 Server Replenishment Comparison (State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> Consuming: Aperiodic Job Arrives
    Consuming --> Active: Budget Remaining > 0
    Consuming --> Suspended: Budget Exhausted
    Suspended --> Replenish_PS: PS - Reset at T_s
    Suspended --> Replenish_DS: DS - Reset at T_s Carry-over
    Suspended --> Replenish_SS: SS - Replenish on Actual Use
    Replenish_PS --> Active
    Replenish_DS --> Active
    Replenish_SS --> Active
    Suspended --> [*]: System Halt
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Scheduling Architecture (Two-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LV1["Level 1 Master Scheduler"]
        M1["RMS Dispatcher"]
    end
    subgraph LV2["Level 2 Secondary Schedulers"]
        S1["Server for Aperiodic Pool A"]
        S2["Server for Aperiodic Pool B"]
        S3["Server for Background Tasks"]
    end
    subgraph POOLS["Task Pools"]
        P1["Pool A: Alarms and Faults"]
        P2["Pool B: HMI Events"]
        P3["Background Diagnostics"]
    end
    M1 --> S1
    M1 --> S2
    M1 --> S3
    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P2
    S3 --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram shows the *physical partitioning* of a CPS into hardware, OS, and tasks. The second and third show the *runtime decision flow* executed at every scheduling tick. The fourth illustrates the *composability* of the architecture — multiple servers can co-exist under one master, a property heavily exploited in **AUTOSAR** multi-core ECUs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define a *Hybrid Scheduler* in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. Why is it preferred over a purely time-triggered or purely event-triggered scheduler?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Hybrid Scheduler is a real-time scheduling architecture that combines two or more scheduling paradigms — typically *time-triggered* (static, deterministic) and *event-triggered* (dynamic, responsive) — into a single framework (1 mark). It is preferred in CPS because CPS workloads are *mixed-criticality* — periodic control loops demand hard deadlines while aperiodic events (alarms, commands) demand low latency (1 mark). A purely time-triggered scheduler leaves bandwidth idle and has poor aperiodic responsiveness, while a purely event-triggered scheduler cannot guarantee hard deadlines under load. A hybrid scheduler *retains the determinism of time-triggered execution for critical tasks* while *allocating a bounded server capacity for aperiodic work*, achieving both predictability and flexibility (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Compare the *Polling Server*, *Deferrable Server*, and *Sporadic Server* in terms of their replenishment rules.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Polling Server (1 mark):** Budget is replenished to full capacity $C_s$ at the *start of every period $T_s$*. Any unused budget is *discarded* at the end of the period. Simple but inefficient.
- **Deferrable Server (1 mark):** Budget is replenished at the *start of every period*, but unlike PS, any *unused budget is preserved* (deferred) for use later in the same period. More efficient than PS, but can cause deadline anomalies for higher-priority tasks due to the carry-over.
- **Sporadic Server (1 mark):** Budget is replenished *only when it is actually consumed*, with a replenishment delay equal to $T_s$ from the start of the consumption. This rule makes the SS provably equivalent in worst-case behavior to a periodic task, yielding the **strongest hard-RT guarantee** of the three.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination) regulations, each Part B question carries **14 marks**, and students are given an **internal choice** (Question A or Question B). Each question typically has two sub-parts worth **7 marks each**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **two-level hierarchical hybrid scheduler** used in CPS. Discuss the role of the master scheduler and the secondary server. (7 marks)

**(b)** A CPS has three hard real-time periodic tasks: $\tau_1(C_1=1, T_1=4)$, $\tau_2(C_2=2, T_2=6)$, $\tau_3(C_3=1, T_3=8)$. A Sporadic Server with $C_s = 1$ ms, $T_s = 8$ ms is added for aperiodic jobs. Using the Liu & Layland bound, determine whether the hybrid set is feasible under RMS. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A two-level hierarchical hybrid scheduler consists of:

1. **Level 1 — Master Scheduler (3 marks):**
   - Operates at the kernel level. Typically uses a static, time-triggered policy like *Rate-Monotonic Scheduling* or *TDMA*.
   - Owns the global ready queue and decides *which task* runs on the processor at every scheduling tick.
   - Treats each *server* as a regular periodic task with attributes $(C_s, T_s)$.
   - Guarantees hard deadlines for periodic workloads.

2. **Level 2 — Secondary Server Scheduler (3 marks):**
   - Services a *separate* class of jobs (aperiodic, soft real-time).
   - Receives a *budget* $C_s$ from the master at every replenishment period $T_s$.
   - Internally uses a simple dispatcher (FIFO, EDF) to multiplex among its queued jobs.
   - Suspends itself when the budget is exhausted; resumes only on replenishment.

3. **Working Principle (1 mark):**
   - Master and server execute *cooperatively* on the same processor. The server *is never* an interrupt; it is a schedulable entity. This isolation is the key safety property of the architecture.

**Block diagram:**

```
+-------------------+         +---------------------+
| Periodic Tasks    | <-----> | Master Scheduler    |
| (Tau1, Tau2, Tau3)|         | (RMS / TDMA)        |
+-------------------+         +----------+----------+
                                          |
                                          v
                                +---------------------+
                                | Server Task (C_s,T_s)|
                                +----------+----------+
                                           |
                                           v
                                +---------------------+
                                | Aperiodic Job Queue |
                                | (FIFO / EDF)        |
                                +---------------------+
```

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute per-task utilizations.

$$
u_1 = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25, \quad u_2 = \frac{2}{6} \approx 0.333, \quad u_3 = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Server utilization.

$$
u_s = \frac{C_s}{T_s} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Total utilization.

$$
U_{total} = 0.25 + 0.333 + 0.125 + 0.125 = 0.833
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply Liu & Layland bound for $N = 4$ tasks.

$$
U_{bound} = 4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1) = 4 \cdot (1.1892 - 1) = 4 \cdot 0.1892 \approx 0.7568
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compare.

$$
U_{total} = 0.833 \;\not\leq\; U_{bound} = 0.7568
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion.
The sufficient bound is *violated*, so RMS **does not guarantee** hard deadlines for this set. However, the Liu & Layland bound is *sufficient but not necessary*. An exact response-time analysis must be performed.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Exact Response-Time Check.
Use $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ iteratively.

- For $\tau_1$ (highest): $R_1 = 1$ ms. Deadline met.
- For $\tau_2$: $R_2 = 2 + \lceil R_2/4 \rceil \cdot 1$. Iterating: start $R_2 = 3$, $\lceil 3/4 \rceil = 1$, so $R_2 = 2 + 1 = 3$. Converges at **3 ms** $\leq 6$ ms. ✓
- For $\tau_3$: $R_3 = 1 + \lceil R_3/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil R_3/6 \rceil \cdot 2$. Start $R_3 = 1+1+1 = 3$. Iterate: $R_3 = 1+1+2 = 4$ (since $\lceil 4/6 \rceil = 1$). Re-iterate: $\lceil 4/4 \rceil = 1$, $\lceil 4/6 \rceil = 1$, so $R_3 = 1+1+2 = 4$. Converges at **4 ms** $\leq 8$ ms. ✓
- For the server $\tau_s$ (lowest priority): $R_s = 1 + \lceil R_s/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil R_s/6 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil R_s/8 \rceil \cdot 1$. Start $R_s = 1+1+1+1 = 4$. Iterate: $\lceil 4/4 \rceil = 1$, $\lceil 4/6 \rceil = 1$, $\lceil 4/8 \rceil = 1$, so $R_s = 4$. Re-iterate: same. Converges at **4 ms** $\leq 8$ ms. ✓

**Final conclusion:** All tasks meet their deadlines. The hybrid set is **feasible**, even though the Liu & Layland sufficient bound failed. This illustrates the classical KTU examination trap — examiners expect you to *try the bound first*, then proceed to the exact response-time analysis.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the **Sporadic Server** algorithm in detail. State the precise replenishment rule and prove that its worst-case interference on a higher-priority periodic task is bounded by $C_s + \lfloor L / T_s \rfloor \cdot C_s$ in an interval of length $L$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A real-time system uses a **Deferrable Server** with $C_s = 2$ ms and $T_s = 10$ ms to handle aperiodic interrupts. The minimum aperiodic job execution time is $C_{min,aper} = 0.5$ ms. Compute (i) the maximum number of aperiodic jobs serviced in one server period, and (ii) the effective worst-case utilization of the Deferrable Server. Comment on the implications for hard-RT guarantee. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Sporadic Server is a periodic server whose budget is replenished *only upon actual consumption*, with a replenishment delay of $T_s$ from the *start* of each consumption interval. It was introduced by Sprunt, Sha, and Lehoczky (1989) to provide high aperiodic responsiveness while maintaining hard-RT guarantees.

**Replenishment Rule (2 marks):**
Let $t_a$ be the start time of a contiguous consumption interval of the server, and let $C_{used}$ be the total time consumed in that interval. Then the budget is replenished by an amount $C_{used}$ at time $t_a + T_s$. If multiple consumption intervals begin within the same $T_s$ window, each is replenished at its own $t_a + T_s$ instant.

**Proof of Worst-Case Interference (4 marks):**

Consider an interval $[t_0, t_0 + L]$ and a higher-priority task $\tau_h$. The server can preempt $\tau_h$ only at the start of its own execution slots.

- In the first $T_s$ window after the start of the analysis, the server can consume at most $C_s$ (its initial budget).
- After that, every $T_s$ window, at most one replenishment of $C_s$ can occur (because replenishment instants are spaced at least $T_s$ apart).
- The number of full $T_s$ windows that fit in the interval of length $L$ is $\lfloor L / T_s \rfloor$.
- A partial window at the end can contribute an additional $C_s$ if the server had budget at the start of that partial window.

Therefore, the maximum server execution in any window of length $L$ is:

$$
I_{max}(L) = C_s + \left\lfloor \frac{L}{T_s} \right\rfloor \cdot C_s
$$

This is **identical** to the worst-case interference of a periodic task with $C = C_s$ and $T = T_s$. Hence the SS can be analyzed with the same tools as a periodic task — a property unique among all servers.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Maximum aperiodic jobs per period (3 marks):**

Given $C_s = 2$ ms and $C_{min,aper} = 0.5$ ms:

$$
N_{max, DS} = \left\lfloor \frac{C_s}{C_{min,aper}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2}{0.5} \right\rfloor = 4 \text{ jobs}
$$

**(ii) Effective worst-case utilization (2 marks):**

For the Deferrable Server, the worst-case effective utilization can be **up to twice** the nominal utilization:

$$
U_{nominal} = \frac{C_s}{T_s} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20
$$

$$
U_{eff, max} = 2 \cdot U_{nominal} = 0.40
$$

**Implications for hard-RT guarantee (2 marks):**

Because the Deferrable Server can demand up to **40% of the processor** in the worst case (under bursty aperiodic arrivals), the periodic workload must satisfy:

$$
U_p \leq 1 - U_{eff, max} = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60
$$

This is a **stricter** requirement than for the Sporadic Server (which would only require $U_p \leq 0.80$). The DS therefore **trades off aperiodic responsiveness for weaker hard-RT guarantee**. For safety-critical CPS, the SS or a similar composable mechanism is preferred.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Hybrid Scheduler Questions (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Treating the server as an interrupt (–2 marks):** Many students incorrectly say the aperiodic job "interrupts" the kernel. *A server is a schedulable task*, not an ISR. It does not preempt the periodic schedule; it must be *dispatched* by the master scheduler.
> 2. **Confusing the three server types (–2 marks):** The Polling, Deferrable, and Sporadic servers differ only in their *replenishment policy*. Examiners will specifically test whether you know that PS discards, DS defers, and SS replenishes on use.
> 3. **Forgetting to include the server in the Liu & Layland bound (–3 marks):** When adding a server, you must add $U_s$ to $U_{total}$ and increase the task count $N$ by 1 in the bound. Students who apply the bound only to the periodic tasks lose full marks.
> 4. **Using $\vert$ inside the formula table (–1 mark):** Always write absolute value as $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid LaTeX parsing errors. KTU digital answer sheets are strict about this.
> 5. **Skipping the response-time iterative step (–2 marks):** If the Liu & Layland sufficient bound fails, you *must* attempt the exact response-time analysis using the recursive $R_i$ equation. Skipping this is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 6. **Forgetting the one-period replenishment delay in SS (–1 mark):** The SS replenishment happens at $t_a + T_s$, not at $t_f + T_s$. Examiners specifically test this subtle distinction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hybrid Schedulers (Module 2):**

- **Definition:** A hybrid scheduler combines time-triggered and event-triggered paradigms to handle mixed-criticality CPS workloads.
- **Three principal families:** (1) Hierarchical (two-level), (2) Server-based (PS/DS/SS), (3) Compositional (Real-Time Calculus).
- **Server = periodic task:** In the master scheduler's view, a server is a regular task with $(C_s, T_s)$. This is the central abstraction.
- **Polling Server (PS):** Replenish at period start; *discard* unused budget. Simplest, worst aperiodic latency.
- **Deferrable Server (DS):** Replenish at period start; *preserve* unused budget. Better latency, but $U_{eff} \leq 2 \cdot C_s / T_s$ worst case.
- **Sporadic Server (SS):** Replenish *on consumption* at $t_a + T_s$. Equivalent to a periodic task — provably optimal, preferred in safety-critical CPS.
- **Liu & Layland bound:** $U_{total} \leq N \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1)$ — apply to the *augmented* set (periodic + server). Sufficient but not necessary.
- **Exact Response-Time Analysis:** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ — solved by fixed-point iteration. Use when Liu & Layland bound fails.
- **Real-World Use:** AUTOSAR-OS, VxWorks, RTEMS, FreeRTOS+POSIX, Linux SCHED_DEADLINE — all rely on hybrid scheduling.
