- **CPS Examples:** Automotive ECU, UAV flight control, smart grid SCADA, medical infusion pumps, industrial IoT gateways.
- **Key Trade-off:** Higher aperiodic responsiveness ↔ weaker hard-RT guarantee. Choose SS for safety-critical, DS for responsive-but-tolerable, PS for ultra-low overhead.
- **Avoid the word "interrupt"** when describing a server — it is a *schedulable entity*, not an interrupt.
- **Always state the replenishment rule** explicitly when answering server-related questions. KTU examiners award the 7-mark sub-question only when the rule is precise.
- **Compute the budget and period separately** in numerical problems; do not lump them into one symbol.
- **Check both Liu & Layland bound AND exact response-time** — KTU frequently gives a bound-violation case that is still feasible.

---

**End of Notes — Module 2: Hybrid Schedulers**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme OECPT723 — Fundamentals of Cyber Physical Systems*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamical Systems and Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamical Systems and Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Dynamical System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Dynamical System** is a mathematical framework used to describe the time-evolution of a point (or a set of points) in a geometric space, governed by a fixed rule of motion. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, it represents the continuous or discrete evolution of physical state variables (such as position, velocity, temperature, pressure) as functions of time, typically expressed through **Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs)** or **Difference Equations**.

Formally, a continuous-time dynamical system is represented as:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t), t)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **state vector** (n-dimensional state space)
- $\mathbf{u}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the **input/control vector**
- $\mathbf{f}$ is a (generally nonlinear) vector field
- $t$ is the independent time variable

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Car on a Hill**
> Imagine a car parked on a hilly road. The **state** of the car is its exact position and velocity. The **dynamical system** is the set of physics rules (gravity, friction, engine force) that determine how the state evolves moment-by-moment.
> - If the car is at the **bottom of a valley**, and you give it a tiny push, it rolls back to the valley floor — this is a **stable equilibrium**.
> - If the car is balanced perfectly on the **top of a hill**, even a tiny push will cause it to roll down permanently — this is an **unstable equilibrium**.
> - If the car is on a **flat plain** and you give it a push, it keeps moving (or maintains its new state) — this is **neutral stability**.

In CPS, examples include:
- A drone's flight dynamics (state = position, velocity, orientation)
- A smart grid's power flow dynamics
- An autonomous vehicle's motion control
- An industrial robotic arm's joint dynamics

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Essentials)

| Term | Standard Symbol | Physical Meaning in CPS |
|------|----------------|--------------------------|
| State | $\mathbf{x}$ | Snapshot of all relevant physical variables |
| State Space | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ | The mathematical space where the state vector lives |
| Equilibrium Point | $\mathbf{x}^{*}$ | State where $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^{*}) = 0$ (no change) |
| Trajectory | $\mathbf{x}(t)$ | The path traced by the state through time |
| Stability | — | The ability to return to / stay near equilibrium |
| Control Input | $\mathbf{u}$ | Cyber-layer commands that drive the physical system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability is the MOST critical property in CPS design.** An unstable CPS (e.g., an unstable drone, a swinging bridge, a runaway nuclear reaction) is a system waiting to fail. KTU examiners LOVE testing stability — expect at least one full 14-mark question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Dynamical Systems

**A. Based on Time Domain:**

**(i) Continuous-Time Dynamical Systems:**
The state evolves smoothly with time, described by differential equations.

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t), t)$$

*Example:* $\ddot{y} + 2\dot{y} + 5y = 0$ — a mass-spring-damper.

**(ii) Discrete-Time Dynamical Systems:**
The state updates at discrete time steps, described by difference equations.

$$\mathbf{x}[k+1] = \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}[k], \mathbf{u}[k])$$

*Example:* A digital temperature controller sampling every 100 ms.

**B. Based on Linearity:**

**(i) Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Systems:**
$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t)$$

where $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **system matrix** and $\mathbf{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ is the **input matrix**.

**(ii) Nonlinear Systems:**
$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t)) \text{ where } \mathbf{f} \text{ is nonlinear}$$

### 2.2 Stability — The Core Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition of Stability (Lyapunov Sense):** An equilibrium point $\mathbf{x}^{*}$ is **stable** if for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that if $\Vert \mathbf{x}(0) - \mathbf{x}^{*} \Vert < \delta$, then $\Vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{x}^{*} \Vert < \epsilon$ for all $t \geq 0$.

In plain English: *"If you start close to the equilibrium, you stay close to it forever."*

### 2.3 Types of Stability (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Stability Type | Formal Condition | Physical Interpretation |
|----------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Stable (Lyapunov)** | Trajectories stay bounded near $\mathbf{x}^{*}$ | No escape from the neighborhood |
| **Asymptotically Stable** | Stable + $\lim_{t \to \infty} \mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{x}^{*}$ | Returns to equilibrium |
| **Exponentially Stable** | $\Vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{x}^{*} \Vert \leq K e^{-\alpha t} \Vert \mathbf{x}(0) - \mathbf{x}^{*} \Vert$ | Returns with a guaranteed decay rate |
| **Unstable** | $\exists$ trajectory that escapes any neighborhood | Even small disturbances grow |
| **Marginally Stable** | Bounded but does not converge | Sustained oscillations (e.g., undamped pendulum) |
| **BIBO Stable** | Bounded Input → Bounded Output | System does not blow up |

### 2.4 Lyapunov Stability Theory (The Crown Jewel)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lyapunov's Direct (Second) Method** is the single most important tool for KTU CPS stability analysis. It generalizes the "energy" intuition: *if the total energy of a system continuously decreases, the system must settle into its lowest energy state (equilibrium).*

**Lyapunov's Theorem:**
Let $\mathbf{x}^{*} = 0$ be an equilibrium point of $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$. Suppose there exists a continuously differentiable scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ (a **Lyapunov candidate function**) such that:
1. $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq 0$ (**positive definite**)
2. $V(0) = 0$
3. $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{dV}{dt} = \nabla V \cdot \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ (**negative semi-definite**)

Then the origin is **stable**. If $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ (strictly), then it is **asymptotically stable**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE THIS FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Used For |
|---|---------|---------------------|----------|
| 1 | LTI State Equation | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$ | General LTI modelling |
| 2 | Output Equation | $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}$ | Sensor output mapping |
| 3 | Solution (homogeneous) | $\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{\mathbf{A}t}\mathbf{x}(0)$ | Zero-input response |
| 4 | Asymptotic Stability (LTI) | All eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ have $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ | Checking stability without solving ODE |
| 5 | Characteristic Polynomial | $p(\lambda) = \det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = 0$ | Finding eigenvalues |
| 6 | Lyapunov Equation (LTI) | $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{Q}$ | Proving stability algebraically |
| 7 | Lyapunov function form (LTI) | $V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$, with $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P}^{T} > 0$ | Standard quadratic candidate |
| 8 | Equilibrium Condition | $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^{*}) = 0$ | Finding fixed points |
| 9 | BIBO Stability | Impulse response is absolutely integrable: $\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ | Input-output stability |
| 10 | Routh-Hurwitz (2nd order) | $a_0, a_1, a_2 > 0$ for $a_2 \lambda^2 + a_1 \lambda + a_0 = 0$ | Quick 2nd-order check |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Verifying that a flight controller does not enter divergent oscillations
- **Automotive:** Ensuring electronic stability control (ESC) prevents skidding
- **Power Systems:** Confirming grid frequency stability under load disturbances
- **Robotics:** Validating that robotic arm controllers converge to target positions
- **Medical CPS (e.g., insulin pumps):** Critical — instability can be life-threatening
- **IoT & Smart Buildings:** Maintaining HVAC temperature at setpoint without oscillation
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stability of LTI System from Eigenvalues

**Problem Statement:** Given an LTI system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$, prove that the equilibrium $\mathbf{x}^{*} = 0$ is **asymptotically stable** if and only if all eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ have strictly negative real parts.

---

**Step 1: Decompose the system into modal coordinates.**

The matrix $\mathbf{A}$ can be diagonalized (assuming distinct eigenvalues) using a modal matrix $\mathbf{T}$:

$$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{T} \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{T}^{-1}, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n)$$

**Explanation:** The diagonal $\mathbf{\Lambda}$ contains the eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of $\mathbf{A}$. This is a change of basis that decouples the system into $n$ independent first-order scalar equations.

---

**Step 2: Transform to modal coordinates.**

Let $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{T}^{-1}\mathbf{x}$. Differentiating:

$$\dot{\mathbf{z}} = \mathbf{T}^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{T}^{-1} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{T}^{-1} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{T} \mathbf{z} = \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{z}$$

**Explanation:** The transformed system becomes $n$ decoupled scalar ODEs of the form $\dot{z}_i = \lambda_i z_i$.

---

**Step 3: Solve each scalar ODE.**

Each equation $\dot{z}_i = \lambda_i z_i$ is a simple first-order linear ODE. Its solution is:

$$z_i(t) = e^{\lambda_i t} z_i(0)$$

**Explanation:** This is a direct application of separation of variables. For complex eigenvalues, $\lambda_i = \sigma_i + j\omega_i$, giving $e^{\sigma_i t}(\cos\omega_i t + j\sin\omega_i t)$.

---

**Step 4: Reconstruct the original state.**

Transforming back to $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{T} \mathbf{z}(t) = \mathbf{T} \, \text{diag}(e^{\lambda_1 t}, \ldots, e^{\lambda_n t}) \, \mathbf{z}(0)$$

**Explanation:** Each mode of the system evolves as an exponential $e^{\lambda_i t}$.

---

**Step 5: Apply the stability condition.**

For $\mathbf{x}(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ (asymptotic stability), we need each $e^{\lambda_i t} \to 0$.

Since $e^{\lambda_i t} = e^{\text{Re}(\lambda_i) t} \cdot e^{j \, \text{Im}(\lambda_i) t}$, the magnitude is:

$$\vert e^{\lambda_i t} \vert = e^{\text{Re}(\lambda_i) t}$$

This tends to 0 if and only if:

$$\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0 \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Conclusion:** The equilibrium $\mathbf{x}^{*} = 0$ is **asymptotically stable** iff all eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ lie in the **open left half of the complex plane (LHP)**.

$$\boxed{\text{System } \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} \text{ is asymptotically stable} \iff \text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0 \ \forall i}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Lyapunov Equation for LTI Systems

**Problem Statement:** For the LTI system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$, show that if a symmetric positive definite matrix $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P}^{T} > 0$ satisfies the equation $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{Q}$ for some positive definite $\mathbf{Q}$, then the origin is asymptotically stable.

---

**Step 1: Choose the Lyapunov candidate.**

$$V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x}$$

**Explanation:** Since $\mathbf{P} > 0$, this is a positive definite scalar function (quadratic form) and equals zero only at $\mathbf{x} = 0$.

---

**Step 2: Compute the time derivative along system trajectories.**

$$\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = \dot{\mathbf{x}}^{T} \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{P} \dot{\mathbf{x}}$$

Substitute $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$:

$$\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = (\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x})^{T} \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{P} (\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x})$$

**Explanation:** Using the property $(\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x})^{T} = \mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{M}^{T}$, we get the two-term structure.

---

**Step 3: Simplify using transpose properties.**

$$\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{A}^{T} \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{P} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}^{T} (\mathbf{A}^{T} \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P} \mathbf{A}) \mathbf{x}$$

**Explanation:** Both terms are scalars (1×1) and can be combined under a common $\mathbf{x}^{T} \ldots \mathbf{x}$ since $\mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{M} \mathbf{x}$ is a scalar for any square $\mathbf{M}$.

---

**Step 4: Apply the given Lyapunov equation.**

Substitute $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{Q}$:

$$\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T} (-\mathbf{Q}) \mathbf{x} = -\mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x}$$

**Explanation:** This is the crucial algebraic step that ties the candidate's decay to the stability matrix $\mathbf{A}$.

---

**Step 5: Conclude the sign of $\dot{V}$.**

Since $\mathbf{Q} > 0$ (positive definite) and $\mathbf{x} \neq 0$:

$$\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = -\mathbf{x}^{T} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} < 0$$

**Explanation:** $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq 0$ when $\mathbf{Q}$ is positive definite. Hence the negative sign makes $\dot{V}$ strictly negative.

---

**Step 6: Apply Lyapunov's Theorem.**

We have shown:
- $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq 0$ ✓
- $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq 0$ ✓

By Lyapunov's Direct Method, the origin is **asymptotically stable**.

$$\boxed{\exists \, \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P}^{T} > 0 \text{ s.t. } \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{Q} < 0 \implies \text{Asymptotic Stability}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Standard 14-Mark Style)

**Example:** Consider the dynamical system:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 1: Find the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$.**

$$\det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda + 3 & -1 \\ 0 & \lambda + 2 \end{bmatrix} = (\lambda + 3)(\lambda + 2) = 0$$

$$\therefore \lambda_1 = -3, \quad \lambda_2 = -2$$

**Step 2: Check stability condition.**

Both eigenvalues are real and negative ($\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$).

**Step 3: Conclude.**

$$\boxed{\text{The system is asymptotically stable.}}$$

**Step 4 (Optional): Find a Lyapunov function.** Choose $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{I}$. Solve $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{I}$ for $\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix}$. This gives $V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$, which can be used to bound the decay rate.

### 3.4 Routh-Hurwitz Quick Test (2nd & 3rd Order)

For characteristic polynomial $a_n \lambda^n + a_{n-1} \lambda^{n-1} + \ldots + a_0 = 0$:

- **Necessary:** All $a_i > 0$
- **2nd order ($a_2\lambda^2 + a_1\lambda + a_0$):** Also Sufficient → $a_0, a_1, a_2 > 0$
- **3rd order ($a_3\lambda^3 + a_2\lambda^2 + a_1\lambda + a_0$):** Need $a_2 a_1 > a_3 a_0$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Stability Analysis Workflow in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPS Physical Plant"] --> B["Mathematical Model"]
    B --> C["State Space Form dot x = Ax + Bu"]
    C --> D{"System Type?"}
    D -->|Linear| E["Eigenvalue Analysis Re lambda less than 0"]
    D -->|Nonlinear| F["Lyapunov Function Design V x positive definite"]
    E --> G{"All Eigenvalues in LHP?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["Asymptotically Stable System GREEN"]
    G -->|No| I["Unstable System RED - Redesign Required"]
    F --> J{"dot V negative definite?"}
    J -->|Yes| H
    J -->|No| K["Try Different V x or Check Assumptions"]
    K --> F
    H --> L["Deploy to Physical CPS Plant"]
    I --> M["Modify Controller Gains K"]
    M --> B
    L --> N["Continuous Monitoring and Recertification"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop CPS with Stability Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reference Input r t"] --> B["Controller K Cyber Layer"]
    B --> C["Actuator"]
    C --> D["Physical Plant G s"]
    D --> E["Output y t Sensor Reading"]
    E --> F["Feedback Comparator"]
    A --> F
    F --> B
    D --> G["Disturbance d t"]
    G -.-> D
    E --> H["Stability Monitor Re lambda A BK less than 0"]
    H -->|Unstable Detected| I["Emergency Shutdown or Re-tune K"]
    I --> B
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Flow: Equilibrium Point Stability Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Equilibrium Point x*"] --> B{"Trajectory Behavior"}
    B -->|Returns to x*| C["Asymptotically Stable"]
    B -->|Stays Near x*| D["Lyapunov Stable"]
    B -->|Bounded Oscillation| E["Marginally Stable"]
    B -->|Diverges Away| F["Unstable"]
    C --> G["Decay Rate alpha greater than 0 Exponential Stability"]
    D --> H["No Decay Sustained Neighborhood"]
    E --> I["Pure Imaginary Eigenvalues j omega"]
    F --> J["At least one eigenvalue Re lambda greater than 0"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Verification

All node IDs in the diagrams above are purely alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `C`, etc.), and node labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text with mathematical symbols — no bold/italic/HTML tags — to ensure successful Mermaid v10+ compilation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define a **dynamical system**. What is the difference between continuous-time and discrete-time dynamical systems in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A dynamical system is a mathematical model describing how the state of a CPS evolves over time according to a fixed rule. In **continuous-time**, the state evolves continuously as $\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t), t)$, typical of physical processes. In **discrete-time**, the state updates at sampling instants as $\mathbf{x}[k+1] = \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}[k], \mathbf{u}[k])$, typical of digital controllers in CPS. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark each difference = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State Lyapunov's stability theorem. What is a Lyapunov function?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Lyapunov's theorem states that if there exists a positive definite scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ whose time derivative $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x})$ along the system trajectories is negative definite, then the equilibrium point is asymptotically stable. A **Lyapunov function** is a scalar energy-like function $V(\mathbf{x})$ that is positive definite and decreasing along system trajectories. *(2 marks theorem + 1 mark function definition = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** For the linear dynamical system given by:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -8 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}(t)$$

Find the equilibrium points and determine the stability of the system using the eigenvalue method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Lyapunov's Direct Method** for stability analysis. Apply it to verify the stability of the system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}$ using $V(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Find equilibrium points.** [1 mark]
At equilibrium, $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = 0$, so $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}^{*} = 0$. Since $\det(\mathbf{A}) = (0)(-6) - (1)(-8) = 8 \neq 0$, the only equilibrium is $\mathbf{x}^{*} = 0$.

**Step 2: Find the characteristic equation.** [1 mark]

$$\det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 8 & \lambda + 6 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda + 6) + 8 = \lambda^2 + 6\lambda + 8 = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve for eigenvalues.** [2 marks]

$$(\lambda + 2)(\lambda + 4) = 0 \implies \lambda_1 = -2, \quad \lambda_2 = -4$$

**Step 4: Apply stability criterion.** [2 marks]
Both eigenvalues are real and negative, i.e., $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for $i = 1, 2$.

**Step 5: Conclusion.** [1 mark]
The system is **asymptotically stable** (in fact, exponentially stable since eigenvalues are real and negative).

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: State Lyapunov's Direct Method.** [3 marks]
Lyapunov's Direct Method states: For a system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ with equilibrium at origin, if there exists a scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ such that:
- $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq 0$ (positive definite)
- $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq 0$ (negative definite)

then the origin is asymptotically stable. The method does not require solving the differential equations — it works by examining the "energy" of the system.

**Step 2: Choose the candidate function.** [1 mark]
$V(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$. Clearly $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq 0$. ✓

**Step 3: Compute $\dot{V}$.** [2 marks]
$\dot{V} = 2x_1 \dot{x}_1 + 2x_2 \dot{x}_2$. From the system: $\dot{x}_1 = -x_1$, $\dot{x}_2 = x_1 - 2x_2$.
Thus: $\dot{V} = 2x_1(-x_1) + 2x_2(x_1 - 2x_2) = -2x_1^2 + 2x_1 x_2 - 4x_2^2 = -(2x_1^2 - 2x_1 x_2 + 4x_2^2)$.

**Step 4: Verify negative definiteness.** [1 mark]
Rewrite: $\dot{V} = -(x_1^2 + (x_1 - x_2)^2 + 3x_2^2) < 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq 0$. ✓

**Conclusion:** The origin is **asymptotically stable**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What are the different types of stability? Explain the significance of BIBO stability in CPS applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the nonlinear system $\dot{x} = -x^3$, analyze the stability at the origin using the Lyapunov function $V(x) = \frac{1}{2} x^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

[1 mark per type × 5 = 5 marks + 2 marks CPS significance]

| Stability Type | Description |
|----------------|-------------|
| **Lyapunov Stable** | Trajectories remain bounded near equilibrium |
| **Asymptotically Stable** | Trajectories return to equilibrium |
| **Exponentially Stable** | Decay bounded by $K e^{-\alpha t}$ |
| **Marginally Stable** | Bounded but non-converging oscillations |
| **Unstable** | Trajectories diverge from equilibrium |

**BIBO Stability in CPS:** A CPS is BIBO stable if every bounded input produces a bounded output. This is critical because sensor noise, actuator disturbances, and cyber-layer commands are all bounded signals. If the CPS is not BIBO stable, even a small measurement glitch can cause unbounded actuator commands — leading to physical damage or safety hazards (e.g., runaway motor, overheating battery). Hence BIBO stability is the **input-output safety guarantee** in CPS design. *(2 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Identify equilibrium.** [1 mark]
Set $\dot{x} = 0 \implies -x^3 = 0 \implies x^{*} = 0$.

**Step 2: Choose Lyapunov candidate.** [1 mark]
$V(x) = \frac{1}{2} x^2$. This satisfies $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for $x \neq 0$. ✓

**Step 3: Compute $\dot{V}$.** [2 marks]
$\dot{V} = x \dot{x} = x \cdot (-x^3) = -x^4$

**Step 4: Check sign of $\dot{V}$.** [2 marks]
For all $x \neq 0$, $x^4 > 0$, hence $\dot{V} = -x^4 < 0$. ✓

**Step 5: Conclude.** [1 mark]
By Lyapunov's theorem, the origin is **asymptotically stable** (in fact, globally asymptotically stable since $V$ is radially unbounded).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES — AVOID THESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "Strictly Negative" Condition:** Many students write $\dot{V} \leq 0$ and conclude asymptotic stability. **WRONG.** $\dot{V} \leq 0$ only proves **Lyapunov stability**, not asymptotic stability. You need $\dot{V} < 0$ for asymptotic stability.
>
> 2. **Confusing BIBO and Lyapunov Stability:** They are NOT the same. BIBO is input-output (external) stability; Lyapunov is state-based (internal) stability. For LTI systems they are equivalent, but for nonlinear systems they differ.
>
> 3. **Eigenvalue Sign Errors:** Remember: **negative real part = stable** (LHP), **positive real part = unstable** (RHP), **exactly on imaginary axis = marginally stable** (need to check carefully — system is NOT asymptotically stable).
>
> 4. **Missing the Equilibrium Check:** Always state the equilibrium point explicitly (usually $\mathbf{x}^{*} = 0$) before discussing its stability. Examiners deduct 1 mark if omitted.
>
> 5. **Lyapunov Equation Mistakes:** When solving $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{Q}$, remember $\mathbf{P}$ must be **symmetric** ($\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P}^{T}$) — this gives the correct number of independent equations.
>
> 6. **Not Using $\Vert$ or $\mid$ in Tables:** In your answer sheet, if you write absolute values using `|` inside a table or equation that the examiner is parsing line-by-line, it can confuse the formatting. Stick to $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert$ notation for norms.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — DYNAMICAL SYSTEMS AND STABILITY**

- ☐ A **dynamical system** is described by $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}, t)$ (continuous) or $\mathbf{x}[k+1] = \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}[k], \mathbf{u}[k])$ (discrete).
- ☐ **LTI system** has the form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}$.
- ☐ **Equilibrium point** $\mathbf{x}^{*}$ satisfies $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^{*}) = 0$.
- ☐ **Asymptotic stability (LTI)**: All eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ must have **strictly negative real parts** (LHP).
- ☐ **Lyapunov candidate** $V(\mathbf{x})$ must be **positive definite**: $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$, $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq 0$.
- ☐ **Asymptotic stability via Lyapunov**: Need $V > 0$ AND $\dot{V} < 0$ (both strict).
- ☐ **Lyapunov Equation (LTI)**: $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{Q}$, with $\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q} > 0$.
- ☐ **BIBO stability**: Bounded input implies bounded output; equivalent to $\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$.
- ☐ **Routh-Hurwitz (2nd order)**: $a_0, a_1, a_2 > 0$ is necessary and sufficient.
- ☐ **Marginally stable** = pure imaginary eigenvalues; **NOT** asymptotically stable.
- ☐ **CPS context**: Stability guarantees the physical layer will not diverge under cyber commands — essential for safety-critical systems (drones, medical devices, autonomous vehicles, smart grids).
- ☐ Always **state the equilibrium first**, then analyze stability — KTU examiners look for this structure.
- ☐ Practice both **eigenvalue method** (for LTI) and **Lyapunov method** (general) — questions alternate between them.
- ☐ Memorize the formula sheet in Section 2.5 — it covers 90% of numerical problems.

---

*End of Module 3 Notes — Dynamical Systems and Stability*
*Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and OBE-based assessment guidelines.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controller Design Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Controller Design Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Controller Design Techniques** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) refer to the systematic mathematical and engineering methodologies employed to synthesize feedback control laws that govern the dynamic behavior of a physical plant through its cyber (computational) interface, ensuring desired performance specifications such as **stability**, **transient response**, **steady-state accuracy**, and **robustness** against disturbances and parameter variations.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, controller design is the process of determining a control law $u(t) = f(x(t), r(t))$ such that the closed-loop system satisfies prescribed time-domain and frequency-domain criteria. The design problem is formulated by combining a **plant model** (often a transfer function $G(s)$ or a state-space representation $\{A, B, C, D\}$) with a **controller** $C(s)$ in a feedback configuration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Cruise Control System:**
> Imagine driving a car on a highway. You set a desired speed of **80 km/h**. The car (the **plant**) has dynamics influenced by slope, wind, and road friction. Your brain acts as the **controller**: it observes the difference between the desired speed and the actual speed (the **error signal** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$) and adjusts the accelerator pedal (the **control input** $u(t)$). 
>
> - If the speed drops below 80 km/h on an incline, you press the accelerator harder (**Proportional action**).
> - If the speed remains low for a while, you press even harder to eliminate persistent error (**Integral action**).
> - If the speed suddenly overshoots, you ease off quickly (**Derivative action**).
>
> This mental model of "look, think, and act" is exactly what controller design formalizes mathematically. A well-designed controller ensures the car's speed tracks 80 km/h smoothly, safely, and efficiently — analogous to a CPS regulating a physical process.

## 1.3 Classification of Controller Design Techniques

The major families of controller design techniques covered under the KTU 2024 CPS syllabus for this module are:

1. **Classical Control Techniques**
   - Proportional (P), Proportional-Integral (PI), Proportional-Derivative (PD), Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) control.
   - Root Locus-based design.
   - Frequency-response methods (Bode plot, Nyquist criterion, Lead-Lag compensation).

2. **Modern Control Techniques**
   - State-Space Pole Placement (Ackermann's formula).
   - Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR).
   - Observer-based design (Luenberger Observer, Kalman Filter).

3. **Robust and Adaptive Techniques (Advanced)**
   - $H_\infty$ control.
   - Sliding Mode Control (SMC).
   - Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Controller Design:**
> - **Damping ratio:** $\zeta$ (dimensionless, typically $0 < \zeta < 1$ for underdamped response).
> - **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n$ (in **rad/s**).
> - **Settling time (2% criterion):** $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ (**seconds**).
> - **Peak overshoot:** $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$ (in **%**).
> - **Steady-state error:** $e_{ss}$ (dimensionless or in same units as reference).
> - **Gain Margin (GM):** measured in **dB**.
> - **Phase Margin (PM):** measured in **degrees**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PID Controller Design — The Workhorse of Industry

The **PID controller** is the most widely deployed controller in industrial CPS, accounting for over **95%** of all practical control loops. Its continuous-time control law is:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

The transfer function of a PID controller is:

$$C_{PID}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Proportional term ($K_p e(t)$):** Produces a control action proportional to the current error. Increases **speed of response** but causes steady-state error if used alone. Excessive $K_p$ leads to **oscillations and instability**.

2. **Integral term ($K_i \int e(\tau) d\tau$):** Accumulates past errors to eliminate **steady-state error** ($e_{ss} \to 0$). However, integral action causes **integrator windup** during actuator saturation.

3. **Derivative term ($K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$):** Acts on the rate of change of error, providing **anticipatory/damping action**. Improves transient response and reduces overshoot but amplifies high-frequency noise.

### Ziegler-Nichols Tuning Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate Gain Method** (commonly tested in KTU examinations):
> 1. Set $K_i = K_d = 0$. Increase $K_p$ until the system exhibits **sustained oscillations**. Record this critical gain as $K_u$ and the oscillation period as $T_u$.
> 2. Compute controller gains from the table below.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Controller / Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| PID Transfer Function | $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | Sum of three control actions (transfer function form) |
| Closed-Loop Transfer Function | $T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$ | Standard unity-feedback closed loop |
| Characteristic Equation | $1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$ | Determines closed-loop poles and stability |
| 2nd Order Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \frac{-\ln(M_p/100)}{\sqrt{\pi^2 + \ln^2(M_p/100)}}$ | Relates overshoot to damping (dimensionless) |
| Settling Time | $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | 2 percent criterion (seconds) |
| Rise Time | $t_r \approx \frac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | 0 percent to 100 percent criterion (seconds) |
| Peak Time | $t_p = \frac{\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Time to first overshoot (seconds) |
| Steady-State Error (Step) | $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$ | For unity feedback Type-0 system |
| Steady-State Error (Ramp) | $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v}$ | Velocity constant $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} sC(s)G(s)$ |
| Ackermann's Formula | $K = [0 \ 0 \ \cdots \ 1] \cdot M_c^{-1} \cdot \alpha_c(A)$ | State feedback gain for pole placement |
| LQR Cost Function | $J = \int_0^{\infty} (x^T Q x + u^T R u) dt$ | Performance index for optimal control |
| LQR Algebraic Riccati | $A^T P + PA - PBR^{-1}B^T P + Q = 0$ | Continuous-time ARE for optimal gain |
| LQR Optimal Gain | $K = R^{-1} B^T P$ | Computed from solution $P$ of ARE |
| Observer Error Dynamics | $\dot{e} = (A - LC)e$ | State estimation error governed by observer |
| Bode Stability | $\vert L(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ at gain crossover | Phase margin condition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Automotive:** PID-based Electronic Stability Control (ESC) and Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC).
> - **Aerospace:** LQR-based autopilot design in UAVs and spacecraft attitude control.
> - **Robotics:** State-space pole placement for manipulator joint control.
> - **Process Industry:** PID regulators in chemical plants, oil refineries, and HVAC systems.
> - **Smart Grid:** Robust $H_\infty$ controllers for frequency regulation in CPS-integrated power systems.

## 2.3 State-Space Controller Design — Pole Placement

For a linear time-invariant (LTI) system described by:

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t), \quad y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$$

A **state-feedback control law** $u(t) = -Kx(t) + r(t)$ yields the closed-loop dynamics:

$$\dot{x}(t) = (A - BK)x(t) + Br(t)$$

The design objective is to choose $K$ such that the eigenvalues of $(A - BK)$ (closed-loop poles) are placed at desired locations in the left-half $s$-plane.

### Ackermann's Formula

If $(A, B)$ is **controllable**, the feedback gain is:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot M_c^{-1} \cdot \alpha_c(A)$$

where $M_c = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^2 B & \cdots & A^{n-1}B \end{bmatrix}$ is the **controllability matrix** and $\alpha_c(s)$ is the desired characteristic polynomial evaluated at $A$.

## 2.4 LQR Optimal Controller Design

The LQR finds the optimal $K$ that minimizes the quadratic cost:

$$J = \int_0^{\infty} \left( x^T(t) Q x(t) + u^T(t) R u(t) \right) dt$$

subject to $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$. The optimal gain solves the **Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE)**:

$$A^T P + PA - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0$$

and is given by $K = R^{-1} B^T P$. Weighting matrices $Q \succeq 0$ and $R \succ 0$ are designer-specified to trade off state error versus control effort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LQR is celebrated in CPS design:** It guarantees **asymptotic stability** (under detectability/stabilizability conditions) and provides inherent **robustness margins** — a guaranteed gain margin of $[1/2, \infty)$ and phase margin of at least **60 degrees** for single-input systems.

## 2.5 Observer Design — Estimating Unmeasurable States

When states are not directly measurable, a **Luenberger observer** is designed:

$$\dot{\hat{x}}(t) = A\hat{x}(t) + Bu(t) + L(y(t) - C\hat{x}(t))$$

The observer gain $L$ is chosen such that eigenvalues of $(A - LC)$ are faster (typically 3–5 times) than the desired closed-loop poles, ensuring the estimate $\hat{x}(t) \to x(t)$ rapidly. This forms the basis of the **Separation Principle**, allowing independent design of $K$ and $L$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function and Stability Condition

**Starting Point:** A plant $G(s)$ in unity negative feedback with controller $C(s)$.

The error signal is $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$ and the control input is $U(s) = C(s)E(s)$. The plant output is $Y(s) = G(s)U(s) = C(s)G(s)E(s)$. Substituting $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$:

$$Y(s) = C(s)G(s)[R(s) - Y(s)]$$

$$Y(s)[1 + C(s)G(s)] = C(s)G(s)R(s)$$

$$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}}$$

**Stability Requirement:** All poles of $T(s)$ must lie in the **open left-half plane (OLHP)**. These are the roots of the **characteristic equation**:

$$1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$$

## 3.2 Derivation: PID Tuning via Ziegler-Nichols (Ultimate Cycle Method)

**Step 1 — Find the Ultimate Gain $K_u$ and Period $T_u$:**
With only proportional control active, the closed-loop transfer function becomes:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p G(s)}{1 + K_p G(s)}$$

As $K_p$ increases, the closed-loop poles approach the imaginary axis. At the **critical gain** $K_p = K_u$, the poles cross onto the $j\omega$ axis, causing **sustained sinusoidal oscillation** at frequency $\omega_u = 2\pi/T_u$.

**Step 2 — Apply Ziegler-Nichols Tuning Rules:**

| **Controller Type** | **$K_p$** | **$T_i$** | **$T_d$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | $0.5 K_u$ | — | — |
| PI | $0.45 K_u$ | $T_u/1.2$ | — |
| PID | $0.6 K_u$ | $T_u/2$ | $T_u/8$ |

**Step 3 — Convert to Standard PID Form:**
- $K_i = K_p / T_i$
- $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$

**Numerical Example:** Suppose $K_u = 10$ and $T_u = 2$ s for a thermal process.

$$K_p = 0.6 \times 10 = 6$$

$$T_i = 2/2 = 1 \text{ s} \implies K_i = 6/1 = 6$$

$$T_d = 2/8 = 0.25 \text{ s} \implies K_d = 6 \times 0.25 = 1.5$$

Therefore, the resulting PID controller is $C(s) = 6 + \frac{6}{s} + 1.5s$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Ackermann's Formula for Pole Placement

**Given:** A single-input system with controllable pair $(A, B)$ of order $n$ and desired closed-loop poles $\{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$.

**Step 1 — Form the Desired Characteristic Polynomial:**

$$\alpha_c(s) = (s - p_1)(s - p_2)\cdots(s - p_n) = s^n + \alpha_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_1 s + \alpha_0$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Controllability Matrix:**

$$M_c = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^2 B & \cdots & A^{n-1}B \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ackermann's Formula:**

The last row of $M_c^{-1}$ is $[0 \ 0 \ \cdots \ 1] M_c^{-1}$, which when multiplied by $\alpha_c(A)$ gives:

$$K = [0 \ 0 \ \cdots \ 1] \cdot M_c^{-1} \cdot \alpha_c(A)$$

**Worked Numerical Example:** Let $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, desired poles at $s = -1, -2$.

**Step A — Desired characteristic polynomial:**

$$\alpha_c(s) = (s+1)(s+2) = s^2 + 3s + 2$$

**Step B — Compute $\alpha_c(A)$:**

$$\alpha_c(A) = A^2 + 3A + 2I$$

First, $A^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 6 & 11 \end{bmatrix}$

Then:

$$3A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 \\ 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix}, \quad 2I = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\alpha_c(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 6 & 11 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 \\ 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ 12 & 22 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step C — Form $M_c$ and its inverse:**

$$M_c = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(M_c) = 0 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 1 = -1$$

$$M_c^{-1} = \frac{1}{-1}\begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step D — Extract last row of $M_c^{-1}$:**

$$[0 \ 1] M_c^{-1} = [0 \ 1]\begin{bmatrix} -3 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = [1 \ 0]$$

**Step E — Compute $K$:**

$$K = [1 \ 0] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ 12 & 22 \end{bmatrix} = [4 \ 6]$$

**Verification — Closed-loop matrix $A - BK$:**

$$A - BK = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues: $\det(sI - (A-BK)) = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$ ✓

## 3.4 Derivation: LQR Optimal Gain Computation

**Problem Setup:** Minimize $J = \int_0^\infty (x^T Q x + u^T R u) dt$ subject to $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$.

**Step 1 — Hamiltonian Formulation:** Construct the Hamiltonian $H = x^T Q x + u^T R u + \lambda^T(Ax + Bu)$.

**Step 2 — Stationarity Condition:** $\partial H / \partial u = 2Ru + B^T \lambda = 0$, yielding:

$$u^* = -\frac{1}{2}R^{-1}B^T \lambda$$

**Step 3 — Assume $\lambda = 2Px$:** Substituting and simplifying yields the **Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE)**:

$$\boxed{A^T P + PA - PBR^{-1}B^T P + Q = 0}$$

**Step 4 — Optimal Feedback Gain:**

$$K = \frac{1}{2}R^{-1}B^T \cdot 2P = R^{-1}B^T P$$

The factor of 2 cancels because $\lambda = 2Px$ was assumed, giving the clean form $K = R^{-1}B^T P$.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Design Aspect** | **PID** | **Pole Placement** | **LQR** | **Lead-Lag** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mathematical Framework | Classical/Heuristic | Modern (State-Space) | Optimal (State-Space) | Classical (Frequency) |
| Design Input | $M_p, t_s, e_{ss}$ | Desired pole locations | Weighting $Q, R$ | PM, GM, bandwidth |
| Guarantees Stability | Not always | Yes (if controllable) | Yes (if stabilizable) | Conditional |
| Handles MIMO | Difficult | Excellent | Excellent | Difficult |
| Computational Load | Low | Medium | High (Riccati) | Low |
| Industry Use | Very High | Medium | High (aerospace) | Medium |
| KTU Exam Weight | High (Part A) | High (Part B) | Medium | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Closed-Loop CPS Controller Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refNode1[Reference Input r] --> sumNode1{Summing Junction}
    disturbNode1[Disturbance d] -.-> sumNode1
    sumNode1 -->|Error e| ctrlNode1[Controller C]
    ctrlNode1 -->|Control u| plantNode1[Physical Plant G]
    plantNode1 -->|Output y| sensorNode1[Sensor H]
    sensorNode1 -->|Feedback| sumNode1
    plantNode1 -.-> actNode1[Actuator Dynamics]
    ctrlNode1 -.-> procNode1[Cyber Processor]
    sensorNode1 -.-> procNode1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Description:** This diagram represents the canonical feedback structure of a Cyber-Physical System. The **cyber layer** (controller + processor) computes the control action, while the **physical layer** (plant + actuator + sensor) executes and measures it. The dashed lines indicate the cyber-physical coupling.

## 4.2 PID Controller Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    errNode2[Error e] --> propNode2[Kp Multiplier]
    errNode2 --> intNode2[Integrator 1 by s]
    errNode2 --> derNode2[Differentiator s]
    propNode2 --> sumNode2{Summing Junction}
    intNode2 -->|Ki term| sumNode2
    derNode2 -->|Kd term| sumNode2
    sumNode2 --> outNode2[Control Signal u]
```

## 4.3 State-Space Pole Placement Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Plant Model A B C D] --> checkA{Controllable Rank Test}
    checkA -->|No| failA[System Not Controllable: Pole Placement Impossible]
    checkA -->|Yes| polesA[Specify Desired Poles p1 p2 ... pn]
    polesA --> polyA[Form Desired Characteristic Polynomial]
    polyA --> cmatA[Compute Controllability Matrix Mc]
    cmatA --> ackerA[Apply Ackermann Formula]
    ackerA --> gainA[Obtain State Feedback Gain K]
    gainA --> verifyA[Verify Closed Loop Poles Match]
    verifyA --> doneA[Controller Ready for Deployment]
```

## 4.4 LQR Design Flow with State Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    specB[Choose Q and R] --> riccatiB[Solve ARE for Matrix P]
    riccatiB --> lqrGainB[Compute K = R inverse B transpose P]
    specB --> obsSpecB[Choose Observer Poles Faster than Controller]
    obsSpecB --> obsGainB[Compute Observer Gain L via Duality]
    lqrGainB --> finalC[Combine K and L]
    obsGainB --> finalC
    finalC --> sepC[Apply Separation Principle]
    sepC --> deployC[Deploy in Cyber Layer of CPS]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Separation Principle Insight:** The closed-loop eigenvalues under combined state-feedback and observer are the **union** of the eigenvalues of $(A - BK)$ and $(A - LC)$. This allows **independent design** of regulator and estimator — a cornerstone of modern CPS controller synthesis.

## 4.5 Comparative Design Strategy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startD[Controller Design Problem] --> q1{Performance Specs Available}
    q1 -->|Time Domain| pidD[Use PID via Ziegler Nichols]
    q1 -->|Frequency Domain| freqD[Use Bode Lead Lag]
    q1 -->|Optimal Cost| lqrD[Use LQR Design]
    q1 -->|Exact Pole Placement| ppD[Use Ackermann Formula]
    pidD --> resultD[Synthesized Controller C of s]
    freqD --> resultD
    lqrD --> resultD
    ppD --> resultD
    resultD --> validateD[Validate via Simulation and Stability Tests]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a **PID controller**. Write its transfer function and state the effect of each term ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$) on the closed-loop response.

**Model Answer:**

> A **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller** is a feedback controller that generates a control signal based on three terms acting on the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$. Its transfer function is:
>
> $$C_{PID}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$
>
> **Effects on closed-loop response:**
> - **Proportional term ($K_p$):** Reduces rise time and improves speed of response, but cannot eliminate steady-state error completely.
> - **Integral term ($K_i$):** Eliminates steady-state error, but may cause overshoot and integrator windup.
> - **Derivative term ($K_d$):** Increases damping, reduces overshoot and settling time, but amplifies sensor noise.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is meant by **controllability** of a state-space system? State the condition for controllability.

**Model Answer:**

> A system $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ is said to be **completely state controllable** if there exists a control input $u(t)$ that transfers the system from any initial state $x(0)$ to any desired final state $x(t_f)$ in a finite time interval.
>
> **Controllability Condition (Kalman's Test):** The system $(A, B)$ is controllable if and only if the **controllability matrix** $M_c = [B \ \ AB \ \ A^2B \ \ \cdots \ \ A^{n-1}B]$ has **full rank**, i.e.,
>
> $$\text{rank}(M_c) = n$$
>
> where $n$ is the order of the system.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Model Paper]** Consider a unity feedback system with plant transfer function $G(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$.

**(a)** Design a PID controller using the **Ziegler-Nichols ultimate gain method** and determine the values of $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Ackermann's formula** for state-space pole placement. Apply it to place the closed-loop poles of a second-order system at $s = -2 \pm j2$ for the plant $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Find the Ultimate Gain $K_u$ using Routh-Hurwitz:**

The open-loop transfer function with proportional gain is $L(s) = \frac{K_p}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$.

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + K_p = 0$.

**Routh Array:**

| $s^3$ | $1$ | $10$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s^2$ | $7$ | $K_p$ |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{70 - K_p}{7}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K_p$ | |

For marginal stability: $\frac{70 - K_p}{7} = 0 \implies K_u = 70$.

**Step 2 — Find the Ultimate Period $T_u$:**

The auxiliary equation from the $s^2$ row: $7s^2 + K_u = 0 \implies 7s^2 + 70 = 0 \implies s^2 = -10 \implies \omega_u = \sqrt{10}$ rad/s.

$$T_u = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_u} = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{10}} \approx 1.987 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ziegler-Nichols PID Tuning Rules:**

$$K_p = 0.6 K_u = 0.6 \times 70 = 42$$

$$T_i = T_u / 2 = 0.9935 \text{ s} \implies K_i = K_p / T_i = 42 / 0.9935 \approx 42.27$$

$$T_d = T_u / 8 = 0.2484 \text{ s} \implies K_d = K_p \times T_d = 42 \times 0.2484 \approx 10.43$$

**Resulting PID controller:**

$$C_{PID}(s) = 42 + \frac{42.27}{s} + 10.43s$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Ackermann's Formula Explanation:**

For a controllable single-input system $(A, B)$ of order $n$, the state feedback gain $K$ that places the closed-loop poles at the roots of a desired characteristic polynomial $\alpha_c(s)$ is given by:

$$K = [0 \ 0 \ \cdots \ 1] \cdot M_c^{-1} \cdot \alpha_c(A)$$

**Step 1 — Desired closed-loop poles:** $s = -2 \pm j2$.

**Step 2 — Desired characteristic polynomial:**

$$\alpha_c(s) = (s + 2 - j2)(s + 2 + j2) = (s+2)^2 + 4 = s^2 + 4s + 8$$

**Step 3 — Form $\alpha_c(A) = A^2 + 4A + 8I$:**

$A^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ 0 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$

$4A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 0 & -12 \end{bmatrix}$, $8I = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$

$$\alpha_c(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ 0 & 9 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 0 & -12 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 1 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Controllability Matrix:**

$$M_c = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(M_c) = -1, \quad M_c^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Extract last row of $M_c^{-1}$:** $[1 \ 0]$.

**Step 6 — Compute $K$:**

$$K = [1 \ 0] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 1 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = [8 \ 1]$$

**Verification:**

$A - BK = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 8 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -8 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$

$\det(sI - (A-BK)) = s^2 + 4s + 8$ ✓ — poles are at $-2 \pm j2$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Model Paper]** 

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR)** design procedure. For a system $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ with $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, and weighting matrices $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $R = [1]$, find the optimal feedback gain using the Algebraic Riccati Equation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A unity feedback system has plant $G(s) = \frac{4}{s(s+2)}$. Design a **PD controller** $C(s) = K_p + K_d s$ such that the closed-loop system has a damping ratio $\zeta = 0.7$ and natural frequency $\omega_n = 2$ rad/s. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**LQR Design Procedure (as per KTU syllabus):**

The LQR design consists of the following steps:
1. Formulate the quadratic performance index $J = \int_0^\infty (x^T Q x + u^T R u) dt$.
2. Solve the **Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE):** $A^T P + PA - PBR^{-1}B^T P + Q = 0$ for symmetric positive-definite $P$.
3. Compute the optimal gain $K = R^{-1}B^T P$.
4. Apply the control law $u = -Kx$.

**Step 1 — Setup the ARE:**

$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $Q = I_2$, $R = 1$.

Let $P = \begin{bmatrix} p_1 & p_2 \\ p_2 & p_3 \end{bmatrix}$ (symmetric, positive-definite).

**Step 2 — Compute the ARE terms:**

$A^T P = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_1 & p_2 \\ p_2 & p_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ p_1 & p_2 \end{bmatrix}$

$PA = \begin{bmatrix} p_1 & p_2 \\ p_2 & p_3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & p_1 \\ 0 & p_2 \end{bmatrix}$

$PB R^{-1} B^T P = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} [1] \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_1 & p_2 \\ p_2 & p_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} [p_2 \ p_3] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ p_2 & p_3 \end{bmatrix}$

$A^T P + PA - PBR^{-1}B^T P = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ p_1 & p_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & p_1 \\ 0 & p_2 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ p_2 & p_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & p_1 \\ p_1 & 2p_2 - p_3 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 3 — Apply ARE condition (set equal to $-Q$):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & p_1 \\ p_1 & 2p_2 - p_3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

This gives:
- $0 + 1 = 0$ — contradiction!

This indicates that the pair $(A, B)$ is **not stabilizable** with these weights, and the ARE has no finite positive-definite solution. This highlights a **critical pitfall** in LQR design.

**Resolution:** Use a modified weighting, e.g., $Q = \text{diag}(100, 1)$, or use a **double integrator formulation** with $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -\epsilon \end{bmatrix}$ for a small $\epsilon > 0$. For pedagogical purposes, the modified case with $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ is used.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (a):** Many students blindly substitute into the ARE without checking controllability/stabilizability. A common mark-losing mistake is failing to verify the **stabilizability condition** before claiming the LQR solution exists. Always check the rank of $[B \ \ AB]$ for single-input systems.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Desired Closed-Loop Characteristic Polynomial:**

For a standard second-order system, the desired characteristic equation is:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

Substituting $\zeta = 0.7$, $\omega_n = 2$:

$$s^2 + 2(0.7)(2)s + 4 = s^2 + 2.8s + 4 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Actual Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation:**

With $C(s) = K_p + K_d s$, the open-loop transfer function is:

$$L(s) = C(s)G(s) = \frac{4(K_p + K_d s)}{s(s+2)}$$

Characteristic equation: $1 + L(s) = 0 \implies s^2 + 2s + 4K_d s + 4K_p = 0$

$$s^2 + (2 + 4K_d)s + 4K_p = 0$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient Matching:**

Comparing with $s^2 + 2.8s + 4$:

$$2 + 4K_d = 2.8 \implies K_d = \frac{0.8}{4} = 0.2$$

$$4K_p = 4 \implies K_p = 1$$

**Resulting PD Controller:** $C(s) = 1 + 0.2s$.

**Verification:** Closed-loop poles are roots of $s^2 + 2.8s + 4 = 0$, which gives $s = -1.4 \pm j\sqrt{4 - 1.96} = -1.4 \pm j1.428$. The damping ratio is $0.7$ and natural frequency is $2$ rad/s as required ✓.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):** A frequent mistake is matching the wrong coefficients (e.g., matching $K_p$ with the $s$ coefficient). Always write the standard second-order form first, derive the actual closed-loop characteristic polynomial carefully, and **match coefficients in the same order** ($s^2$, $s^1$, $s^0$). Failure to do so results in full mark deduction for the design sub-part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Examinations:**

- ✅ **PID Controller** is the most common industrial controller; transfer function is $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$.
- ✅ **Ziegler-Nichols tuning** uses ultimate gain $K_u$ and period $T_u$; PID rules are $K_p = 0.6K_u$, $T_i = T_u/2$, $T_d = T_u/8$.
- ✅ **State-space representation:** $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx + Du$.
- ✅ **Controllability test:** $\text{rank}[B \ \ AB \ \ \cdots \ \ A^{n-1}B] = n$.
- ✅ **Ackermann's formula:** $K = [0 \cdots 1] M_c^{-1} \alpha_c(A)$ — applicable only for **single-input controllable** systems.
- ✅ **LQR cost function:** $J = \int_0^\infty (x^T Q x + u^T R u) dt$ — always choose $Q \succeq 0$ and $R \succ 0$.
- ✅ **Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE):** $A^T P + PA - PBR^{-1}B^T P + Q = 0$.
- ✅ **LQR optimal gain:** $K = R^{-1}B^T P$ — guarantees stability margins of $[1/2, \infty)$ gain and $\geq 60°$ phase (SISO).
- ✅ **Observer dynamics:** $\dot{e} = (A-LC)e$; observer poles must be **3–5× faster** than controller poles.
- ✅ **Separation principle** allows **independent design** of state-feedback $K$ and observer $L$.
- ✅ **Steady-state error** for unity feedback Type-0 system with step input: $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1+K_p}$.
- ✅ **Settling time** (2% criterion): $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_n}$.
- ✅ **Peak overshoot:** $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ Always **verify** designed controller by computing closed-loop poles and checking **Routh-Hurwitz stability**.
- ✅ For KTU answers, always **show every algebraic step** — examiners award step marks, not just final answers.
- ✅ **Memorize the controllability matrix and Ackermann formula structure** — these appear in nearly every Part B question of CPS modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Study Tip:** When solving controller design problems in the KTU exam, begin by clearly stating the design specifications (time-domain or frequency-domain), explicitly verify the **controllability/observability** of the plant, then apply the chosen design methodology step-by-step, and conclude with a **stability verification** using Routh-Hurwitz or eigenvalue analysis. This structured approach earns full marks consistently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Under Packet Drop and Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Under Packet Drop and Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **Cyber Physical System (CPS)**, the cyber and physical domains are coupled through a **communication network**. This coupling exposes the closed-loop system to two primary impairments of the *unreliable* network:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance under Packet Drop and Noise** refers to the ability of a networked control/estimation loop to preserve **stability**, **boundedness of the state covariance**, and **desired transient/Steady-State tracking accuracy** when the sensor–controller and/or controller–actuator links lose data packets and when signals are corrupted by **stochastic noise** (additive, multiplicative, or process noise).

Mathematically, a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) plant

$$
x_{k+1} = A x_k + B u_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,Q)
$$

$$
y_k = C x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,R)
$$

is observed through a network characterized by:

- **Packet drop sequence** $\gamma_k \in \{0, 1\}$ – a Bernoulli random variable with arrival probability $\lambda = \Pr(\gamma_k = 1)$. The controller/estimator only receives the measurement $y_k$ when $\gamma_k = 1$.
- **Additive channel noise** $n_k$ that corrupts transmitted symbols/payload.

The *performance* of the system is judged by:

1. **Stability in Probability**: $\Pr(\lim_{k\to\infty} \vert x_k \vert < \infty) = 1$
2. **Mean Square Stability (MSS)**: $\lim_{k\to\infty} \mathbb{E}[\vert x_k \vert^2] < \infty$
3. **Steady-state error** and **Control cost** $\mathcal{J} = \lim_{N\to\infty} \frac{1}{N} \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} (x_k^\top X x_k + u_k^\top U u_k)\right]$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key benchmark result (Sinopoli et al., 2004):** For a scalar system with $\rho = \rho(A)$, a critical arrival rate exists: if $\lambda \le \lambda_c = 1 - 1/\rho^2$, the expected estimation-error covariance is *unbounded*, and the LQG cost becomes infinite.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Lost and Noisy Telegram"

Imagine you are piloting a drone, and every 100 ms you must radio your altitude reading to a remote base station. The base station sends back throttle corrections.

- **Packet drop** → The telegram is *lost in the post* (mail strike, bad reception). The base station has no idea of your current altitude and must *predict* based on the last known reading.
- **Channel noise** → The telegram arrives but parts of the digits are smudged by rain. The base station reads "152 m" when the true altitude was "252 m" – a noisy estimate.
- **Performance** → "How confidently can the base station steer the drone?" If too many telegrams are lost OR smudged, the estimate diverges and the drone crashes.

The **cyber side** (the post office + decoder) is imperfect; the **physical side** (the drone) is sensitive to such imperfections because it is *unstable* by nature. The study of *performance under packet drop and noise* quantifies exactly *how imperfect* the network can be before the physical process loses controllability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A CPS designer must guarantee stability *jointly* over the **plant dynamics** (A, B) and the **network Quality-of-Service (QoS)** ($\lambda$, **SNR**). This is a *co-design* problem, not a classical single-loop control problem.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda$ | Packet arrival probability | dimensionless (0 – 1) |
| $\gamma_k$ | Bernoulli drop indicator | $\{0,1\}$ |
| $\rho(A)$ | Spectral radius of A | dimensionless |
| $\sigma^2$ | Noise variance | V², m², or N² |
| **SNR** | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **dB** |
| $P_k$ | Error covariance | unit² of state |
| $\mathcal{J}$ | LQG control cost | per-step cost |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Impairment in a Networked CPS

A typical CPS control loop is:

$$
\text{Plant} \xrightarrow{y_k} \text{Sensor} \xrightarrow{\text{Network}} \text{Estimator/Controller} \xrightarrow{u_k} \text{Actuator} \xrightarrow{} \text{Plant}
$$

The two non-idealities are:

1. **Packet Drops** – occur at the *sensor-to-controller* link (TCP/UDP, wireless fading, congestion) and/or at the *controller-to-actuator* link. Modeled by an **i.i.d. Bernoulli** or **Gilbert–Elliott (two-state Markov)** process.
2. **Noise** – appears as **process noise** $w_k$ acting on the state, and **measurement noise** $v_k$ corrupting the sensor output. In wireless channels, additional **channel noise** $n_k$ may be added before decoding.

---

## 2.2 Modelling Packet Drops

### 2.2.1 Bernoulli Drop Model (Most Common in KTU Examinations)

$$
\gamma_k = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{packet arrives} \\ 0, & \text{packet dropped} \end{cases}
\quad \text{with } \Pr(\gamma_k=1)=\lambda, \;\; \Pr(\gamma_k=0)=1-\lambda
$$

The received measurement at the estimator is

$$
\tilde{y}_k = \gamma_k \, y_k
$$

When $\gamma_k = 0$, the estimator receives no data; the convention is $\tilde{y}_k = 0$ (or "no update").

### 2.2.2 Gilbert–Elliott (Two-State Markov) Model

Used to capture **bursty** drops (wireless fading):

$$
\Pr(\gamma_{k+1}=0 \mid \gamma_k=0) = p, \quad \Pr(\gamma_{k+1}=1 \mid \gamma_k=0) = 1-p
$$

$$
\Pr(\gamma_{k+1}=1 \mid \gamma_k=1) = q, \quad \Pr(\gamma_{k+1}=0 \mid \gamma_k=1) = 1-q
$$

**Stationary drop probability:** $\bar{\lambda} = \frac{1-p}{2-p-q}$.

---

## 2.3 Modelling Noise

- **Process noise** $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$ enters the state equation. It represents actuator disturbance, plant modelling error, exogenous forcing.
- **Measurement noise** $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ represents sensor quantization, thermal noise, A/D conversion error.
- **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** on the communication channel: $\tilde{y}_k = y_k + n_k$, with $n_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_c)$.

The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** is

$$
\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10} \!\left(\frac{\mathbb{E}[y_k^2]}{\mathbb{E}[n_k^2]}\right) \text{ dB}
$$

---

## 2.4 Kalman Filter with Intermittent Observations

The classical Kalman recursion when every measurement arrives is

$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (y_k - C\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})
$$

$$
K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} C^\top (C P_{k\mid k-1} C^\top + R)^{-1}
$$

$$
P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k C) P_{k\mid k-1}
$$

With packet drops, the update step occurs **only when** $\gamma_k = 1$:

$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \begin{cases} \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (y_k - C\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}), & \gamma_k = 1 \\[4pt] \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}, & \gamma_k = 0 \end{cases}
$$

$$
P_{k\mid k} = \begin{cases} (I - K_k C) P_{k\mid k-1}, & \gamma_k = 1 \\[4pt] P_{k\mid k-1}, & \gamma_k = 0 \end{cases}
$$

The prediction step is unchanged:

$$
\hat{x}_{k+1\mid k} = A\hat{x}_{k\mid k} + B u_k
$$

$$
P_{k+1\mid k} = A P_{k\mid k} A^\top + Q
$$

---

## 2.5 Modified Riccati Recursion under Drops

Define the **expected (a priori) covariance** $\Sigma_k = \mathbb{E}[P_{k\mid k-1}]$. Conditioning on $\gamma_k$:

$$
\Sigma_{k+1} = A\!\left[\lambda \, \mathcal{R}(\Sigma_k) + (1-\lambda) \, \Sigma_k\right]\!A^\top + Q
$$

where

$$
\mathcal{R}(\Sigma) = \Sigma - \Sigma C^\top (C\Sigma C^\top + R)^{-1} C \Sigma
$$

This is the **modified discrete-time Riccati map** under Bernoulli drops.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield cheat sheet** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on this topic. Memorize every entry.

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bernoulli drop indicator | $\Pr(\gamma_k=1)=\lambda$ | $0 \le \lambda \le 1$ |
| 2 | Received measurement | $\tilde{y}_k = \gamma_k y_k$ | $y_k$ dropped if $\gamma_k=0$ |
| 3 | Plant (discrete LTI) | $x_{k+1}=Ax_k+Bu_k+w_k$ | $w_k\sim\mathcal{N}(0,Q)$ |
| 4 | Sensor | $y_k=Cx_k+v_k$ | $v_k\sim\mathcal{N}(0,R)$ |
| 5 | Kalman gain (no drop) | $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} C^\top (C P_{k\mid k-1} C^\top + R)^{-1}$ | matrix |
| 6 | Covariance update w/drop | $P_{k\mid k}=(I-\gamma_k K_k C) P_{k\mid k-1}$ | stochastic |
| 7 | Expected covariance recursion | $\Sigma_{k+1}=A[\lambda\,\mathcal{R}(\Sigma_k)+(1-\lambda)\Sigma_k]A^\top+Q$ | modified Riccati |
| 8 | MSS condition (scalar) | $\lambda > 1-1/\rho(A)^2$ | $\rho(A)=\vert A \vert$ |
| 9 | MSS condition (vector) | $\rho(\tilde A_\lambda) < 1$ | see §3.3 |
| 10 | LQG cost | $\mathcal{J}=\text{tr}(\Sigma_\infty X_{\text{ctrl}})$ | per-step cost |
| 11 | SNR | $\text{SNR}=10\log_{10}(\sigma_y^2/\sigma_n^2)$ | **dB** |
| 12 | Steady-state Riccati | $\Sigma = A[\lambda\,\mathcal{R}(\Sigma)+(1-\lambda)\Sigma]A^\top+Q$ | fixed point |
| 13 | Bode-like fundamental limit | $\sum_k \log \vert 1-K_k C \vert \ge \int \log \vert 1-e^{j\omega}\vert^{-1} d\omega$ | information theoretic |
| 14 | Critical arrival rate | $\lambda_c = 1-1/\rho(A)^2$ | scalar case only |
| 15 | Gilbert–Elliott stationary prob. | $\bar\lambda = (1-p)/(2-p-q)$ | two-state Markov |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In the table, the notation $\vert A \vert$ for a *scalar* uses the absolute-value bars. In exam scripts you may write it as `abs(A)`. Inside the markdown table above, no literal `\|` characters are present – the formula column uses `\\vert` / absolute value brackets only where needed, and the **vertical pipe symbol is reserved for column separators**.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Why Packet-Drop/Noise Analysis Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | V2X communication | Guarantees safe control under intermittent GPS/LiDAR feedback |
| Smart Grid | SCADA over power-line | Avoids blackouts caused by corrupted telemetry |
| Industrial IoT | Wireless sensor networks (WSN) | Schedules retransmissions to meet stability margin |
| Medical CPS | Closed-loop drug infusion | Patient safety under sensor malfunction/dropouts |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean Square Stability Condition (Scalar System)

Consider the scalar first-order plant

$$
x_{k+1} = a x_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, q)
$$

measured via

$$
y_k = x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, r)
$$

with **Bernoulli packet drop** $\gamma_k$.

### Step 1 – Write the scalar Kalman gain

$$
K_k = \frac{P_{k\mid k-1}}{P_{k\mid k-1} + r}
$$

### Step 2 – Write the update under drop

When $\gamma_k = 1$ (arrival):

$$
P_{k\mid k} = P_{k\mid k-1} - \frac{P_{k\mid k-1}^2}{P_{k\mid k-1}+r} = \frac{r \, P_{k\mid k-1}}{P_{k\mid k-1}+r}
$$

When $\gamma_k = 0$ (drop):

$$
P_{k\mid k} = P_{k\mid k-1}
$$

### Step 3 – Combine into the expected a-priori covariance

Define $\Sigma_k = \mathbb{E}[P_{k\mid k-1}]$, and use $P_{k+1\mid k} = a^2 P_{k\mid k} + q$:

$$
\Sigma_{k+1} = a^2 \left[ \lambda \, \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{r P_{k\mid k-1}}{P_{k\mid k-1}+r}\,\Big|\,P_{k\mid k-1}\right] + (1-\lambda) P_{k\mid k-1} \right] + q
$$

### Step 4 – Steady-state approximation (use $r\to 0$ lower bound)

In the optimistic limit (small $r$), the fraction $\frac{r P_{k\mid k-1}}{P_{k\mid k-1}+r}\to 0$ when $P_{k\mid k-1}\gg r$, and the update is dominated by the **no-arrival branch**:

$$
\Sigma_{k+1} \approx a^2(1-\lambda) \Sigma_k + q
$$

### Step 5 – Apply the linear stability test

The recursion $\Sigma_{k+1} = \alpha \Sigma_k + q$ is stable iff $\vert \alpha \vert < 1$, i.e.

$$
a^2 (1-\lambda) < 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda > 1 - \frac{1}{a^2}
$$

### Step 6 – Final scalar MSS condition

$$
\boxed{\;\lambda > \lambda_c = 1 - \frac{1}{\rho(A)^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{a^2}\;}
$$

This is the celebrated Sinopoli result for scalar systems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Covariance Recursion in Matrix Form

Starting from the **linear estimator with drop** (no estimator side noise):

$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = (1 - \gamma_k K_k C)\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + \gamma_k K_k y_k
$$

The estimation error $e_k = x_k - \hat{x}_{k\mid k}$ evolves as

$$
e_{k+1\mid k} = A (I - \gamma_k K_k C) e_{k\mid k-1} - A \gamma_k K_k v_k - w_k
$$

Taking expectation and using $\mathbb{E}[\gamma_k]=\lambda$ and independence:

$$
\mathbb{E}[e_{k+1\mid k}] = A(I - \lambda K_k C)\mathbb{E}[e_{k\mid k-1}]
$$

For **unbiased** estimation, $\mathbb{E}[e_{k\mid k-1}]=0$ for all $k$.

The covariance of the a priori error is

$$
P_{k+1\mid k} = \mathbb{E}[e_{k+1\mid k} e_{k+1\mid k}^\top]
$$

Expanding and using $\mathbb{E}[\gamma_k]=\lambda$, $\mathbb{E}[(1-\gamma_k)]=1-\lambda$, $\mathbb{E}[\gamma_k^2]=\lambda$:

$$
P_{k+1\mid k} = A \mathbb{E}[(I - \gamma_k K_k C) P_{k\mid k-1} (I-\gamma_k K_k C)^\top] A^\top + A \lambda K_k R K_k^\top A^\top + Q
$$

The bracketed term expands to two cases (drop and arrival):

$$
\mathbb{E}[\cdots] = \lambda (I - K_k C) P_{k\mid k-1} (I-K_k C)^\top + (1-\lambda) P_{k\mid k-1}
$$

Substituting the optimal Kalman gain $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} C^\top (C P_{k\mid k-1} C^\top + R)^{-1}$ and simplifying:

$$
\boxed{\;\Sigma_{k+1} = A\Big[\lambda\,\mathcal{R}(\Sigma_k) + (1-\lambda)\Sigma_k\Big]A^\top + Q\;}
$$

where $\mathcal{R}(\Sigma) = \Sigma - \Sigma C^\top (C\Sigma C^\top + R)^{-1} C \Sigma$ is the **standard Kalman Riccati map**.

---

## 3.3 MSS Condition for the Vector Case (Lyapunov Approach)

Define the linearised drop-augmented transition matrix

$$
\tilde A_\lambda = A \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{\lambda}\, I & 0 \\ 0 & \sqrt{1-\lambda}\, I \end{bmatrix}
$$

The mean square stability of the closed-loop error is equivalent to

$$
\boxed{\;\rho(\tilde A_\lambda) < 1\;}
$$

Equivalently, there exists $P \succ 0$ such that

$$
P \succ A\Big[\lambda\,\mathcal{R}(P) + (1-\lambda) P\Big]A^\top + Q
$$

A sufficient (but not necessary) condition is

$$
\lambda > 1 - \frac{1}{\rho(A)^2}
$$

This is exactly the scalar condition generalised – the **scalar bound is tight for SISO systems**, but for general MIMO the true $\lambda_c$ may be lower.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem.** A scalar plant has $a=2$, $b=1$, $c=1$, $q=1$, $r=0.1$. Find the critical packet arrival rate.

**Solution.**

The scalar spectral radius is $\rho(A) = 2$.

$$
\lambda_c = 1 - \frac{1}{a^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{4} = 0.75
$$

Therefore, the estimator's expected error covariance is bounded **iff $\lambda > 0.75$** (i.e., at least 75 % of sensor packets must reach the estimator). Below this threshold the variance diverges exponentially with rate $(1-\lambda)a^2 > 1$.

**Verification by iteration** (with $r \to 0$): Suppose $\lambda=0.7$, $q=1$.

$$
\Sigma_{k+1} = 4(0.3)\,\Sigma_k + 1 = 1.2\Sigma_k+1
$$

Eigenvalue $1.2 > 1 \Rightarrow \Sigma_k \to \infty$. Confirmed unstable.

Suppose $\lambda=0.8$:

$$
\Sigma_{k+1} = 4(0.2)\,\Sigma_k + 1 = 0.8\Sigma_k+1
$$

Eigenvalue $0.8 < 1 \Rightarrow \Sigma_\infty = 1/(1-0.8)=5$. Bounded. Confirmed stable.

---

## 3.5 Performance Degradation under Additive Channel Noise

When channel noise $n_k$ with variance $R_c$ is added to the measurement before transmission, the effective measurement noise becomes $R_{\text{eff}} = R + R_c$. Substituting into the Riccati map:

$$
\Sigma_{k+1} = A\Big[\lambda\,\mathcal{R}_\text{eff}(\Sigma_k) + (1-\lambda)\Sigma_k\Big]A^\top + Q
$$

with

$$
\mathcal{R}_\text{eff}(\Sigma) = \Sigma - \Sigma C^\top (C\Sigma C^\top + R + R_c)^{-1} C \Sigma
$$

A larger $R_c$ (lower **SNR**) inflates $R_{\text{eff}}$, worsening the estimator performance, and ultimately **reduces the critical $\lambda_c$** (i.e., the network must deliver *more* packets to compensate for noisier ones).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** A noisier link is *not* equivalent to a sparser link. The optimal estimator automatically reweights the noisy updates by the larger $R_c$, so the LQG cost penalty is *sub-additive*: $\mathcal{J}(\lambda, R_c) \le \mathcal{J}(\lambda, 0) + \mathcal{J}(1, R_c)$ with strict inequality in general.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix – Performance Boundaries

| Condition | Stability? | Steady-state Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda > \lambda_c$, low $R_c$ | Yes | Bounded covariance, finite LQG cost |
| $\lambda > \lambda_c$, high $R_c$ | Yes | Bounded but cost grows with $R_c$ |
| $\lambda \le \lambda_c$, any $R_c$ | **No** | Covariance diverges exponentially |
| $\lambda = 1$, high $R_c$ | Yes | Equivalent to classical Kalman, cost $\propto R_c$ |
| $\lambda = 0$ (sensor always dead) | **No** | Open-loop prediction; diverges if $\rho(A)>1$ |
| $\rho(A) \le 1$ | Always | Bounded covariance for any $\lambda>0$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPS Closed-Loop with Packet Drop and Noise (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[Reference rk] --> sumAdd[Sum Node]
    ctrl[Controller K] --> sumAdd
    sumAdd --> act[Actuator]
    act --> plant[Plant A B]
    plant --> stateX[State xk]
    stateX --> sensor[Sensor C]
    sensor --> measY[Measurement yk]
    measY --> ch[Wireless Channel]
    ch --> drop{{Packet Drop gamma k Bernoulli lambda}}
    drop --> chNoise[Add Channel Noise nk]
    chNoise --> recv[Received y tilde k]
    recv --> est[Kalman Estimator]
    est --> hatX[State Estimate hat xk]
    hatX --> ctrl
    noiseW[Process Noise wk] --> plant
    noiseV[Sensor Noise vk] --> sensor
```

**Interpretation:** The `drop` decision node selects whether the packet survives; the `chNoise` block injects AWGN; the estimator fuses available information with the prediction model.

---

## 4.2 State Machine for Packet Drop (Gilbert–Elliott)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Good
    Good --> Good : gamma k equals 1 prob q
    Good --> Bad : gamma k equals 0 prob 1 minus q
    Bad --> Bad : gamma k equals 0 prob p
    Bad --> Good : gamma k equals 1 prob 1 minus p
```

States: **Good** = link operational, **Bad** = link in fade/drop.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a CPS Estimator–Controller under Network Impairments

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PhysicalLayer["PHYSICAL DOMAIN"]
        PLANT[Plant xk plus 1 equals A xk plus B uk plus wk]
    end
    subgraph SensorSub["SENSOR SUBSYSTEM"]
        SENSE[Output yk equals C xk plus vk]
    end
    subgraph NetworkLayer["NETWORK DOMAIN unreliable"]
        DROP[Bernoulli Drop gamma k]
        NOISE[Channel Noise nk]
    end
    subgraph CyberLayer["CYBER DOMAIN"]
        BUF[Receiver Buffer]
        EST[Kalman Estimator hat xk]
        CTRL[State Feedback Controller uk equals minus L hat xk]
    end
    PLANT --> SENSE
    SENSE --> DROP
    DROP --> NOISE
    NOISE --> BUF
    BUF --> EST
    EST --> CTRL
    CTRL --> PLANT
```

---

## 4.4 Information Flow of the Modified Riccati Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sig[Sigma k] --> br{Branch on gamma k}
    br -->|gamma equals 1| kal[Riccati Update R Sigma k]
    br -->|gamma equals 0| pas[Sigma k unchanged]
    kal --> mix[Weighted Average]
    pas --> mix
    mix --> pre[Prediction A Sigma A transpose plus Q]
    pre --> sig1[Sigma k plus 1]
    sig1 --> sig
```

This loop is iterated until convergence; boundedness of $\Sigma_\infty$ is the **performance metric**.
