# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define packet drop in a Cyber Physical System. How is it modelled mathematically?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **packet drop** is the loss of a data packet during transmission between the sensor, controller, and/or actuator of a networked CPS, due to channel fading, congestion, or link failure. It is modelled by a discrete random variable $\gamma_k \in \{0,1\}$ with $\Pr(\gamma_k=1)=\lambda$ (i.i.d. **Bernoulli**), so the received measurement becomes $\tilde{y}_k = \gamma_k y_k$. When $\gamma_k=0$ the estimator receives no update and resorts to prediction only. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**What is the role of Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) in CPS performance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SNR** quantifies the power of the useful signal relative to the corrupting noise on the communication channel:

$$
\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10} \!\left(\frac{\sigma_y^2}{\sigma_n^2}\right) \text{ dB}
$$

A low SNR inflates the effective measurement noise variance $R_{\text{eff}} = R + R_c$, worsening the Kalman estimator's accuracy and increasing the LQG cost. In a CPS, SNR must be maintained above a threshold so that the closed-loop system remains stable and the steady-state error is bounded. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the modified discrete-time Riccati recursion for the expected a-priori error covariance $\Sigma_{k+1}$ in a CPS where the sensor–controller link suffers **Bernoulli packet drops** with arrival probability $\lambda$. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the scalar plant $x_{k+1}=2x_k+w_k$ with measurement $y_k = x_k + v_k$, derive the **critical packet arrival rate** $\lambda_c$ below which the expected error covariance is unbounded. Verify with a numerical iteration for $\lambda=0.7$ and $\lambda=0.8$ assuming $q=1$, $r\to 0$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) – Riccati Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumptions** (1 mark):

1. Plant is LTI Gaussian: $x_{k+1}=Ax_k+Bu_k+w_k$, $w_k\sim\mathcal{N}(0,Q)$.
2. Sensor is LTI: $y_k=Cx_k+v_k$, $v_k\sim\mathcal{N}(0,R)$.
3. Packet drops are **i.i.d. Bernoulli** with $\Pr(\gamma_k=1)=\lambda$, independent of state and noise.
4. The controller uses the **optimal Kalman estimator** when a measurement arrives, and **prediction only** otherwise.

**Kalman update under drop** (2 marks):

$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + \gamma_k K_k (y_k - C\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})
$$

$$
P_{k\mid k} = (I - \gamma_k K_k C) P_{k\mid k-1}
$$

where the optimal gain (no drop) is $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} C^\top (C P_{k\mid k-1} C^\top + R)^{-1}$.

**Prediction step** (1 mark):

$$
P_{k+1\mid k} = A P_{k\mid k} A^\top + Q
$$

**Expectation over $\gamma_k$** (2 marks): Using $\mathbb{E}[\gamma_k]=\lambda$ and $\mathbb{E}[1-\gamma_k]=1-\lambda$, the expected a-priori covariance is

$$
\Sigma_{k+1} = A\Big[\lambda\,\mathcal{R}(\Sigma_k) + (1-\lambda)\Sigma_k\Big]A^\top + Q
$$

with $\mathcal{R}(\Sigma) = \Sigma - \Sigma C^\top (C\Sigma C^\top + R)^{-1} C \Sigma$.

**Interpretation** (1 mark): This is the **modified Riccati map**; the term in brackets blends the *updated* covariance (when packet arrives) with the *propagated* covariance (when dropped), weighted by $\lambda$ and $1-\lambda$.

---

**Part (b) – Critical $\lambda_c$ for Scalar Case (7 marks)**

**Set up the scalar recursion** (2 marks): With $a=2$, $q=1$, $c=1$, and in the optimistic limit $r \to 0$ (small sensor noise), the Kalman gain $K_k \to 1$ so the *arrival* update drives the posterior covariance to ~0, while the *drop* branch leaves $P_{k\mid k}=P_{k\mid k-1}$. The dominant branch for large $P$ is therefore the **drop branch**:

$$
\Sigma_{k+1} = a^2 (1-\lambda) \Sigma_k + q
$$

**Stability test** (2 marks): The linear recursion $\Sigma_{k+1} = \alpha \Sigma_k + q$ is stable iff $\vert \alpha \vert < 1$:

$$
a^2 (1-\lambda) < 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \lambda > 1 - \frac{1}{a^2}
$$

**Critical arrival rate** (1 mark):

$$
\lambda_c = 1 - \frac{1}{a^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{4} = 0.75
$$

**Numerical verification** (2 marks):

- **$\lambda = 0.7$:** $\alpha = a^2(1-\lambda) = 4 \cdot 0.3 = 1.2 > 1$. Iterating with $q=1$ and $\Sigma_0 = 1$:

$$
\Sigma_1 = 1.2 \cdot 1 + 1 = 2.2, \quad \Sigma_2 = 1.2 \cdot 2.2 + 1 = 3.64, \quad \Sigma_k \to \infty
$$

- **$\lambda = 0.8$:** $\alpha = 4 \cdot 0.2 = 0.8 < 1$.

$$
\Sigma_\infty = \frac{q}{1-\alpha} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5
$$

Both confirm the theoretical $\lambda_c = 0.75$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Bernoulli** and **Gilbert–Elliott** packet drop models. Derive the **stationary drop probability** for the Gilbert–Elliott model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plant $x_{k+1} = 1.5\,x_k + u_k + w_k$ is measured through a noisy link with $\text{SNR} = 20$ dB. The channel noise has variance $\sigma_n^2 = 0.01$. The measurement is $y_k = x_k + v_k$ with $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.05)$. Find the effective measurement noise variance and compute the **critical arrival rate** for mean square stability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) – Drop Models (7 marks)**

**Bernoulli model** (2 marks): Each packet is independently retained with probability $\lambda$:

$$
\Pr(\gamma_k = 1) = \lambda, \quad \Pr(\gamma_k = 0) = 1-\lambda
$$

Memoryless, simple, widely used in first-order analysis.

**Gilbert–Elliott model** (2 marks): A two-state Markov chain captures **bursty** behaviour with state $S_k \in \{\text{Good}, \text{Bad}\}$:

- $\Pr(S_{k+1}=\text{Good}\mid S_k=\text{Bad}) = 1-p$
- $\Pr(S_{k+1}=\text{Bad}\mid S_k=\text{Good}) = 1-q$
- Packet dropped iff $S_k=\text{Bad}$: $\gamma_k = 1 - \mathbb{1}(S_k=\text{Bad})$.

**Stationary drop probability** (3 marks): Let $\pi_G$ and $\pi_B$ be the stationary probabilities. Detailed balance:

$$
\pi_G(1-q) = \pi_B(1-p), \quad \pi_G + \pi_B = 1
$$

Solving:

$$
\pi_B = \frac{1-q}{2-p-q}, \quad \pi_G = \frac{1-p}{2-p-q}
$$

The stationary **drop** probability is $\bar\lambda = \pi_B = \dfrac{1-q}{2-p-q}$.

---

**Part (b) – Effective Noise and Critical Rate (7 marks)**

**Compute $\sigma_y^2$ from SNR** (2 marks):

$$
\text{SNR} = 20 \text{ dB} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{\sigma_y^2}{\sigma_n^2} = 10^{20/10} = 100
$$

$$
\sigma_y^2 = 100 \cdot 0.01 = 1.0
$$

**Effective measurement noise variance** (2 marks): The total noise corrupting the measurement reaching the estimator is sensor + channel:

$$
R_{\text{eff}} = R + R_c = 0.05 + 0.01 = 0.06
$$

**Critical arrival rate** (2 marks): With $\rho(A) = 1.5$:

$$
\lambda_c = 1 - \frac{1}{1.5^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.25} = 1 - 0.4444 = 0.5556
$$

**Conclusion** (1 mark): At least **55.56 %** of packets must arrive for the estimator covariance to remain bounded. If $\lambda \le 0.5556$, the CPS is **not mean-square stable** and the system must be redesigned (better channel coding, more retransmissions, or plant-side damping).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\lambda$ and $1-\lambda$**: Many students mix up the packet *arrival* and *drop* probabilities. Always state explicitly: $\Pr(\gamma_k=1) = \lambda$ is *arrival*. (Penalty: -1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the prediction term $Q$**: When writing the Riccati map, students often omit the process noise $Q$. (Penalty: -1 to -2 marks)
> 3. **Applying the scalar formula to MIMO directly**: The bound $\lambda > 1 - 1/\rho(A)^2$ is **only** valid (and tight) for scalar/SISO systems. For MIMO, the test is $\rho(\tilde A_\lambda) < 1$. (Penalty: -2 marks)
> 4. **Mixing up process and measurement noise variances**: $Q$ acts on the state; $R$ acts on the output. They cannot be interchanged. (Penalty: -1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting to convert dB**: SNR in dB must be converted via $10^{x/10}$ before substituting into variance expressions. (Penalty: -1 mark)
> 6. **Not verifying numerical examples**: Always iterate at least 2–3 steps to confirm theoretical predictions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **CPS Performance** = stability + bounded error covariance + low LQG cost, evaluated *jointly* over plant and network.
- **Packet drop** = loss of a transmitted data unit; modelled by **Bernoulli** $\gamma_k\in\{0,1\}$ with $\Pr(\gamma_k=1)=\lambda$ or **Gilbert–Elliott** Markov chain.
- **Noise** = process $w_k\sim\mathcal{N}(0,Q)$, measurement $v_k\sim\mathcal{N}(0,R)$, channel $n_k\sim\mathcal{N}(0,R_c)$.
- **Effective measurement noise** $R_{\text{eff}} = R + R_c$ governs Kalman performance.
- **Kalman update under drops**:
  - *Arrival* ($\gamma_k=1$): standard $K_k$ correction.
  - *Drop* ($\gamma_k=0$): $P_{k\mid k}=P_{k\mid k-1}$ (no update).
- **Modified Riccati recursion**:

$$
\Sigma_{k+1} = A\Big[\lambda\,\mathcal{R}(\Sigma_k) + (1-\lambda)\Sigma_k\Big]A^\top + Q
$$

- **Scalar MSS condition**: $\lambda > 1 - 1/\rho(A)^2$.
- **MIMO MSS condition**: $\rho(\tilde A_\lambda) < 1$ where $\tilde A_\lambda$ is the drop-augmented transition matrix.
- **Critical arrival rate** $\lambda_c$: the threshold above which expected error covariance is bounded; below it, covariance diverges.
- **Gilbert–Elliott stationary drop prob.**: $\bar\lambda = (1-q)/(2-p-q)$.
- **SNR in dB**: $\text{SNR}=10\log_{10}(\sigma_y^2/\sigma_n^2)$.
- **Co-design principle**: improving **SNR** lowers the demand on $\lambda$ (and vice-versa) – a fundamental trade-off in CPS.
- **Open-loop failure mode**: if $\rho(A) > 1$ and $\lambda \to 0$, the plant diverges since the estimator receives no information.
- **Stable plant mode**: if $\rho(A) \le 1$, the system is *intrinsically* stable; packet drops only degrade performance, not stability.
- **LQG cost under drops** $\mathcal{J}$ is finite iff MSS holds; it grows super-linearly as $\lambda \to \lambda_c^+$.
- **Bursty drops are worse than i.i.d. drops** at the same mean rate – Gilbert–Elliott with high $p$ and $1-q$ violates MSS more aggressively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Mantra for KTU 2024 Exam:** *"No packet, no proof. No proof, no stability."* Always check the arrival rate against the critical value **before** claiming your CPS design is stable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: From Features to Automotive Software Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** From Features to Automotive Software Components # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** and automotive software engineering, a **Feature** is defined as a *user-observable behavior* or *functionality* of a vehicle that can be distinguished from other functionalities by its **end-user perceivable characteristics** and is typically described in the *problem space* (customer-facing domain). An **Automotive Software Component (SWC)**, on the other hand, is a *modular, self-contained, and reusable software entity* that implements part of the vehicle's functionality in the *solution space* (engineering domain). Components are characterized by well-defined **Ports**, **Interfaces**, and **Runnables**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Frequently asked in exams):** > The transformation from **Features → Software Components** is known as the **Feature-to-Architecture Mapping** or **Software Architecture Synthesis**. It bridges the *customer requirements layer* with the *implementation layer* of an automotive ECU (Electronic Control Unit). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "Ordering a Custom Car"** 🚗 Imagine you are configuring a new car on a manufacturer's website: - **Features** are what you, the *customer*, see and select: "Adaptive Cruise Control," "Lane Keep Assist," "Automatic Emergency Braking." - **Software Components** are the *engineering artifacts* hidden under the hood: the radar signal processing module, the actuator control module, the sensor fusion algorithm running on the ECU. Think of it like ordering a **pizza**: - **Features** = The toppings you choose (mushrooms, olives, extra cheese). - **Software Components** = The kitchen processes (dough prep, sauce spreading, oven temperature control). - **Feature-to-Component Mapping** = The chef's recipe that translates your topping list into kitchen operations. The customer never sees the software, but they experience the **feature**. The engineering team takes those features and breaks them down into **components** that can be coded, tested, and deployed on hardware. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary | Term | Definition | Domain | |------|------------|--------| | **Feature** | User-visible functionality | Problem Space (Customer) | | **Use Case** | Sequence of interactions to achieve a goal | Problem Space | | **Software Component (SWC)** | Atomic software unit with defined ports | Solution Space | | **ECU** | Electronic Control Unit - the hardware | Platform | | **Runnable** | Executable code segment triggered by events | Implementation | | **Port** | Communication endpoint of an SWC | Interface | | **RTE** | Run-Time Environment (middleware) | AUTOSAR | | **AUTOSAR** | AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture | Standard | ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants - **AUTOSAR Release Versions**: 4.0, 4.2, 4.4, 4.6, R20-11 (latest, as of 2023) - **Typical ECU Count in Modern Vehicle**: **70–100 ECUs** (high-end vehicles can exceed **150**) - **Lines of Code in Premium Vehicle**: ~**100 million lines** of code - **Feature Deployment Cycle**: 3–5 years for new platforms - **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level) Levels**: A, B, C, D (per ISO 26262) - **A typical Runnable execution frequency**: **1 ms – 100 ms** cycle # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Feature-to-Component Pipeline (Structured Logic Steps) The transformation from features to software components follows a structured engineering pipeline. Let us break it down step-by-step: ### Step 1: Feature Elicitation - **What**: Gathering all customer-visible requirements. - **Why**: Features originate from market analysis, regulations (e.g., Euro NCAP), and brand differentiation. - **How**: Stakeholder interviews, regulatory compliance checklists, competitor analysis. ### Step 2: Feature Modeling - **What**: Representing features and their relationships using a **Feature Model**. - **Why**: To capture variability (optional/mandatory features) and commonality across vehicle variants. - **How**: Using **FODA (Feature-Oriented Domain Analysis)** notation, with mandatory, optional, alternative, and OR-features. ### Step 3: Use Case Refinement - **What**: Each feature is decomposed into **Use Cases** that describe actor-system interactions. - **Why**: Features are too coarse; use cases provide fine-grained behavior. - **How**: UML Use Case Diagrams, sequence diagrams. ### Step 4: Logical Architecture - **What**: Abstract software structure is defined — components and their interactions. - **Why**: To separate "what" from "how" and enable parallel development. - **How**: Component diagrams, port-based design (AUTOSAR methodology). ### Step 5: Software Component (SWC) Design - **What**: Each logical component is refined into **Atomic SWCs** with specific runnables. - **Why**: Enables code generation, unit testing, and integration. - **How**: AUTOSAR XML descriptions, ARXML configuration. ### Step 6: ECU Integration & Deployment - **What**: SWCs are mapped to physical ECUs. - **Why**: Hardware constraints (memory, CPU, network bandwidth) drive the mapping. - **How**: AUTOSAR RTE generation, ECU extract configuration. ## 2.2 Feature Modeling — Detailed Rules A **Feature Diagram** uses the following relationships: | Relationship | Symbol | Meaning | Example | |--------------|--------|---------|---------| | **Mandatory** | Solid filled circle (●) | Child feature is required if parent is selected | "Vehicle" must include "Engine" | | **Optional** | Hollow circle (○) | Child feature may or may not be selected | "Vehicle" may include "Sunroof" | | **Alternative** | Arc with a single line | Exactly one child must be selected | "Transmission" → "Manual" XOR "Automatic" | | **Or** | Arc with filled arc | One or more children must be selected | "Safety" → "ABS" OR "ESC" OR both | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables | Units | |---------|---------------|-----------|-------| | Number of valid feature combinations | $\prod_{i=1}^{n}(k_i + 1)$ | $k_i$ = number of optional children of node $i$ | Count (dimensionless) | | Alternative group combinations | $\binom{n}{1} = n$ | $n$ = number of alternatives | Count | | OR-group combinations | $2^n - 1$ | $n$ = number of optional children | Count | | AUTOSAR SWC communication latency | $T_{latency} = T_{send} + T_{RTE} + T_{receive}$ | Time components | **ms** | | ECU CPU utilization | $U = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i}{P} \times 100$ | $C_i$ = computation time, $P$ = period | **%** | | Signal-to-interface ratio | 1:N (one signal can map to many interfaces) | Mapping ratio | Dimensionless | | Software reuse ratio | $R = \frac{L_{reused}}{L_{total}} \times 100$ | $L$ = lines of code | **%** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The formula $\prod_{i=1}^{n}(k_i + 1)$ is high-yield. If asked "How many variants does a feature model produce?", draw the tree and multiply. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The feature-to-component paradigm is the **backbone of modern automotive development** because it enables: 1. **Variant Management** — Same code base can serve different car models (BMW 3-series vs 5-series). 2. **Parallel Development** — Teams can work on independent SWCs simultaneously. 3. **Reusability** — A component designed for one ECU can be ported to another. 4. **Safety Certification** — ISO 26262 requires modular components with clear interfaces. 5. **Over-the-Air (OTA) Updates** — Granular SWC-level updates are possible. **Industries using this paradigm:** Automotive (AUTOSAR), Aerospace (DO-178C), Industrial Automation (IEC 61131), Medical Devices (IEC 62304). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Feature Combination Counting **Problem Statement:** A luxury car manufacturer offers a feature model with the following structure: - **Vehicle** (root, mandatory) - **Powertrain** (mandatory) - Engine Type: [Electric, Hybrid, Petrol] (alternative group) - **Comfort** (optional) - Sunroof (optional) - Massage Seats (optional) - **Safety** (mandatory) - ABS (mandatory) - Airbags: [Front-only, Front+Side, Front+Side+Curtain] (alternative group) **Derive:** Total number of valid vehicle configurations. ### Step 1: Identify Mandatory vs Optional Sub-trees $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mandatory nodes} &= \{\text{Powertrain, Engine, Safety, ABS}\} \\ \text{Optional nodes} &= \{\text{Comfort, Sunroof, Massage Seats}\} \\ \text{Alternative groups} &= \{\text{Engine Type, Airbag Config}\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Count Mandatory Choices (each has exactly 1 way) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mandatory paths} &= 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Count Alternative Group Choices For **Engine Type** (alternative group with 3 options, pick exactly 1): $$ \begin{aligned} N_{engine} &= \binom{3}{1} = 3 \end{aligned} $$ For **Airbag Config** (alternative group with 3 options, pick exactly 1): $$ \begin{aligned} N_{airbags} &= \binom{3}{1} = 3 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Count Optional Choices (each can be present or absent) For **Comfort** package (optional, 2 choices: present or absent): $$ \begin{aligned} N_{comfort} &= 2^1 = 2 \end{aligned} $$ If Comfort is present, then: - Sunroof: $2$ choices - Massage Seats: $2$ choices So conditional on Comfort being present: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{comfort\_features} &= 2 \times 2 = 4 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Combine via Multiplication Principle $$ \begin{aligned} N_{total} &= N_{mandatory} \times N_{engine} \times N_{airbags} \times N_{comfort} \times N_{comfort\_features} \\ &= 1 \times 3 \times 3 \times 2 \times 4 \\ &= 72 \text{ configurations} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6: Verification We can independently verify using the **Generalized Formula**: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{total} &= \prod_{i=1}^{n}(k_i + 1) \quad \text{(for optional sub-trees)} \\ &\quad \times \prod_{j=1}^{m} \binom{a_j}{1} \quad \text{(for alternative groups)} \\ &= (2)(4) \times (3)(3) \\ &= 8 \times 9 = 72 \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Feature vs Component Mapping Example Consider the feature **"Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)"**: | Layer | Element | Description | |-------|---------|-------------| | **Feature Layer** | Adaptive Cruise Control | User sets desired speed and gap; car maintains both | | **Use Case Layer** | UC1: Set speed
UC2: Set gap
UC3: Detect lead vehicle
UC4: Adjust throttle
UC5: Apply brake | Discrete interactions | | **Logical Component** | ACC Controller | Orchestrates the ACC behavior | | **Atomic SWC 1** | Radar Signal Processing SWC | Filters radar sensor noise | | **Atomic SWC 2** | Sensor Fusion SWC | Combines radar + camera data | | **Atomic SWC 3** | Longitudinal Controller SWC | Computes throttle/brake commands | | **Atomic SWC 4** | Driver Interface SWC | HMI button/display logic | | **Runnable 1** | `Radar_Filter_10ms()` | Runs every **10 ms** | | **Runnable 2** | `Compute_Accel_20ms()` | Runs every **20 ms** | | **Port** | P-Port: ThrottleCmd
R-Port: RadarData | Sender/Receiver ports | | **ECU Mapping** | Radar ECU → ADAS ECU → Engine ECU → HMI ECU | Distributed deployment | ## 3.3 Derivation: AUTOSAR Latency Equation For a signal flowing from SWC-A on ECU-1 to SWC-B on ECU-2: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{latency} &= T_{producer} + T_{bus} + T_{consumer} \\ T_{producer} &= T_{RTE\_sender} + T_{COM} + T_{bus\_tx} \\ T_{bus} &= \frac{L_{frame} \times 8}{R_{bus}} \\ T_{consumer} &= T_{bus\_rx} + T_{COM} + T_{RTE\_receiver} \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $L_{frame}$ = frame length in bytes - $R_{bus}$ = bus bit rate (CAN: **500 kbps**, FlexRay: **10 Mbps**, Ethernet: **100 Mbps – 1 Gbps**) - $T_{COM}$ = COM module processing time **Example:** For a CAN frame of 8 bytes at 500 kbps: $$ T_{bus} = \frac{8 \times 8}{500{,}000} = 128 \, \mu s $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is why critical safety signals (brake, steering) move to **FlexRay** or **Automotive Ethernet** — to minimize latency. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Feature Model Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD rootA[Vehicle Platform] rootA --> nodeB[Powertrain Mandatory] rootA --> nodeC[Comfort Optional] rootA --> nodeD[Safety Mandatory] nodeB --> nodeE[Engine Type Alternative] nodeE --> nodeF[Electric] nodeE --> nodeG[Hybrid] nodeE --> nodeH[Petrol] nodeC --> nodeI[Sunroof] nodeC --> nodeJ[Massage Seats] nodeD --> nodeK[ABS Mandatory] nodeD --> nodeL[Airbags Alternative] nodeL --> nodeM[Front Only] nodeL --> nodeN[Front and Side] nodeL --> nodeO[Full Curtain] ``` ## 4.2 Feature-to-Component Architecture Flow ```mermaid graph LR stepA[Customer Feature Request] --> stepB[Feature Model] stepB --> stepC[Use Case Analysis] stepC --> stepD[Logical Architecture] stepD --> stepE[Atomic Software Components] stepE --> stepF[Runnable Implementation] stepF --> stepG[ECU Deployment via RTE] stepG --> stepH[Vehicle Validation] stepH --> stepA ``` ## 4.3 AUTOSAR Layered Architecture (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid graph TB layer1[Application Layer SWCs] layer2[Run Time Environment RTE] layer3[Service Layer] layer4[ECU Abstraction Layer] layer5[Microcontroller Abstraction Layer] layer6[Hardware ECU] layer1 --> layer2 layer2 --> layer3 layer3 --> layer4 layer4 --> layer5 layer5 --> layer6 layer6 --> layer5 layer5 --> layer4 layer4 --> layer3 layer3 --> layer2 layer2 --> layer1 ``` ## 4.4 SWC Port Communication Pattern ```mermaid graph LR swc1[SWC A Sensor Processing] -->|P-Port Sender| rte1[RTE Middleware] rte1 -->|R-Port Receiver| swc2[SWC B Actuator Control] swc2 -->|P-Port Sender| rte2[RTE Middleware] rte2 -->|R-Port Receiver| swc3[SWC C HMI Display] rte1 -.Inter-ECU.-> bus1[CAN or FlexRay Bus] rte2 -.Inter-ECU.-> bus1 ``` ## 4.5 Component Interaction Matrix (Visual Block Flow) ```mermaid graph TD radar[Radar Sensor ECU] -->|RadarData| fusion[Sensor Fusion SWC] camera[Camera ECU] -->|ObjectList| fusion fusion -->|TrackedObjects| ctrl[ACC Controller SWC] ctrl -->|ThrottleCmd| engine[Engine ECU] ctrl -->|BrakeCmd| brake[Brake ECU] driver[Driver HMI SWC] -->|SetSpeed SetGap| ctrl ctrl -->|Status| driver ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Each box represents an **Atomic Software Component**, each labeled edge represents a **Port-based Interface**, and the entire network depicts a *single feature* (Adaptive Cruise Control) decomposed into SWCs. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define the term "Feature" in the context of automotive Cyber-Physical Systems. How does it differ from a Software Component?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Feature**: A user-observable behavior or functionality of a vehicle, described in the **problem space** (customer domain). Example: "Automatic Headlamp Control." 2. **Software Component (SWC)**: A modular, self-contained software unit with defined ports and runnables, described in the **solution space** (engineering domain). Example: A Light Control SWC running on the BCM ECU. 3. **Key Difference**: Features are *what the user experiences*; components are *how it is implemented*. Multiple components can collectively realize a single feature. *(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for feature definition, 1 mark for SWC definition, 1 mark for differentiation)* --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: List and briefly explain the four types of relationships in a Feature Model.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Mandatory** (●): Child feature must be selected if parent is selected. 2. **Optional** (○): Child feature may or may not be selected. 3. **Alternative** (single arc): Exactly one child from a group must be selected. 4. **OR** (filled arc): One or more children from a group must be selected. --- --- ## 📝 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **Q: (a)** Explain the concept of "Feature-Oriented Domain Analysis (FODA)" and describe how features are modeled in automotive software engineering. Use a suitable example with at least 3 relationships. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the AUTOSAR layered architecture. Discuss the role of the **Run-Time Environment (RTE)** in mapping software components to ECUs. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **FODA** is a method introduced by **Kang et al. (1990)** for analyzing a family of systems in terms of their common and variable features. **Steps of FODA in Automotive Context:** 1. **Domain Analysis**: Identify the domain — Automotive CPS. 2. **Feature Identification**: List all customer-facing features (Cruise Control, ABS, Airbags). 3. **Feature Modeling**: Build a tree with relationships: - Vehicle → Safety (mandatory) - Safety → ABS (mandatory) - Safety → Airbags (alternative: Front / Front+Side / Curtain) - Vehicle → Infotainment (optional) - Infotainment → Apple CarPlay (optional) 4. **Feature Dependency**: Use constraints (e.g., "Lane Assist requires Rear Camera"). **Example Feature Model (3 relationships used):** - Mandatory: Vehicle has Engine - Optional: Vehicle may have Sunroof - Alternative: Transmission → Manual XOR Automatic **Importance:** - Captures **commonality** (reusable across variants). - Captures **variability** (different models). - Enables **mass customization**. *(Mark split: FODA definition 2, steps 2, example with 3 relationships 2, importance 1)* #### **Model Solution for (b):** **AUTOSAR Architecture** has three main software layers and a hardware layer: **Layer 1: Application Layer** - Contains **Software Components (SWCs)** that implement vehicle features. - Each SWC has **Ports** and **Runnables**. **Layer 2: Run-Time Environment (RTE)** - Acts as the **middleware** between SWCs and the underlying layers. - Implements the **virtual integration bus** concept. - Handles inter-SWC communication (sender-receiver, client-server). **Layer 3: Basic Software (BSW)** - **Service Layer**: OS, memory services, communication services. - **ECU Abstraction Layer**: Drivers abstraction. - **Microcontroller Abstraction Layer**: Direct hardware drivers. **Layer 4: Hardware (ECU)** - Physical processor, memory, peripherals. **Role of RTE in ECU Mapping:** - The RTE abstracts the **physical location** of SWCs. - A SWC on ECU-1 can communicate with a SWC on ECU-2 *as if they were local*. - RTE handles **bus protocol translation** (CAN, FlexRay, Ethernet). - During deployment, the system integrator generates RTE configuration per ECU, making **ECU extraction** straightforward. *(Mark split: Diagram 2, 4 layers described 3, RTE role 2)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Alternative] **Q: (a)** Discuss the step-by-step transformation process from a customer **Feature** to an **Automotive Software Component**. Use the feature "Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)" as a running example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A feature model for an SUV has the following: Powertrain (mandatory) with Engine Type as an alternative group of 4 options; Comfort (optional) with 2 optional children; and Safety (mandatory) with 2 mandatory children. Calculate the total number of valid vehicle configurations. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1: Feature Elicitation** AEB = Automatic Emergency Braking. Customer feature: "If a collision is imminent, the car brakes automatically." **Step 2: Feature Refinement to Use Cases** - UC1: Detect obstacle ahead - UC2: Estimate Time-To-Collision (TTC) - UC3: Decide braking action - UC4: Actuate brakes **Step 3: Logical Architecture** - AEB Controller component orchestrates 4 child components. **Step 4: Atomic SWC Design** - `RadarPreprocessing_SWC` (Runnable: `filterRadar_10ms`) - `CameraFusion_SWC` (Runnable: `fuseObjects_20ms`) - `TTC_Calculator_SWC` (Runnable: `computeTTC_10ms`) - `BrakeActuator_SWC` (Runnable: `applyBrake_5ms`) **Step 5: Port and Interface Definition** - P-Port: `BrakeCommand` (sender) - R-Port: `RadarObjects`, `CameraObjects` (receivers) - Client-Server: `AEB_Request` (for system-level activation) **Step 6: ECU Deployment** - Radar ECU, Camera ECU, ADAS ECU, Brake ECU. - RTE generated for inter-ECU communication via CAN-FD or FlexRay. **Step 7: Validation & ISO 26262 Compliance** - AEB is ASIL-D rated; each SWC must pass unit tests at appropriate ASIL. *(Mark split: 1 mark per step, 1 mark for ASIL mention)* #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - Powertrain: mandatory, Engine Type = alternative of 4 - Comfort: optional, 2 children optional - Safety: mandatory, 2 children mandatory **Step 1: Mandatory Paths** $$ N_{mandatory} = 1 \times 1 = 1 $$ **Step 2: Engine Alternative** $$ N_{engine} = \binom{4}{1} = 4 $$ **Step 3: Safety Mandatory Children (already counted in $N_{mandatory}$)** **Step 4: Comfort Optional Choices** If Comfort is absent: 1 way. If Comfort is present: each of 2 children can be present or absent = $2 \times 2 = 4$ ways. $$ N_{comfort} = 1 + 4 = 5 \text{ ways} $$ **Step 5: Total Configurations** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{total} &= N_{mandatory} \times N_{engine} \times N_{comfort} \\ &= 1 \times 4 \times 5 \\ &= 20 \text{ configurations} \end{aligned} $$ *(Mark split: Mandatory 1, alternative 2, optional calculation 2, multiplication 1, final answer 1)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Feature with Use Case**: Students often define "feature" as a "use case." A feature is the *what*, a use case is the *how* of interaction. > 2. **Wrong Alternative Formula**: Using $2^n$ instead of $\binom{n}{1}$ for alternative groups. Alternative = **pick exactly one**, OR = **pick one or more**. > 3. **Forgetting Optional "Absent" Case**: When counting optional subtrees, students forget that *not selecting* is itself a valid choice. > 4. **Confusing P-Port vs R-Port**: P-Port = Sender (Producer); R-Port = Receiver. This is a **2-mark loss** in many valuation keys. > 5. **RTE Misconception**: RTE is **not** an OS; it is a **communication middleware** that abstracts inter-ECU messaging. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Feature** = Customer-visible behavior (problem space). **SWC** = Engineering implementation (solution space). - The pipeline is: **Feature → Use Case → Logical Component → Atomic SWC → Runnable → ECU**. - **Feature Model** has 4 relationships: **Mandatory (●), Optional (○), Alternative, OR**. - Combination formula: **Mandatory = 1 way; Optional = $2^k$ ways; Alternative = $\binom{n}{1}$ ways; OR = $2^n - 1$ ways**. - **AUTOSAR** = AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture. Has **4 layers**: Application, RTE, BSW (Service + ECU Abstraction + MCAL), Hardware. - **RTE** is the **middleware** that abstracts SWC location; it implements the *virtual functional bus*. - **Ports**: P-Port (Sender/Producer), R-Port (Receiver), and Client-Server ports. - **Runnables** are the actual C-functions triggered by events (timing, data, operation invocation). - **ISO 26262 ASIL levels** (A–D) must be considered when designing SWCs for safety features. - **Modern vehicles** contain **70–150 ECUs** and ~**100 million LOC** — feature-to-component mapping is essential for managing this complexity. - **Variant management** and **OTA updates** are the two biggest engineering drivers of the feature-component paradigm. - Always remember: **one feature → many SWCs**, but **one SWC → may contribute to many features** (reusability).

topics.title: Mapping Software Components to ECUs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mapping Software Components to ECUs ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the domain of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), and more specifically in **Automotive Embedded Systems** governed by the **AUTOSAR (AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture)** standard, the term **Mapping Software Components to ECUs** refers to the systematic process of allocating atomic, reusable **Software Components (SWCs)** onto specific **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** within a distributed in-vehicle network architecture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Software Component (SWC):** A modular, atomic software unit (defined in AUTOSAR as a *VFB - Virtual Functional Bus* element) that encapsulates a specific application function. An SWC has well-defined **Ports** (Required or Provided), internal behavior (Runnables), and interacts with other SWCs strictly through the **RTE (Run-Time Environment)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electronic Control Unit (ECU):** A physically distributed embedded microcontroller node containing a processor (µC/µP), memory (ROM/**128 KB to 8 MB** Flash, **32 KB to 4 MB** RAM), communication controllers (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Ethernet), and I/O drivers. A modern premium vehicle contains **70 to 150 ECUs**. **Mapping** is therefore the design-time decision that binds a logical software function to physical hardware resources, subject to computational, memory, communication, timing, safety, and security constraints. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Building a City** > Imagine you are a **city planner** tasked with placing different **businesses** (Software Components) into the right **buildings** (ECUs) across a city. > > - A **Bakery** (Engine SWC) needs a building near the **flour warehouse** (Sensor) and the **fuel station** (Actuator). It generates heat and needs a **ventilated, high-power** location. > - A **Bank** (Cryptographic SWC) needs a **high-security** building with thick walls (Hardware Security Module / TPM). > - A **News Station** (Infotainment SWC) needs **fast internet** (High-bandwidth Ethernet) and a **large display** (GPU). > > You cannot place a Bakery in a Bank (resource mismatch). You also cannot put two Bakeries in the same small shop (capacity overflow). The job of the CPS architect is exactly this: **decide which business goes into which building, why, and at what communication/timing cost**. ### 1.3 Why Mapping is a Critical CPS Problem Modern vehicles are a federation of **Distributed Real-Time Embedded Systems**. The same CPS principles apply to industrial robots, avionics (DO-178C), and IoT edge networks. Mapping is critical because: 1. **Resource Heterogeneity** — ECUs have varying CPU speeds (e.g., **8-bit** body controllers vs **32-bit/64-bit** domain controllers). 2. **Safety Isolation** — ASIL-D (ISO 26262) functions (e.g., braking) must not share a processor with QM functions (e.g., seat adjustment) without partitioning. 3. **Communication Cost** — Cross-ECU communication over CAN (typically **500 kbps**) is **1000× slower** and more expensive than intra-ECU RTE calls. 4. **Timing Determinism** — End-to-end latency budgets (typically **1 ms to 100 ms**) must be met. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The AUTOSAR Software Architecture Context Mapping operates within the **AUTOSAR Layered Architecture**, which decouples the application from the hardware: | Layer (Top → Bottom) | Role | Mapping Relevance | |---|---|---| | **Application Layer** | Software Components (SWCs) — *what the system does* | These are the **objects being mapped** | | **Runtime Environment (RTE)** | Virtual Function Bus (VFB) abstraction — *middleware* | Generated *per ECU* after mapping | | **Basic Software (BSW)** | OS, Services, ECU Abstraction, MCAL | ECU-specific support code | | **Microcontroller Abstraction Layer (MCAL)** | Hardware register drivers | Bound 1:1 to the ECU hardware | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A Software Component is *hardware-independent*. Its code does not know which ECU it will run on. The mapping decision is committed **after** software design and is enforced at system generation time by tools like **Vector DaVinci**, **ETAS ASCET**, or **Elektrobit EB tresos**. ### 2.2 Types of Software Components (SWCs) | SWC Type | Function | Typical Mapping Target | |---|---|---| | **Application SWC** | Implements vehicle features (e.g., Cruise Control) | Domain / High-Performance ECUs | | **Sensor-Actuator SWC** | Interfaces directly with hardware | The ECU physically connected to the sensor | | **Composition SWC** | Aggregates other SWCs hierarchically | High-resource ECUs | | **Service SWC** | Provides AUTOSAR services (DCM, NvM) | Gateway / Central ECUs | | **Complex Device Driver (CDD)** | Bypasses AUTOSAR for tight hardware access | Same ECU as the target hardware | ### 2.3 ECU Classification (Hardware Tiers) | ECU Class | Processor | Memory (Flash/RAM) | Typical Use-Case | |---|---|---|---| | **8-bit Microcontroller ECU** | 8-bit µC | < 64 KB / < 4 KB | Body control (window, mirror) | | **16-bit ECU** | 16-bit µC | 64–256 KB / 4–16 KB | Sensor clusters, simple actuators | | **32-bit Automotive MCU** | ARM Cortex-R/M, RH850 | 1–8 MB / 128 KB–1 MB | Powertrain, Chassis, ADAS | | **High-Performance Domain ECU** | ARM Cortex-A (multi-core) | > 4 GB / > 1 GB | Infotainment, ADAS domain | | **Central/Zonal ECU** | Multi-core SoC | > 8 GB / > 2 GB | Vehicle Computer, Gateway | ### 2.4 The Mapping Process (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **Build the System Model** — Enumerate all SWCs ($n$ total) and their inter-connections (signals/sender-receiver, client-server). 2. **Enumerate Available ECUs** — Define $m$ ECUs with capacity vectors $(CPU_i, RAM_i, Flash_i, Comm_i)$. 3. **Define Constraints**: - **Hard Constraints (must satisfy):** safety isolation (ASIL), hardware proximity (an SWC using a physical sensor must be on the ECU connected to that sensor), OEM-mandated placement. - **Soft Constraints (optimize):** communication cost, memory fragmentation, thermal load. 4. **Formulate the Cost Function** — Quantify total system cost. 5. **Solve the Optimization** — Using ILP, heuristics (genetic algorithms, greedy), or rule-based assignment. 6. **Validate via Simulation** — Check timing, load, and communication bus utilization. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formulas for ECU Mapping Optimization** | Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression | |---|---|---| | $x_{ij}$ | Binary mapping variable | $x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$: **1** if SWC $i$ is on ECU $j$, **0** otherwise | | $n, m$ | Number of SWCs, Number of ECUs | $n \in [50, 5000]$, $m \in [10, 150]$ (premium vehicle) | | $c_{ij}$ | Mapping cost of SWC $i$ on ECU $j$ | $c_{ij} = w_1 C^{comp}_{ij} + w_2 C^{mem}_{ij} + w_3 C^{safety}_{ij}$ | | $C^{comm}$ | Cross-ECU communication cost | $C^{comm} = \sum_{(i,k) \in E} d_{ik} \cdot \left(1 - \sum_{j} x_{ij} x_{kj}\right)$ | | $d_{ik}$ | Data exchange between SWCs $i$ and $k$ | Bytes/sec, messages/sec, or signal occurrences | | $T^{E2E}_i$ | End-to-end latency for SWC $i$ | $T^{E2E}_i \le D_i$ (deadline) | | $U^{CPU}_j$ | CPU utilization of ECU $j$ | $U^{CPU}_j = \sum_{i} x_{ij} \cdot u_i \le 1.0$ | | $M^{RAM}_j$ | RAM usage of ECU $j$ | $M^{RAM}_j = \sum_{i} x_{ij} \cdot r_i \le RAM^{max}_j$ | | $\Phi$ | Objective function | $\Phi = \min \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} x_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}$ | | $AS_i$ | ASIL rating of SWC $i$ | $AS_i \in \{QM, A, B, C, D\}$ (ISO 26262) | > **Weights** $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are tuning parameters: typically $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$, with $w_3$ (safety) given priority scaling. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Mapping decisions directly determine the **Bill of Materials (BOM) cost**, **wiring harness weight** (critical for EV range), **vehicle E/E architecture** (Centralized vs Distributed), and **functional safety certification** feasibility. A poor mapping leads to bus overload (CAN Error Frames), deadline misses, and costly ECUs (e.g., 32-bit where 16-bit suffices). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Formal Integer Linear Programming (ILP) Formulation The **Mapping Problem** is canonically modeled as a **Binary Integer Programming (BIP)** problem. We derive each component explicitly. --- #### **Step 1: Define the Decision Variable** We introduce a binary decision variable for every possible SWC–ECU pair: $$x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if Software Component } i \text{ is allocated to ECU } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ The total number of decision variables is $n \times m$. --- #### **Step 2: Formulate the Objective Function (Cost to Minimize)** The total system cost $\Phi$ is the sum of mapping cost and cross-ECU communication cost. **Component A — Static Mapping Cost:** $$\Phi^{map} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}$$ **Component B — Cross-ECU Communication Cost:** Two SWCs $i$ and $k$ communicate *without* a bus penalty **iff** they share the same ECU. Define a co-location indicator: $$\delta_{ikj} = x_{ij} \cdot x_{kj}$$ If both are on ECU $j$, $\delta_{ikj} = 1$, meaning no bus cost for that pair on that ECU. Therefore, the *cross-ECU* data exchange cost is: $$\Phi^{comm} = \sum_{(i,k) \in E} d_{ik} \cdot \left(1 - \sum_{j=1}^{m} x_{ij} \cdot x_{kj}\right)$$ **Combined Objective:** $$\Phi = \min \left[ \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij} + \beta \sum_{(i,k) \in E} d_{ik} \left(1 - \sum_{j=1}^{m} x_{ij} x_{kj}\right) \right]$$ where $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}^{\ge 0}$ are weighting factors, and $E$ is the set of communicating SWC pairs. --- #### **Step 3: Hard Constraints (Must Hold)** **Constraint 1 — Each SWC must be assigned to exactly one ECU:** $$\sum_{j=1}^{m} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$ **Constraint 2 — ECU CPU utilization must not exceed 100%:** $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i \cdot x_{ij} \le U^{max}_j \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$$ where $u_i$ is the CPU load fraction required by SWC $i$ (e.g., $u_i = 0.15$ means 15% of one core). **Constraint 3 — ECU RAM must not be exceeded:** $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i \cdot x_{ij} \le R^{max}_j \quad \forall j$$ **Constraint 4 — Safety Co-location (ISO 26262):** An ASIL-D SWC cannot share an MCU with an ASIL-A SWC unless hardware isolation (e.g., lockstep cores, MPU partitions) is proven. For mixed-ASIL ECUs (without proven freedom from interference): $$x_{ij} + x_{kj} \le 1 \quad \text{if } AS_i = D \text{ and } AS_k = A \text{ and ECU } j \text{ is mixed-criticality}$$ **Constraint 5 — Hardware Affinity:** An SWC that drives a physical sensor must be on the ECU wired to that sensor. $$x_{i,j^*} = 1 \quad \text{if SWC } i \text{ requires hardware on ECU } j^*$$ --- #### **Step 4: Worked Numerical Example** **Problem Statement:** Map 3 SWCs (Engine Ctrl, Brake Ctrl, Display) onto 2 ECUs (Powertrain ECU, Body ECU). **Given Data:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | $c_{11}$ (Engine on Powertrain) | **2 units** | | $c_{12}$ (Engine on Body) | **8 units** (unsafe, wrong hardware) | | $c_{21}$ (Brake on Powertrain) | **3 units** | | $c_{22}$ (Brake on Body) | **9 units** (unsafe) | | $c_{31}$ (Display on Powertrain) | **7 units** (resource mismatch) | | $c_{32}$ (Display on Body) | **1 unit** | | Communication $d_{13}$ | **5 units** (Engine talks to Display) | | $\alpha = 1, \beta = 1$ | Equal weighting | **Solution by Enumeration (4 possible assignments):** We need each SWC on exactly one ECU. Total assignments = $2^3 = 8$, but with constraints only **valid** ones are: | Assignment | Engine | Brake | Display | $\Phi^{map}$ | $\Phi^{comm}$ | $\Phi$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Powertrain | Powertrain | Body | 2+3+1=**6** | Engine–Display cross: 5 | **11** | | 2 | Powertrain | Body | Body | 2+9+1=**12** | Brake–Display cross: 0; Engine–Display cross: 5 | **17** | | 3 | Body | Powertrain | Body | 8+3+1=**12** | Engine–Display on same ECU: 0 | **12** | | 4 | Powertrain | Powertrain | Powertrain | 2+3+7=**12** | Engine–Display on same ECU: 0 | **12** | **Optimal Solution: Assignment 1** ($\Phi = 11$) - Engine SWC → Powertrain ECU ✓ - Brake SWC → Powertrain ECU ✓ - Display SWC → Body ECU ✓ > [!NOTE] > Even though Engine and Display communicate 5 units of data, splitting them across ECUs is still cheaper (cost 6) than forcing Display onto the (overloaded) Powertrain ECU (cost 12). The mapping tool must evaluate the full objective. --- #### **Step 5: Analytical Matrix Summary (Mapping Decision Factors)** | Factor | Direction of Preference | Metric | |---|---|---| | Communication volume | Keep heavy talkers on same ECU | Bytes/sec between SWC pairs | | Safety class (ASIL) | Isolate ASIL-D on dedicated ECU | ISO 26262 decomposition | | Real-time deadline | Place near sensors to minimize $T^{E2E}$ | µs / ms budget | | Memory footprint | Match SWC $r_i$ to ECU $R^{max}_j$ | KB / MB | | Reusability | Same SWC across variants on flexible ECU | OEM platform strategy | | Power/thermal | Spread load on multi-core SoCs | Watts, junction T | --- ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *Define a Software Component (SWC) in the AUTOSAR context. List any two types of SWCs.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Software Component (SWC)** is an atomic, modular, reusable software unit in the AUTOSAR Application Layer that encapsulates a specific vehicle function. It communicates with other SWCs only through well-defined **Ports** (Sender/Receiver, Client/Server) over the **Virtual Functional Bus (VFB)**. *(2 marks)* Two types: (1) **Application SWC** – implements vehicle features like window control. (2) **Sensor-Actuator SWC** – directly interfaces with ECU hardware sensors/actuators. *(1 mark)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *What is meant by "mapping" software components to ECUs? Why is it a non-trivial design decision?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Mapping** is the design-time allocation of AUTOSAR Software Components to physical ECUs in a vehicle's distributed E/E architecture. *(1 mark)* It is non-trivial because: (i) ECUs have **heterogeneous resources** (CPU, RAM, Flash); (ii) **Safety (ASIL) constraints** under ISO 26262 require isolation; (iii) **Communication cost** is high across buses (CAN, FlexRay) versus zero within an ECU; (iv) **Real-time deadlines** must be respected; (v) It directly affects **BOM cost, wiring weight, and reliability**. *(2 marks)* --- ### **Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]* **(a)** Explain the AUTOSAR layered architecture with a neat diagram. How does the concept of the **Virtual Functional Bus (VFB)** decouple software components from ECU hardware? *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Discuss the role of **ISO 26262 ASIL classification** in the mapping decision. Illustrate with an example why co-locating an ASIL-D and an ASIL-A software component on the same ECU without proper hardware isolation is unsafe. *(7 Marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **(a) AUTOSAR Layered Architecture & VFB Decoupling** *(7 marks)* The AUTOSAR architecture is divided into three primary software layers running on a single ECU: **Application Layer, Runtime Environment (RTE), and Basic Software (BSW)**. 1. **Application Layer:** Contains all **Software Components (SWCs)** — atomic units implementing vehicle functions. They are *pure* functions with no direct hardware access. *(1 mark)* 2. **Runtime Environment (RTE):** Acts as the **Virtual Functional Bus (VFB)** realized *per ECU*. It implements inter-SWC communication (Sender-Receiver, Client-Server) and SWC-to-BSW service calls. *(1 mark)* 3. **Basic Software (BSW):** Subdivided into: - **Services Layer** — OS, NvM (Non-Volatile Memory Manager), DCM (Diagnostics). - **ECU Abstraction Layer** — abstracts MCAL from application. - **Microcontroller Abstraction Layer (MCAL)** — direct register-level drivers. *(1 mark)* **Diagram (1 mark):** ``` +-----------------------------+ | Application Layer | (SWCs) +-----------------------------+ | Runtime Environment RTE | (VFB realized) +-----------------------------+ | Services (OS, NvM, COM) | | ECU Abstraction | Basic Software | MCAL | +-----------------------------+ | Microcontroller Hardware | +-----------------------------+ ``` **VFB Decoupling (3 marks):** The VFB is a *virtual* communication medium where SWCs connect via **Ports** as if all SWCs were on a single processor. The RTE *realizes* this abstraction on each ECU — converting inter-SWC port connections into either: - **Intra-ECU function calls** (if both SWCs are mapped to the same ECU), or - **Bus messages** (CAN, FlexRay, Ethernet) if mapped to different ECUs. This means **the same SWC source code can be re-mapped to a different ECU without code changes** — only the system configuration XML and the generated RTE differ. This is the *key* decoupling benefit of AUTOSAR. --- **(b) ASIL and Mapping Safety** *(7 marks)* **ISO 26262** defines four Automotive Safety Integrity Levels: **ASIL A, B, C, D**, with D being the most stringent (lowest tolerable failure rate, e.g., $<10^{-8}$/hr for brake-by-wire). QM denotes non-safety functions. *(2 marks)* **Example Scenario:** Consider a vehicle with two SWCs: - **BrakeCtrl** (ASIL-D) — controls hydraulic brake actuators. - **SeatAdjuster** (ASIL-A or QM) — adjusts the driver seat. **Why co-location is unsafe without isolation:** *(4 marks)* 1. **Cascading Failures:** A software bug or memory corruption in SeatAdjuster (e.g., a stray pointer writing beyond an array) could overwrite BrakeCtrl's memory space, causing **unintended braking or loss of braking** — a life-critical hazard. 2. **Shared Resource Interference:** Both SWCs share the same CPU. A compute spike in SeatAdjuster (e.g., during a self-test routine) could starve BrakeCtrl of CPU cycles, causing it to **miss its 10 ms control loop deadline** — brake lag. 3. **Common-Cause Failure:** Both run on the same silicon. A hardware fault (e.g., voltage glitch, SEU bit-flip) affects both simultaneously. 4. **Lack of Freedom from Interference (FFI):** ISO 26262 Part 6 requires **spatial and temporal partitioning** for mixed-criticality software. A single-core MCU with no Memory Protection Unit (MPU) cannot guarantee FFI. **Safe Mapping:** Place BrakeCtrl on a **dedicated, lockstep-core ASIL-D capable MCU** (e.g., Infineon AURIX TC39x with lockstep), and SeatAdjuster on a cheaper **body-domain MCU**. The mapping tool must enforce this as a **hard constraint** $x_{ij} + x_{kj} \le 1$ for mixed-ASIL pairs on non-isolated ECUs. *(1 mark)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]* **(a)** Formulate the **ECU mapping problem as an Integer Linear Program (ILP)**. Clearly define the decision variable, objective function, and at least three hard constraints. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A vehicle has 3 Software Components (SWC-A, SWC-B, SWC-C) and 2 ECUs (ECU-1, ECU-2). The communication data rates are: $d_{AB} = 10$, $d_{AC} = 4$, $d_{BC} = 6$ (in KB/s). The mapping cost matrix $c_{ij}$ is given below: | | ECU-1 | ECU-2 | |---|---|---| | SWC-A | 5 | 12 | | SWC-B | 4 | 10 | | SWC-C | 8 | 3 | Take $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 1$. Find the optimal mapping that minimizes total cost. *(7 Marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **(a) ILP Formulation** *(7 marks)* **Decision Variable:** *(1 mark)* $$x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n; \quad j = 1, \ldots, m$$ $x_{ij} = 1$ if SWC $i$ is mapped to ECU $j$, else 0. **Objective Function:** *(2 marks)* $$\Phi = \min \left[ \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{m} c_{ij} x_{ij} + \beta \sum_{(i,k) \in E} d_{ik}\left(1 - \sum_{j=1}^{m} x_{ij} x_{kj}\right) \right]$$ **Hard Constraints:** *(4 marks)* 1. **Single Assignment:** $\sum_{j=1}^{m} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall i$ — each SWC on exactly one ECU. 2. **CPU Capacity:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i x_{ij} \le U^{max}_j, \quad \forall j$ — no CPU overload. 3. **Memory Capacity:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i x_{ij} \le R^{max}_j, \quad \forall j$ — RAM not exceeded. 4. **Safety Isolation (Optional 4th):** $x_{ij} + x_{kj} \le 1$ for mixed-ASIL pairs on non-isolated ECUs. --- **(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)* We enumerate the $2^3 = 8$ possible assignments, eliminating those violating constraints (assuming ECU capacities permit all — a reasonable assumption for this small problem). | Assignment | A | B | C | $\Phi^{map}$ | Cross-ECU Communication | $\Phi^{comm}$ | $\Phi$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | ECU-1 | ECU-1 | ECU-1 | 5+4+8 = 17 | None | 0 | **17** | | 2 | ECU-1 | ECU-1 | ECU-2 | 5+4+3 = 12 | $d_{AC}$ = 4, $d_{BC}$ = 6 | 10 | **22** | | 3 | ECU-1 | ECU-2 | ECU-1 | 5+10+8 = 23 | $d_{BC}$ = 6 | 6 | **29** | | 4 | ECU-1 | ECU-2 | ECU-2 | 5+10+3 = 18 | $d_{AB}$ = 10 | 10 | **28** | | 5 | ECU-2 | ECU-1 | ECU-1 | 12+4+8 = 24 | $d_{AB}$ = 10 | 10 | **34** | | 6 | ECU-2 | ECU-1 | ECU-2 | 12+4+3 = 19 | $d_{AC}$ = 4 | 4 | **23** | | 7 | ECU-2 | ECU-2 | ECU-1 | 12+10+8 = 30 | $d_{BC}$ = 6 | 6 | **36** | | 8 | ECU-2 | ECU-2 | ECU-2 | 12+10+3 = 25 | None | 0 | **25** | **Optimal Mapping: Assignment 1** — All SWCs on ECU-1, with $\Phi = 17$. *(1 mark)* **Interpretation:** The communication savings (avoiding 20 KB/s of cross-bus traffic) outweigh the slightly higher mapping cost of placing SWC-C on ECU-1 (cost 8 vs 3). However, in a real vehicle, **Assignment 1 would fail the ECU-1 CPU capacity constraint** if $u_A + u_B + u_C > U^{max}_1$, forcing a split. *(1 mark for real-world comment)* --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones:** > 1. **Confusing "Mapping" with "Port Connection":** Students often write about connecting SWC ports, which is a *logical* design activity. **Mapping is the physical allocation to an ECU** — the next step. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this. > 2. **Forgetting the VFB concept:** Any answer on AUTOSAR mapping that omits the **Virtual Functional Bus** and **RTE** will lose at least 2 marks. > 3. **Skipping safety/ISO 26262:** A mapping question without ASIL discussion is considered incomplete. Always mention **freedom from interference (FFI)**. > 4. **Writing cost in ILP without explaining units:** Specify that $c_{ij}$ is in *normalized cost units* and $d_{ik}$ is in *bytes/sec*. > 5. **Not verifying constraints in numerical problems:** Just writing the objective value without checking CPU/RAM feasibility in real-world scenario — examiner's pet peeve. > 6. **Ignoring the difference between intra-ECU and inter-ECU communication costs:** Intra-ECU ≈ **0** (function call), inter-ECU = bus latency + serialization. Quantify both. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - **Mapping** = allocating AUTOSAR **Software Components (SWCs)** to physical **ECUs**. - AUTOSAR = **AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture**; layered: **Application → RTE → BSW (Services / ECU Abstraction / MCAL) → Hardware**. - **VFB (Virtual Functional Bus)** = abstraction; **RTE** = its per-ECU realization. - **SWC types:** Application, Sensor-Actuator, Composition, Service, **Complex Device Driver (CDD)**. - **ECU classes:** 8-bit → 16-bit → 32-bit (Cortex-R, RH850) → 64-bit multi-core (Cortex-A domain controllers). - **Decision variable:** $x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$. - **Objective:** $\Phi = \min [\alpha \sum c_{ij} x_{ij} + \beta \sum d_{ik}(1 - \sum x_{ij}x_{kj})]$. - **Hard constraints:** single assignment, CPU/RAM capacity, ASIL isolation, hardware affinity. - **ISO 26262 ASIL** levels: **QM, A, B, C, D**; D is most stringent. - **Mixed-criticality** SWCs on same ECU require **Freedom from Interference (FFI)** via MPU/MMU/lockstep cores. - **Tools:** Vector DaVinci, ETAS ASCET, EB tresos, Artop. - **Communication buses & typical speeds:** LIN **~20 kbps**, CAN **125 kbps–1 Mbps**, FlexRay **up to 10 Mbps**, Automotive Ethernet **100 Mbps–1 Gbps**. - Modern trend: **Centralized / zonal E/E architectures** are reducing ECU count by merging software onto high-performance domain controllers. - **Mapping directly impacts:** BOM cost, wiring harness weight, real-time latency, functional safety certification, scalability, and OTA update complexity. ---

topics.title: CPS Performance Analysis: Effect of Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CPS Performance Analysis: Effect of Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), **Scheduling** refers to the deterministic assignment of computational tasks (sensing, computation, and actuation jobs) to the available processing resources such that the system's real-time correctness properties are preserved. **CPS Performance Analysis: Effect of Scheduling** is the analytical study of how a chosen scheduling policy (Rate Monotonic, Earliest Deadline First, Least Laxity First, Deadline Monotonic, etc.) influences the temporal correctness, processor utilization, and deadline-meeting capability of the embedded real-time controller within the CPS feedback loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Paraphrased):**
> Performance of a CPS is largely governed by the scheduling algorithm running on its embedded controller. Different scheduling policies yield different *schedulability bounds*, *worst-case response times*, *jitter*, and *overhead* — all of which directly impact control-loop stability, latency, and quality-of-control (QoC).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **busy toll-plaza** with several toll booths (the CPU/processor) and a stream of cars arriving at random times (the real-time tasks). Each car has a deadline — for instance, an ambulance must reach the hospital within 10 minutes. The **plaza manager's policy** (the scheduling algorithm) decides which car is sent to which booth and when.

- If the manager always picks the **shortest car first** (like *Rate Monotonic* picks the most frequent task first), small cars (periodic short tasks) breeze through, but occasionally an ambulance (long-period critical task) is delayed.
- If the manager picks the **car with the closest hospital deadline** (like *Earliest Deadline First*), the most urgent cars always get through, maximizing the plaza's throughput — but the manager has to constantly check deadlines, which adds overhead.
- If the manager picks the **car with the least spare time** (like *Least Laxity First*), he balances urgency and length — but small, almost-finished cars may starve.

This trade-off is exactly what *Effect of Scheduling* on CPS performance captures: **how the choice of dispatch policy changes the system's ability to guarantee deadlines, the processor's efficiency, and the stability of the physical process being controlled.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics used in KTU Evaluation:**
> - **Processor Utilization ($U$):** Ratio of busy time to total time. Unitless, range [0, 1].
> - **Worst-Case Response Time ($R_i$):** Maximum time from task release to completion. Measured in **seconds (s)** or **milliseconds (ms)**.
> - **Schedulability Bound ($U_b$):** Theoretical maximum utilization below which a scheduling algorithm guarantees no deadline misses.
> - **Jitter ($J_i$):** Variation in actual response time between successive jobs.

## 1.3 Why Scheduling Matters in CPS

CPS are **time-critical** because they close a feedback loop with the physical world. A delayed control action may cause:
- Loss of synchronization in robotic arms
- Missed braking deadlines in autonomous vehicles
- Inaccurate insulin delivery in medical devices

Hence, the scheduling algorithm is **not merely a software concern — it is a first-class design parameter of the physical system's safety contract.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Task Model Assumed in CPS Scheduling Analysis

The standard Liu & Layland (1973) task model assumed throughout this topic:

- A finite set $\tau = \{\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_n\}$ of **$n$ periodic, independent tasks**.
- Each task $\tau_i$ is characterized by a triplet $(T_i, D_i, C_i)$:
  - $T_i$ = Period (release interval), in seconds.
  - $D_i$ = Relative deadline, in seconds. For implicit-deadline systems, $D_i = T_i$.
  - $C_i$ = Worst-case execution time (WCET), in seconds.
- All tasks are ready at time $t = 0$ (synchronous release, the *critical instant*).
- Preemption is allowed at no cost; context-switch overhead is ignored.
- No shared resources / no inter-task blocking (basic model).

## 2.2 Classification of Scheduling Algorithms

### A. Static / Fixed Priority
- **Rate Monotonic (RM):** Priority $\propto 1/T_i$ (shorter period $\Rightarrow$ higher priority).
- **Deadline Monotonic (DM):** Priority $\propto 1/D_i$ (shorter deadline $\Rightarrow$ higher priority).

### B. Dynamic Priority
- **Earliest Deadline First (EDF):** At any instant, run the ready job with the earliest absolute deadline.
- **Least Laxity First (LLF):** Run the job with minimum $(D_i - \text{remaining execution} - \text{current time})$.

### C. Hybrid / Multiprocessor
- **Partitioned EDF, Global EDF, P-Fair, RUN, U-EDF** — relevant for multi-core CPS platforms.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU 2024 ESE / SEE. Memorize the bolded ones.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Processor Utilization of task $\tau_i$ | $U_i = C_i \,/\, T_i$ | dimensionless | Range $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | Total Processor Utilization | $U = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | dimensionless | Critical schedulability metric |
| 3 | **Liu & Layland RM Sufficient Bound** | $U_{\text{RM}}(n) = n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | dimensionless | Lower bound for RM; sufficient (not necessary) |
| 4 | RM Bound (limit, $n \to \infty$) | $U_{\text{RM}}(\infty) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$ | dimensionless | ~**69.31 %** is the asymptotic ceiling |
| 5 | EDF Schedulability (uniprocessor) | $U \le 1$ is sufficient **and** necessary | dimensionless | EDF is optimal on uniprocessor |
| 6 | **Response-Time Equation (RTA)** | $R_i = C_i + \displaystyle\sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | seconds | Solved by fixed-point iteration |
| 7 | Initial guess for RTA | $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ | seconds | Lower bound for iteration |
| 8 | Schedulability under FP | $R_i \le D_i \;\; \forall i$ | — | Fixed-priority sufficient condition |
| 9 | Hyperperiod (LCM of periods) | $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$ | seconds | Length of one full schedule cycle |
| 10 | Deadline Monotonic Bound (asymptotic) | $U_{\text{DM}}(\infty) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$ | dimensionless | Same as RM for implicit deadlines |
| 11 | LLF Sufficient Condition | $U \le 1$ with bounded context switches | — | Optimal but costly at runtime |
| 12 | Laxity (Slack) of a job | $L(t) = D_i - \text{remaining work} - t$ | seconds | Drives LLF dispatch decision |

> **Critical caution:** In any row containing absolute value, write as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$, never with a raw `|`.

## 2.4 Operational Logic — Why Each Formula Matters

- **Why Equation (3) matters:** It gives a *fast, closed-form, O(1)* check. Even before running RTA, a student can declare "this task set passes RM" by simply summing utilizations and comparing with the bound.
- **Why Equation (5) matters:** EDF saturates the processor, but at the cost of higher runtime overhead (deadline recomputation, priority queueing). The trade-off is the *classic CPS design dilemma*.
- **Why Equation (6) matters:** RTA is the **exact** schedulability test for fixed-priority systems, used when utilization bound (3) is violated but tasks might still be schedulable.
- **Why Equation (9) matters:** In CPS simulations (Simulink, TrueTime), simulation only needs to run for one *hyperperiod* to capture all steady-state behaviour.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Scheduling Analysis |
|--------|----------------------------|
| **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | Schedules OSEK/VDX tasks for engine control, brake-by-wire. |
| **Avionics (DO-178C)** | Schedules ARINC-653 partitions; RM/RMA used for IMA. |
| **Industrial Robotics** | LLF ensures precise motion control with low jitter. |
| **Medical CPS** | DM/RM for pacemakers, infusion pumps (FDA-approved schedulers). |
| **IoT-Edge CPS** | EDF variants on battery-powered MCUs to maximize sleep time. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound

We derive $U_{\text{RM}}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ for $n$ harmonic-free tasks. We use the **critical instant theorem** of Liu & Layland.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption:** Implicit deadlines $D_i = T_i$, no self-suspension, no blocking.

### Step 1 — Define Critical Instant
A *critical instant* for task $\tau_i$ is the instant at which the release of $\tau_i$ produces its largest response time. Liu & Layland proved this occurs when:
- $\tau_i$ is released simultaneously with **all** higher-priority tasks, and
- the higher-priority jobs that interfere are released as **early as allowed by their periods**.

### Step 2 — Counting Interference in $[0, t]$
For a higher-priority task $\tau_j$ with period $T_j$ in the interval $[0, t]$:
- The number of jobs released in $[0, t]$ is $\lceil t / T_j \rceil$.
- Each job consumes at most $C_j$ time.
- Total interference from $\tau_j$ in $[0, t]$: $\lceil t/T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$.

Summing over all higher-priority tasks:

$$
I_i(t) = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

### Step 3 — Worst-Case Response Time Equation
The task $\tau_i$ finishes by time $R_i$ if and only if it receives $C_i$ units of CPU before $R_i$:

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

This is solved iteratively starting from $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ until convergence.

### Step 4 — Specialize to the Worst-Case Task
For RM, the most-loaded task set that **just fails** the bound consists of $n$ tasks where every higher-priority task saturates the processor. The most adversarial configuration is one where all $T_j$ are *just enough* to release an extra job in the deadline window, i.e., $T_j \to R_i$.

Under this worst-case, the bound derivation considers the **latest finishing time** of $\tau_n$ (lowest priority). The sufficient condition becomes:

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 + \frac{C_i}{T_i}\right) \le 2
$$

This is the *Liu & Layland product form* of the schedulability condition.

### Step 5 — From Product Form to Closed-Form Bound
We use the AM–GM inequality: for $n$ positive numbers $a_i$,

$$
\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \;\ge\; \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} a_i\right)^{1/n}
$$

Apply with $a_i = (1 + U_i) = (1 + C_i / T_i)$:

$$
\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (1 + U_i) \;\ge\; \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 + U_i)\right)^{1/n}
$$

Now, from the product form, the boundary is $\prod (1+U_i) = 2$, so the right side becomes $2^{1/n}$. The left side equals $(1 + U_{\text{avg}})$ where $U_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum U_i$. Thus:

$$
1 + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i \;\ge\; 2^{1/n}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i \;\le\; n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

Hence the **Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound**:

$$
\boxed{\,U_{\text{RM}}(n) \;=\; n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)\,}
$$

### Step 6 — Asymptotic Limit
Take the limit as $n \to \infty$. Write $2^{1/n} = e^{(\ln 2)/n}$ and Taylor-expand $e^{x} \approx 1 + x + \tfrac{x^2}{2} + \dots$ for small $x$:

$$
2^{1/n} = 1 + \frac{\ln 2}{n} + O\!\left(\frac{1}{n^2}\right)
$$

Substitute:

$$
U_{\text{RM}}(n) = n \left(\frac{\ln 2}{n} + O\!\left(\frac{1}{n^2}\right)\right) = \ln 2 + O\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{\text{RM}}(n) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931\,}
$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Cross-Check

| $n$ | $U_{\text{RM}}(n)$ | Decimal |
|-----|--------------------|---------|
| 1 | $1 \cdot (2^1 - 1)$ | 1.0000 |
| 2 | $2 \cdot (2^{0.5} - 1)$ | 0.8284 |
| 3 | $3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1)$ | 0.7798 |
| 4 | $4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1)$ | 0.7568 |
| 5 | $5 \cdot (2^{1/5} - 1)$ | 0.7435 |
| $\infty$ | $\ln 2$ | 0.6931 |

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (RTA)

**Task set** (implicit deadline, RM priorities: $\tau_1$ highest):

| Task | $T_i$ (ms) | $C_i$ (ms) | $U_i$ |
|------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| $\tau_1$ | 5 | 1 | 0.200 |
| $\tau_2$ | 10 | 3 | 0.300 |
| $\tau_3$ | 20 | 5 | 0.250 |

Total $U = 0.750$. RM bound for $n=3$ is $0.7798$. Bound is satisfied, so the set is **schedulable under RM**. (This is the quick test.) Let us *also* verify by RTA for educational rigor.

### RTA for $\tau_3$ (lowest priority)

Higher-priority tasks: $\tau_1, \tau_2$.

Iteration:

$$
R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 5
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 5 + 1 + 3 = 9
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 5 + 2 + 3 = 10
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 5 + 2 + 3 = 10
$$

Converged: $R_3 = 10$ ms $\le D_3 = T_3 = 20$ ms. ✅ Task $\tau_3$ is schedulable.

Hence, the complete task set meets all deadlines under RM.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Effect of Scheduling

| Metric | Rate Monotonic (RM) | Earliest Deadline First (EDF) | Least Laxity First (LLF) | Deadline Monotonic (DM) |
|--------|--------------------|-------------------------------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| Priority type | Static (period-based) | Dynamic (deadline-based) | Dynamic (slack-based) | Static (deadline-based) |
| Optimality (uniproc.) | Optimal *among static-priority* | Optimal *overall* | Optimal (with caveats) | Optimal *among static-priority for $D_i \le T_i$* |
| Utilization bound | $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ ≤ **0.6931** | **1.0** | **1.0** (in theory) | $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ ≤ 0.6931 |
| Runtime overhead | **Low** | Moderate (sort deadlines) | **High** (recompute slack) | **Low** |
| Implementation | FreeRTOS, OSEK | Linux SCHED_DEADLINE | Research / niche | VxWorks, ThreadX |
| Suitability for CPS | ✅ Hard real-time control loops | ✅ Mixed-criticality systems | ⚠ Compute-intensive jitter-sensitive | ✅ Constrained deadlines |
| Predictability | High | High | Medium (preemption storms) | High |

## 3.4 Stability Coupling (Why CPS Performance Depends on Scheduling)

A CPS with digital controller having sampling period $T_s$ and computational delay $\tau_d$ has a closed-loop transfer function whose phase margin is degraded by the delay. A higher worst-case response time $R_i$ means a larger $\tau_d$, which reduces phase margin and may destabilize the loop. The *control-theoretic* bound is:

$$
\tau_d \;\le\; \frac{T_s}{\pi} \cdot \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2 \zeta}{\sqrt{\sqrt{1 + 4\zeta^4} - 2\zeta^2}}\right)
$$

where $\zeta$ is the damping ratio. This shows the direct link between scheduling analysis ($R_i$) and CPS *physical* stability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPS Control Loop with Scheduling Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHYS["Physical Plant"]
        sense["Sensors"]
        plant["Plant Dynamics"]
        act["Actuators"]
    end
    subgraph CPS["Cyber / Embedded Controller"]
        s_task["Sensing Task τ_s"]
        c_task["Computation Task τ_c"]
        a_task["Actuation Task τ_a"]
        sched["Scheduler Kernel RM or EDF or LLF"]
        cpu["CPU Processor"]
    end
    sense -->|Analog Signal| s_task
    s_task -->|Periodic Release T_s| sched
    c_task -->|Periodic Release T_c| sched
    a_task -->|Periodic Release T_a| sched
    sched -->|Dispatch Decision| cpu
    cpu --> c_task
    c_task --> a_task
    a_task -->|Control Signal| act
    act --> plant
    plant --> sense
```

> **Reading guide:** Tasks compete for the CPU; the *Scheduler* decides at every tick which task runs. The resulting delay is the *worst-case response time* $R_i$.

## 4.2 RM vs EDF — Comparative Timeline (Gantt-style Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EDFline["EDF Schedule Higher Utilization Allowed"]
        direction LR
        E1["Job 1 of τ1 D=4"] --> E2["Job 1 of τ2 D=7"] --> E3["Job 1 of τ3 D=10"] --> E4["Job 2 of τ1 D=4"]
    end
    subgraph RMline["RM Schedule Fixed Priority by Period"]
        direction LR
        R1["τ1 short T=2 always first"] --> R2["τ2 medium T=5"] --> R3["τ3 long T=10"] --> R4["τ1 again"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Scheduling Decision Pipeline (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Task Release Event"] --> B{"Ready Queue Empty?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Run Newly Released Task"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Scheduler Type?"}
    D -- "RM" --> E["Pick task with smallest period"]
    D -- "EDF" --> F["Pick task with earliest absolute deadline"]
    D -- "LLF" --> G["Compute Laxity D minus remaining minus now"]
    G --> H["Pick task with minimum laxity"]
    E --> I["Dispatch to CPU"]
    F --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Preempt on next release or completion"]
    J --> A
```

## 4.4 Performance Trade-off Spider

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tradeoff["Effect of Scheduling on CPS Performance"]
        direction TB
        axis1["Utilization Bound"] --> axis2["Runtime Overhead"]
        axis2 --> axis3["Worst-Case Response Time"]
        axis3 --> axis4["Jitter"]
        axis4 --> axis5["Schedulability Test Complexity"]
        axis5 --> axis1
    end
```

> **Reading guide:** Improving *Utilization Bound* (e.g., switching RM→EDF) raises *Runtime Overhead*. Lowering *Jitter* (e.g., LLF) increases *Schedulability Test Complexity*. Designers must balance these.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 4]`
**Define the Rate Monotonic scheduling algorithm. State Liu & Layland's utilization bound for $n$ tasks.**

**Model Answer:**
Rate Monotonic (RM) is a static-priority scheduling algorithm in which tasks with **shorter periods are assigned higher priorities**; ties are broken arbitrarily. It is optimal among all fixed-priority algorithms for preemptive, independent, periodic tasks on a uniprocessor (Liu & Layland, 1973). The utilization bound is:

$$
U_{\text{RM}}(n) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \;\le\; n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

For large $n$, this bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$. If the bound is satisfied, the task set is guaranteed to be schedulable. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Module 4]`
**Differentiate between Rate Monotonic and Earliest Deadline First scheduling with respect to priority assignment and utilization bound.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Rate Monotonic | Earliest Deadline First |
|--------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Priority assignment | Static, based on **period** | Dynamic, based on **absolute deadline** |
| Utilisation bound | $n(2^{1/n} - 1) \le 0.6931$ | **1.0** (sufficient and necessary) |
| Runtime overhead | Low (precomputed) | Moderate (sorted deadline queue) |
| Optimality | Optimal among fixed-priority | Optimal overall on uniprocessor |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU allows the student to attempt EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** of the same module.

### `Question A (14 Marks)`

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4, Q. Pattern]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of *critical instant* in fixed-priority scheduling. Derive the **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** equation and hence the **Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound** $U_{\text{RM}}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ for $n$ periodic tasks with implicit deadlines. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Critical Instant Definition (1 mark):** A critical instant of a task $\tau_i$ is the time instant at which a job release of $\tau_i$ yields the largest possible response time. Liu & Layland proved that this occurs when $\tau_i$ is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks, and the higher-priority tasks are then re-released as early as their periods allow.

2. **Interference Counting in $[0, t]$ (2 marks):** Within any interval of length $t$, a higher-priority task $\tau_j$ releases $\lceil t / T_j \rceil$ jobs, each demanding $C_j$ units. Total interference on $\tau_i$:

$$
I_i(t) = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

3. **RTA Equation (2 marks):** Worst-case response time $R_i$ satisfies:

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

Solved by fixed-point iteration with $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$.
