4. **Liu & Layland Product Form (1 mark):** The worst-case low-priority task $\tau_n$ finishes by its deadline iff:

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{n}\left(1 + \frac{C_i}{T_i}\right) \;\le\; 2
$$

5. **AM-GM Application (1 mark):** Applying the arithmetic-mean ≥ geometric-mean inequality to $(1+U_i)$:

$$
1 + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i \;\ge\; \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1+U_i)\right)^{1/n} \;\ge\; 2^{1/n}
$$

Therefore $\sum U_i \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. ∎

**(b)** Consider three tasks with the following parameters:

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) |
|------|-------------------|-----------------|
| $\tau_1$ | 6 | 1 |
| $\tau_2$ | 12 | 3 |
| $\tau_3$ | 24 | 6 |

Using Rate Monotonic priorities, determine whether the task set is schedulable. If not, check schedulability under EDF. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute utilizations (1 mark):**
- $U_1 = 1/6 = 0.1667$
- $U_2 = 3/12 = 0.2500$
- $U_3 = 6/24 = 0.2500$
- Total $U = 0.6667$

**Step 2 — Apply RM bound (1 mark):**
For $n=3$:

$$
U_{\text{RM}}(3) = 3\left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) \approx 0.7798
$$

Since $U = 0.6667 \le 0.7798$, **the bound is satisfied**, hence schedulable under RM. (Sufficient condition — pass.)

**Step 3 — Verify with RTA for $\tau_3$ (3 marks):**
Higher priority set: $hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$.

- $R_3^{(0)} = 6$
- $R_3^{(1)} = 6 + \lceil 6/6\rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 6/12\rceil \cdot 3 = 6 + 1 + 3 = 10$
- $R_3^{(2)} = 6 + \lceil 10/6\rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 10/12\rceil \cdot 3 = 6 + 2 + 3 = 11$
- $R_3^{(3)} = 6 + \lceil 11/6\rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 11/12\rceil \cdot 3 = 6 + 2 + 3 = 11$

Converged: $R_3 = 11$ ms $\le D_3 = 24$ ms. ✅

**Step 4 — EDF check (2 marks):**
For EDF, the sufficient and necessary condition on a uniprocessor is $U \le 1$. Here $U = 0.6667 \le 1$, so the task set is **definitely schedulable under EDF**, with significant headroom. **Conclusion: Task set is schedulable under both RM and EDF**, but RM is preferred in this CPS for lower runtime overhead.

---

### `Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice`

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4, Q. Pattern]`**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, describe how the **scheduling policy** of an embedded controller influences the **performance and stability** of a Cyber-Physical System. Specifically discuss (i) the effect of worst-case response time on control-loop phase margin, and (ii) the role of jitter in quantization of sampled signals. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Worst-Case Response Time vs Phase Margin (4 marks):**
In a sampled-data CPS, the digital controller introduces a computational delay $\tau_d$ bounded above by the worst-case task response time $R_i$. The closed-loop characteristic equation depends on $e^{-s\tau_d}$, which subtracts phase. A larger $R_i \Rightarrow$ larger $\tau_d \Rightarrow$ reduced phase margin $\Rightarrow$ higher overshoot, possible instability. Therefore a *better* scheduler (lower $R_i$) directly improves the stability margin. A *block diagram* would show: [Plant] → [Sampler] → [Scheduler+Controller] → [Zero-Order Hold] → [Plant] with delay $\tau_d = R_i$ inside the loop.

(ii) **Jitter and Sampled Signal Quantization (3 marks):**
Jitter $J_i$ is the variation in $R_i$ between successive jobs of the same task. In the time domain, jitter translates to **non-uniform sampling**, which causes:
- Phase noise in the feedback loop.
- Effective reduction in achievable control bandwidth.
- Distortion equivalent to additive noise on the sampled state.
A *fixed-priority* scheduler (RM/DM) typically has *deterministic* jitter bounded by $R_i^{\max} - R_i^{\min}$, whereas EDF may produce less jitter at high utilizations.

**(b)** A CPS contains three periodic tasks scheduled by **Least Laxity First (LLF)**. The task parameters are:

| Task | $T_i$ | $D_i$ | $C_i$ |
|------|-------|-------|-------|
| $\tau_1$ | 8 | 8 | 2 |
| $\tau_2$ | 12 | 12 | 3 |
| $\tau_3$ | 20 | 20 | 5 |

(i) Show that the task set is schedulable. (ii) Compute the laxity of each task at time $t=4$ assuming all three were released at $t=0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Schedulability check (3 marks):**
Total utilization: $U = 2/8 + 3/12 + 5/20 = 0.250 + 0.250 + 0.250 = 0.750 \le 1$. By the LLF optimality property (when context-switch cost is bounded), the task set is schedulable. Equivalently, simulate in $[0, 20]$:

- $t=0$: laxities $L_1=6, L_2=9, L_3=15$ → run $\tau_1$.
- $t=2$: $\tau_1$ done; $L_2=7, L_3=13$ → run $\tau_2$.
- $t=5$: $\tau_2$ done; $L_1=2$ (since next release at $t=8$? — actually, $\tau_1$ has not been re-released; $\tau_1$ instance releases again at $t=8$). At $t=5$, only $\tau_3$ is ready, so run $\tau_3$.
- Continue… Hyperperiod $H = \text{lcm}(8,12,20) = 120$ ms. Tracing all jobs confirms no deadline is missed.

(ii) **Laxity at $t=4$ (4 marks):**
At $t=4$, the elapsed work assumption: $\tau_1$ completed 2 units at $t=2$, now waiting; $\tau_2$ completed 2 of its 3 units (ran from $t=2$ to $t=4$); $\tau_3$ not yet started.

Remaining work:
- $\tau_1$: 0 (finished), so its *next* job releases at $t=8$ with laxity $D_1 - 0 - 8 = 0$. Laxity of *current* job = N/A.
- $\tau_2$: remaining $= 1$, deadline $D_2 = 12$, current time $t=4$. Laxity = $D_2 - \text{remaining} - t = 12 - 1 - 4 = 7$ ms.
- $\tau_3$: remaining $= 5$, deadline $D_3 = 20$, current time $t=4$. Laxity = $20 - 5 - 4 = 11$ ms.

LLF will dispatch $\tau_2$ (minimum laxity = 7).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing utilization bound with schedulability test:** The bound $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ is *sufficient*, not necessary. A task set with $U > U_{\text{RM}}(n)$ may still be schedulable — confirm by RTA.
> 2. **Iteration loop in RTA:** Many students stop after one iteration. You must iterate **until $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$**.
> 3. **Ceiling function errors:** $\lceil t/T_j \rceil$ counts the *number of interfering jobs in $(0, t]$*, not the *number in $[0,t)$*. Wrong ceiling breaks the bound.
> 4. **Hyperperiod vs Period:** For periodic scheduling, the steady-state schedule repeats every *hyperperiod* $H = \text{lcm}(T_i)$, not at the maximum period.
> 5. **Jitter ≠ Response Time:** Jitter is the *variation* in response time, not the response time itself.
> 6. **LLF is not always optimal in practice** — context switches can make it worse than RM in some scenarios. State assumptions clearly.
> 7. **Implicit vs Constrained vs Arbitrary deadlines:** RM bound $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ only holds for $D_i = T_i$ (implicit). For constrained deadlines, the bound is tighter.
> 8. **Multiprocessor caveat:** $U \le 1$ is **not sufficient** under multiprocessor scheduling. Do not blindly transfer EDF results to multi-core CPS.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Three pillars of CPS scheduling analysis:** (1) Utilization bound (fast), (2) Response-Time Analysis (exact), (3) Simulation over hyperperiod.
- ✅ **Liu & Layland, 1973** is the foundational paper. Know the *year* for KTU exam.
- ✅ **RM bound table to memorize:** $n=1\to 1.0$; $n=2\to 0.828$; $n=3\to 0.780$; $n=4\to 0.757$; $n\to\infty \to 0.693$.
- ✅ **EDF is optimal on uniprocessor** with $U_b = 1$, but has runtime overhead.
- ✅ **Critical Instant Theorem** ⇒ worst-case response occurs at simultaneous release of all higher-priority tasks.
- ✅ **RTA is iterative** — always start at $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ and stop only when stable.
- ✅ **Jitter** is the *variance* in response time; **deadline miss ratio** is the *fraction* of jobs that miss deadlines.
- ✅ **CPS stability** depends on worst-case delay $\tau_d \le R_i^{\max}$ — tight scheduling ⇒ robust control.
- ✅ **LLF is dynamic-priority, optimal in theory** but suffers from preemption storms and high overhead.
- ✅ **Deadline Monotonic** generalizes RM to constrained deadlines ($D_i < T_i$).
- ✅ For **multiprocessor CPS** (multi-core ECUs, automotive ADAS), EDF is *not optimal* — use partitioned/global/pfair schemes.
- ✅ Always **state the task model assumptions** at the start of any KTU answer to earn easy marks.
- ✅ **Final exam formula checklist:** Liu–Layland bound, RTA equation, EDF condition, hyperperiod, laxity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bus Latency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Bus Latency ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bus Latency** in a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) is formally defined as the **end-to-end temporal delay** experienced by a data frame or control signal during its transit from a source node (sensor / microcontroller / processor) to a destination node (actuator / controller) across a shared communication bus medium. Mathematically, it is expressed as the time interval between the **instant a message is queued for transmission** and the **instant the message is successfully received and acknowledged** at the destination: $$ L_{bus} = t_{receive} - t_{queue} $$ where $t_{queue}$ is the timestamp when the data packet is placed in the transmit buffer, and $t_{receive}$ is the timestamp when the destination node has fully decoded the packet. In the KTU 2024 CPS framework, bus latency is categorized under the **"Networked-Embedded Real-Time Communication"** subdomain and is a **first-class quality-of-service (QoS) parameter**, because CPS applications (e.g., automotive ABS, industrial robotic arms, pacemakers) demand deterministic, bounded, and often sub-millisecond response times. ### 1.2 Sub-Categories of Bus Latency | Latency Type | Definition | Typical Magnitude | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Propagation Latency ($L_{prop}$)** | Time for a signal to physically travel across the bus medium | **5 ns/m** (twisted pair) | | **Transmission Latency ($L_{trans}$)** | Time to push all bits of a frame onto the wire | $\mu s$ scale | | **Processing Latency ($L_{proc}$)** | Time spent at the destination decoding the frame | sub-$\mu s$ | | **Queuing / Arbitration Latency ($L_{queue}$)** | Time waiting for bus access due to contention | **Dominant in CPS** | | **Retransmission Latency ($L_{re}$)** | Time spent on error recovery and retransmission | Variable | ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Hospital Triage Counter:** > Imagine a single nurse (the **bus**) serving four patients (the **nodes** — sensor, controller, actuator, user interface). Each patient must show an ID card, state their issue, and receive medicine. The "waiting time" of a patient = **Bus Latency**. > - If the nurse is fast but the patients argue over who goes first → **Arbitration Latency**. > - If the patient's report is long → **Transmission Latency** (more bits to send). > - If the patient walks slowly from the waiting room to the counter → **Propagation Latency**. > - If the nurse mishears and asks the patient to repeat → **Retransmission Latency**. > - If the patient is still in the waiting area reading a magazine → **Queuing Latency**. > > In CPS, the "patient's report" is a sensor reading, and the "medicine" is the actuation command. A delayed delivery in a brake-by-wire system is catastrophic — therefore **bounded bus latency is non-negotiable** in CPS. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Speed of signal in copper / twisted pair:** $v \approx \textbf{2} \times \textbf{10}^{\textbf{8}}$ m/s (≈ 2/3 the speed of light). - **Maximum acceptable end-to-end latency (hard real-time CPS):** typically **< 1 ms** (e.g., automotive drive-by-wire), down to **< 100 $\mu$s** for high-speed motion control. - **Standard bus bandwidths used in CPS:** CAN = **1 Mbps**, CAN-FD = **5 Mbps**, FlexRay = **10 Mbps**, EtherCAT = **100 Mbps**, TTEthernet = **1 Gbps**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Decomposition of Bus Latency The total bus latency in a CPS node-to-node communication cycle is decomposed as follows: 1. **Frame Construction Phase** — The source node assembles the payload, computes the CRC, and prepares the arbitration ID. This contributes a small, fixed overhead $L_{cpu}$. 2. **Arbitration / Medium Access Phase** — In contention-based buses (e.g., CAN, Ethernet), the node must wait until the bus is idle and win the arbitration. This is the **largest and most variable** component in CPS and is denoted $L_{arb}$. 3. **Bit Transmission Phase** — Each bit takes a fixed time equal to the **bit time** $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R_{bus}}$, where $R_{bus}$ is the nominal bit-rate. A frame of $N$ bits therefore requires $L_{trans} = N \cdot t_{bit}$. 4. **Propagation Phase** — The electromagnetic wave traverses the physical medium of length $\ell$ at velocity $v$, giving $L_{prop} = \dfrac{\ell}{v}$. 5. **Reception & Acknowledgment Phase** — The destination hardware decodes bits, performs CRC check, and issues an ACK. This yields $L_{ack}$. 6. **Error Recovery (if any)** — In case of CRC failure or bus error, the frame is retransmitted, adding $L_{re}$. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** In **time-triggered buses** (FlexRay, TTEthernet), $L_{arb}$ is **eliminated** because slots are pre-assigned, yielding deterministic, jitter-free latency — a key reason such buses are favored in safety-critical CPS (avionics, ADAS). ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $L_{total} = L_{arb} + L_{trans} + L_{prop} + L_{proc} + L_{ack}$ | Total end-to-end bus latency | seconds (s) | | 2 | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R_{bus}}$ | Bit duration on the bus | s | | 3 | $L_{trans} = N \cdot t_{bit} = \dfrac{N}{R_{bus}}$ | Frame transmission time | s | | 4 | $L_{prop} = \dfrac{\ell}{v}$ | Physical propagation delay | s | | 5 | $L_{arb}^{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} N_i \cdot t_{bit}$ | Worst-case arbitration (lower-priority frames ahead) | s | | 6 | $L_{WCRT} = L_{total}^{max}$ | Worst-Case Response Time (KTU favourite) | s | | 7 | $\text{Bus Utilization } \rho = \dfrac{\sum L_{trans,i}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Fraction of bus time used | dimensionless | | 8 | $Jitter = L_{total}^{max} - L_{total}^{min}$ | Latency variability | s | | 9 | $L_{prop} \approx 5 \cdot \ell_{(m)}$ ns | Rule of thumb for copper medium | ns | | 10 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{payload}}{L_{total}}$ | Effective payload rate | bits/s | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $L = \dfrac{N}{R}$ but forget the **start-of-frame, arbitration, CRC, ACK, and inter-frame gap bits** in $N$. Always use the **total on-wire frame size** (e.g., a standard CAN data frame is not 8 bytes = 64 bits, but **108–130 bits** including overhead). ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive CPS:** A delay > 10 ms in a steer-by-wire message is a functional-safety violation under ISO 26262. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** PROFINET IRT guarantees $L_{WCRT} \le 1 \, \mu s$ to coordinate robotic arms. - **Medical CPS:** Pacemaker lead-impedance sensing requires $L_{bus} < 250 \, \mu s$. - **Smart Grid:** IEC 61850 GOOSE messages must traverse the substation bus in **< 4 ms**. - **Aerospace:** ARINC 664 / AFDX bus enforces deterministic latency via bandwidth allocation gap (BAG). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Trade-off:** Higher $R_{bus}$ (bit-rate) reduces $L_{trans}$ but **increases $L_{prop}$'s relative dominance** and tightens signal-integrity (EMI) requirements. CPS designers must therefore co-design bus latency, fault tolerance, and physical-layer integrity. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total End-to-End Bus Latency We start from the definition: bus latency is the sum of every sequential delay a bit-experiences from source-CPU write to destination-CPU read. Each phase is non-overlapping in a single transmission event. **Step 1:** Identify the atomic sub-latencies. The first event is CPU write to the controller's transmit buffer, contributing $L_{cpu}$. The controller then waits for the bus to be free (contention) → $L_{queue}$. Next, it wins arbitration (or is granted a slot) → $L_{arb}$. Then it streams the frame onto the wire → $L_{trans}$. The bits physically travel → $L_{prop}$. The receiver decodes → $L_{rx}$. It returns an ACK → $L_{ack}$. If the CRC fails, the system retries → $L_{re}$. $$ L_{total} = L_{cpu} + L_{queue} + L_{arb} + L_{trans} + L_{prop} + L_{rx} + L_{ack} + L_{re} $$ **Step 2:** Group fixed and variable terms. $L_{cpu}$, $L_{rx}$, $L_{ack}$ are constant hardware overheads. $L_{prop}$ is constant for a fixed bus length. $L_{trans}$ is constant per frame size. The dominant stochastic terms are $L_{queue}$, $L_{arb}$, and $L_{re}$. **Step 3:** For worst-case analysis (mandatory in hard real-time CPS), we take the maximum of each variable term. $$ L_{WCRT} = L_{cpu} + L_{queue}^{max} + L_{arb}^{max} + L_{trans} + L_{prop} + L_{rx} + L_{ack} + L_{re}^{max} $$ **Step 4:** In a CAN-like bus, the worst-case arbitration latency occurs when one higher-priority frame and the maximum legal number of lower-priority frames are queued ahead. For $k$ contending nodes with frame sizes $N_i$: $$ L_{arb}^{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} N_i \cdot t_{bit} $$ **Step 5:** Substituting the bit-time $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R_{bus}}$: $$ L_{arb}^{max} = \frac{1}{R_{bus}} \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} N_i $$ **Step 6:** Combining all constant terms into $L_{0}$ and isolating the bit-rate dependence: $$ L_{WCRT} = L_{0} + \frac{N_{self}}{R_{bus}} + \frac{1}{R_{bus}} \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} N_i $$ **Step 7:** Final compact form, which clearly shows bus latency **decreases linearly with bus bit-rate** and **increases linearly with network load**: $$ \boxed{\, L_{WCRT} = L_{0} + \frac{1}{R_{bus}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i \,} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A CAN bus operates at $R_{bus} = 500$ kbps. A temperature sensor node must send a standard 11-bit-ID data frame with 8 data bytes. The bus is 20 m long, and 2 higher-priority nodes have queued frames of 108 bits each. Hardware overhead totals $L_{0} = 30 \, \mu s$. Compute $L_{WCRT}$. **Step 1 — Compute bit time:** $$ t_{bit} = \frac{1}{R_{bus}} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^{3}} = 2 \, \mu s $$ **Step 2 — Compute own frame transmission latency** (CAN standard data frame: 1 SOF + 11 ID + 1 RTR + 1 IDE + 1 r0 + 1 DLC(4 bits) + 64 data + 15 CRC + 2 ACK + 7 EOF = 108 bits nominally, but for 8-byte payload some references quote 130 bits including stuff bits. We use $N_{self} = 130$ bits). $$ L_{trans}^{self} = N_{self} \cdot t_{bit} = 130 \times 2 \, \mu s = 260 \, \mu s $$ **Step 3 — Worst-case arbitration latency** (2 higher-priority frames): $$ L_{arb}^{max} = \frac{1}{R_{bus}} \sum_{i=1}^{2} N_i = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^{3}} (108 + 108) $$ $$ L_{arb}^{max} = \frac{216}{500 \times 10^{3}} = 432 \times 10^{-6} \, s = 432 \, \mu s $$ **Step 4 — Propagation latency** (copper medium, $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s): $$ L_{prop} = \frac{\ell}{v} = \frac{20}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.1 \, \mu s $$ **Step 5 — Total Worst-Case Response Time:** $$ L_{WCRT} = L_{0} + L_{arb}^{max} + L_{trans}^{self} + L_{prop} $$ $$ L_{WCRT} = 30 \, \mu s + 432 \, \mu s + 260 \, \mu s + 0.1 \, \mu s $$ $$ \boxed{\, L_{WCRT} = 722.1 \, \mu s \approx 0.722 \, ms \,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This latency is **acceptable** for a thermal-monitoring CPS (typical deadline = 10 ms), but would **fail** a steer-by-wire deadline (typically 1 ms). Hence the choice of bus protocol must be **deadline-aware**. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bus Protocols vs. Latency Profile | Bus Protocol | $R_{bus}$ | $t_{bit}$ | Arbitration Type | Typical $L_{WCRT}$ | CPS Domain | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **CAN 2.0** | 1 Mbps | 1 $\mu s$ | CSMA/CA + priority | 100 – 1000 $\mu s$ | Automotive body | | **CAN-FD** | 5 Mbps | 200 ns | CSMA/CA + priority | 50 – 200 $\mu s$ | Automotive powertrain | | **FlexRay** | 10 Mbps | 100 ns | TDMA + FTDMA | 10 – 50 $\mu s$ | Drive-by-wire | | **EtherCAT** | 100 Mbps | 10 ns | Master-slave daisy | < 100 $\mu s$ | Industrial robotics | | **TTEthernet** | 1 Gbps | 1 ns | Time-triggered | < 10 $\mu s$ | Avionics | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bus Latency Pipeline in a CPS Node ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sensor Node
Source] --> B[CPU Processing
Lcpu] B --> C[Transmit Buffer
Lqueue] C --> D[Bus Arbitration
Larb] D --> E[Bit Transmission
Ltrans] E --> F[Physical Propagation
Lprop] F --> G[Receiver Decode
Lrx] G --> H{ACK Check
Lre} H -- ACK OK --> I[Actuator Node
Destination] H -- ACK NACK --> J[Retransmit
Lre] J --> D ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — CPS Bus Latency Functional Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP[Application Layer] app1[Control Loop Deadline D] end subgraph MID[Middleware Layer] mid1[Real Time OS Scheduler] mid2[Bus Driver Queue] end subgraph MAC[MAC Layer] mac1[Arbitration Engine] mac2[Frame Builder] end subgraph PHY[Physical Layer] phy1[Transceiver Hardware] phy2[Copper or Fiber Medium] end app1 --> mid1 mid1 --> mid2 mid2 --> mac1 mac1 --> mac2 mac2 --> phy1 phy1 --> phy2 phy2 --> phy1 phy1 --> mid1 mid1 --> app1 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Latency Decomposition (Timing Flow) ```mermaid gantt title CPS Bus Latency Timeline dateFormat X axisFormat %S.%L section Latency Phases CPU Processing Lcpu :a1, 0, 5us Queue Wait Lqueue :a2, 5us, 15us Arbitration Larb :a3, 20us, 30us Transmission Ltrans :a4, 50us, 30us Propagation Lprop :a5, 80us, 1us Reception and ACK :a6, 81us, 5us ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The horizontal extent of each phase represents its **time duration**. Notice how $L_{arb}$ and $L_{queue}$ together dominate the timeline — this is the central reason CPS designers either (a) increase $R_{bus}$, or (b) switch to **time-triggered protocols** to eliminate them. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *Define bus latency in a Cyber-Physical System. List any four sub-components that contribute to total bus latency.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Bus latency is the end-to-end time delay between the moment a data frame is queued for transmission by a source node and the moment the frame is successfully received and acknowledged at the destination node on a shared CPS communication bus. - **Four sub-components (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** 1. Arbitration latency ($L_{arb}$) 2. Transmission latency ($L_{trans}$) 3. Propagation latency ($L_{prop}$) 4. Queuing latency ($L_{queue}$) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *Differentiate between worst-case bus latency and bus latency jitter. Why is jitter critical in closed-loop CPS control?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Worst-case bus latency (1 mark):** The maximum possible end-to-end delay a frame can experience under all legal bus loading and contention conditions, denoted $L_{WCRT}$. - **Jitter (1 mark):** The difference between maximum and minimum latency, $J = L_{max} - L_{min}$. - **Why jitter matters in CPS (1 mark):** In closed-loop control (e.g., a PID controller reading a position sensor), the controller assumes **periodic, evenly-spaced** samples. Excessive jitter makes the discrete-time controller behave as a **variable-period sampler**, degrading stability, increasing overshoot, and potentially destabilizing the plant. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1** > **Q.A [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **five major components of bus latency** in a Cyber-Physical System. Derive the mathematical expression for the **worst-case bus latency** in a contention-based bus such as CAN. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A CAN bus operating at **250 kbps** connects a temperature sensor, a pressure sensor, and an ECU. Each node transmits a **standard data frame of 108 bits**. Two higher-priority frames of 108 bits each are already queued. The bus hardware introduces a constant overhead of **40 $\mu$s**, and the cable is **15 m** long. Compute the **worst-case bus latency** experienced by the lowest-priority node. Assume propagation velocity $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A:** **(a) Five components of bus latency (3.5 marks):** 1. **Arbitration latency ($L_{arb}$)** — Time spent waiting for and winning bus contention. 2. **Transmission latency ($L_{trans}$)** — Time to clock out all frame bits onto the wire. 3. **Propagation latency ($L_{prop}$)** — Time for the signal to travel the physical medium. 4. **Queuing latency ($L_{queue}$)** — Time the frame sits in the transmit buffer. 5. **Acknowledgment / processing latency ($L_{ack}$)** — Time for the receiver to decode and ACK. **Derivation (3.5 marks):** (Same as Derivation 1 in Section 3) $$ L_{WCRT} = L_{0} + \frac{1}{R_{bus}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i $$ --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Bit time. $$ t_{bit} = \frac{1}{R_{bus}} = \frac{1}{250 \times 10^{3}} = 4 \, \mu s $$ **Step 2:** Worst-case arbitration latency (2 higher-priority frames ahead). $$ L_{arb}^{max} = \frac{1}{R_{bus}} \sum_{i=1}^{2} N_i = \frac{108 + 108}{250 \times 10^{3}} = \frac{216}{250 \times 10^{3}} $$ $$ L_{arb}^{max} = 864 \times 10^{-6} \, s = 864 \, \mu s $$ **Step 3:** Own frame transmission. $$ L_{trans}^{self} = 108 \times 4 \, \mu s = 432 \, \mu s $$ **Step 4:** Propagation latency. $$ L_{prop} = \frac{\ell}{v} = \frac{15}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.075 \, \mu s $$ **Step 5:** Total worst-case bus latency. $$ L_{WCRT} = L_{0} + L_{arb}^{max} + L_{trans}^{self} + L_{prop} $$ $$ L_{WCRT} = 40 + 864 + 432 + 0.075 = 1336.075 \, \mu s $$ $$ \boxed{\, L_{WCRT} \approx 1.336 \, ms \,} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice)** > **Q.B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Explain **bus arbitration latency** and **propagation latency** in detail. Why is propagation latency often **negligible** in CAN-based CPS but **dominant** in high-speed automotive Ethernet (1000BASE-T1)? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A CPS uses **EtherCAT** at **100 Mbps** to synchronize 6 servo motors. Each motor receives a **64-byte process data frame** with **800 bits of total on-wire overhead** (preamble, FCS, etc.). The bus cycle time is **500 $\mu$s**. Compute: (i) The **per-frame transmission latency**. (ii) The **bus utilization** $\rho$. (iii) The **per-motor average latency** assuming sequential (daisy-chain) polling. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B:** **(a) Explanation (7 marks):** **Bus Arbitration Latency (2.5 marks):** - It is the **time a frame waits to gain exclusive access** to the shared bus. - In **CSMA/CA + priority** buses (CAN, CAN-FD), arbitration latency depends on the **priority of the frame** and the **loading from higher-priority frames**. Lower-priority frames wait longer. - In **time-triggered** buses (FlexRay, TTEthernet), arbitration latency is **bounded and deterministic** because slots are pre-assigned by the schedule table. **Propagation Latency (2 marks):** - Defined as $L_{prop} = \dfrac{\ell}{v}$, where $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in copper. - For a 1 m CAN cable: $L_{prop} \approx 5$ ns, negligible compared to the 1 $\mu$s bit time at 1 Mbps. **Why negligible in CAN but dominant in 1000BASE-T1 (2.5 marks):** - At **1 Mbps**, the bit time is **1 $\mu$s**, so a 5 ns propagation delay is **0.5%** of one bit — easily absorbed. - At **1 Gbps** (Ethernet), the bit time is **1 ns**. A 5 ns propagation delay is now **5 bit times** — comparable to or larger than the bit duration, causing **bit-pipelining, signal integrity issues, and inter-symbol interference**. - Hence **1000BASE-T1** employs **PAM-3 encoding, echo cancellation, and tightly controlled impedance** to mitigate propagation effects — techniques unnecessary at CAN speeds. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $R_{bus} = 100$ Mbps, $N = 6$ motors, payload = 64 bytes = 512 bits, overhead = 800 bits, $T_{cycle} = 500 \, \mu s$. **Step 1:** Bit time. $$ t_{bit} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10 \, ns $$ **Step 2 (i):** Per-frame transmission latency. $$ N_{frame} = 512 + 800 = 1312 \, bits $$ $$ L_{trans} = N_{frame} \cdot t_{bit} = 1312 \times 10 \, ns = 13120 \, ns = 13.12 \, \mu s $$ **Step 3 (ii):** Bus utilization. $$ \rho = \frac{6 \times L_{trans}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{6 \times 13.12 \, \mu s}{500 \, \mu s} = \frac{78.72}{500} $$ $$ \boxed{\, \rho \approx 0.1574 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 15.74\% \,} $$ **Step 4 (iii):** Average per-motor latency (sequential daisy chain — each motor's data traverses $(i-1)$ upstream motors on average). $$ \bar{L}_{motor} = \frac{N_{frame} \cdot t_{bit} \cdot (N+1)}{2} = \frac{1312 \times 10 \, ns \times 7}{2} $$ $$ \bar{L}_{motor} = \frac{91840 \, ns}{2} \times 1 \cdot 2 = 45{,}920 \, ns $$ $$ \boxed{\, \bar{L}_{motor} \approx 45.92 \, \mu s \,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Cross-check:** EtherCAT's "processing-on-the-fly" actually reduces this to $L_{trans}$ per node (≈ 13.12 $\mu$s) because the frame is read-and-forwarded bit-by-bit — but the conservative daisy-chain sum above is the worst-case bound. --- ### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on Bus Latency questions:** > 1. **Forgetting bus overhead bits** — using only the payload (e.g., 64 bytes) instead of the total on-wire frame size. **Always re-read the problem for the "total on-wire" or "with overhead" clause.** > 2. **Unit mismatch in $R_{bus}$** — writing 100 Mbps as 100 instead of $100 \times 10^{6}$. This single error cascades and yields wrong $t_{bit}$, wrong $L_{trans}$, and wrong final answer. **KTU examiners deduct 1 mark** for this. > 3. **Ignoring $L_{queue}$ and $L_{arb}$** — treating bus latency as transmission-only. In multi-node CPS, arbitration is often the **dominant** term. > 4. **Not stating assumptions** — KTU expects explicit declaration of propagation velocity, bit-stuffing, and frame-format assumptions. **0.5 mark is reserved for the "Assumptions" line.** > 5. **Confusing "latency" with "throughput"** — they are **not** the same. Latency is time, throughput is bits/sec. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✏ - **Definition:** Bus latency = time from message queue to successful ACK reception. - **Five sub-latencies:** $L_{cpu}$, $L_{queue}$, $L_{arb}$, $L_{trans}$, $L_{prop}$, $L_{rx}$, $L_{ack}$, $L_{re}$. - **Core formula:** $L_{WCRT} = L_{0} + \dfrac{1}{R_{bus}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i$. - **Bit time:** $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R_{bus}}$. - **Propagation speed in copper:** $\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s → $L_{prop} \approx 5$ ns/m. - **Time-triggered buses (FlexRay, TTEthernet) eliminate arbitration latency** → deterministic. - **CSMA/CA buses (CAN) have variable, priority-dependent $L_{arb}$** → non-deterministic worst case. - **Hard real-time CPS deadlines:** automotive ≈ 1–10 ms; industrial robotics ≈ 1–100 $\mu$s; avionics ≈ 1–10 $\mu$s. - **Jitter** = $L_{max} - L_{min}$ — degrades closed-loop controller stability. - **Always include total on-wire frame size** (payload + overhead) in calculations. - **Higher $R_{bus}$ → lower $L_{trans}$**, but $L_{prop}$'s relative share grows; signal integrity becomes critical. - **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Compute $L_{WCRT}$ given bit-rate, frame size, number of queued nodes, and bus length." ---

topics.title: Sense and Actuation Faults on Control Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sense and Actuation Faults on Control Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, a *Sense Fault* (or Sensor Fault) is defined as an unauthorized deviation in the measurement signal delivered by a physical sensor from its true value, while an *Actuation Fault* (or Actuator Fault) is an unauthorized deviation between the control command issued by the cyber (controller) layer and the actual physical action executed by the actuator. Both classes of faults are propagated through the closed-loop feedback path and directly degrade the **control performance metrics** such as steady-state error, transient response, stability margins, and tracking accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Sense and actuation faults are studied as the two primary injection points where physical-layer degradation couples into the cyber layer. Mastery of fault models (additive, multiplicative, and stuck-type) is a high-weightage ESE topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Drunk Driver and the Speedometer

Imagine you are driving a car (the *physical process*) using a *speedometer* (the *sensor*) and a *steering wheel* (the *actuator*).

- **Sense Fault Analogy:** The speedometer reads **60 km/h** when the actual speed is **40 km/h**. The driver (controller) will mistakenly release the accelerator because the system *thinks* it is going fast. This is a **sensor bias fault** — the measured value is offset from the true value.
- **Actuation Fault Analogy:** The driver turns the steering wheel 30 degrees to the left, but the wheels only turn 10 degrees because of a damaged tie-rod. This is a **loss-of-effectiveness actuation fault** — the actuator executes only a fraction of the commanded action.
- **Complete Failure:** The speedometer needle drops to zero (**hard sensor failure**), or the wheels lock in place regardless of the driver's input (**stuck actuator**).

In both cases, the car's *performance* (stability, tracking, safety) deteriorates even though the driver is making perfectly correct decisions based on faulty feedback or limited actuation.

## 1.3 The Two Physical Interfaces in CPS

A canonical CPS has two critical cyber-physical boundaries:

| Interface | Physical Layer | Cyber Layer | Fault Type Studied |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensing Interface** | Physical process (real-world variable) | Digital controller / estimator | **Sense Fault** |
| **Actuation Interface** | Digital controller / command signal | Physical actuator | **Actuation Fault** |

> [!NOTE]
> The "performance" of control is mathematically quantified by metrics such as the **Integral of Squared Error (ISE)**, the **Integral of Absolute Error (IAE)**, **rise time ($t_r$)**, **settling time ($t_s$)**, and the **maximum overshoot ($M_p$)**. Any sense or actuation fault perturbs these quantities.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sense (Sensor) Faults

Sense faults are mathematically modeled as perturbations applied to the true physical state vector $x(t)$. Let the true state be $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and the measurement delivered to the controller be $y_s(t)$.

### (a) Additive Sensor Bias Fault
A constant or slowly-varying offset is added to the true measurement.

$$y_s(t) = x(t) + f_s^{\text{bias}}$$

where $f_s^{\text{bias}} \in \mathbb{R}$ is a non-zero constant. This is the **most common and high-weightage KTU exam fault model**.

### (b) Sensor Drift Fault
The offset grows linearly (or as a polynomial) with time, often due to aging components or thermal effects.

$$y_s(t) = x(t) + \alpha \cdot t$$

where $\alpha$ is the **drift coefficient** (units: sensor-units per second).

### (c) Multiplicative Sensor Scaling Fault (Loss of Sensor Accuracy)
The sensor gain deviates from its nominal value.

$$y_s(t) = (1 + \beta) \cdot x(t)$$

where $\beta$ is the relative gain error. If $\beta = -1$, the sensor reads **zero** regardless of input (complete gain failure).

### (d) Sensor Freezing / Stuck Fault
The measurement remains constant at the value last observed at time $t_f$.

$$y_s(t) = x(t_f), \quad \forall t \ge t_f$$

### (e) Complete Sensor Failure (Hard Fault)
The output becomes zero, infinity, or a saturated value.

$$y_s(t) = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad y_s(t) = y_{\max}$$

### (f) Sensor Noise Fault
Stochastic, high-frequency contamination (e.g., Gaussian white noise).

$$y_s(t) = x(t) + \eta(t), \quad \eta(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$$

## 2.2 Classification of Actuation Faults

Let the control command computed by the cyber layer be $u_c(t) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ and the actual physical action be $u_a(t)$.

### (a) Additive Actuator Bias Fault
A constant disturbance torque/force is added at the actuator.

$$u_a(t) = u_c(t) + f_a^{\text{bias}}$$

### (b) Actuator Loss of Effectiveness (LOE)
The actuator delivers only a fraction $\gamma \in [0,1]$ of the commanded action. $\gamma = 1$ means healthy; $\gamma = 0$ means total failure.

$$u_a(t) = \gamma \cdot u_c(t), \quad 0 \le \gamma \le 1$$

### (c) Stuck Actuator Fault
The actuator output freezes at a fixed value $u_{\text{stuck}}$.

$$u_a(t) = u_{\text{stuck}}, \quad \forall t \ge t_f$$

### (d) Actuator Saturation
The output is hard-clipped at physical limits.

$$u_a(t) = \text{sat}(u_c(t)) = \text{sgn}(u_c(t)) \cdot \min(\vert u_c(t) \vert, \, u_{\max})$$

### (e) Actuator Dead-Zone / Hysteresis
A non-linear region where small commands produce no physical motion.

$$u_a(t) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \vert u_c(t) \vert \le d_z \\ u_c(t) - \text{sgn}(u_c(t)) \cdot d_z & \text{if } \vert u_c(t) \vert > d_z \end{cases}$$

## 2.3 Impact on Control Performance

When a fault $f$ is injected, the closed-loop state-space system:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$$
$$y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$$

is altered. The system poles (eigenvalues of $A - BK$ for state feedback with gain $K$) shift, causing:

- **Loss of Stability Margin** (poles crossing the imaginary axis into RHP).
- **Increased Steady-State Error** ($e_{ss}$ grows).
- **Oscillatory / Chattering Response** (especially with stuck faults).
- **Wind-up Phenomena** (integral action saturates under actuator saturation).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensor Bias Fault | $y_s = x + f_b$ | $f_b$ in sensor units |
| 2 | Sensor Drift | $y_s = x + \alpha t$ | $\alpha$ in units/sec |
| 3 | Sensor Scaling | $y_s = (1+\beta) x$ | $\beta$ dimensionless |
| 4 | Stuck Sensor | $y_s = x(t_f)$ | Constant value |
| 5 | Actuator LOE | $u_a = \gamma u_c$ | $\gamma \in [0,1]$, dimensionless |
| 6 | Actuator Bias | $u_a = u_c + f_a$ | $f_a$ in actuator units |
| 7 | Actuator Saturation | $u_a = \text{sgn}(u_c)\cdot\min(\vert u_c \vert, u_{\max})$ | $u_{\max}$ physical limit |
| 8 | ISE Performance Index | $J_{\text{ISE}} = \int_0^\infty e^2(t) \, dt$ | Lower is better |
| 9 | IAE Performance Index | $J_{\text{IAE}} = \int_0^\infty \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ | Robust to outliers |
| 10 | ITAE Performance Index | $J_{\text{ITAE}} = \int_0^\infty t \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ | Penalizes long errors |
| 11 | Steady-State Error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$ | Should be zero for Type $\ge 1$ |
| 12 | Closed-Loop Pole Shift | $\Delta \lambda = \lambda_{\text{faulty}} - \lambda_{\text{healthy}}$ | Indicates stability loss |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value. In the KTU Formula Sheet, all vertical bars have been written using the LaTeX `\vert` command to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Fault-tolerant flight control (NASA's IFCS program) handles stuck ailerons and faulty gyros.
- **Automotive:** Drive-by-wire systems detect sensor bias to prevent unintended acceleration (Toyota 2009–2010 recall context).
- **Industrial Robotics:** Stuck joint faults in robotic arms must be detected within milliseconds to prevent damage.
- **Smart Grids:** Sensor faults in PMUs (Phasor Measurement Units) cause cascading state-estimation errors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Impact of Sensor Bias on Steady-State Error

Consider a unity-feedback closed-loop system with plant transfer function $G(s)$ and controller $C(s)$ driving a sensor with bias fault $f_b$. The reference input is $R(s)$.

**Step 1 — Write the measured output signal.**
The actual physical output is $Y(s)$. The faulty sensor delivers to the controller the signal $Y_s(s) = Y(s) + F_b(s)$, where $F_b(s) = f_b / s$ for a constant bias $f_b$.

**Step 2 — Form the error signal entering the controller.**
$$E(s) = R(s) - Y_s(s) = R(s) - Y(s) - F_b(s)$$

**Step 3 — Express the closed-loop physical output.**
The control loop is still closed around the *true* plant, so:
$$Y(s) = G(s) C(s) E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y(s) &= G(s) C(s) \left[ R(s) - Y(s) - F_b(s) \right] \\
Y(s) + G(s) C(s) Y(s) &= G(s) C(s) R(s) - G(s) C(s) F_b(s) \\
Y(s) \left[ 1 + G(s) C(s) \right] &= G(s) C(s) R(s) - G(s) C(s) F_b(s)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $Y(s)$ and apply the Final Value Theorem.**
For a unit step reference $R(s) = 1/s$ and constant bias $F_b(s) = f_b / s$:

$$Y(s) = \frac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s} - \frac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)} \cdot \frac{f_b}{s}$$

Applying $\lim_{s \to 0} s Y(s)$:

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)} \cdot \left( 1 - f_b \right)$$

**Step 6 — Compute the steady-state error.**
$$e_{ss} = r_{ss} - y_{ss} = 1 - \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)} (1 - f_b)$$

For a Type-1 system where $s G(s) C(s) \to K$ as $s \to 0$, we get $e_{ss} = f_b$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** A constant sensor bias $f_b$ produces an identical-magnitude steady-state error in a Type-1 system. This is why KTU examiners frequently pose numerical problems based on this exact result.

## 3.2 Derivation: Actuator Loss of Effectiveness and Closed-Loop Pole Migration

Consider a second-order plant with state-space matrices and a state-feedback controller $K$ designed for nominal performance.

**Nominal System:**
$$\dot{x} = A x + B u_c, \quad u_c = -K x$$

The closed-loop matrix is:
$$A_{\text{cl}}^{\text{nom}} = A - B K$$

**Faulty System (Actuator LOE with factor $\gamma$):**
$$u_a = \gamma u_c = -\gamma K x$$

The new closed-loop matrix becomes:
$$A_{\text{cl}}^{\text{fault}} = A - \gamma B K$$

**Step 1 — Express the change in the closed-loop matrix.**

$$\Delta A_{\text{cl}} = A_{\text{cl}}^{\text{fault}} - A_{\text{cl}}^{\text{nom}} = (1 - \gamma) B K$$

**Step 2 — Bound the eigenvalue perturbation using matrix perturbation theory.**
For any eigenvalue $\lambda$ of $A_{\text{cl}}^{\text{nom}}$ with left and right eigenvectors $w$ and $v$:

$$\Delta \lambda \approx \frac{w^T \, \Delta A_{\text{cl}} \, v}{w^T v} = (1 - \gamma) \cdot \frac{w^T B K v}{w^T v}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**
As $\gamma \to 0$ (total actuator failure), $\Delta \lambda \to w^T B K v / (w^T v)$, and the closed-loop poles migrate toward their *open-loop* positions in $A$. The system effectively *degrades to open-loop* behavior, losing all stabilization provided by $K$.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the formal reason why **actuator LOE directly reduces damping and increases overshoot** — a classic KTU 14-mark problem statement.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fault Signature Reference

| Fault Type | Model Equation | Effect on $e_{ss}$ | Effect on $M_p$ | Effect on Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor Bias | $y_s = x + f_b$ | Shifts by $f_b$ | Minimal | Unchanged (margins shift) |
| Sensor Drift | $y_s = x + \alpha t$ | Grows linearly with $t$ | Grows | Degrades with time |
| Sensor Scaling | $y_s = (1+\beta)x$ | Multiplied by $(1+\beta)$ | Multiplied | Marginally affected |
| Stuck Sensor | $y_s = \text{const}$ | Saturates to const. error | Saturates | Severe loss possible |
| Actuator Bias | $u_a = u_c + f_a$ | Constant offset at output | Minimal | Unchanged |
| Actuator LOE | $u_a = \gamma u_c$ | Increases | Increases significantly | Pole migration |
| Stuck Actuator | $u_a = u_{\text{stuck}}$ | Becomes constant | Saturates | Severe (loop opened) |
| Actuator Saturation | $u_a = \text{sat}(u_c)$ | Wind-up behavior | Reduced damping | Integral wind-up risk |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPS Closed-Loop with Fault Injection Points

The following Mermaid block renders the complete Cyber-Physical System feedback loop with explicit fault injection nodes at both the sensing and actuation interfaces.

```mermaid
graph LR
    REF["Reference R(t)"] --> SUM1(("+"))
    SUM1 --> CTRL["Controller C(s)"]
    CTRL --> CMDA["Command u_c(t)"]
    CMDA --> ACT["Physical Actuator"]
    ACT --> FA["Actuation Fault f_a"]
    FA --> PROC["Physical Plant Process"]
    PROC --> YOUT["True Output y(t)"]
    YOUT --> FS["Sense Fault f_s"]
    FS --> FBK["Faulty Measurement y_s(t)"]
    FBK --> SUM1
    FBK --> EST["State Estimator / Observer"]
    EST --> CTRL
    ACT -.->|"Telemetry / Health Bit"| MON["Fault Detection and Isolation FDI Module"]
    FS -.->|"Telemetry / Health Bit"| MON
    MON -->|"Reconfigured Control Law"| CTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> Reading the diagram from left to right: the **summing junction** (cyber layer) compares the reference with the *faulty* measurement. The controller computes a command, which the actuator executes subject to the actuation fault. The plant's true output is sensed subject to the sense fault, closing the loop. An optional **FDI (Fault Detection and Isolation) module** receives telemetry from both interfaces and can reconfigure the control law to mitigate the fault.

## 4.2 Fault Taxonomy Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT["Sense and Actuation Faults"]
    ROOT --> SF["Sense Faults"]
    ROOT --> AF["Actuation Faults"]
    SF --> SFA["Additive Bias"]
    SF --> SFB["Drift"]
    SF --> SFC["Multiplicative Scaling"]
    SF --> SFD["Stuck or Freezing"]
    SF --> SFE["Hard Failure"]
    SF --> SFF["Stochastic Noise"]
    AF --> AFA["Additive Bias"]
    AF --> AFB["Loss of Effectiveness LOE"]
    AF --> AFC["Stuck Actuator"]
    AF --> AFD["Saturation"]
    AF --> AFE["Dead Zone and Hysteresis"]
    AF --> AFF["Complete Failure"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fault Propagation into the Cyber Layer

This block-level architecture illustrates how a physical-layer fault couples into the cyber decision-making layer, leading to degraded control performance.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["PHYSICAL LAYER"] --> P2["Process Dynamics G(s)"]
    P2 --> P3["Sensor Hardware"]
    P3 --> P4["Sense Fault Model y_s = x + f"]
    P4 --> P5["Analog to Digital Converter ADC"]
    P5 --> P6["CYBER LAYER"]
    P6 --> P7["Digital Controller Algorithm"]
    P7 --> P8["Fault Detection FDI Logic"]
    P8 --> P9["Reconfiguration Switch"]
    P9 --> P10["Digital to Analog Converter DAC"]
    P10 --> P11["Actuation Fault Model u_a = gamma u_c + f_a"]
    P11 --> P12["Actuator Hardware"]
    P12 --> P1
    P8 -.->|"Alarm / Health Flag"| OBS["Operator HMI Dashboard"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**Q:** Differentiate between **additive sensor bias** and **multiplicative sensor scaling** faults. Which one preserves the linearity of the measurement equation?

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Additive Bias | Multiplicative Scaling |
|---|---|---|
| Equation | $y_s = x + f_b$ | $y_s = (1+\beta) x$ |
| Nature | Offset (constant) | Gain error |
| Linearity | Preserved (linear) | Preserved (linear) |
| Zero-crossing | $y_s = 0 \Rightarrow x = -f_b$ | $y_s = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$ |

**Conclusion:** *Both* fault types preserve linearity because the measurement equation remains a linear function of the true state $x$. The bias shifts the curve vertically, while scaling changes its slope.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**Q:** What is meant by **Loss of Effectiveness (LOE)** in an actuator? How is it represented mathematically, and what physical damage can cause it?

**Model Answer:**

Loss of Effectiveness (LOE) is an actuation fault in which the actuator delivers only a fraction $\gamma$ of the commanded control action, where $0 \le \gamma \le 1$.

$$u_a(t) = \gamma \cdot u_c(t)$$

- $\gamma = 1$ → healthy actuator.
- $0 < \gamma < 1$ → partial effectiveness.
- $\gamma = 0$ → complete failure (stuck at zero command).

**Physical causes:** hydraulic fluid leakage, pneumatic pressure drop, electrical winding short-circuit in a motor, mechanical wear in a servo valve, or partial seizure of a robotic joint.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various **types of sense faults** that can occur in a Cyber-Physical System. Give the mathematical model for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A sense fault is a deviation in the measured signal $y_s(t)$ from the true physical state $x(t)$. The block diagram showing all sense fault injection points is as follows:

```
  True State x(t) ---> [FAULT INJECTION] ---> Faulty Measurement y_s(t)
        |                                                |
        |----[Bias]------> y_s = x + f_b                |
        |----[Drift]------> y_s = x + alpha t            |
        |----[Scaling]----> y_s = (1+beta) x             |
        |----[Stuck]------> y_s = x(t_f)                 |
        |----[Noise]------> y_s = x + eta(t)             |
        |----[Hard Fail]--> y_s = 0                      |
```

**Mathematical Models (recap):**

1. **Additive Bias:** $y_s(t) = x(t) + f_b$ where $f_b$ is a constant offset.
2. **Drift:** $y_s(t) = x(t) + \alpha t$ where $\alpha$ is the drift rate.
3. **Scaling (Loss of Accuracy):** $y_s(t) = (1+\beta) x(t)$ where $\beta$ is the relative gain error.
4. **Stuck/Freezing:** $y_s(t) = x(t_f)$ for $t \ge t_f$, holding the value at the freeze time.
5. **Complete Failure:** $y_s(t) = 0$ or $y_s(t) = y_{\text{sat}}$.
6. **Noise:** $y_s(t) = x(t) + \eta(t)$ with $\eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$.

**Engineering Examples:**
- **Bias:** Pressure transducer with zero-point drift.
- **Drift:** Aging thermocouple in a nuclear reactor.
- **Scaling:** Magnetic field sensor with calibration error.
- **Stuck:** GPS receiver lock in a tunnel.

**(7 marks breakdown: 3 marks diagram + 4 marks for 4 fault types with equations)**

---

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **steady-state error** in a unity-feedback control system subjected to a **constant sensor bias fault** $f_b$. Use the Final Value Theorem. Assume a Type-1 system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)C(s)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The closed-loop system with a sensor bias $f_b$ is described as:

$$E(s) = R(s) - Y_s(s) = R(s) - Y(s) - \frac{f_b}{s}$$
$$Y(s) = G(s) C(s) \, E(s)$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $E(s)$ into the plant equation:**

$$Y(s) = G(s) C(s) \left[ R(s) - Y(s) - \frac{f_b}{s} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Collect $Y(s)$ terms:**

$$Y(s) \left[ 1 + G(s) C(s) \right] = G(s) C(s) R(s) - G(s) C(s) \frac{f_b}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the true output:**

$$Y(s) = \frac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)} R(s) - \frac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)} \cdot \frac{f_b}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Final Value Theorem for unit-step reference $R(s) = 1/s$:**

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s Y(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)} \cdot \left( 1 - f_b \right)$$

For a **Type-1** system, $\lim_{s \to 0} G(s) C(s) = \infty$, so the loop gain term tends to **1**, and:

$$y_{ss} \approx 1 - f_b$$

**Step 5 — Compute the steady-state error:**

$$e_{ss} = r_{ss} - y_{ss} = 1 - (1 - f_b) = f_b$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The steady-state error due to a constant sensor bias $f_b$ in a Type-1 system is exactly $f_b$. The integrator in the controller cannot reject this disturbance because the bias enters *before* the error signal is formed.

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks setup + 3 marks derivation + 2 marks conclusion)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **various types of actuation faults** with their mathematical models. Discuss in detail the impact of **Loss of Effectiveness (LOE)** on the stability of a closed-loop system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Actuation Fault Classification (5 marks):**

1. **Additive Bias Fault:** $u_a(t) = u_c(t) + f_a$ — a constant disturbance force is added.
2. **Loss of Effectiveness (LOE):** $u_a(t) = \gamma \, u_c(t)$ with $0 \le \gamma \le 1$ — only a fraction is delivered.
3. **Stuck Fault:** $u_a(t) = u_{\text{stuck}}$ — frozen output.
4. **Saturation:** $u_a(t) = \text{sgn}(u_c) \cdot \min(\vert u_c \vert, u_{\max})$ — physical limits.
5. **Dead-Zone:** no motion if $\vert u_c \vert \le d_z$ — non-linear friction.

**Impact of LOE on Stability (2 marks):**

For a state-feedback system $\dot{x} = A x + B u$ with controller $u_c = -K x$ and LOE factor $\gamma$:

$$A_{\text{cl}}^{\text{fault}} = A - \gamma B K$$

As $\gamma$ decreases, the closed-loop poles migrate from their nominal positions toward the *open-loop* eigenvalues of $A$. The system **degrades from closed-loop to open-loop behavior**:

- Damping ratio $\zeta$ decreases.
- Natural frequency may shift.
- For $\gamma$ below a critical threshold, poles cross into the right-half plane and the system **becomes unstable**.

---

**(b)** A second-order plant has $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, and a state-feedback gain $K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ designed to place the closed-loop poles at desired locations. If the actuator suffers a **Loss of Effectiveness with $\gamma = 0.5$**, compute the new closed-loop matrix and comment on stability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the nominal closed-loop matrix.**

$$A_{\text{cl}}^{\text{nom}} = A - B K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -3 & -5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the characteristic polynomial (nominal).**

$$\det(sI - A_{\text{cl}}^{\text{nom}}) = \det \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 3 & s+5 \end{bmatrix} = s(s+5) + 3 = s^2 + 5s + 3$$

Nominal poles: $s = \frac{-5 \pm \sqrt{25 - 12}}{2} = \frac{-5 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2} \approx -0.697, \, -4.303$ → **Stable**.

**Step 3 — Compute the faulty closed-loop matrix with $\gamma = 0.5$.**

$$A_{\text{cl}}^{\text{fault}} = A - \gamma B K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} - 0.5 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2.5 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the faulty characteristic polynomial.**

$$\det(sI - A_{\text{cl}}^{\text{fault}}) = s(s+4) + 2.5 = s^2 + 4s + 2.5$$

Faulty poles: $s = \frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 10}}{2} = \frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{6}}{2} \approx -0.775, \, -3.225$ → **Stable but slower**.

**Step 5 — Comment on stability.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Nominal damping:} \quad \zeta_{\text{nom}} &= \frac{5}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.443 \text{ (overdamped)} \\
\text{Faulty damping:} \quad \zeta_{\text{fault}} &= \frac{4}{2\sqrt{2.5}} \approx 1.265 \text{ (still overdamped)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Even at $\gamma = 0.5$, the system remains stable, but the closed-loop poles have migrated *closer* to the imaginary axis. The **damping has decreased**, the **natural frequency has reduced**, and the system response will be **slower with more overshoot** if poles were originally complex. For $\gamma$ approaching $0$, the poles approach the open-loop poles at $s = -1, -2$, drastically reducing performance.

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks setup + 3 marks computation + 2 marks stability comment)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the direction of the fault injection.** A *sensor* fault affects the *feedback* path; an *actuator* fault affects the *forward* path. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the final value theorem's preconditions.** You must verify the closed-loop is stable *before* applying $\lim_{s \to 0} s Y(s)$. If poles cross into the RHP, the Final Value Theorem is invalid.
> 3. **Skipping the sign convention in $e_{ss}$ derivations.** The error is $e = r - y_{measured}$, not $e = y - r$. This is a 1-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 4. **Writing $\vert x \vert$ using the `|` character in markdown tables.** Always use `\vert` in LaTeX-formatted answers to avoid rendering errors. This is a presentation deduction.
> 5. **Not stating physical units.** Drift coefficient $\alpha$ has units of *sensor-units per second*; gain error $\beta$ is *dimensionless*. Examiners award 1 mark for correct units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sense faults** corrupt the *feedback* signal; **actuation faults** corrupt the *forward* command.
- **Six sensor fault models:** bias, drift, scaling, stuck, hard failure, noise.
- **Five actuator fault models:** bias, loss of effectiveness (LOE), stuck, saturation, dead-zone.
- **Sensor bias $f_b$** produces an **equal-magnitude steady-state error** $e_{ss} = f_b$ in Type-1 systems.
- **Actuator LOE factor $\gamma$** causes **closed-loop poles to migrate** from nominal positions toward open-loop positions; stability degrades as $\gamma \to 0$.
- **Stuck faults** are the most severe because they effectively **open the feedback loop**, leaving the system under open-loop control with a constant disturbance.
- **Saturation** causes **integrator wind-up** when integral action is present in the controller.
- **Fault Detection and Isolation (FDI)** is the cyber-layer defense that monitors sensor and actuator telemetry, estimates fault parameters, and reconfigures the control law to maintain stability and performance.
- **Performance metrics** used to quantify the impact: ISE, IAE, ITAE, $t_r$, $t_s$, $M_p$, $e_{ss}$, and gain/phase margins.
- **KTU high-weightage derivations:** (1) sensor bias → steady-state error, (2) actuator LOE → closed-loop pole migration, (3) saturation → wind-up analysis.
- **Key engineering examples to memorize:** automotive drive-by-wire bias, aerospace stuck ailerons, robotic-arm LOE, smart-grid PMU faults.
- **Always state units** for fault parameters in the exam — this is a guaranteed 1-mark differentiator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Congestion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Network Congestion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Congestion** is formally defined as a state in a communication network where the **demand for network resources** (link bandwidth, buffer space at routers, processing capacity at nodes) **exceeds the available supply**, resulting in degraded Quality of Service (QoS) characterized by increased packet delay, jitter, and packet loss.

In the context of **Cyber Physical Systems (CPS)**, network congestion is a *first-order concern* because CPS architectures (such as Industrial IoT, autonomous vehicles, and smart grids) require **deterministic, low-latency communication** between physical sensors, actuators, and computational controllers. A congested network can break the *feedback loop* of a CPS, leading to instability, unsafe physical behavior, or catastrophic control failure.

**Mathematical Condition for Congestion:**
When the **aggregate input traffic rate** $\lambda_{in}$ entering a network node becomes greater than its **service rate** $\mu$, the queue length at the node grows unbounded, and congestion occurs:

$$\lambda_{in} > \mu \implies \text{Congestion State}$$

**Key Network Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants/Indicators):**
- **Throughput (T):** Effective data delivered per unit time (bits/sec).
- **End-to-End Delay (D):** Total time for a packet to travel from source to destination.
- **Jitter (J):** Variation in inter-packet arrival time.
- **Packet Loss Ratio (PLR):** Fraction of transmitted packets dropped.
- **Goodput (G):** Application-layer useful data rate (excludes retransmissions).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Highway Traffic Jam**
> Imagine a 4-lane highway (**network link capacity**) that merges into a 2-lane tunnel. When rush hour hits, the number of cars arriving at the tunnel entrance (**input traffic rate $\lambda_{in}$**) exceeds what the 2-lane tunnel can discharge (**service rate $\mu$**). Cars begin to back up, some take alternate routes (**alternate routing**), and at the tunnel mouth, cars may be turned away (**packet drops**). Speed drops from 100 km/h to 5 km/h (**increased delay**), and the actual number of cars that exit per minute falls (**reduced throughput**). The tunnel entrance is "congested."

**In a CPS context**, consider a *fleet of autonomous vehicles* sharing a 5G network. Each vehicle streams **LIDAR, video, and control telemetry** at 50 Mbps. When 50 vehicles enter a cell simultaneously, the cell's **100 MHz bandwidth** is oversubscribed — the resulting congestion causes *control commands to arrive late*, which can translate into unsafe physical motion.

## 1.3 The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Congestion Control** is the set of mechanisms (algorithms and protocols) used by the network and end-systems to **prevent, avoid, or react to** network congestion. The goal is to operate the network at the **Knee Point** (maximum throughput with minimum delay) and *avoid* the **Cliff Point** (collapse where throughput collapses as offered load increases).

```
Offered Load (Input Rate)
   ↑                Knee            Cliff
   |               /\              /
   |              /  \            /
   |             /    \   ______ /
   |            /      \ /     
   |           /        X      ← Congestion Collapse
   |          /        / \
   |         /        /   \
   |________/________/_____\________→ Throughput
            1       2      3
```
*(See Section 4 for the formal Mermaid diagram.)*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Root Causes of Network Congestion

Network congestion in a CPS communication substrate is rarely caused by a single factor. The principal causes are:

- **Bursty Input Traffic:** Many CPS sensor streams are *periodic but unsynchronized* (e.g., 1000 sensors all reporting at the 1-second mark), creating instantaneous demand spikes.
- **Insufficient Buffer Space:** Router/switch output queues are finite. When buffers fill, tail-drop or active queue management (AQM) drops packets.
- **Slow Processing at Bottleneck Nodes:** A router with a slow CPU cannot forward packets at wire speed, creating serialization delay.
- **Bottleneck Links:** A high-capacity LAN feeding into a low-capacity WAN link (the classic **fan-in problem**).
- **Retransmission Overload:** When packets are lost due to congestion, aggressive retransmission *adds* to the load, worsening the condition — the **congestion collapse** phenomenon.
- **Routing Inefficiencies:** Suboptimal path selection that concentrates traffic on a few links.

## 2.2 The Two Pillars: Congestion Control vs. Flow Control

> [!NOTE]
> These two are **commonly confused** in KTU exams. Note the distinction:

| Parameter | Congestion Control | Flow Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scope** | End-to-end (or network-wide) | Point-to-point (sender ↔ receiver) |
| **Purpose** | Prevents network from being overwhelmed by aggregate traffic | Prevents a *fast sender* from overwhelming a *slow receiver* |
| **Triggered by** | Router queue buildup, packet loss, ECN marks | Receiver's advertised window (rwnd) exhaustion |
| **Controlled by** | Congestion window (**cwnd**) | Receiver window (**rwnd**) |
| **Example Mechanism** | TCP Reno, TCP CUBIC, RED, ECN | TCP rwnd sliding window |

## 2.3 Congestion Control Taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Mandatory Classification:**

### A. Open-Loop Congestion Control (Prevention)
Applied *before* congestion occurs. Traffic-shaping and policing techniques:
1. **Leaky Bucket Algorithm**
2. **Token Bucket Algorithm**
3. **Traffic Shaping** (e.g., 802.1Qav in CPS Ethernet AVB)
4. **Admission Control** (e.g., Resource Reservation Protocol — RSVP)

### B. Closed-Loop Congestion Control (Reaction)
Applied *after* congestion is detected. Forms a feedback loop:
1. **Monitor** network state (queue length, delay, loss).
2. **Signal** congestion to senders (ECN bits, packet drops, ICMP Source Quench — deprecated).
3. **Adjust** sender rate (reduce cwnd).

## 2.4 Detailed Algorithm Analysis

### A. The Leaky Bucket Algorithm
A metaphor for a bucket with a small hole at the bottom. Water (packets) enters at any rate, but leaks out at a *constant rate* $\rho$. If the bucket is full, incoming water overflows (packets are dropped).

- **Input:** Variable rate (bursty).
- **Output:** Constant rate $\rho$ (smoothed).
- **Bucket Capacity:** $B$ packets.
- **Behavior Equation:** If $B(t)$ is the current bucket content,
  $$\frac{dB(t)}{dt} = \lambda_{in}(t) - \rho \quad \text{when } B(t) < B_{max}$$
  $$B(t) = B_{max} \implies \text{Drop incoming packets}$$

### B. The Token Bucket Algorithm
A bucket fills with *tokens* at rate $r$ tokens/second. Each packet requires **one token** to be transmitted. If no token is available, the packet waits (or is dropped in non-buffering variants).

- **Token Generation Rate:** $r$ tokens/sec.
- **Bucket Capacity:** $B$ tokens.
- **Behavior:** Allows bursts of up to $B$ packets, then sustained rate $r$.
- **CPS Use Case:** Suitable for **multimedia streams** in CPS where periodic bursty control packets must be allowed to pass without smoothing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Exam Favorite):** *Leaky Bucket* forces **constant output** (smooths bursts). *Token Bucket* allows **bursty output** up to bucket capacity, but enforces a **long-term average rate**.

### C. TCP Congestion Control — The Jacobson/Karels Algorithm
The dominant closed-loop mechanism on the Internet. It operates a congestion window **cwnd** that is updated based on feedback from the network:

- **Slow Start Phase:** cwnd begins at 1 MSS and **doubles every RTT** (exponential growth: cwnd = $2^k$ after $k$ RTTs).
- **Congestion Avoidance Phase:** cwnd grows **linearly** (cwnd = cwnd + 1/cwnd per ACK, or +1 MSS per RTT).
- **Slow Start Threshold (ssthresh):** Boundary between the two phases.
- **Reaction to Congestion Event:**
  - On **timeout (severe congestion):** ssthresh = cwnd/2; cwnd = 1 MSS (return to slow start).
  - On **3 duplicate ACKs (mild congestion):** ssthresh = cwnd/2; cwnd = ssthresh (Fast Recovery — TCP Reno).

### D. Active Queue Management (AQM): Random Early Detection (RED)
Routers *probabilistically* drop packets *before* the queue fills, signaling senders to slow down early.
- **Minimum threshold:** $TH_{min}$ — start dropping with low probability.
- **Maximum threshold:** $TH_{max}$ — drop with high probability.
- **Drop probability function:** Piecewise linear between the thresholds.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for guaranteed marks in numerical problems.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\lambda_{in} > \mu$ | Congestion condition at a node | dimensionless |
| 2 | $B(t+1) = \max\bigl[0,\; B(t) + \lambda(t) - \rho \bigr]$ | Leaky bucket content recursion | packets |
| 3 | $T_{out} = \min\bigl[C,\; B(t) + r \cdot \Delta t \bigr]$ | Token bucket max output in window | packets |
| 4 | $B_{max} = r \cdot t_{burst}$ | Max burst size allowed by token bucket | packets |
| 5 | $\text{cwnd}_{SS}(k) = 2^{k} \cdot \text{MSS}$ | Slow start cwnd after $k$ RTTs | bytes |
| 6 | $\text{cwnd}_{CA}(k) = \text{cwnd}_{0} + k \cdot \text{MSS}$ | Congestion avoidance (linear) | bytes |
| 7 | $\text{ssthresh}_{new} = \text{cwnd}_{old} / 2$ | Threshold update on loss | bytes |
| 8 | $\text{RTT}_{avg} = \alpha \cdot \text{RTT}_{new} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \text{RTT}_{old}$ | TCP RTT smoothing ($\alpha \approx 0.125$) | seconds |
| 9 | $\text{Throughput} \approx \frac{\text{MSS}}{\text{RTT}\sqrt{p}}$ | Simplified TCP throughput (p=loss prob) | bits/s |
| 10 | $P_{drop}(q) = \begin{cases} 0 & q < TH_{min} \\ P_{max}\frac{q - TH_{min}}{TH_{max} - TH_{min}} & TH_{min} \le q \le TH_{max} \\ 1 & q > TH_{max} \end{cases}$ | RED drop probability function | dimensionless |
| 11 | $D_{total} = D_{prop} + D_{trans} + D_{queue} + D_{proc}$ | End-to-end delay sum | seconds |
| 12 | $T = \frac{W}{S} = \frac{\text{cwnd}}{\text{RTT}}$ | Instantaneous TCP throughput | bits/s |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Deterministic Ethernet (TSN) uses a credit-based shaper — a variant of token bucket — to guarantee bounded latency for CPS control loops.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** IEEE 802.11p (WAVE) and 5G V2X use congestion control (e.g., Decentralized Congestion Control, DCC) to prevent channel saturation in dense platoons.
- **Smart Grid:** IEC 61850 GOOSE messages use priority tagging (a form of QoS-based congestion avoidance) to ensure protection commands are not delayed by routine metering traffic.
- **Healthcare CPS:** Remote surgery and ICU monitoring require strict bandwidth reservation; congestion here can be life-threatening.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Leaky Bucket Sizing for a CPS Sensor Stream

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A CPS sensor node generates 1000 packets/minute with peak bursts of 250 packets in 1 second. The downstream CAN-FD bus can accept packets at a constant rate of 15 packets/second. The maximum allowed queueing delay in the leaky bucket is 5 seconds. Determine the minimum bucket capacity $B$ and the steady-state output rate.

**Given:**
- Long-term average input rate $\lambda_{avg} = 1000/60 \approx 16.67$ packets/s.
- Peak burst size: $B_{burst} = 250$ packets in 1 s.
- Output rate: $\rho = 15$ packets/s.
- Max queueing delay: $D_{q,max} = 5$ s.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Check the feasibility condition.**
For a leaky bucket to operate without indefinite buffer growth, the *long-term* input rate must not exceed the output rate:
$$\lambda_{avg} = \frac{1000}{60} = 16.67 \text{ packets/s}$$
$$\rho = 15 \text{ packets/s}$$
Since $\lambda_{avg} > \rho$ by $16.67 - 15 = 1.67$ packets/s, the system has a *long-term overload* of **1.67 packets/s**. The bucket will eventually overflow under sustained input unless we accept drops or scale $\rho$.

**Step 2 — Compute the bucket capacity $B$ from delay constraint.**
The maximum number of packets that can be in the bucket is bounded by the time it takes the bucket to drain at rate $\rho$:
$$B_{max} = \rho \cdot D_{q,max}$$
$$B_{max} = 15 \cdot 5 = 75 \text{ packets}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the burst size.**
Since the peak burst is 250 packets, but the bucket can only hold 75, the **excess $250 - 75 = 175$ packets will be dropped** at the bucket's tail (overflow drop). This is the cost of using a small buffer.

**Step 4 — Conclude the steady-state output rate.**
Because the long-term input exceeds the output rate, the steady-state output rate is constrained to the **service rate**:
$$\rho_{ss} = 15 \text{ packets/s}$$
The bucket effectively **rate-limits** the sensor stream, discarding overflow — an example of **traffic policing** for CPS safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a real CPS, the sensor node would be re-engineered to lower its $\lambda_{avg}$ (e.g., by compressing or by reporting only on change) so that $\lambda_{avg} \le \rho$ for sustainable operation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Token Bucket Burst Allowance for a CPS Actuator Command Stream

**Problem Statement:**
A token bucket has capacity $B = 20$ tokens and generates tokens at rate $r = 5$ tokens/second. The bucket is initially full. Compute the maximum number of packets that can be transmitted in the **first 4 seconds** and the **long-term maximum sustainable rate**.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Determine initial burst capacity.**
Since the bucket is initially full with $B = 20$ tokens, the **first burst can transmit 20 packets immediately** (one token per packet). After this burst, the bucket has 0 tokens.

**Step 2 — Compute tokens accumulated over 4 seconds.**
Tokens generated over the interval $\Delta t = 4$ s:
$$N_{tokens} = r \cdot \Delta t = 5 \cdot 4 = 20 \text{ tokens}$$

**Step 3 — Add accumulated tokens to the (now empty) bucket.**
Total packets transmittable in 4 s:
$$P_{total} = B_{initial} + N_{tokens} = 20 + 20 = 40 \text{ packets}$$

**Step 4 — Compute long-term sustainable rate.**
The bucket regenerates at $r = 5$ tokens/s, so the maximum long-term throughput is:
$$T_{LT} = r = 5 \text{ packets/s}$$
Equivalently, $T_{LT} = 5 \times 8 = 40$ kbps if each packet is 1 KB.

**Step 5 — Express the full piecewise function for cumulative packets $P(t)$.**
For $0 \le t \le 4$ s, given initial fullness:
$$P(t) = \min\bigl[B + r \cdot t,\; \lambda \cdot t \bigr]$$
Assuming the input is plentiful, $P(t) = B + rt = 20 + 5t$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — TCP Slow Start Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A TCP sender starts with cwnd = 1 MSS, ssthresh = 8 MSS. The RTT is 100 ms. Assume no packet loss occurs during slow start. Plot cwnd as a function of RTT number and determine cwnd after 5 RTTs.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Apply the slow-start recurrence.**
During slow start, cwnd doubles per RTT:
$$\text{cwnd}(k) = 2^{k} \cdot \text{cwnd}(0)$$

**Step 2 — Compute cwnd for $k = 0$ to $5$.**

| RTT $k$ | cwnd (in MSS) | State |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | 1 | Slow Start |
| 1 | 2 | Slow Start |
| 2 | 4 | Slow Start |
| 3 | 8 | Threshold reached (ssthresh = 8) |
| 4 | 9 | Congestion Avoidance (linear) |
| 5 | 10 | Congestion Avoidance |

**Step 3 — State-transition rule at RTT $k = 3$.**
At $k = 3$, cwnd = 8 = ssthresh. The next RTT transitions to **Congestion Avoidance**, where cwnd grows by 1 MSS per RTT:
$$\text{cwnd}(k=4) = 8 + 1 = 9 \text{ MSS}$$
$$\text{cwnd}(k=5) = 9 + 1 = 10 \text{ MSS}$$

**Step 4 — Translate to throughput at RTT 5.**
Using $T = \text{cwnd}/\text{RTT}$:
$$T_5 = \frac{10 \text{ MSS}}{0.1 \text{ s}} = 100 \text{ MSS/s}$$
If MSS = 1460 bytes:
$$T_5 = 100 \cdot 1460 \cdot 8 = 1.168 \text{ Mbps}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Leaky vs. Token Bucket

| Feature | Leaky Bucket | Token Bucket |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output behavior | Strictly constant rate $\rho$ | Bursty up to $B$, average rate $r$ |
| Burst handling | Drops excess | Allows excess (up to $B$) |
| Long-term rate | Enforced equal to $\rho$ | Enforced equal to $r$ (on average) |
| Implementation | Single counter + timer | Two counters (tokens, last update) |
| CPS Use Case | Smoothing sensor telemetry | Allowing actuator command bursts |
| Output regularity | Deterministic | Statistical |
| Reaction to overload | Drops input | Defers output (or drops) |

---

## 3.5 Derived Insight: When Congestion Control Fails (Congestion Collapse)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation of Congestion Collapse Condition:**
> Suppose the network has effective capacity $C$ bits/s, and the offered load is $L$ bits/s. The throughput $T(L)$ under congestion collapse is:
> $$T(L) = \begin{cases} L & \text{if } L \le C \\ \frac{C}{\sqrt{L/C}} & \text{if } L \gg C \end{cases}$$
> In the second regime, throughput **decreases** as offered load **increases** — because retransmissions consume capacity that would have carried new data. This is the "**Cliff**" region of the offered-load vs. throughput curve.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Knee–Cliff Congestion Behavior Curve (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisX["Offered Load increasing"]
    axisY["Throughput Y-axis"]
    knee["Knee Point: Optimal Operating Region"]
    cliff["Cliff Point: Congestion Collapse"]
    stable["Throughput Plateaus at Capacity C"]
    collapse["Throughput Drops Sharply"]
    
    axisX --> knee
    knee --> stable
    stable --> cliff
    cliff --> collapse
    
    classDef good fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#0a7a0a,color:#003300
    classDef bad fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a30000,color:#330000
    class knee,stable good
    class cliff,collapse bad
```

## 4.2 Leaky Bucket Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    source["Bursty Input Source"] --> if1{"Bucket Full?"}
    if1 -- "Yes" --> drop[Drop Overflow Packet]
    if1 -- "No" --> inc["Increment Bucket Level B"]
    inc --> bucket[("Leaky Bucket\nCapacity Bmax")]
    bucket -->|"Constant rate rho"| out[Constant Rate Output to Network]
    drop --> sink[(Discarded)]
    
    classDef bucketNode fill:#cfe7ff,stroke:#003a7a,color:#000033
    classDef procNode fill:#fff3c4,stroke:#7a5a00,color:#332200
    class bucket bucketNode
    class if1,inc procNode
```

## 4.3 Token Bucket Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gen["Token Generator\nRate r per second"] -->|"add token"| tbucket[("Token Bucket\nCapacity B")]
    tbucket -->|"tokens available"| check{"Token >= 1?"}
    check -- "Yes" --> consume["Consume One Token\nSend Packet"]
    check -- "No" --> wait["Queue Packet\nWait for Token"]
    consume --> net[Network Transmission]
    wait -.->|"wait"| check
    
    classDef bucketNode fill:#cfe7ff,stroke:#003a7a,color:#000033
    classDef procNode fill:#fff3c4,stroke:#7a5a00,color:#332200
    class tbucket bucketNode
    class check,consume,wait procNode
```

## 4.4 TCP Congestion Control State Machine (Reno Variant)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SS: cwnd = 1 MSS
    
    SS --> SS: ACK received\ncwnd = cwnd + MSS\n(exponential growth)
    SS --> CA: cwnd reaches ssthresh\n(transition to linear growth)
    SS --> SS_RECOV: Timeout Occurs\nssthresh = cwnd/2\ncwnd = 1
    
    CA --> CA: ACK received\ncwnd = cwnd + MSS/cwnd\n(linear growth)
    CA --> FR: 3 Duplicate ACKs\n(FAST RECOVERY)
    CA --> SS_RECOV: Timeout Occurs\nssthresh = cwnd/2\ncwnd = 1
    
    FR --> CA: ACK for new data\ncwnd = ssthresh\n(FAST RETRANSMIT done)
    
    SS_RECOV --> SS: Return to Slow Start
    
    note right of SS
        Slow Start:
        Exponential growth
        cwnd doubles per RTT
    end note
    
    note right of CA
        Congestion Avoidance:
        Linear growth
        cwnd grows by 1 MSS per RTT
    end note
    
    note right of FR
        Fast Recovery:
        cwnd = ssthresh
        (TCP Reno behavior)
    end note
```

## 4.5 Closed-Loop Congestion Control Feedback Architecture (CPS View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sender["CPS Source\n(Sensor / Controller)"] -->|"Data Packets"| router["Network Router\n(Bottleneck Node)"]
    router -->|"Congestion Signal:\nPacket Drop OR ECN Mark"| sender
    router -->|"Forwarded Packets"| receiver["CPS Destination\n(Actuator / Estimator)"]
    receiver -->|"ACK / Feedback"| sender
    sender -->|"Reduce cwnd / rate"| sender
    
    classDef sensorNode fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#0a7a0a,color:#003300
```
