```mermaid
    classDef routerNode fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a30000,color:#330000
    classDef actNode fill:#cfe7ff,stroke:#003a7a,color:#000033
    class sender sensorNode
    class router routerNode
    class receiver actNode
```

## 4.6 RED (Random Early Detection) Drop Probability Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    q1["Queue Length q < THmin"] --> p0["Drop Probability = 0"]
    q2["THmin <= q <= THmax"] --> pvar["Drop Probability = Pmax * (q-THmin) / (THmax-THmin)"]
    q3["Queue Length q > THmax"] --> p1["Drop Probability = 1"]
    
    classDef green fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#0a7a0a
    classDef yellow fill:#fff3c4,stroke:#7a5a00
    classDef red fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a30000
    class q1,p0 green
    class q2,pvar yellow
    class q3,p1 red
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Network Congestion
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Network congestion is the degradation of network performance that occurs when the **demand for network resources** (bandwidth, buffer space, processing capacity) **exceeds the available supply** at one or more nodes in the network. It manifests as **increased queuing delay, packet loss, and reduced throughput**. Congestion is triggered when the aggregate input traffic rate $\lambda_{in}$ at a node surpasses its service rate $\mu$, causing unbounded queue growth and eventual buffer overflow. In Cyber Physical Systems, congestion is critical because it can disrupt the deterministic timing required for control loops.

*(3 marks — definition 1, condition 1, CPS relevance 1)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Congestion Control and Flow Control
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Congestion Control | Flow Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scope | End-to-end / network-wide | Point-to-point (sender–receiver) |
| Trigger | Network queue buildup, packet loss, ECN | Receiver buffer overflow |
| Mechanism | Congestion window (cwnd) | Receiver advertised window (rwnd) |
| Purpose | Prevents network overload | Prevents receiver overload |
| Example | TCP Reno, RED, ECN | TCP sliding window rwnd |

*(3 marks — 1 per correct row, comparison conclusion 0 marks extra but adds clarity)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Comprehensive Treatment of Network Congestion

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Define network congestion. Explain the **Knee and Cliff points** on an offered-load vs. throughput curve. Discuss how this relates to the operation of a Cyber Physical System. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Leaky Bucket Algorithm**. A host generates 12000 packets/minute at a peak rate of 800 packets/second. The leaky bucket has a constant output rate of 200 packets/second. Compute: (i) the maximum burst size that can be handled without loss if the bucket capacity is 400 packets; (ii) the steady-state output rate. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
Network congestion is the condition in which the demand for network resources (link bandwidth, buffer memory, processing capacity) **exceeds** the available supply at one or more nodes, resulting in increased delay, packet loss, and reduced throughput. The mathematical condition is $\lambda_{in} > \mu$.

**Step 2 — Knee and Cliff Points (3 marks):**
- **Knee Point:** The point of maximum throughput where delay begins to rise sharply. Operating here is optimal — high throughput with low delay.
- **Cliff Point:** The point beyond which the throughput *collapses* as offered load increases. This is the **congestion collapse** region, caused by excessive retransmissions consuming capacity.
- The graph plots *Throughput* (y-axis) against *Offered Load* (x-axis). Throughput rises linearly up to the knee, plateaus, then drops past the cliff.

**Step 3 — CPS Relevance (2 marks):**
In a CPS, control loops must close within bounded time (e.g., 1 ms for a robotic arm). If the network enters the cliff region, control packets are dropped or delayed, the feedback loop becomes unstable, and the physical system can enter unsafe states (e.g., a self-driving car failing to brake). Therefore, CPS networks must be engineered to **operate at or below the knee point**, often using traffic shaping and QoS.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Leaky Bucket Explanation + Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2. Key points: bursty input, constant output rate $\rho$, finite bucket capacity $B_{max}$, overflow drops.

**Step 2 — Given Data Extraction (1 mark):**
- $\lambda_{avg} = 12000/60 = 200$ packets/s.
- $\lambda_{peak} = 800$ packets/s.
- $\rho = 200$ packets/s.
- $B_{max} = 400$ packets.

**Step 3 — Compute Maximum Burst (2 marks):**
A burst arrives at 800 packets/s. The bucket can absorb packets at the rate $\lambda_{peak} - \rho = 800 - 200 = 600$ packets/s above the drain rate. The maximum burst duration the bucket can absorb:
$$T_{burst} = \frac{B_{max}}{\lambda_{peak} - \rho} = \frac{400}{600} \approx 0.667 \text{ s}$$
Maximum burst size (input during this time):
$$N_{burst} = \lambda_{peak} \cdot T_{burst} = 800 \cdot 0.667 \approx 533 \text{ packets}$$
*Alternative interpretation (excess above $\rho$):* Maximum number of *additional* packets absorbed beyond what would be drained:
$$N_{excess} = B_{max} = 400 \text{ packets}$$ (when bucket starts empty).
**Examiner's note:** Either interpretation may be accepted; clearly state the assumption.

**Step 4 — Steady-State Output (1 mark):**
Since the long-term average input equals $\rho$:
$$T_{ss} = \rho = 200 \text{ packets/s}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Token Bucket and TCP Congestion Control

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a diagram, explain the **Token Bucket Algorithm**. Compare it with the Leaky Bucket Algorithm in terms of burst handling. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **TCP Congestion Control** mechanism. Describe the Slow Start and Congestion Avoidance phases with suitable cwnd-vs-RTT graphs. A TCP connection has initial cwnd = 1 MSS and ssthresh = 16 MSS. Assuming no loss, find the cwnd value after 6 RTTs and identify the state of the connection at each RTT. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Token Bucket Explanation + Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3. Key points: tokens generated at rate $r$, bucket capacity $B$, one token consumed per packet transmission, allows bursts up to $B$.

**Step 2 — Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Leaky Bucket | Token Bucket |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output rate | Constant $\rho$ | Bursty up to $B$, average $r$ |
| Burst handling | Drops excess input | Allows excess (up to $B$) |
| Long-term rate | Enforced as $\rho$ | Enforced as $r$ on average |
| CPS use | Sensor stream smoothing | Actuator command bursts |

**Step 3 — Burst Handling Discussion (1 mark):**
Token bucket permits initial bursts (e.g., a robot controller sending a sequence of coordinated motion commands at start-up), making it preferable for CPS applications that need to deliver *sporadic but time-critical* bursts.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — TCP Congestion Control Overview (2 marks):**
TCP uses a congestion window **cwnd** to limit unacknowledged data. The window evolves based on ACK feedback and loss signals, aiming to converge to the network's available bandwidth.

**Step 2 — Slow Start (2 marks):**
cwnd begins at 1 MSS and **doubles every RTT** (exponential). It continues until cwnd reaches **ssthresh** or a loss event occurs.

**Step 3 — Congestion Avoidance (1 mark):**
Beyond ssthresh, cwnd grows **linearly** by 1 MSS per RTT to probe for additional bandwidth cautiously.

**Step 4 — RTT-by-RTT Computation (2 marks):**
Using cwnd(k) = $2^k$ in slow start, transitioning when cwnd = ssthresh = 16:

| RTT $k$ | cwnd (MSS) | State |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | 1 | Slow Start |
| 1 | 2 | Slow Start |
| 2 | 4 | Slow Start |
| 3 | 8 | Slow Start |
| 4 | 16 | Threshold reached |
| 5 | 17 | Congestion Avoidance |
| 6 | 18 | Congestion Avoidance |

**Final cwnd after 6 RTTs = 18 MSS.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing cwnd and rwnd:** Examiners frequently award partial credit to students who mix up congestion window with receiver window. Always state *which* window your answer refers to.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** "cwnd = 16" is incomplete — write "cwnd = **16 MSS**" or convert to bytes.
> 3. **Leaky vs. Token bucket confusion:** Examiners expect students to clearly state the *burst-handling philosophy*: leaky = strict constant output; token = burst-allowing with average rate enforcement.
> 4. **Skipping the graph/curve:** In 7-mark questions, marks are explicitly allocated for diagrams. A hand-drawn or described Knee-Cliff graph earns ≥1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring CPS context:** KTU 2024 scheme questions on this topic often carry a 1–2 mark "**CPS application**" sub-part. Generic network answers that omit CPS relevance *lose those marks*.
> 6. **Slow Start math error:** Slow start doubles *per RTT*, not per ACK. Writing cwnd = $2^{k}$ where $k$ is the RTT number is correct; using the ACK count is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read Before Every Exam:**

- **Congestion Definition:** Demand exceeds supply at a network node; condition $\lambda_{in} > \mu$.
- **Knee Point:** Optimal operating region (max throughput, low delay).
- **Cliff Point:** Congestion collapse region (throughput decreases as load increases).
- **Congestion Control vs. Flow Control:** Network-wide vs. point-to-point; cwnd vs. rwnd.
- **Open-Loop:** Prevention-based — Leaky Bucket, Token Bucket, Traffic Shaping, Admission Control.
- **Closed-Loop:** Reaction-based — Monitor → Signal → Adjust. Examples: TCP Reno, RED, ECN.
- **Leaky Bucket:** Constant output rate $\rho$; drops bursts; smooths traffic.
- **Token Bucket:** Bursts allowed up to $B$; average rate $r$; tokens regenerated continuously.
- **TCP Slow Start:** cwnd = $2^k$ MSS after $k$ RTTs; exponential growth.
- **TCP Congestion Avoidance:** cwnd grows by 1 MSS per RTT; linear.
- **Timeout Reaction:** ssthresh = cwnd/2; cwnd = 1 MSS.
- **3-Dup-ACK Reaction (Fast Recovery):** ssthresh = cwnd/2; cwnd = ssthresh.
- **RED:** Probabilistic early drop; thresholds $TH_{min}$, $TH_{max}$, max probability $P_{max}$.
- **CPS-Critical:** In CPS, congestion breaks the control loop; use deterministic scheduling, TSN, and traffic shaping to operate at or below the Knee.
- **Key Trade-off:** Leaky bucket = predictable, no bursts. Token bucket = flexible, allows bursts. Choose based on whether the CPS traffic is *periodic-tolerable* or *burst-essential*.
- **Exam Trick:** When asked to "compare" two algorithms, always use a **table** + a **one-sentence CPS-context conclusion**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Real-Time Networks for CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT723 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Building Real-Time Networks for CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

A **Real-Time Network (RTN)** for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) is a deterministic communication infrastructure that guarantees bounded **end-to-end latency**, bounded **jitter**, and a guaranteed minimum **bandwidth** for the exchange of sensor, actuator, control, and coordination messages between the cyber (computation) and physical (sensing/actuation) subsystems of a CPS.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, real-time networks for CPS are characterized by the following axiomatic properties:

- **Determinism**: For every message *m*, the worst-case delivery time $D_{wc}(m)$ is provably bounded by a known constant that is independent of network load (up to a stated design limit).
- **Timeliness**: Messages are delivered before their **deadline** $D(m)$, which is derived from the underlying control loop's stability margin.
- **Synchrony**: All nodes share a common notion of time with bounded **clock skew** $\delta_{skew} \le \delta_{max}$, typically realized through protocols such as IEEE 802.1AS (gPTP) or IEEE 1588 (PTP).
- **Reliability**: Packet loss probability is bounded by $P_{loss} \le P_{max}$, with formal fault-tolerance contracts (e.g., 1oo2, 2oo3 duplexing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A real-time network in a CPS is **not** a "fast" network. It is a **predictable** network. Speed is a means; bounded timing is the contract.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart City Ambulance Corridor"

Imagine a busy city where ambulances must reach hospitals within a strict 8-minute window. A *conventional* network is like normal city traffic — sometimes fast, sometimes stuck in a jam. A *real-time CPS network* is like a city that has:

- **Dedicated green corridors** that *always* open for ambulances at the precise moment they are needed (time-triggered scheduling).
- **Traffic light pre-emption** for any emergency event (event-triggered pre-emption).
- **Synchronized traffic signals** so all intersections are coordinated to a master clock (global time synchronization).
- **Guaranteed alternative routes** if one corridor is blocked (redundancy and fault tolerance).

The **ambulance** is a control message, the **corridor** is a guaranteed time slot, the **traffic signals** are switches/routers, and the **master clock** is the grandmaster time source. The CPS network engineer "builds" these corridors mathematically — that is the essence of *building* real-time networks.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Bound for Hard CPS |
|---|---|---|
| End-to-End Latency | $L_{e2e}$ | **1 µs – 10 ms** |
| Jitter | $J$ | **< 1 µs** (motion control) |
| Clock Skew | $\delta_{skew}$ | **< 100 ns** (TSN/802.1AS) |
| Bandwidth | $B$ | **1 Mbps – 10 Gbps** |
| Packet Loss | $P_{loss}$ | **< 10⁻⁹** (functional safety) |
| Availability | $A$ | **99.9999 %** ("six-nines") |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Tag:** These six metrics are the canonical "Quality of Service (QoS) tuple" evaluated in KTU Part A 3-mark questions for this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Real-Time Network Architectures

Real-time networks are classified along two orthogonal axes:

### Axis A — Triggering Paradigm
1. **Time-Triggered (TT) Networks**
   - Transmission slots are pre-computed *offline* and assigned to a global schedule table.
   - Examples: TTEthernet (SAE AS6802), FlexRay (static segment), TSN Time-Aware Shaper (TAS, IEEE 802.1Qbv).
   - **Strength:** Provably deterministic; ideal for safety-critical CPS.
   - **Weakness:** Inflexible to topology changes; complex schedule synthesis.

2. **Event-Triggered (ET) Networks**
   - Transmission occurs when a node arbitrates for the bus upon an event.
   - Examples: Classic CAN, ARINC 429, Ethernet with CSMA/CD.
   - **Strength:** Flexible, low idle bandwidth.
   - **Weakness:** Bounded only under specific load assumptions (e.g., < 100 % utilization for CAN).

3. **Hybrid (TT+ET) Networks**
   - Mix guaranteed slots and best-effort slots. Examples: FlexRay (dual segment), TSN with TAS + Best-Effort, TTEthernet.

### Axis B — Physical Topology
1. **Bus / Linear** (CAN, FlexRay, LIN)
2. **Star** (EtherCAT, 100 Mbit Ethernet variants)
3. **Ring** (EtherCAT, PROFIBUS, Token Ring)
4. **Mesh / Switched** (Full TSN, AFDX, Time-Triggered Ethernet)
5. **Wireless Mesh** (WirelessHART, ISA 100.11a, 6TiSCH for IIoT)

## 2.2 The Latency Composition Equation

The end-to-end latency of a CPS message traversing *N* switches is decomposed as:

$$L_{e2e} = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{proc}^{(i)}}_{\text{processing}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} T_{queue}^{(i)}}_{\text{queuing}} + \underbrace{T_{trans}}_{\text{tx}} + \underbrace{T_{prop}}_{\text{propagation}}$$

Where:
- $T_{proc}^{(i)}$ = per-hop processing/forwarding delay (typically **0.5 µs – 5 µs** for a cut-through TSN switch).
- $T_{queue}^{(i)}$ = queuing delay, dominated by the **Credit-Based Shaper (CBS)** or **TAS** behaviour at hop *i*.
- $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L_{frame}}{R_{link}}$ with $L_{frame}$ = frame length in bits, $R_{link}$ = link rate in bps.
- $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v_p}$ with *d* = physical length, $v_p \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in copper.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield.** Memorize the units — examiners explicitly deduct for unitless answers.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Frame Transmission Time | $T_{tx} = \dfrac{L_{frame}}{R_{link}}$ | $L_{frame}$ [bits], $R_{link}$ [bps], $T_{tx}$ [s] |
| 2 | Worst-Case Response Time (single-hop) | $R = W + C + J$ | $W$ [s] worst-case waiting, $C$ [s] worst-case transmission, $J$ [s] release jitter |
| 3 | CAN Bit Time | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{f_{baud}}$ | $f_{baud}$ [baud] |
| 4 | CAN Worst-Case Latency | $D_{wc} = (F_{max} + 1) \cdot t_{bit} + t_{prop}$ | $F_{max}$ = worst-case frame count, $t_{bit}$ [s], $t_{prop}$ [s] |
| 5 | Rate-Monotonic Utilization Bound | $U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | *n* = task/frame count, $U_{bound}$ is dimensionless |
| 6 | Utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $C_i$ [s] WCET, $T_i$ [s] period, *U* dimensionless |
| 7 | Response-Time Recurrence | $w_{i}^{(k+1)} = B_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{w_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | $B_i$ blocking term, $hp(i)$ higher-priority set |
| 8 | Clock Offset (PTP) | $\theta = \dfrac{(t_2 - t_1) + (t_3 - t_4)}{2}$ | Four-timestamp sync; *θ* [s] |
| 9 | Round-Trip Time | $d = (t_2 - t_1) - \theta$ | Master-to-slave delay |
| 10 | Network Calculus — Delay Bound | $D(t) \le \sup_{s \le t}\{\alpha(s) - \beta(s)\}$ | $\alpha$ arrival curve, $\beta$ service curve |
| 11 | Effective Bandwidth (Exponential Bounding) | $E_b(s) = \dfrac{1}{s} \log \mathbb{E}[e^{sA(0,t)}]$ | $A$ cumulative arrivals |
| 12 | Maximum Latency (TSN TAS, single class) | $D_{max} = T_{cycle} - T_{guard}$ | $T_{cycle}$ [s] gate cycle, $T_{guard}$ [s] guard band |
| 13 | Network Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $MTBF$ [h], $MTTR$ [h], $A$ dimensionless |
| 14 | Residual Error Probability | $R = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | *p* bit-error prob, *n* bits per frame |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU TYPING TIP:** In your answer sheets, write "\vert x \vert" or "\mid x \mid" if you must show absolute value — never use the bare pipe character inside a sentence that could be confused with the markdown table delimiter.

## 2.4 Industrial-Use Real-Time Protocols — Engineer's Quick Reference

| Protocol | Standard | Latency Class | Topology | KTU Mention |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CAN | ISO 11898 | Soft RT (≤ 1 ms @ 1 Mbps) | Bus | Mandatory |
| CAN-FD | ISO 11898-1:2015 | Soft RT (≤ 100 µs) | Bus | Frequently tested |
| FlexRay | ISO 17458 | Hard RT (≤ 10 µs) | Bus/Star | Frequently tested |
| TTEthernet | SAE AS6802 | Hard RT (≤ 100 µs, zero jitter) | Switched | High-yield |
| EtherCAT | ETG.1000 | Hard RT (≤ 12.5 µs cycle) | Daisy-chain | High-yield |
| PROFINET IRT | IEC 61784-2 | Hard RT (≤ 1 ms, jitter < 1 µs) | Switched | Frequently tested |
| TSN (802.1Qcc/Qbv/Qbu) | IEEE 802.1 | Hard RT (sub-µs, 802.1Qbv) | Switched | **Most-tested in 2024** |
| AFDX (Avionics) | ARINC 664 P7 | Hard RT (≤ 500 µs) | Switched | Aerospace CPS |
| WirelessHART | IEC 62591 | Soft RT (≤ 10 ms) | Mesh | IIoT |
| 6TiSCH | IETF RFC 9030 | Soft RT (≤ 100 ms) | Wireless mesh | Industry 4.0 |
| 5G URLLC | 3GPP Rel-15+ | Hard RT (≤ 1 ms, 99.999 %) | Cellular | Emerging |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive CPS** → CAN-FD / FlexRay / Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1) in drive-by-wire and ADAS.
- **Avionics** → AFDX in Boeing 787, Airbus A380; TTEthernet in NASA Orion MPCV.
- **Industrial Robotics** → EtherCAT dominates; cycle times of 62.5 µs to 250 µs.
- **Smart Grid** → IEC 61850-9-2 (Sampled Values) over process bus with 4 kHz sampling.
- **Medical CPS** → TSN for MRI, robotic surgery (e.g., da Vinci systems).
- **Autonomous Vehicles** → TSN backbone + 5G URLLC V2X.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Response Time for a CAN Frame

**Statement (KTU 14-Mark pattern):** Derive the worst-case transmission latency for the highest-priority CAN frame, accounting for blocking from lower-priority frames and the 8-bit inter-frame space.

### Step 1 — Set up the model
The standard CAN bus uses **non-destructive bit-wise arbitration**. The *i*-th highest priority message has identifier $ID_i$ (lower ID = higher priority). Let:
- $C_i$ = worst-case transmission time of frame *i* (including stuffing, in bit times).
- $B_i$ = worst-case blocking from a single lower-priority frame that has just started transmission = $C_{i-1}^{max}$ in bit times.
- $hp(i)$ = set of frames with *strictly higher* priority than frame *i* (i.e., lower CAN ID).

### Step 2 — Write the response-time recurrence
For CAN, the worst-case waiting time $w_i$ for frame *i* is given by the fixed-point iteration:

$$w_i^{(k+1)} = B_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{w_i^{(k)} + \tau_{bit}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

The iteration starts with $w_i^{(0)} = C_i$ and converges (or diverges to indicate infeasibility) when $w_i^{(k+1)} = w_i^{(k)}$.

### Step 3 — Compute the total response time

$$R_i = w_i + C_i$$

The deadline check for schedulability is:

$$R_i \le D_i \quad \forall i$$

### Step 4 — Numerical worked example (KTU 14-mark style)
Consider three CAN messages transmitted at $f_{baud} = 1$ Mbps on a 10 m bus. The propagation delay for one bit is $t_{bit} = 1 \mu s$ and physical delay $t_{prop} \approx 50$ ns (negligible for this baud).

| Message | Period $T$ (ms) | Worst-case frame $C$ (bits) | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| $m_1$ | 2 | 130 | Highest |
| $m_2$ | 5 | 200 | Middle |
| $m_3$ | 10 | 250 | Lowest |

**Step 4.1** — Convert bits to seconds: $C_1 = 130 \mu s$, $C_2 = 200 \mu s$, $C_3 = 250 \mu s$.

**Step 4.2** — Evaluate $w_1$ (no higher-priority frames and no blocking since $m_1$ is highest):
$$w_1^{(0)} = C_1 = 130 \mu s$$
$$w_1^{(1)} = 0 + 0 = 0 \;\; \text{(no } hp, \text{ no } B \text{)}$$
Fixed point: $w_1 = 0$. Hence:
$$R_1 = 0 + 130 = 130 \mu s$$

**Step 4.3** — Evaluate $w_2$ (blocking from $m_3$ at most $C_3$):
$$w_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 200 \mu s$$
$$w_2^{(1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{200}{2000} \right\rceil \cdot 130 = 250 + 130 = 380 \mu s$$
$$w_2^{(2)} = 250 + \left\lceil \dfrac{380}{2000} \right\rceil \cdot 130 = 250 + 130 = 380 \mu s$$
Fixed point: $w_2 = 380 \mu s$. Hence:
$$R_2 = 380 + 200 = 580 \mu s$$

**Step 4.4** — Evaluate $w_3$ (blocking from nothing — it is lowest priority — but preemption by $m_1, m_2$):
$$w_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 250 \mu s$$
$$w_3^{(1)} = 0 + \left\lceil \dfrac{250}{2000} \right\rceil \cdot 130 + \left\lceil \dfrac{250}{5000} \right\rceil \cdot 200$$
$$w_3^{(1)} = 0 + 1 \cdot 130 + 1 \cdot 200 = 330 \mu s$$
$$w_3^{(2)} = 130 + \left\lceil \dfrac{330}{5000} \right\rceil \cdot 200 = 130 + 200 = 330 \mu s$$
Fixed point: $w_3 = 330 \mu s$. Hence:
$$R_3 = 330 + 250 = 580 \mu s$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** If deadlines $D_1 = 1$ ms, $D_2 = 2$ ms, $D_3 = 5$ ms, the system is schedulable. $R_i \le D_i$ for all *i*.

## 3.2 Derivation: Worst-Case Latency for a TSN Time-Aware Shaper (TAS)

**Statement:** For an IEEE 802.1Qbv TAS schedule with cycle time $T_c$ and a single protected class *A* window of length $T_A$ within each cycle, derive the upper bound on the worst-case delay of a class-A frame.

### Step 1 — Identify the worst-case arrival
A class-A frame may arrive at the **exact instant the gate closes** for class A (worst case). It must then wait until the next gate-open event.

### Step 2 — Compute the gate-open latency
The maximum time until the next open of the class-A gate is bounded by the time between the gate closing and the next gate opening. In the worst case, this equals:

$$D_{max} = T_c - T_A + T_{guard}$$

where $T_{guard}$ is the guard band reserved to prevent partial-frame transmission across gate boundaries.

### Step 3 — Add the residual transmission time
After the gate re-opens, the frame must also transmit, so the full worst-case latency is:

$$L_{wc} = (T_c - T_A + T_{guard}) + T_{tx,A}$$

### Step 4 — Worked numerical example
Suppose $T_c = 500 \mu s$, $T_A = 200 \mu s$, $T_{guard} = 10 \mu s$, and the class-A frame is 1500 B at 1 Gbps.

$$T_{tx,A} = \dfrac{1500 \times 8}{10^9} = 12 \mu s$$

$$L_{wc} = (500 - 200 + 10) + 12 = 322 \mu s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 322 µs bound is **provable** and **independent of other traffic** — this is the engineer's "contract" with the control system.

## 3.3 Derivation: PTP Clock-Offset Computation

**Statement:** A PTP master sends a Sync message at $t_1$ (master clock). The slave receives it at $t_2$ (slave clock) and records $t_2 - t_1 = 0.9984$ ms. The slave immediately sends a Delay_Req message at $t_3$ (slave clock) which the master receives at $t_4$ (master clock), with $t_4 - t_3 = 1.0016$ ms. Assume symmetric path delay. Compute clock offset *θ* and one-way delay *d*.

### Step 1 — Set up the four equations
Let *d* = one-way propagation delay (assumed symmetric) and *θ* = constant offset (slave − master). Then:

$$t_2 = t_1 + \theta + d$$
$$t_4 = t_3 - \theta + d$$

### Step 2 — Solve for *θ*
Adding both equations and rearranging:

$$\theta = \dfrac{(t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3)}{2} = \dfrac{0.9984 - 1.0016}{2} = -0.0016 \text{ ms} = -1.6 \mu s$$

### Step 3 — Solve for *d*
Subtracting both equations:

$$d = \dfrac{(t_2 - t_1) + (t_4 - t_3)}{2} = \dfrac{0.9984 + 1.0016}{2} = 1.0 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
The slave clock is ahead of the master by **1.6 µs**; the path delay is **1 ms**. The slave will correct its hardware clock by subtracting 1.6 µs. After convergence, residual skew is bounded by the PTP implementation's granularity — typically **< 100 ns** for 802.1AS hardware-assist.

## 3.4 Derivation: Rate-Monotonic Utilization Bound

**Statement (KTU 14-Mark pattern):** Prove that for *n* periodic tasks with deadlines equal to periods, Rate Monotonic Scheduling is feasible if

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

### Step 1 — Worst-case phasing
The Liu & Layland proof considers the *critical instant* — all tasks released simultaneously at $t = 0$.

### Step 2 — Critical response of task *i*
Under RMS, the worst-case response time of the lowest-priority task occurs when higher-priority tasks are released as often as possible. In the interval $[0, T_i]$:

$$W_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \dfrac{T_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

### Step 3 — Bounding the ceiling
Use the inequality $\lceil x \rceil \le x + 1$:

$$W_i \le \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left( \dfrac{T_i}{T_j} + 1 \right) C_j = T_i \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \dfrac{C_j}{T_j} + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} C_j$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and rearrange
The feasibility condition $C_i + W_i \le T_i$ combined with the worst-case phasing yields, after simplification, the Liu–Layland bound:

$$U_{bound}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

For large *n*, $U_{bound} \to \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$ (i.e., **69.31 %**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity:** $U_{bound}(1) = 1.0$, $U_{bound}(2) = 0.828$, $U_{bound}(3) = 0.779$, $U_{bound}(4) = 0.756$, $U_{bound}(\infty) = 0.6931$. This sequence appears verbatim in KTU 14-mark questions.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — CPS Real-Time Network Functional Architecture

This diagram maps the layered architecture of a real-time CPS network, showing how the physical plant, edge controller, time-aware switch fabric, and supervisory control plane inter-relate.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        sensorA["SENSOR NODE A"]
        sensorB["SENSOR NODE B"]
        actuatorA["ACTUATOR NODE A"]
        actuatorB["ACTUATOR NODE B"]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE CONTROL LAYER"]
        edgeCtl["EDGE CONTROLLER RTOS"]
        localSched["LOCAL SCHEDULER FCFS RM EDF"]
    end

    subgraph SWCORE["SWITCHED NETWORK FABRIC"]
        sw1["TSN SWITCH 1 QBV TAS"]
        sw2["TSN SWITCH 2 QBV TAS"]
        sw3["TSN SWITCH 3 QBV TAS"]
        queue1["CBS QUEUE CLASS A"]
        queue2["CBS QUEUE CLASS B"]
        queue3["BE QUEUE BEST EFFORT"]
    end

    subgraph CLOCK["TIME SYNCHRONIZATION PLANE"]
        grandmaster["GRANDMASTER CLOCK 802.1AS"]
        ptpEng["PTP TIMESTAMP ENGINE"]
    end

    subgraph SUP["SUPERVISORY LAYER"]
        scada["SCADA HMI"]
        analytics["CLOUD ANALYTICS"]
        safetyMon["SAFETY MONITOR SIL3"]
    end

    sensorA --> sw1
    sensorB --> sw1
    sw1 --> sw2
    sw2 --> sw3
    sw3 --> edgeCtl
    edgeCtl --> localSched
    localSched --> sw3
    sw3 --> actuatorA
    sw3 --> actuatorB
    grandmaster --> ptpEng
    ptpEng --> sw1
    ptpEng --> sw2
    ptpEng --> sw3
    sw1 --> queue1
    sw1 --> queue2
    sw1 --> queue3
    edgeCtl --> scada
    scada --> analytics
    safetyMon --> edgeCtl
```

**How to read the diagram (KTU board-style annotation):**
1. Sensors push periodic **Class A** (hard RT) frames into the switch fabric.
2. The **Grandmaster Clock** injects PTP Sync/Follow_Up messages that timestamp every egress port of every switch.
3. The TSN switches execute the **Time-Aware Shaper (Qbv)** gate-control list, opening Class-A queues deterministically.
4. The edge controller executes a **fixed-priority scheduler** (Rate-Monotonic or Deadline-Monotonic) and emits control commands back through the same fabric.
5. Supervisory traffic (SCADA, analytics) shares the network in a **Best-Effort** queue — it is *guaranteed* not to disturb Class-A timing.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Protocol Stack and Latency Budget Allocation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L7["APPLICATION LAYER CONTROL APP PERIOD 1 MS"]
    L6["PRESENTATION OPC UA MQTT"]
    L5["SESSION TSN STREAM ID"]
    L4["TRANSPORT UDP SRTP DTLS"]
    L3["NETWORK IPV6 6LOWPAN FOR WIRELESS"]
    L2A["DATA LINK L2 IEEE 802.1 QBV TAS"]
    L2B["DATA LINK L2 IEEE 802 1 CB FRER"]
    L1["PHYSICAL L1 100BASE T1 1000BASE T1 OPTICAL"]
    T["TIMING PLANE IEEE 802 1AS PTP GRANULARITY 100 NS"]
    S["SAFETY PLANE IEC 61508 SIL 3 ISO 26262 ASIL D"]

    L7 --> L6
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2A
    L2A --> L1
    L2B --> L1
    T -.-> L2A
    T -.-> L1
    S -.-> L7
    S -.-> L2A
```

**Annotation for the student:**
- The **vertical** path is the *data plane* — the actual frame flow.
- The **horizontal dashed** connections are *sidecar planes*: timing and safety are not "layers" in the OSI sense; they are *cross-cutting concerns* that constrain every other layer.
- In your KTU answer, when asked "draw the protocol stack for a CPS real-time network," always draw the timing plane and safety plane as **dashed sidecars**, not as horizontal layers.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — TT vs ET vs Hybrid Schedule Visualization

```mermaid
gantt
    title Gantt Style Schedule Comparison TT vs ET vs Hybrid
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section Time Triggered TT
    Slot 1 Msg A (0 to 100us)    :a1, 0, 100
    Slot 2 Msg B (100 to 200us)  :a2, 100, 100
    Slot 3 Idle Guard Band       :a3, 200, 50
    section Event Triggered ET
    Arbitration Window           :crit, active, 0, 250
    Backoff and Retransmission   :b1, 80, 170
    section Hybrid TT plus ET
    TT Slot Msg A                :h1, 0, 80
    ET Arbitration CSMA CA       :h2, 80, 120
    TT Slot Msg B                :h3, 200, 50
    ET Window Best Effort        :h4, 250, 100
```

**Reading guide for examiners:**
- In **TT**, transmission slots are *pre-allocated*; no contention.
- In **ET**, the channel is shared with arbitration; latency is a function of load.
- **Hybrid** is the dominant pattern in modern CPS: deterministic TT slots for safety-critical traffic, ET for everything else.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between time-triggered and event-triggered communication in CPS. State one example protocol for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Time-Triggered (TT) | Event-Triggered (ET) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Pre-scheduled global time slots | Occurrence of an event |
| Determinism | Provable, zero jitter (in TAS) | Probabilistic; depends on load |
| Latency bound | Exact (worst-case = cycle − slot) | Statistical or worst-case load-bound |
| Flexibility | Low; requires offline schedule synthesis | High; dynamic |
| Bandwidth efficiency | May waste idle slots | High utilization possible |
| Example | TTEthernet (SAE AS6802), TSN TAS (802.1Qbv) | Classic CAN, ARINC 429 |

(3 marks = 1 for definition each + 1 for example)

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define jitter in the context of CPS real-time networks. Why is bounded jitter more critical than low average latency for control loops?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Jitter* $J$ is the variation in end-to-end latency of messages belonging to the same flow, formally $J = L_{max} - L_{min}$ or the standard deviation $\sigma_L$ of $L_{e2e}$.

**Why bounded jitter matters (2 marks):** A control loop's stability margin is a function of the *worst observed* delay, not the average. A linear-quadratic regulator (LQR) with state-feedback gain *K* becomes unstable when the round-trip delay exceeds the loop's *Maximum Allowable Delay Interval* (MADI). Even a low-mean, high-variance latency can intermittently exceed MADI and destabilize the plant. Hence, for control, **bounded jitter is non-negotiable**; the *mean* is a secondary metric.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice Option)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the layered architecture of a real-time network for a Cyber-Physical System. Clearly label the timing plane, safety plane, and data plane. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Three periodic messages are scheduled on a CAN bus at 500 kbps. Frame lengths (including stuff bits) are $C_1 = 80 \mu s$, $C_2 = 120 \mu s$, $C_3 = 160 \mu s$. Periods are $T_1 = 1$ ms, $T_2 = 2$ ms, $T_3 = 4$ ms. Priorities are assigned by Rate-Monotonic (lower index = higher priority). Compute the worst-case response time of each message and verify schedulability. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks)

1. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the four-layer architecture (Physical → Data Link → Network → Application) with two **sidecar planes** (Timing Plane, Safety Plane) attached as dashed cross-cutting overlays. Reference Diagram 4.2 above.

2. **Identification of planes (2 marks):**
   - **Data plane** carries the actual sensor/control frames.
   - **Timing plane** (IEEE 802.1AS / IEEE 1588) ensures clock synchronization with < 100 ns skew.
   - **Safety plane** (IEC 61508 / ISO 26262) enforces fail-operational / fail-silent behaviour.

3. **Mapping to real protocols (2 marks):** For each layer, cite a representative standard: Physical = 100BASE-T1 / BroadR-Reach; Data Link = 802.1Qbv (TAS) for hard RT, 802.1Qbu (pre-emption) for express frames; Network = IPv6 with 6LoWPAN for low-power wireless; Application = OPC UA Pub/Sub over TSN.

#### Part (b) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Given:** $f_{baud} = 500$ kbps, so $t_{bit} = 2 \mu s$. The $C_i$ are already in seconds.

**Rate-Monotonic assignment (0.5 mark):** Higher rate → higher priority. Since $T_1 < T_2 < T_3$, priorities are $m_1 > m_2 > m_3$. ✓

**Compute $w_1$ (no higher priority, no blocking): (1 mark)**
$$w_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 = w_1 + C_1 = 80 \mu s$$

**Compute $w_2$ (blocking from $m_3$): (2 marks)**
$$w_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 120 \mu s$$
$$w_2^{(1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{120}{1000} \right\rceil C_1 = 160 + 0 = 160 \mu s$$
$$w_2^{(2)} = 160 + \left\lceil \dfrac{160}{1000} \right\rceil \cdot 80 = 160 + 80 = 240 \mu s$$
$$w_2^{(3)} = 160 + \left\lceil \dfrac{240}{1000} \right\rceil \cdot 80 = 160 + 80 = 240 \mu s$$
**Fixed point:** $w_2 = 240 \mu s$. Hence $R_2 = 240 + 120 = 360 \mu s$.

**Compute $w_3$ (no blocking, preemption by $m_1, m_2$): (2 marks)**
$$w_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 160 \mu s$$
$$w_3^{(1)} = \left\lceil \dfrac{160}{1000} \right\rceil \cdot 80 + \left\lceil \dfrac{160}{2000} \right\rceil \cdot 120 = 80 + 0 = 80 \mu s$$
$$w_3^{(2)} = \left\lceil \dfrac{80}{1000} \right\rceil \cdot 80 + \left\lceil \dfrac{80}{2000} \right\rceil \cdot 120 = 0 + 0 = 0$$

Wait — this gives $w_3 = 0$ only if the worst-case preemption count fits; re-evaluate over a full busy period of $m_3$:

Re-check: $m_3$ period $T_3 = 4$ ms. In one period of $m_3$, the number of $m_1$ releases is $\left\lceil 4000 / 1000 \right\rceil = 4$ and the number of $m_2$ releases is $\left\lceil 4000 / 2000 \right\rceil = 2$.

$$w_3 = 4 \cdot C_1 + 2 \cdot C_2 = 4 \cdot 80 + 2 \cdot 120 = 560 \mu s$$
$$R_3 = 560 + 160 = 720 \mu s$$

**Schedulability verification (1.5 marks):**

| Message | $R_i$ | $D_i = T_i$ | $R_i \le D_i$? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $m_1$ | 80 µs | 1000 µs | ✓ |
| $m_2$ | 360 µs | 2000 µs | ✓ |
| $m_3$ | 720 µs | 4000 µs | ✓ |

**Conclusion:** The system is **schedulable** under Rate-Monotonic priority assignment on the CAN bus.

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`**
> **(a)** Explain the IEEE 802.1Qbv Time-Aware Shaper. Derive the worst-case latency for a class-A frame in a TSN switch with cycle time $T_c$, class-A window $T_A$, guard band $T_g$, and frame transmission time $T_{tx,A}$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a PTP synchronization exchange, the master timestamps the Sync message at $t_1 = 10{:}00{:}00.000000$ s (master clock). The slave timestamps its arrival at $t_2 = 10{:}00{:}00.000\,998$ s (slave clock). The slave sends Delay_Req at $t_3 = 10{:}00{:}00.001\,002$ s (slave clock), and the master receives it at $t_4 = 10{:}00{:}00.001\,995$ s (master clock). Assume symmetric path delay. Compute (i) the clock offset *θ*, (ii) the one-way delay *d*, and (iii) the residual jitter if subsequent syncs show $\pm 50$ ns timestamp granularity. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks)

1. **Qbv concept (3 marks):** 802.1Qbv defines a *gate control list* (GCL) for each egress queue. Each queue has a **transmission gate** that is either *open* (eligible to transmit) or *closed* (frames held). The GCL cycles with period $T_c$ and provides isolated time windows for different traffic classes — this is the *Time-Aware Shaper*. Combined with 802.1Qbu *frame pre-emption*, a low-priority frame in progress can be interrupted mid-transmission to let a high-priority class-A frame through, then resumed.

2. **Diagram (1 mark):** Draw a horizontal time axis showing the cycle, with shaded Class-A window $T_A$ and unshaded Best-Effort window $T_c - T_A - T_g$. Mark $T_g$ at the boundary as a guard band.

3. **Derivation (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.2 above:
   - Worst-case arrival: at the *closing edge* of the class-A gate.
   - Worst-case wait: $T_c - T_A + T_g$.
   - Plus transmission: $L_{wc} = (T_c - T_A + T_g) + T_{tx,A}$.

#### Part (b) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Compute the four intervals (1 mark):**
- $t_2 - t_1 = 0.998$ ms
- $t_4 - t_3 = 0.993$ ms

**Clock offset *θ* (2 marks):**
$$\theta = \dfrac{(t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3)}{2} = \dfrac{0.998 - 0.993}{2} = 0.0025 \text{ ms} = 2.5 \mu s$$

The slave clock is *ahead* of the master by 2.5 µs.

**One-way delay *d* (2 marks):**
$$d = \dfrac{(t_2 - t_1) + (t_4 - t_3)}{2} = \dfrac{0.998 + 0.993}{2} = 0.9955 \text{ ms}$$

**Residual jitter (2 marks):** With timestamp granularity of ±50 ns, the residual slave-to-master skew after the sync is bounded by:
$$J_{resid} = \vert 2 \cdot \Delta t_{ts} \vert = 2 \times 50 \text{ ns} = 100 \text{ ns}$$

This is the standard "transparent clock" bound for 802.1AS.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read This Twice**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks):** Frame lengths in CAN problems are sometimes given in *bits*, sometimes in *µs*. If bits, divide by *baud rate* to get *seconds* before any arithmetic. Examiners check this.
> 2. **Blocking Term Mistake (–2 marks):** In the CAN R-T analysis, the blocking term $B_i$ is the **largest lower-priority frame**, not the sum. Many students incorrectly sum all lower-priority frames.
> 3. **Off-By-One in TAS Window (–1 mark):** The worst-case wait in Qbv is **$T_c - T_A + T_g$**, not $T_c - T_A$. Forgetting $T_g$ loses a mark.
> 4. **PTP Sign Convention (–1 mark):** A *positive* $\theta$ means the slave is *ahead* of the master. State the sign explicitly. Examiners deduct for ambiguous sign.
> 5. **Don't say "fast Ethernet" for CPS:** Saying "we use 10 Gbps Ethernet for real-time CPS" without mentioning **Qbv/Qbu/Qcc/AS** is treated as a *non-answer*. Always pair speed with *determinism mechanisms*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] A **real-time network** is *predictable*, not merely *fast*. The contract is **bounded latency and bounded jitter**.
- [ ] The canonical **QoS tuple** for CPS networks: **latency, jitter, skew, bandwidth, loss, availability**.
- [ ] **TT > ET** for hard real-time; **ET > TT** for flexibility. **Hybrid** is the modern default.
- [ ] **CAN** is soft RT; **FlexRay** and **TTEthernet** are hard RT; **TSN (802.1)** is the modern unified standard.
- [ ] **Worst-case latency** = processing + queuing + transmission + propagation.
- [ ] **Worst-case response time** $R_i$ for CAN = blocking + preemption + transmission. Use the **fixed-point iteration**.
- [ ] **Rate-Monotonic** bound: $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; limit 69.31 % as $n \to \infty$.
- [ ] **TSN TAS (Qbv)** worst-case latency = $T_c - T_A + T_g + T_{tx,A}$.
- [ ] **PTP clock offset** = $\dfrac{(t_2-t_1) - (t_4-t_3)}{2}$; **one-way delay** = $\dfrac{(t_2-t_1) + (t_4-t_3)}{2}$.
- [ ] **802.1AS** target residual skew = **< 100 ns** for hard RT TSN.
- [ ] **Wired ≠ Wireless** for hard RT: 5G URLLC and 6TiSCH are *approaching* hard-RT wireless, but wired TSN/EtherCAT still dominate the strict-deadline tier.
- [ ] **Safety plane** (IEC 61508 / ISO 26262) is a *cross-cutting* concern, **not a horizontal OSI layer**.
- [ ] In answer sheets, *always state units* (µs, ms, ns) — losing a unit often loses a full mark under strict KTU rubrics.
- [ ] When drawing protocol stacks for CPS, include the **timing plane** and **safety plane** as **dashed sidecars**.
